#  Overview {#overview .unnumbered}

Formulas serve as a major tool in pattern identification and clinical
treatment. Based on analysis of etiological factors and establishment of
treatment methods, a formula is composed of appropriate medicinals
according to the hierarchy of *Jun Cheng Zuo Shi* (Monarch, Minister,
Assistant and Envoy), doses, dosage forms and routes of administration.
As a discipline, Chinese Herbal Formulas studies the origin and
evolution of formulas, treatment methods, composition principles,
compatibility and clinical applications.

Through classic formulas, this discipline enables the student to
understand their composition principles and compatibility and then
prescribe or apply formulas in a clinical setting. Consequently, Chinese
Herbal Formulas is a bridge to link basic subjects with clinical
subspecialties.

Physicians of past generations left us a vast number of formulas. Until
the end of the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), there were more than 100,000
named formulas. Formulas are more than prescriptions in disease
treatment; they are deeply rooted in Chinese medical theory and clinical
experience. A multitude of classic formulas are information carriers of
the above-mentioned theory and clinical experience. As a result,
learning the composition, application and modification of classic
formulas enables the student to better understand the regularity behind
formulas.

To learn a formula, one needs to first understand its composition
principle, compatibility and modifications and be familiar with its
actions, indications and clinical application. One also needs to
memorize typical formulas and know the differences in formulas that
share similar ingredients, actions and indications. This can lay a solid
foundation for the further study of clinical subspecialties.

**General Introduction**

## Formulas and Treatment Methods

Treatment methods are established upon pattern identifications through
analyzing etiology and pathogenesis. They are guided by treatment
strategies. Both formulas and treatment methods are essential parts of
*Li Fa Fang Yao* (Theory, Diagnostic & Therapeutic Methods, Formulas and
Medicinals) in Chinese medicine. Treatment methods link pattern
identification with formulas. It is therefore important to be familiar
with treatment methods to learn and use formulas.

Treatment methods can be generalized or specific. Examples of
generalized methods are the 'Eight Methods' (八法*Ba Fa* in Chinese
Pinyin)---sweating (*Han Fa*), vomiting (*Tu Fa*), draining downwards
(Xia *Fa*), harmonizing (*He Fa*), warming (*Wen Fa*), clearing (*Qing
Fa*), tonifying (*Bu Fa*), and reducing (*Xiao* *Fa*). Specific
treatment methods are based on patients' clinical manifestations. Each
formula serves as a specific treatment method. This treatment method
serves as the basis of selecting a patent formula or prescribing a new
formula. Clinically, pathogenesis-directed treatment methods are
essential for the desired efficacy.

**1.1 Treatment methods guide formulas**

Treatment methods are summarized in the long process of using formulas
for disease prevention and treatment. Although developed after formulas,
treatment methods start to guide formulas and their applications once
they are established. During pattern identification and treatment, it's
necessary to analyze causative factors and determine treatment methods
before selecting or making a formula. For example, when treating a
patient with common cold, the first thing is to analyze etiological
factors by the four diagnostic methods. If the patient was diagnosed as
wind cold exterior syndrome, the treatment strategies should be to
induce sweating and release the exterior with warm, pungent herbs. Then
patent formulas or prescriptions are used for the patient. Apparently,
treatment methods come before formulas.

Physicians of past generations left us a large number of highly
effective formulas. However, these formulas need to be modified
according to the patient's constitutional difference, age, gender,
disease severity and duration. These modifications are also based on
treatment methods. In short, treatment methods guide compositions and
modifications of formulas.

**1.2 Formulas are instrumental in embodying and fulfilling the
treatment methods**

Based on pattern identification, formulas are instrumental in fulfilling
clinical treatments, and in turn, in confirming the diagnosis and
treatment methods. For example, *Si Jun Zi Tang* (*Ren Shen*, *Bai Zhu,
Fu Ling and Gan Cao*) is selected to treat patients with spleen qi
deficiency to strengthen the spleen and supplement qi. The treatment
efficacy can prove whether the diagnosis and treatment strategies are
correct.

In Chinese medicine, pattern identification and treatment involves
analyzing pathomechanism, establishing treatment strategies, and
prescribing formulas. As for the relationship between formulas and
treatment methods, "Treatment methods are the basis of formulas, pattern
identifications are the basis of treatment methods. Formulas embody and
verify treatment methods. Treatment methods guide formulas. In summary,
formulas are treatment methods".

**1.3 Common treatment methods**

Cheng Zhongling, a physician in the Qing dynasty (1644-1911) organized
and categorized the eight basic strategies known as the \'Eight Methods'
(八法*Ba Fa* in Chinese Pinyin) in his *Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical
Revelations). These eight methods are sweating (*Han Fa*), vomiting (*Tu
Fa*), draining downward (*Xia Fa*), harmonizing (*He Fa*), warming (*Wen
Fa*), clearing (*Qing Fa*), reducing (*Xiao Fa*) and tonifying (*Bu
Fa*).

**Sweating (*Han Fa*)**

The sweating strategy induces perspiration, facilitates interaction
between the *Ying*-nutrients and *We*i-defense, and disperses lung qi.
This strategy helps to expel six exogenous pathogenic factors through
sweating and thus release the exterior. Consequently, in addition to
exterior syndrome, this method is also indicated for chills, fever and
absence of sweating due to blocked skin pores, disharmony between the
*Ying*-nutrients and *Wei*-defense or unresolved chills and fever upon
sweating. For example, it applies to early stage measles before
eruption, acute edema which is more severe in the upper part of the
body, chills and fever in early stage sores/ulcers, and an exterior
syndrome in early stage malaria/dysentery. However, according to the
pathogenic nature and patient constitution, the sweating method is
subcategorized into pungent warm formulas and pungent cold formulas.
Sometimes, it is used in combination with other methods (e.g.,
tonifying, draining downward and reducing, etc.)

**Harmonizing (*He Fa*)**

This strategy is used to harmonize or moderate different parts of the
body to expel pathogenic factors and regulate functions of the zang-fu
organs. This strategy helps to release the Shaoyang syndrome when the
pathogen lies in between the interior and exterior of the body. It is
also used to harmonize zang-fu organs (e.g. treat the disharmony between
the liver and spleen, disharmony between the gallbladder and stomach or
disharmony between the stomach and intestines) and achieve yin-yang or
interior-exterior balance. This method is indicated for pathogenic
factors affecting Shaoyang, disharmony between the liver and spleen,
gastrointestinal disorders and disharmony between qi, blood,
*Ying*-nutrients and *Wei-*defense. Specifically, this method is
subcategorized into harmonizing Shaoyang, harmonizing the liver and
spleen, soothing the liver & harmonizing the stomach, and regulating
functions of the stomach and intestines.

**Draining Downwards (Xia *Fa*)**

This strategy induces bowel movements to cleanse or eliminate indigested
food, dry feces, cold accumulation, stagnant blood, phlegm, and water
retention. This allows for the removal of pathogenic factors out of the
body. This method is indicated for constipation, fecal impaction with
watery diarrhea, build-up of phlegm fluid, stagnant blood or water
retention. Due to the nature of the pathogen and patient constitution
(deficiency or excess), this method is subcategorized into draining
downward with cold-property herbs (*Han Xia*), warm-property herbs (*Wen
Xia*), moistening-property herbs (*Run Xia*), water retention-resolving
herbs (*Zhu Shui*) and tonifying herbs (*Gong Bu Jian Shi*). Sometimes,
it is used in combination with other methods.

**Clearing (*Qing Fa*)**

This strategy clears heat, reduces fire, removes toxins and cools blood
to remove pathogenic warm, heat fire and toxins. It is indicated for
seasonal febrile disease, toxic fire, damp heat and interior deficiency
heat. This method is subcategorized as follows: clearing heat from the
qi level, clearing the *Ying*-nutrient level and cooling the blood,
clearing heat and removing toxins, clearing heat of the zang-fu organs
and clearing deficiency heat. Since heat consumes yin and qi,
heat-clearing formulas often contain fluid-generating and
qi-supplementing herbs. For heat scorching yin in late stage febrile
diseases or yin deficiency with hidden heat in chronic conditions, this
method should be used in combination with yin-nourishing methods instead
of using bitter, cold herbs alone.

**Warming (*Wen Fa*)**

This strategy warms the interior to eliminate pathogenic cold. It is
indicated for interior cold syndrome due to cold attacking zang-fu
organs and/or meridians. Interior cold syndrome results from external
contraction of pathogenic factors or internal dysfunctions. Contributing
factors include cold directly attacking the interior, constitutional
yang qi deficiency or impaired yang qi due to loss of or delayed
treatment. Interior cold syndrome may involve zang or fu organs and vary
in depth and severity, this method is subcategorized into warming the
middle *jiao* to expel cold, resuscitating yang to rescue collapse, and
warming meridians to dispel cold. Because interior cold syndrome is
often concurrent with yang deficiency or qi deficiency, this method is
often used in combination with yang-warming and qi-supplementing
methods.

**Tonifying (*Bu Fa*)**

This strategy reinforces qi, blood, yin and yang of the body. It's
indicated for deficiency of zang-fu organs, qi, blood, yin and yang.
When anti-pathogenic qi is too weak to expel pathogenic factors, this
method is used in combination with other methods to supplement
anti-pathogenic qi. Common tonifying methods are subcategorized into
tonifying qi, blood, yin and yang (especially of the five-zang organs).

**Reducing (*Xiao Fa*)**

This strategy promotes digestion, circulates qi, moves blood, resolves
phlegm, drains water retention and kills parasites. It is indicated for
indigestion, qi stagnation, blood stasis, masses, water-dampness,
build-up of phlegm fluid, infantile malnutrition/parasites and
sores/ulcers or carbuncles. The reducing method and draining downward
method are both used to treat tangible pathogenic factors. However, the
draining downward method is more indicated for acute, excessive
gastrointestinal stagnation that requires fast removal, whereas the
reducing method is more indicated for build-up of qi and/or blood
stagnation in zang-fu organs, meridians and muscles (e.g. masses,
subcutaneous nodes or scrofula), which can be diagnosed as excess or
deficiency but takes time to resolve. The reducing method is often used
in combination with tonifying, draining downward, warming and clearing
methods.

**Vomiting (*Tu Fa*)**

This strategy induces vomiting to fast remove phlegm, indigested food
and poisons from the throat, thoracic diaphragm or stomach. It is
indicated for post-stroke phlegm accumulation, food/poison retention in
the stomach, mania or depression due to exuberant phlegm, inflammation
of the throat, and absence of vomiting or diarrhea in abdominal colic.
This method is rarely used today except in acute interior excess
(emergency or life-threatening) conditions. In addition, it's only used
in patients with a strong constitution, because vomiting damages stomach
qi and causes adverse reactions. This method is therefore not
recommended to use for those with a compromised immune system, women
after childbirth or pregnant women.

The eight strategies are more than treatment methods. Each strategy has
subcategories, for example, the harmonizing method contains harmonizing
Shaoyang, harmonizing the liver and spleen, soothing the liver &
harmonizing the stomach and regulating the stomach and intestines;
harmonizing the liver and spleen further contains soothing the liver &
regulating the spleen and inhibiting the liver & strengthening the
spleen. Furthermore, due to the complicated condition of patients, one
formula often contains two or more strategies. According to Cheng
Zhongling in his *Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical Revelations), "the eight
strategies exist in one single strategy; a myriad of strategies exist
within the eight strategies".

Today, there are additional strategies in clinical treatment, such as
orifice-opening, mind-tranquilizing, astringing and wind-expelling
methods.

## 2．Formulas and Compatibility {#formulas-and-compatibility .unnumbered}

An effective formula contains compatibility of appropriate ingredients.
Compatibility is a key part and an essential form of formulas.

### **2.1 Concept of compatibility** {#concept-of-compatibility .unnumbered}

Compatibility refers to combined use of two or more ingredients. This
combination is anything but random and disorderly. It is logical and
purposeful. The seven combination interactions were first recorded in
the *Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing* (Shen Nong's Classic of the Materia
Medica). The commonly used six interactions are mutual reinforcement,
mutual assistance, mutual restraint, mutual suppression, mutual
inhibition, and mutual antagonism. This indicates that combination of
different ingredients can have impact on medicinal effect as well as the
patient's body. As a result, it's important to have rational combination
to enhance the efficacy, eliminate or alleviate adverse reactions of
some ingredients, and achieve better outcomes.

### **2.2 Purpose and meaning of compatibility** {#purpose-and-meaning-of-compatibility .unnumbered}

Ingredients in a formula vary in property and function. The purposes of
compatibility are to adjust their peculiarity, inhibit their toxicity,
enhance or modify their functions to eliminate or moderate potential
adverse reactions, extend the indications, and thus obtain the best
results.

Rational compatibility has two meanings: synergism (to maximize the
medicinal efficacy) and attenuation of toxicity (to minimize the toxic
effects).

#### **2.2.1 Synergetic effects** {#synergetic-effects .unnumbered}

When used in combination, ingredients that share similar functions can
enhance the medicinal potency, for example, *Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum)
and *Zhi Mu* (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae) in *Bai Hu Tang* (White Tiger
Decoction) work in combination to clear heat and drain fire; and *Da
Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and *Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas) in *Da
Cheng Qi Tang* (Major Purgative Decoction) work in combination to drain
heat downward. Assistant ingredients that share common functions can
enhance the efficacy of principal ingredients, for example, *Huang Qi*
(Radix Astragali) and *Fu Ling* (Poria) in *Fang Ji Huang Qi Tang*
(Stephania Root and Astragalus Decoction) work in combination to
supplement qi and resolve water retention; *Shao Yao* (Radix Paeoniae)
and *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae) in *Shao Yao Gan Cao Tang*
(Peony and Glycyrrhizae Decoction) work in combination to relieve spasm
and alleviate pain. These synergetic effects are known as mutual
reinforcement and mutual assistance.

#### **2.2.2** **Extension of the indications** {#extension-of-the-indications .unnumbered}

An herbal formula produces efficacies that cannot be obtained by single
herbs. For example, *Chai Hu* (Radix Bupleuri) and *Huang Qin* (Radix
Scutellariae) in *Xiao Chai Hu Tang* (Minor Bupleurum Decoction) act to
harmonize *Shaoyang* only when used in combination; *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus
Cinnamomi) and *Shao Yao* (Radix Paeoniae) in *Gui Zhi Tang* (Cinnamon
Twig Decoction) act to harmonize the *Ying*-nutrients and *Wei-*defense
only when used in combination.

In addition, supplemented herbal formulas are indicated for more
extensive conditions. For example, *Si Jun Zi Tang* (Four Gentlemen
Decoction) supplements qi and strengthens the spleen, *Si Wu Tang* (Four
Substances Decoction) tonifies blood and regulates menstruation, and *Er
Chen Tang* (Two Matured Substances Decoction) dries dampness and
resolves phlegm. Clinically, these basic formulas can be modified to
treat more conditions. For instance, *Si Jun Zi Tang* is indicated for
spleen qi deficiency (signs and symptoms include a pale, sallow
complexion, a low, weak voice, shortness of breath, fatigue, poor
appetite, loose stools, and a deficient, flaccid pulse). It becomes *Yi
Gong San* (Special Achievement Powder) with addition of *Chen Pi*
(Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae); the modified formula is indicated for
spleen qi deficiency coupled with qi stagnation (signs and symptoms
include chest/gastric fullness, vomiting and diarrhea). It becomes *Liu
Jun Zi Tang* (Six Gentlemen Decoction) with further addition of *Ban
Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae). The modified formula is indicated for
spleen/stomach qi deficiency coupled with phlegm-dampness retention
(signs and symptoms include nausea, vomiting, chest/gastric fullness,
and coughs with profuse, thin, and white phlegm). It becomes *Xiang Shao
Liu Jun Zi Tang* (Costusroot and Amomum Six Gentlemen Decoction) with
additional *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) and *Shao Ren* (Fructus
Amomi). The modified formula is indicated for spleen/stomach qi
deficiency coupled with phlegm-qi stagnation (signs and symptoms include
vomiting, stomach fullness, and abdominal pain/distension). Apparently,
new formulas modified from single herbs or basic formulas can be used to
treat more extensive conditions.

#### **2.2.3 Alleviation/elimination of toxic/side effects** {#alleviationelimination-of-toxicside-effects .unnumbered}

Some ingredients can control or moderate the harsh or toxic properties
of ingredients in a formula, and thus alleviate or eliminate the adverse
reactions. This is known as mutual suppression (i.e., the toxic or side
effects of one ingredient are alleviated or moderated by another
ingredient) or mutual restraint (i.e., one ingredient can alleviate or
moderate the toxic or side effects of another ingredient), for example,
*Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) restraints *Sheng Jiang* (Rhizoma
Zingiberis Recens) and *Sheng Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens)
suppresses *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae). In both ways, *Sheng Jiang*
(Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens) alleviates and eliminates the toxicity of
*Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae). Similar examples include *Chang Shan*
(Radix Dichroae) and *Bing Lang* (Semen Arecae); and *Wu Tou* (Radix
Aconiti) and *Bai Mi* (white honey). Alternatively, some ingredients
moderate or alleviate harsh properties of other ingredients, for
example, *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae) moderates the harsh
properties of *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and *Fu Zi* (Radix
Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata); and *Sha Ren* (Fructus Amomi) alleviates
the greasy properties (affecting the spleen) of *Shu Di Huang* (Radix
Rehmanniae Praeparata).

"All medicines have toxic/side effects". One single formula contains
multiple factors in alleviating these toxic/side effects, such as
selection of *Daodi* (genuine) medicinals, processing methods, dosage,
dosage forms, decoction, and methods of administration. In addition,
there are numerous ways to alleviate or moderate toxic/side effects
through combination of ingredients, for example, *Gan Sui* (Radix
Kansui), *Yuan Hua* (Flos Genkwa), and *Da Ji* (Radix Knoxiae) in *Shi
Zao Tang* (Ten Jujubes Decoction) resolve water retention and have harsh
and toxic effects. There are two ways to moderate or alleviate their
toxic/side effects. One is to grind the same dose of the three
ingredients into powders, enabling the total dose to be equivalent to
the normal dose of single herbs. In this way, the combination can
enhance the efficacy and at the same time, alleviate toxic/side effects.
The other is to add *Da Zao* (Fructus Jujubae) to strengthen the spleen,
benefit the stomach and moderate the harsh or toxic properties of other
ingredients.

In summary, compatibility is essential to herbal formulas. Rational,
scientific combination of different ingredients can produce synergetic
effects, extend the indications, and alleviate the toxic/side effects.
Consequently, it is of great significance to clinical efficacy and
safety.

## 3. Formulas and compositions {#formulas-and-compositions .unnumbered}

### **3.1 Basic structure of a formula**  {#basic-structure-of-a-formula .unnumbered}

The basic structure of a formula includes four types of
ingredients---monarch (*Jun*), minister (*Cheng*), assistant (*Zuo*) and
envoy (*Shi*). These four parts are explained as follows:

Monarch(s): The key and principal ingredient(s) that have the greatest
effect upon the pattern/disease. They are often large in dosage, strong
in potency and fewer in number.

Minister(s): Two functions: 1) aids the monarch(s) in treating the
pattern/disease; 2) acts as the main ingredient directed against
associated pattern/disease.

Assistant(s): Three functions: 1) reinforces the effect of the
monarch(s) or minister(s) or directly treats a less important aspect of
a pattern/disease (*Zuo Zhu*); 2) moderates or eliminates the toxicity
of the monarch(s) or minister(s), or moderates their harsh properties
(*Zuo Zhi*); 3) has an effect that is opposite to that of the monarch(s)
(*Fan Zuo*).

Envoy(s): Two functions: 1) guides the ingredients to specific meridians
or body areas to be treated; 2) harmonizes and integrates the actions of
the other ingredients.

To further clarify the roles of monarch(s), minister(s), assistant(s)
and envoy(s), let's take *Ma Huang Tang* (Ephedra Decoction) as an
example. Recorded in the *Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*),
*Ma Huang Tang* is indicated for wind-cold exterior excessive syndrome
(chills, fever, general ache, absence of sweating, panting, a thin,
white tongue coating and a superficial, tense pulse). The pathogenesis
lies in external wind cold affecting the dispersing of lung qi. The
treatment strategies are to induce sweating with warm, pungent herbs,
disperse lung qi, and relieve panting. The basic structure of this
formula is analyzed as follows:

Monarch (*Ma Huang*): Warm and pungent. It induces sweating, releases
the exterior, expels wind cold, disperses lung qi, and thus relieves
panting.

Minister (*Gui Zhi*): Warm, sweet and pungent. It relaxes muscles,
dispels cold, assists *Ma Huang* in inducing sweating and releasing the
exterior, warms meridians, harmonizes the *Ying*-nutrients and
alleviates general aches.

Assistant (*Xing Ren*): Bitter and warm. It bears down lung qi and
assists *Ma Huang* in relieving panting.

Envoy (*Zhi Gan Cao*): Sweet and warm. It coordinates the other
ingredients.

It can be seen that a formula is associated with pathogenesis, treatment
strategy, and individual conditions. A good formula covers basic
structures and takes all aspects into consideration.

In summary, the hierarchy of monarch(s), minister(s), assistant(s) and
envoy(s) is directly based upon their roles. However, not all formulas
cover four types of ingredients and some ingredients may have more than
one role. The ingredients and structure of a formula depend on disease
severity, treatment strategy and medicinal functions. Generally, monarch
ingredient(s) are indispensable: fewer in number and bigger dose than
the other three types. This principle also applies to big (containing
numerous ingredients) or compound formulas.

### **3.2 Variations of formulas** {#variations-of-formulas .unnumbered}

Before selecting a classical formula, it's essential to conduct pattern
identification and modify the formula according to the patients'
constitutional differences, gender, age, climate, geographic features
and disease severities. With regard to classical formulas, "one shall
not rigidly copy the formula but understand the principle and one shall
not rigidly copy the ingredients but comprehend the reason".

#### **3.2.1 Modifications of the ingredients** {#modifications-of-the-ingredients .unnumbered}

The efficacy of a formula is associated with its ingredients. Removal or
addition of some ingredients may change the functions of a formula. The
purpose of modification is to enable the formula to be a better fit for
the patients. This often refers to removal or addition of less important
(assistant) ingredients but retaining the most important ingredients,
such as *Gui Zhi Tang* (Cinnamon Twig Decoction), *Gui Zhi Jia Hou Pu
Xing Zi Tang* (Cinnamon Twig Decoction Plus Officinal Magnolia Bark and
Apricot Kernel), *Gui Zhi Jia Ge Geng Tang* (Cinnamon Twig Decoction
Plus Pueraria) and *Gui Zhi Qu Shao Yao Tang* (Cinnamon Twig Decoction
without Peony) (table 1). All of these formulas are indicated for
Taiyang wind stroke syndrome (wind intolerance, fever, and spontaneous
sweating) and share the same monarch ingredient (*Gui Zhi*);
modifications of minister and assistant ingredients are made to address
different symptoms. Apparently, it's important to be aware that a
modified formula has to share a similar pathogenesis and syndrome as the
original one.

#### **3.2.2** **Variations in dosage** {#variations-in-dosage .unnumbered}

As dosages of ingredients in a formula are directly associated with its
efficacy and potency, variations in these dosages may change the
compatibility interactions among the ingredients and subsequently,
change the actions and indications of this formula. For example, *Si Ni
Tang* (Frigid Extremities Decoction) and *Tong Mai Si Ni Tang*
(Meridian-Unblocking for Frigid Extremities Decoction) contain the same
ingredients---*Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata), *Gan Jiang*
(Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
Praeparata cum Melle); however, with a small dose of *Gan Jiang* and *Fu
Zi*, *Si Ni Tang* acts to resuscitate yang and rescuecollapse and is
indicated for cold limbs, cold intolerance, lassitude, diarrhea, an
extremely thready or deep, slow and thready pulse due to internal cold
resulting from yang deficiency, while with a big dose of *Gan Jiang* and
*Fu Zi*, *Tong Mai Si Ni Tang* acts to resuscitate yang, repel yin,
recover pulsation and rescue collapse and is indicated for cold limbs,
absence of cold intolerance, diarrhea with undigested food in the stool
and an extremely faint pulse due to yin cold expelling yang (table 2).
Another example is *Xiao Cheng Qi Tang* (Minor Purgative Decoction) and
*Hou Pu San Wu Tang* (Three Ingredients Decoction with Officinal
Magnolia Bark). The two formulas contain the same ingredients---*Da
Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei), *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus)
and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis). With 12g of *Da Huang*
(Monarch), 3 pieces of *Zhi Shi* (Minister) and 6g of *Hou Pu*
(Assistant), the former is indicated for mild Yangming Fu-organ syndrome
due to excessive heat in the stomach/intestines. With 24g of *Hou Pu*
(Monarch), 5 pieces of *Zhi Shi* and 12g of Da Huang (Assistant), the
latter is indicated for constipation and abdominal fullness/pain due to
blocked flow of qi. The dosage of *Da Huang* is 1:4. Despite the same
dosage of *Da Huang*, the former is taken twice , while the latter is
taken three times. As a result, the two formulas have different actions
and indications (table 3).

Despite the difference in dosages of some ingredients, *Si Ni Tang* and
*Tong Mai Si Ni Tang* share similar compatibility interactions and are
therefore indicated for similar symptoms (same pathogenesis but in
different severities). As for *Xiao Cheng Qi Tang* and *Hou Pu San Wu
Tang*, the changes in dosages actually modify the compatibility
interactions and are therefore indicated for different pathogenesis and
symptoms. In summary, the increase or decrease in dosages of ingredients
may simply change the potency of the formula and sometimes change its
actions and indications.

#### **3.2.3 Variations in dosage forms** {#variations-in-dosage-forms .unnumbered}

As dosage forms vary in functions, variations in dosage forms may change
the efficacy and indications of formulas. Decoction produces fast and
strong effects. Pills and powders produce slow but lasting effects. For
example, *Li Zhong Wan* (Middle *Jiao*-Regulating Pill) is indicated for
deficiency cold of the spleen and stomach; it can be modified to
decoction to obtain a fast and strong effect in acute and severe
conditions. Likewise, for mild conditions, decoction can be modified to
pills to obtain a slow but lasting effect. The *Shang Han Lun*
(*Treatise on Cold Damage*) records that "*Li Zhong Wan* \[equal parts
of *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma
Atractylodis Macrocephalae), *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Gan
Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae)\] has no strong effect as decoction
form" (table 4). Another example is *Di Dang Tang* (Resistance
Decoction) and *Di Dang Wan* (Resistance Pill), which can be mutually
modified according to disease severities.

**Table 4 Differentiation between *Li Zhong Wan* (Middle**
***Jiao*-Regulating Pill) and *Ren Shen Tang* (Ginseng Decoction)**

  -------------- ----------------- ------- ------ ------- -------------------- -------------
  **Formulas**   **Ingredients**                          **Indications**      **Remarks**

                 Ren Shen          Gan     Bai    Zhi Gan                      
                                   Jiang   Zhu    Cao                          

  Li Zhong Wan   9g                9g      9g     9g      Deficiency cold in   Take 1 pill
                                                          the middle *jiao*,   (egg yolk
                                                          abdominal pain,      size)
                                                          chronic diarrhea, no 
                                                          thirst and           
                                                          salivation           

  Ren Shen Tang  9g                9g      9g     9g      Deficiency cold in   Take one
                                                          the upper and middle decoction 3
                                                          *jiao*, chest        times
                                                          tightness and a      
                                                          sensation of qi      
                                                          gushing up to the    
                                                          chest from the       
                                                          costal area          
  -------------- ----------------- ------- ------ ------- -------------------- -------------

The above administration methods, dosage and dosage forms can be used
alone or in combination. Clinically, physicians apply their variations
to individualized situations to achieve the best therapeutic effect.

## 4．Formulas and Dosage Forms  {#formulas-and-dosage-forms .unnumbered}

Dosage forms refer to the physical form in which medication will be
delivered into the body. Chinese medicine has a long history in this
regard. The *Huang Di Nei Jing* (*The Yellow Emperor's Inner Classic*)
recorded several types of dosage forms---decoction, pills, powder,
paste, medicated liquor and elixir. Following the advance in the
pharmaceutical industry, new dosage forms have been developed, for
example tablets, granules or injections. Characteristics and preparation
of common dosage forms are briefly introduced as follows:

**\[Decoction\]** A decoction is a prepared liquid for oral use. The
process involves soaking processed herbs in water or wine and decocting
for some time. Externally, it can be used for bathing, fumigation and
mouthwash. Its advantages are fast absorption, fast efficacy and
flexible modifications. A decoction is often used for patients with
severe or unstable conditions. Its limitations are ingestion of a large
quantity of liquid, incomplete release or easy evaporation of active
ingredients, and inconvenient to carry.

**\[Powder\]** A powder form can be taken orally or applied topically.
For oral use, a powder is dissolved in warm water before drinking.
Alternatively, it can be directly swallowed, such as, *Qi Li San*
(Anti-Bruise Powder). Sometimes, a powder can be decocted in water
(boiled powder), such as *Yin Qiao San* (Lonicera and Forsythia Powder).
Its advantages are easy preparation, fast absorption, and easy to use
and carry. For external use, a powder can be applied topically to the
wound surface or affected sites, such as *Jin Huang San* (Golden Yellow
Powder) and *Sheng Ji San* (Flesh-Generating Powder). Some extremely
fine powder forms are used for eyes and throat, such as *Ba Bao Yan Yao*
(Eight Treasure Eye-Benefiting Powder) and *Bing Peng San* (Borneolum
and Borax Powder).

**\[Pill\]** A pill is a ball-shaped solid form of fine medicinal
powders coupled with excipients. Unlike a decoction, a pill is
characterized by slow absorption, long-term efficacy and ease of use and
transportation. A pill is often used for chronic deficiency conditions,
such as *Liu Wei Di Huang Wan* (Six Ingredients Rehmannia Pill).
However, some pills cannot be decocted, as they have harsh property and
contain aromatic, orifice-opening and toxic ingredients, such as *An
Gong Niu Huang Wan* (Peaceful Palace Bovine Bezoar Pill) and *San Wu Bei
Ji Wang* (Three Ingredients Emergency Pill). Common pill forms are
honeyed pill, water pill, paste pill and condensed pill.

**\[Honeyed bolus\]** A honeyed bolus is made from fine medicinal powder
and honey (as the excipient). It is moist in nature and can be big or
small. Its advantages include long-term efficacy, nourishing, and taste
modification. As a result, honeyed boluses are often used for chronic
deficiency conditions, such as *Gui Pi Wan* (Spleen-Restoring Pill) and
*Shi Quan Da Bu Wan* (Perfect Major Supplementation Pill).

**\[Water pill\]** A water pill is often small and made from fine
medicinal powder and (cold, boiled or distilled) water. Alternatively,
alcohol, vinegar, honeyed water or medicinal juice can be used as
excipients. Compared with a honeyed bolus, a water pill disintegrates
and dissolves more rapidly and thus has faster absorption and efficacy.
It can be used for a variety of medical conditions, such as *Jin Suo Gu
Jing Wan* (Golden Lock Essence-Securing Pill), *Bao He Wan*
(Harmony-Preserving Pill) and *Que Ju Wan* (Constraint-Resolving Pill).

**\[Paste pill\]** A paste pill is often small and made from fine
medicinal powder and rice paste, flour paste or medicated leaven paste.
It is hard, strongly adhesive and slowly disintegrates and dissolves. A
paste pill can achieve long-term efficacy, reduce adverse reactions of
toxic ingredients, and minimize irritation to the gastrointestinal
tract, such as *Shi Zao Wan* (Ten Jujubes Pill) and *San Wu Bei Ji Wan*
(Three Ingredients Emergency Pill).

**\[Condensed pill\]** A condensed pill is made from concentrated paste
of medicinal decoction coupled with other medicinal powder through
drying, grinding and mixing with water, honey or medicinal juice. Its
advantages are a small dose, high-concentration of active ingredients
and a small dose of administration. Condensed pills can be used for a
variety of conditions.

Other dosage forms include wax-wrapped pills, water-honeyed pills,
pellets and dripping pills.

**\[Medicated paste\]** A medicated paste is made from decocted
medicinals with water or vegetable oil. It can be taken orally or
applied topically. There are three types for oral use---fluid extract,
extractum, and condensed decoction. There are two types for topical
application---ointment and plaster. The fluid extract and extractum are
often used in combination with other preparations, such as mixture,
syrup, granules and tablets. Condensed decoction and external paste are
introduced as follows:

**\[Condensed decoction\]** A condensed decoction, also known as oral
thick paste, is a semi-liquid form made from condensed medicinal
decoction and processed honey/sugar. Its advantages are pleasant taste,
small size, high content, nourishing, and easy to use. Condensed
decoction is often used for patients with chronic deficiency conditions,
such as *E* *Jiao Bu Xue Gao* (Blood-Tonifying Paste with Donkey-hide
Gelatin) and *Ba Zhen Yi Mu Gao* (Eight Treasure Paste with Mothorwort).

**\[Medicated ointment\]** A medicated ointment is a viscous, semi-solid
preparation (for external application) made from fine medicinal powder
and ointment bases. Emulsifying-based ointment is applied to the skin,
mucous membranes or wound surface. External application of ointment
allows for a slow absorption and long-term efficacy. It is often used
for painful, swollen sores/ulcers, burns, and scalds, such as *Jin Huang
Gao* (Golden Yellow Ointment).

**\[Medicated plaster\]** A medicated plaster, also known as a thin
plaster, is a solid preparation that can be spread when heated. The
process involves frying medicinals with vegetable oil, decocting for
some time, removing the residue, mixing with massicot, and then cooling
down. It can be used for topical and systematic conditions from
sores/ulcers, traumatic injuries, and Bi-impediment syndrome to low back
pain or abdominal pain, such as *Gou Pi Gao* (literally means 'dog skin
plaster'; however, today instead of dog skin, patches of linen and
fabric are often used in a medicinal herbal plaster).

**\[Medicated wine/liquor\]** Medicated wine/liquor can be taken orally
or applied topically. Since wine/liquor circulates blood, unblocks
meridians and induces the property of other ingredients, medicated
wine/liquor is orally used to expel wind, unblock collaterals and
reinforce the body, such as *Feng Shi Yao Jiu* (Medicated Wine/Liquor
for Rheumatic/Rheumatoid Conditions) and *Wu Ji Pi Jiu* (Medicated
Wine/Liquor with Eleutherococcus Root Bark). Topically, medicated
wine/liquor can expel wind, circulate blood, alleviate pain, and resolve
swelling.

**\[Elixir\]** Elixir can be taken orally or applied topically. Orally
used elixirs have no fixed dosage forms. They are named elixirs (*Dan*
in Chinese, in the form of pellets or powder) because of their precious
ingredients or significant efficacies, such as *Zhi Bao Dan* (Supreme
Jewel Elixir) and *Huo Luo Dan* (Meridian-Activating Elixir). Externally
elixirs are alchemy powder forms from medicinal minerals. They are often
applied to wound surfaces for sores/ulcers and carbuncles.
Alternatively, they can also be made into medicated sticks/threads or
paste, such as *Hong Sheng Dan* (Wound-Healing Orange Red Crystalline
Powder) and *Bai Jiang Dan* (Wound-Healing White Crystalline Powder).

**\[Medicated tea\]** A medicated tea is crude medicinal powder or
block-shaped preparation. It can be used as tea in boiling water or
after decoction. Medicated tea is often used for common cold,
indigestion, or diarrhea. Recently, it has also been used for wellness
and weight loss, such as *Wu Shi Cha* (Midday Tea) and *San Hua Jian Wei
Cha* (Three-Flower Weight Loss Tea).

\[**Distillate medicinal water\]** Distillate medicinal water often
contains volatile components. It is often used as beverage or summer
heat-clearing drink, such as *Jin Yin Hua Lu* (Distilled Liquid of
honeysuckle flower).

**\[Pastille/troche\]** A pastille/troche is solid form (shaped like a
spindle, cylinder or bar) of fine medicinal powder or coupled with
adhesive agents. It can be taken orally or applied topically. Orally, it
can be ground into powder or juice. Externally, it can be ground into
juice to be applied to the affected sites, such as *Zi Jin Ding*
(Purple-Gold Pastille) and *Wan Ying Ding* (Pastille for Pediatric
Fever, Restlessness, susceptibility to fright, mouth/tongue ulcerations,
and swollen gum/throat).

**\[Medicated roll\]** A medicated roll is made from fine medicinal
powder and mulberry paper. When inserted into a sore/ulcer or fistula,
it helps to remove dead tissues, regenerate flesh and heal the wound,
such as *Hong Sheng Dan Yao Tiao* (Wound-Healing Orange Red Medicated
Roll).

**\[Medicated thread\]** A medicated thread is a dried preparation from
soaking and decocting silk or cotton thread in medicinal liquid. Through
its mild corrosive action and mechanical ligation, a medicated thread
helps to drain pus and speed up atrophy/dropping of a neoplasm. As a
result, it is often used for fistula, hemorrhoids or neoplasm.

**\[Suppository\]** A suppository is a solid dosage form made from fine
medicinal powder and bases. It is inserted into the rectum or vagina,
where it dissolves or melts and exerts local or systemic effects. It
acts to kill parasites/worms, stop itching, moisten and astringe. A
suppository can be used to deliver both systemically and locally acting
medications. Through rectal/vaginal mucosa absorption, 50%-70%
medications directly enter the systematic circulation without passing
through the liver. This can reduce the 'first pass effect' of drug
metabolism in the liver and thus moderate toxic and side effects on the
liver. In addition, it can avoid the effect of GI fluids on medication
as well as irritation to the gastric mucosa. Commonly used suppositories
are *Kang Fu Xiao Yan Shuan* (Gynecological Anti-Inflammatory
Suppository) and *Xiao Zhi Shuan* (Hemorrhoid-Eliminating Suppository).
A rectal suppository is especially convenient for infants, such as *Xiao
Er Jie Re Shuan* (Children's Fever-Reducing Suppository).

**\[Granules\]** Granules are dry or block-shaped preparations made from
medicinal extracts and excipients. They are orally taken with warm
water. Their advantages are a fast efficacy, a small size and ease of
use. Commonly used granules are *Gan Mao Tui Re Ke Li* (Fever-Reducing
Granules for Common Cold) and *Ban Lan Gen Ke Li* (Isatis Root
Granules).

**\[Tablet\]** A tablet comprises a mixture of fine medicinal powder and
excipients, usually in powder form, pressed or compacted from a powder
into a solid dose. Its advantages are that it allows for an accurate
dosage and a small size. Sugar coating is necessary for tablets that
have an unpleasant taste. Enteric coating is necessary for its
dissolution or disintegration in the intestinal environment. Commonly
used tablets are *Yin Qiao Pian* (Lonicera and Forsythia Tablet) and
*Shuang Huang Lian Pian* (Scutellariae and Forsythia Tablet). Other
tablet forms include buccal tablets/throat lozenges, effervescent/carbon
tablets and sustained-release tablets.

**\[Syrup\]** Syrup is prepared by adding an appropriate concentration
of sucrose or sugar to condensed medicinal decoction. Because it is
sweet and fast to be absorbed, syrups are easy to use for children, such
as *Zhi Ke Tang Jiang* (Cough-Relieving Syrup) and *Xiao Er Jian Wei
Tang Jiang* (Infantile Stomach-Benefiting Syrup).

**\[Oral liquid/solutions\]** Oral liquid/solutions are prepared by
extracting medicinals with water or other solvents. Their advantages are
that it allows for a small dose, fast absorption, and ease of use.
Healthcare or tonic oral liquid/solutions have become popular over
recent years, such as *Ren Shen Feng Wang Jiang Kou Fu Ye* (Ginseng
Royal Jelly Oral Liquid) and *Qu Ju Di Huang Kou Fu Ye* (Lycium Berry,
Chrysanthemum and Rehmannia Oral Liquid).

**\[Injectable solution/suspension\]** An injectable solution/suspension
is a liquid form through medicinal extraction, refinery and preparation.
It can be used for subcutaneous, intramuscular or intravenous injection.
Its advantages are that it allows for an accurate dosage and rapid
efficacy. It is often used for emergencies. Since it does not enter the
digestive system, an injectable solution/suspension is often used for
unconscious patients, such as *Qing Kai Ling Zhu She Ye* (Heat-Clearing,
Toxin-Removing and Mind-Calming Injectable Solutions) and *Sheng Mai Zhu
She Ye* (Pulse-Engendering Injectable Solutions).

Other dosage forms include capsules, moxibustion preparations,
compression formulas, enemas, liniments and aerosols. New dosage forms
may emerge in the future. In clinical settings, physicians can choose
appropriate forms according to patients' conditions.

## 5．Formulas and Preparation Methods {#formulas-and-preparation-methods .unnumbered}

Preparation methods are equally important as administration methods for
the efficacy of formulas. Even with an accurate diagnosis, the right
formula, compatible ingredients and correct dosage forms, inappropriate
preparation and administration methods can affect the efficacy.

### **5.1 Decoction method**  {#decoction-method .unnumbered}

This is mainly about the preparation of decoction. As decoction is the
most common dosage form, it's important to use appropriate decoction
method according to the nature of ingredients and patients' conditions.

#### **5.1.1 Decoction containers** {#decoction-containers .unnumbered}

A pottery jar or earthenware pot are good decoction containers.
Enamelware or stainless steel products can also be used. However,
ironware or copperware should be avoided, because chemical changes may
occur when some ingredients were heated with copper or iron, which may
reduce solubility. In addition, the container should be big enough to
allow for boiling medicinals within the pot. It's advisable to cover the
pot during decoction to prevent rapid evaporation and subsequent
incomplete dissolution or excessive volatilization of active
ingredients.

#### **5.1.2 Decoction water** {#decoction-water .unnumbered}

Tap water, well water and distilled water can be used for decoction. In
ancient times, people used running water, spring water, water
ladle-stirred water (with bubbles) and rice-washed water. Sometimes,
alcohol alone or combined water and alcohol are used to decoct. One dose
needs decocting two or three times. The water volume is associated with
the dosage, medicinal nature and decoction time---more water for the
first decoction (3-5 cm above the ingredients) than the second and third
decoction. Each decoction volume should be between 150 and 180 ml.

#### **5.1.3 Heat control during decoction** {#heat-control-during-decoction .unnumbered}

As for heat control during decoction, there is 'strong fire' and 'gentle
fire'. The former means a high flame, whereas the latter means a low
flame. Generally, the decoction starts with a strong fire and switches
to gentle fire after being brought to the boil. A short decoction time,
strong fire, and a small amount of water are used for formulas that
release the exterior and drain downward, whereas a longer decoction
time, gentle fire and a relatively large amount of water . It's not
advisable to use overcooked decoction, as it may cause adverse
reactions.

#### **5.1.4 Decoction procedure** {#decoction-procedure .unnumbered}

Prior to decocting, soak the ingredients for 20-30 minutes in water to
allow for dissolution of active ingredients. It's necessary to specify
ingredients that require special decocting.

Decoct first: 1) Solid shells or minerals are often first decocted for
20 minutes. These include *Gui Ban* (Plastrum Testudinis), *Bie Jia*
(Carapax Trionycis), *Shi Jue Ming* (Gypsum Fibrosum), *Sheng Mu Li*
(Concha Ostreae), *Dai Zhe Shi* (Haematitum), *Sheng Long Gu* (Os
Draconis), *Sheng Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) and *Ci Shi* (Magnetitum);
2) Dusty ingredients such as *Zao Xin Tu* (Terra Flava Usta) and *Nuo
Dao Gen* (Radix Oryzae Glutinosae); 3) Light plants such as *Lu Gen*
(Rhizoma Phragmitis) and *Xia Ku Cao* (Spica Prunellae).

Decoct later: Aromatic ingredients only need to be decocted for
approximately 5 minutes to prevent the scattering of active ingredients,
such as *Bo He* (Herba Menthae), *Sha Ren* (Fructus Amomi) and *Dou Kou*
(Semen Myristicae). For draining downward, *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma
Rhei) needs to be decocted for 10-15 minutes. All these ingredients are
also soaked prior to decocting.

Decoct in packet: Some ingredients need to be decocted in a packet,
because they may cause the medicinal liquid to become turbid, irritate
the throat or become too sticky on the bottom of the container. These
include *Chi Shi Zhi* (Halloysitum Rubrum), *Hua Shi* (Talcum), *Che
Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis), *Xuan Fu Hua* (Flos Inulae) and *Pu Huang*
(Pollen Typhae).

Decoct separately: Precious ingredients need to be decocted separately
to preserve their active ingredients. For example, *Ren Sheng* (Radix et
Rhizoma Ginseng) needs to be placed in a covered bowl and decocted for
1-2 hours; *Ling Yang* *Jiao* (Cornu Saigae Tataricae) needs to be
decocted separately for 2 hours or ground into juice or fine powder.

Melting: Gel, sticky and soluble ingredients need to be melted
separately before mixing with other ingredients, such as *E* *Jiao*
(Colla Corii Asini), *Gui Ban* *Jiao* (Colla Testudinis Plastri) and
*Feng Mi* (Mel).

Mixing with water: Aromatic or precious ingredients need to be ground
into fine powder to mix with medicinal liquid or warm water, such as
*Niu Huang* (Calculus Bovis), *She Xian*g (Moschus) and *Hu Po*
(Succinum). Powder forms, medicinal powder and medicinal juice also need
to be taken with water, such as *Qi Li San* (Anti-Bruise Powder), *San
Qi Fen* (Pseudoginseng Root Powder) and *Sheng Ou Zhi* (Lotus Root
Juice).

In addition, it's advisable to squeeze the decoction dregs to preserve
the remaining liquid and improve the leaching rate of active
ingredients.

### **5.2 Administration of decoction** {#administration-of-decoction .unnumbered}

Administration methods of decoction may affect the clinical efficacy.

#### **5.2.1 Time of administration**  {#time-of-administration .unnumbered}

The time of administration is associated with the disease location,
severity, syndrome differentiation and medicinal category. Generally,
the decoction should be taken 1 hour before meals to allow for fast
absorption. However, formulas that may cause gastrointestinal irritation
should be taken after means to prevent side effects. Patients with
chronic conditions should take the decoction at regular time intervals;
however, those with acute, severe conditions do not have to wait until
regular time intervals. Formulas that reinforce the body or drain
downward should be taken with an empty stomach. Formulas that
tranquilize the mind should be taken before sleep. Formulas for malaria
should be taken 2 hours before paroxysm. Some formulas require specific
time of administration, for example, *Shi Zao Tang* (Ten Jujubes
Decoction) should be taken between 1 and 3 am and *Ji Ming San* (Powder
to take at Cock's Crow) should be taken between 3 and 5 am.

#### **5.2.2 Method of administration** {#method-of-administration .unnumbered}

Generally one takes one dosage (combining liquid from decocting the
medicinals 2 or 3 times) per day, 2-3 times a day. However, on special
occasions, patients can take a draught of medicine (a quantity ordered
as a single dose) or take a couple of times a day. Alternatively,
patients can drink medicinal tea to maintain the efficacy or two doses a
day to enhance the efficacy. Pills, powders, pastes and medicinal
wine/liquor are often taken 2-3 times a day.

In most cases, a decoction should be taken when it is warm. There are
exceptions though, for example, patients with heat syndrome are
recommended to take cold-property formulas when they are cold, or vice
versa; in severe cases, patients should take a cold-property formula
when it is warm or vice versa to prevent 'rejection (vomiting)' after
taking the decoction.

It's worth noting that formulas that contain harsh-property or toxic
ingredients should be taken from a small dose to prevent toxic reactions
or damage to anti-pathogenic qi.

For patients who feel nauseous or vomit after taking a decoction, it's
necessary to take the following measures: add some ginger to the liquid;
take some ginger juice first; clean the tongue with fresh ginger; chew
some dried tangerine/orange peel; take the decoction when it is cold; or
take a small amount of decoction each time over multiple times. Nasal
feeding is used for patients who have lost consciousness or experienced
difficulty swallowing.

#### **5.2.3 Precautions**  {#precautions .unnumbered}

Precautions after taking the decoction help to improve clinical efficacy
and speed up recovery. For example, formulas that induce sweating and
release the exterior should be taken when they are warm and paients are
advised to stay away from wind afterwards. In case of no or inhibited
sweating, patients are advised to eat some warm porridge or put on more
clothes/blankets. Profuse sweating, a pale face, and an extremely faint
pulse are signs of yang depetion due to excessive sweating. It's
essential to take emergency measures to resuscitate yang and rescue it
from collapse. For persistent diarrhea after taking formulas that drain
downward, it's important to stop the decoction and eat cold porridge or
drink boiled water. For severe abdominal pain, persistent diarrhea,
vomiting, profuse sweating, palpitations and shortness of breath due to
qi collapse upon loss of fluids, it's essential to take emergency
measures to supplement qi and rescue it from collapse. As formulas that
resolve water retention and drain downward may damage the spleen and
stomach, it's advisable to eat rice soup or a light diet to protect the
spleen and stomach. Additionally, it's necessary to tell the patients to
avoid overexertion and keep a peaceful mind.

#### **5.2.4. Dietary advice**  {#dietary-advice .unnumbered}

Inappropriate diet may aggravate or induce other complications that
comprise the efficacy of a treatment or trigger adverse reactions. This
mainly manifests in two aspects. One is from the disease. For example,
patients with edema should limit ingestion of salt, patients with
diabetes should avoid sweet food, and patients with diarrhea should
avoid oily food. The other is from the ingredients. For example,
patients who take formulas that contain ginseng should avoid radish,
patients who take the formula that contain *Tu Fu Ling* (Rhizoma
Smilacis Glabrae) should avoid tea, and patients who take formulas that
contain *Jing Jie* (Herba Schizonepetae) should avoid globefish or
leather carp.

## Appendix: Textual research on doses in ancient formulas {#appendix-textual-research-on-doses-in-ancient-formulas .unnumbered}

Because the system of measurement varied in different historical
periods, the doses in historical formulas are greatly different from the
doses we use today. In ancient times, the units of measurement were
*Shu*, *Zhu*, *Liang* and *Jin* (without *Fen*). In the Jin dynasty
(AD266-420 ), 1 *Zhu*=10 Shu, 1 *Fen*=6 *Zhu*, 1 *Liang*=4 *Fen*, and 1
*Jin*=16 *Liang* (i.e., using *Zhu*, *Fen, Liang* and *Jin* as units of
measurement). In the Song dynasty (AD960-1279), 1 *Li*=10 *Hao*, 1
*Fen*=10 *Li*, 1 *Qian*=10 *Fen*, 1 *Liang*=10 *Qian*, 1 *Jin*=16
*Liang* (using *Liang, Qian, Fen, Li* and *Hao* as units of
measurement). The measurement system in the Song dynasty has also been
adopted in the Yuan, Ming and Qing dynasties (AD1206-1911). As a result,
the unit of measurement "Fen (as in 1 *Fen*=10 *Li*)" is different from
"*Fen* (as 1 *Liang*=4 *Fen*)" in the Jin dynasty. In the Qing dynasty
(AD1644-1911), the unit of weight was *Ku Ping* and later changed to
*Shi Cheng*.

The historical capacity units included *Hu*, *Dou*, *Sheng*, *He* and
*Shao* in decimal numeral system: 1 *He*=10 *Shao*, 1 *Sheng*=10 *He*, 1
*Dou*=10 *Sheng* and 1 *Hu*=10 *Dou*. However, these varied in different
dynasties. See the annexed table (the 1960 edition of *Pharmaceutics*
edited by Nanjing Pharmaceutical College) for reference.

The historical units of measurement in weighing medicinal powder
included *Dao Gui*, *Fang Cun Bi,* *Qian B*i and *Yi Zi*. Actually *Fang
Cun Bi* was a spoon. When used as a unit of measurement, it meant to
place the medicinal powder on a full spoon (but the powder does not
fall). *Qian Pi* was a coin. When used as a unit of measurement, it
meant to place the medicinal powder on a five-Zhu coin (so tht the
powder does not fall). The medicinal powder on half of the coin was
known as 0.5 *Qian Bi*. *Yi Zi* literally means one Chinese character.
When used as a unit of measurement, it meant to place the medicinal
powder on one character of a four-character coin (*Kai Yuan Tong Bao* in
Chinese Pinyin). Just like *Fang Cun Bi*, *Dao Gui* was also a spoon
used to measure medicinal powder. One *Fang Cun Bi* approximates 5
*Fen*, 1 *Qian Bi* approximates 3 *Fen* and 1 *Yi Zi* approximates 1
*Fen* (herbal powder is relatively lighter). In addition, an analogy
method was used for measurement of pills, for example, 1 egg yolk=1
bolus=40 Phoenix tree seeds=80 soybeans=160 red beans=480 hemp
seeds=1,440 sesame seeds

Despite the textual researches on doses in ancient formulas, there is no
conclusion yet. The units of measurement in the Han and Jin dynasties
were smaller than those we use today. This also explains the large doses
at that time. This text retained the original doses in classical
formulas to better understand their compatibility, structure and
variations. However, it's essential to modify the doses according to the
*Pharmacopoeia of People\'s Republic of China*, geographic features,
individual constitutional differences, climate, and patient conditions.

The State Council of China stipulated that Chinese medicine
prescriptions use 'gram (g)' as a national standard unit of measurement
as of January 1, 1979. The conversion between hexadecimal numeral system
and national standard units of measurement is as follows (the mantissa
is removed during unit conversion):

1 *Jin* (16 *Liang*) = 0.5kg = 500g

1 *Shi Liang* = 31.25g

1 *Shi Qian* = 3.125g

1 *Shi Fen* = 0.3125g

1 *Shi Li* = 0.03125g

> **Annexed table: Scales and measurements in past dynasties**

+----------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
| **Time**       | **         | *          | **         | *          |

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. How do you understand the relationship between formulas and       |
| treatment methods?                                                    |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. What are the eight methods (*Ba Fa* in Chinese Pinyin)? Describe  |
| the concept and indications of these eight strategies.                |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Describe the concept and significance of compatibility in         |
| formulas.                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| 4\. Describe the basic structure of formula compositions.             |
|                                                                       |
| 5\. Describe the concepts and characteristics of decoctions, pills,   |
| powder and paste.                                                     |
|                                                                       |
| 6\. Describe the cautionary notes in preparation and administration   |
| of decoctions.                                                        |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 1 Formulas that Release the Exterior**

Formulas that release the exterior induce sweating, relieve muscle
soreness, and/or promote the eruption of measles. They are therefore
indicated for exterior syndromes. These formulas fall under the
'sweating' category of the major eight treatment methods.

Typical signs and symptoms of an exterior syndrome include chills,
fever, headache or muscle aches, a white or yellow tongue coating, and a
superficial pulse. Exterior syndromes occur when the six external
pathogenic factors affect the surface of the body, i.e. the
*Wei*-defensive qi. During its early stage, pathogenic factors mainly
affect the superficial areas of the body, the treatment strategy is to
dispel pathogenic factors and prevent them from entering the interior
using light, pungent herbs that have a dispersing capacity.

Based on the pathogenic nature (cold/heat) and individual constitutional
differences, the exterior-releasing formulas are divided into three
categories---pungent and warm exterior-releasing formulas, pungent and
cold exterior-releasing formulas and qi-supplementing exterior-releasing
formulas. In addition, modified exterior-releasing formulas also apply
to an early stage of measles, sores/ulcers, edema, malaria and
dysentery.

Since most exterior-releasing herbs are light and have a dispersing
capacity, it's advisable to increase the soaking time but decrease the
decoction time; to drink the decoction when it is warm and avoid
exposure to wind afterwards. Mild sweating is good; however, too much
sweating may consume qi and yin. It's also important for the patients to
stay away from raw, cold, and oily food as these foods may decrease
absorption and compromise the treatment effect.

**1.1 Release the exterior with pungent, warm herbs**

Pungent and warm exterior-releasing formulas are indicated for exterior
wind-cold syndrome. Clinical signs and symptoms include chills with
absence of sweating, fever, coughing, headache, neck stiffness, muscle
ache, fatigue, a thin white tongue coating and a superficial/tense or
superficial/delayed pulse. Pungent, warm herbs that have a dispersing
capacity include *Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae), *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus
Cinnamomi), *Qiang Huo* (Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii) and *Xiang Ru*
(Herba Moslae). Herbs that bear-down lung qi, stop coughing and resolve
phlegm, such as *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum) and *Ban Xia*
(Rhizoma Pinelliae) are used in combination.

Typical formulas are *Ma Huang Tang* (Ephedra Decoction), *Gui Zhi Tang*
(Cinnamon Twig Decoction), *Jiu Wei Qiang Huo Tang* (Nine-Herb Decoction
with Notopterygium) and *Xiao Qing Long Tang* (Minor Green Dragon
Decoction).

***Ma Huang Tang* (Ephedra Decoction)**

*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| M      | > 麻黄 | *má    | 9g | Herba Ephedrae                     |
| onarch |        | huáng* |    |                                    |
+========+========+========+====+====================================+
| Mi     | > 桂枝 | *guì   | 6g | Ramulus Cinnamomi                  |
| nister |        | zhī*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 杏仁 | *xìng  | 6g | Semen Armeniacae Amarum            |
| stants |        | rén*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy  | >      | *zhì   | 3g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|        | 炙甘草 | gān    |    | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|        |        | căo*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ma Huang* induces sweating, releases the exterior, disperses
the lung and relieves coughing.

Minister: *Gui Zhi* induces sweating, relaxes muscle, warms and unblocks
meridians as well as fascilitating the effect of *Ma Huang* in releasing
the exterior and inducing sweating.

Assistants: *Xing Ren* bears down lung qi, stops coughing and works in
combination with the effect of *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi* in dispersing
qi.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* moderates the strong diaphoretic action of *Ma
Huang* and *Gui Zhi* in inducing sweating and harmonizes the other
ingredients.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Induces sweating, releases the exterior, disperses
lung qi, and stops coughing.

**\[Indications\]** Excessive wind-cold exterior syndrome: chills,
fever, absence of sweating, coughing, headache, general ache, a thin
white tongue coating and a superficial, tense pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for excessive
wind-cold exterior syndrome. Wind-cold attacks the surface of the body
and binds yang qi, leading to chills. Internal obstruction of yang qi
causes fever. Absence of sweating occurs when cold blocks the skin
pores. Obstructed flow of meridian qi causes headache and general ache.
Failure of lung qi to descend causes cough. A thin, white tongue coating
and a superficial, tense pulse are typical signs indicative of an
exterior cold syndrome. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
induce sweating, release the exterior, disperse lung qi and stop
coughing.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and esymptoms: Chills, fever, no sweating,
cough and a superficial, tense pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For mild wind-cold syndrome, decrase the dose of *Ma Huang* or
    remove *Gui Zhi* and add *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae) and
    *Jing Jie* (Herba Schizonepetae) to remove wind and release the
    exterior.

-   For severe productive cough, remove *Gui Zhi* and add *Ban Xia*
    (Rhizoma Pinelliae) and *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae) to
    resolve phlegm and stop cough.

3\. Modern use: Common cold, flu, bronchitis, bronchial asthma and
pneumonia that can be diagnosed as excessive wind-cold exterior
syndrome.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for spontaneous
sweating and deficiency of yin blood.

**\[Annex formula\]** *San Niu Tang* (Rough and Ready Three Decoction)
from the *Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the
Taiping Imperial Pharmacy)

\[Ingredients\] *Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae), *Xing Ren* (Semen
Armeniacae Amarum) and *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae)

\[Functions\] Disperses lung qi and releases the exterior

\[Indications\] Common cold due to contraction of wind: a stuffy nose,
hoarse voice, cough and chest tightness.

Out of the ingredients in *Ma Huang Tang*, *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi*
induce sweating and release the exterior; *Ma Huang* and *Xing Ren*
disperse lung qi and stop coughing. This formula is indicated for
wind-cold exterior syndrome chills, fever, absence of sweating, cough
and a superficial, tense pulse due to wind-cold exterior syndrome. By
removing *Gui Zhi* from *Ma Huang Tang* and modifying the preparation
method and parts of the herbs, *San Niu Tang* acts to disperse lung qi
and stop coughing rather than induce sweating and release the exterior.
It's indicated more for cough due to wind-cold exterior syndrome.

***Gui Zhi Tang* (Cinnamon Twig Decoction)**

*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| M      | > 桂枝 | *guì   | 9g | Ramulus Cinnamomi                  |
| onarch |        | zhī*   |    |                                    |
+========+========+========+====+====================================+
| Mi     | > 白芍 | *bái   | 9g | Radix Paeoniae Alba                |
| nister |        | sháo*  |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 生姜 | *shēng | 9g | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens          |
| stants |        | jiāng* |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
|        | > 大枣 | *dà    | 3  | Fructus Jujubae                    |
|        |        | zăo*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy  | >      | *zhì   | 6g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|        | 炙甘草 | gān    |    | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|        |        | căo*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Gui Zhi* is pungent and has a dispersing capacity. It removes
wind, dispels cold and releases the exterior.

Minister: *Bai Shao* is acid and has an astringing tendency. It stops
sweating and is used in combination with *Gui Zhi* to harmonize the
*Ying*-nutrients and *Wei-*defense.

Assistants: *Sheng Jiang* assists *Gui Zhi* in inducing sweating and
releasing the exterior, it harmonizes the stomach and stops vomiting;
*Da Zao* assists *Bai Shao* in harmonizing the *Ying*-nutrients,
generates fluids and tonifies blood.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* harmonizes the other ingredients.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Induces sweating, releases the exterior, and
harmonizes the *Ying*-nutrients and *Wei*-defense.

**\[Indications\]** Deficient wind-cold exterior syndrome: headache,
fever, sweating, wind intolerance, nasal congestion, dry heaving
(retching), absence of thirst, a white tongue coating and a superficial,
delayed or superficial, weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for deficient
wind-cold exterior syndrome. Wind cold attacks the surface of the body
and the struggle between *Wei*-defensive qi and pathogenic wind causes
fever and wind intolerance. Wind opens the skin pores and causes
*sweating*, which is explained in the *Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold
Damage*) as disharmony between the *Ying*-nutrients and *Wei*-defense. A
white tongue coating, no thirst and a superficial, delayed pulse are
typical signs indicative of an exterior wind-cold syndrome. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to induce sweating, release the exterior,
disperse lung qi and stop coughing. Wind often attacks the upper body
and disturbs the clear yang, causing headache. Wind cold attacking the
exterior affects the descending of lung/stomach qi, causing nasal
congestion and dry heaving. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
induce sweating, release the exterior and harmonize the *Ying*-nutrients
and *Wei*-defense.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, wind intolerance, sweating
and a superficial delayed pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For neck and back stiffness, add *Ge Gen* (Radix Puerariae Lobatae)
    to generate fluids and relax the tendons.

-   For cough and dyspnea, add *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis)
    and *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum) to bear down qi and relieve
    cough/dyspnea.

3\. Modern use: Common cold, flu, and upper respiratory tract infection
that can be diagnosed as deficient wind-cold exterior syndrome.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for excessive
wind-cold exterior syndrome.

***Jiu Wei Qiang Huo Tang* (Nine-Herb Notopterygium Decoction)**

*Ci Shi Nan Zhi* (*Hard-Won Knowledge*) by Zhang Yuansu

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
| M      | > 羌活 | *qiāng   | 6g    | Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii  |
| onarch |        | huó*     |       |                                |
+========+========+==========+=======+================================+
| Mi     | > 防风 | *fáng    | 6g    | Radix Saposhnikoviae           |
| nister |        | fēng*    |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 苍术 | *cāng    | 6g    | Rhizoma Atractylodis           |
|        |        | zhú*     |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 细辛 | *xì xīn* | 2g    | Radix et Rhizoma Asari         |
| stants |        |          |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 白芷 | *bái     | 3g    | Radix Angelicae Dahuricae      |
|        |        | zhĭ*     |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 川芎 | *chuān   | 3g    | Rhizoma Chuanxiong             |
|        |        | xiōng*   |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 生地 | *shēng   | 3g    | Radix Rehmanniae               |
|        |        | dì*      |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 黄芩 | *huáng   | 3g    | Radix Scutellariae             |
|        |        | qín*     |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān     | 3g    | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae  |
|        |        | căo*     |       |                                |
+--------+--------+----------+-------+--------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Qiang Huo* expels cold, releases the exterior, removes wind,
resolves dampness and relieves joint pain. It is an essential herb to
remove wind, cold and dampness of the upper body.

Minister: Pungent, sweet in taste and mildly warm in property, *Fang
Feng* removes wind, resolves dampness, dispels cold and relieves pain.
Pungent, bitter in taste and dry warm in property, *Cang Zhu* drains
dampness and releases the exterior.

Assistants: *Xi Xin* and *Bai Zhi* dispel cold, remove wind and relieve
pain. *Xi Xin* enters the Shaoyin meridian and *Bai Zhi* enters the
Yangming meridian. With a tendency to ascend, *Chuan Xiong* circulates
blood, relieves pain and enters the Shaoyang and Jueyin meridians.
*Sheng Di* cools blood. *Huang Qin* clears heat and moderates the
dry-warm property of other herbs.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* harmonizes the other ingredients.

This formula dispels cold, drains dampness, and at the same time, clears
interior heat. In addition, five herbs enter six meridians.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Dispels cold, resolves dampness and clears interior
heat.

**\[Indications\]** External contraction of wind, cold and dampness
coupled with interior heat: Chills, fever, headache, neck stiffness,
general ache, a bitter taste, mild thirst, a white or slightly yellow
coating and a superficial pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for external
contraction of wind, cold and dampness coupled with interior heat. Wind
cold binding the Wei-defensive yang causes chills and fever. Absence of
sweating occurs when cold blocks the skin pores. Dampness obstructing
the flow of meridian qi and blood causes headache, neck stiffness and
general ache. Interior heat causes a bitter taste and mild thirst. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to expel wind/cold, drain dampness
and clear interior heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chills, fever, headache, absence
of sweating, general ache, a bitter taste and mild thirst.

2\. Modifications

-   For absent interior heat, remove *Sheng Di* and *Huang Qin*.

-   For severe joint pain due to dampness, double the dose of *Qiang
    Huo* and add *Du Huo* (Radix Angelicae Pubescentis), *Wei Ling Xian*
    (Radix et Rhizoma Clematidis) and *Sang Ji Sheng* (Herba Taxilli) to
    facilitate the effect of draining dampness and relieving pain.

-   For severe headache, identify the location of pain and then increase
    the dose of herbs to enter the related meridian.

3\. Modern use: Common cold, flu, rheumatic arthritis and migraine that
can be diagnosed as external contraction of wind, cold and dampness
coupled with interior heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for wind-cold
exterior syndrome or internal heat due to yin deficiency.

***Xiao Qing Long Tang* (Minor Green Dragon Decoction)**

*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
| M      | > 麻黄 | *má      | 9g | Herba Ephedrae                     |
| onarch |        | huáng*   |    |                                    |
+========+========+==========+====+====================================+
|        | > 桂枝 | *guì     | 9g | Ramulus Cinnamomi                  |
|        |        | zhī*     |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
| Mi     | > 干姜 | *gān     | 6g | Rhizoma Zingiberis                 |
| nister |        | jiāng*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
|        | > 细辛 | *xì xīn* | 6g | Radix et Rhizoma Asari             |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 半夏 | *bàn     | 9g | Rhizoma Pinelliae                  |
| stants |        | xià*     |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
|        | >      | *wŭ wèi  | 6g | Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis      |
|        | 五味子 | zĭ*      |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
|        | > 芍药 | *sháo    | 3g | Radix Paeoniae                     |
|        |        | yào*     |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
|        | > 黄芩 | *huáng   | 3g | Radix Scutellariae                 |
|        |        | qín*     |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy  | >      | *zhì gān | 3g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|        | 炙甘草 | căo*     |    | Praeparata cum Melle               |
+--------+--------+----------+----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi* expel cold and mutually reinforce
each other. In addition, *Ma Huang* disperses lung qi and relieves
dyspnea.

Ministers: *Gan Jiang* and *Xi Xin* assist *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi* in
releasing the exterior and help to warm lung qi and drain fluid
retention.

Assistants: *Ban Xia* drains dampness, resolves phlegm and relieves
dyspnea. Because all aforementioned herbs are warm and pungent, which
may impair the *Wei*-defense and consume fluids and blood, *Wu Wei Zi*
is combined to astringe lung qi and *Shao Yao* is combined to harmonize
the *Ying*-nutrients and nourish blood.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* supplements qi, harmonizes the middle *jiao* and
coordinates other herbs.

Characteristics of ingredients in this formula

1\) Combination of dispersing and astringing: *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi*
expels wind/cold; *Bai Shao* nourishes blood, astringes yin and
moderates the diaphoretic action of *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi*. 2)
Combination of opening and closing: *Gan Jiang*, *Xi Xin* and *Ban Xia*
assist *Ma Huang* and *Gui Zhi* in dispersing lung qi and resolving
fluid retention in the lung and work with *Wu Wei Zi* to astringe the
lung, stop cough and protect lung qi from being damaged by the opening
and dispersing properties of other herbs.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Releases the exterior, dispels cold, warms lung qi and
resolves fluid retention in the lung.

**\[Indications\]** External contraction of wind cold coupled with
internal cold fluid retention: Chills, fever, absence of sweating, chest
