discomfort, cough/dyspnea, profuse thin and clear phlegm, facial
puffiness, edema in lower limbs, a white and slippery tongue coating and
a superficial pulse. Alternatively, orthopnea and body ache may be
present.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for external
contraction of wind cold and internal retention of cold fluid. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to release the exterior, expel
cold, warm lung qi and drain fluid retention. Cold binding the
*Wei*-defense and blocking the skin pores causes chills, fever, absence
of sweating and general ache. External cold stirring the internal fluid
causes cough with profuse thin, clear phlegm. Fluid retention below the
heart obstructs the flow of qi and causes chest discomfort. Water
retention affecting the heart causes an inability to lie flat
(orthopnea). Water retention affecting the skin causes facial puffiness
and edema in lower limbs. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
release the exterior, expel cold, warm lung qi and drain fluid
retention.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chills, fever, absence of
sweating, cough/dyspnea, profuse thin and clear phlegm, a white and
slippery tongue coating and a superficial pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For absent interior heat, remove *Sheng Di* and *Huang Qin*.

-   For severe joint pain due to dampness, double the dose of *Qiang
    Huo* and add *Du Huo* (Radix Angelicae Pubescentis), *Wei Ling Xian*
    (Radix et Rhizoma Clematidis) and *Sang Ji Sheng* (Herba Taxilli) to
    facilitate the effect of draining dampness and relieving pain.

-   For severe headache, identify the location of pain and then increase
    the dose of herbs to enter the related meridian.

3\. Modern use: Acute attack of chronic bronchitis, bronchial asthma and
senile emphysema that can be diagnosed as external cold with internal
fluid retention.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for unproductive
cough due to yin deficiency, yellow, sticky phlegm, a yellow tongue
coating, thirst and a rapid pulse. The elderly or those with a weak
constitution should use with caution.

**1.2 Release the exterior with pungent, cool herbs**

Pungent and cool exterior-releasing formulas are indicated for exterior
wind-heat syndrome or an early stage of febrile disease. Clinical signs
and symptoms include fever, mild wind/cold intolerance, scratchy or sore
throat, thirst, cough, a thin and white or mild yellow tongue coating,
and a superficial and rapid pulse. Common pungent, cool herbs that
release the exterior include *Sang Ye* (Folium Mori), *Ju Hua* (Flos
Chrysanthemi), *Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae Japonicae), *Liao Qiao*
(Fructus Forsythiae) and *Bo He* (Herba Menthae).

Typical formulas are *Sang Ju Yin* (Mulberry Leaf and Chrysanthemum
Beverage), *Yin Qiao San* (Lonicera and Forsythia Powder) and *Ma Huang
Xing Ren Gan Cao Shi Gao Tang* (Ephedra, Apricot Kernel, Gypsum and
Licorice Decoction).

***Sang Ju Yin* (Mulberry Leaf and Chrysanthemum Beverage)**

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (*Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| M      | > 桑叶 | *sāng  | 1.5g   | Folium Mori                    |
| onarch |        | yè*    |        |                                |
+========+========+========+========+================================+
|        | > 菊花 | *jú    | 3g     | Flos Chrysanthemi              |
|        |        | huā*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| Mi     | > 桔梗 | *jié   | 6g     | Radix Platycodonis             |
| nister |        | gĕng*  |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 杏仁 | *xìng  | 6g     | Semen Armeniacae Amarum        |
|        |        | rén*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 薄荷 | *bò    | 2.5g   | Herba Menthae                  |
| stants |        | hé*    |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 连翘 | *lián  | 5g     | Fructus Forsythiae             |
|        |        | qiào*  |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 芦根 | *lú    | 6g     | Rhizoma Phragmitis             |
|        |        | gēn*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān   | 2.5g   | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae  |
|        |        | căo*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: Sweet, bitter in taste and cool in property, *Sang Ye* and *Ju
Hua* act to dispel wind, clear heat and benefit the head and eyes. *Sang
Ye* enters the lung meridian, moistens the lung and relieves cough. *Ju
Hua* dispels wind, clears heat and removes toxins.

Ministers: *Jie Geng* is neutral in property, enters the lung meridian,
and disperses lung qi. Bitter in taste and warm in property, *Xing Ren*
bears down lung qi and relieves cough/dyspnea.

Assistants: With a tendency to ascend, *Bo He* removes wind and clears
heat of the upper *Jiao*. Bitter in taste and mildly cold in property,
*Liao Qiao* clears heat, removes toxins and expels wind. Sweet in taste
and cold in property, *Lu Gen* clears heat, generates fluids and stops
thirst.

### Envoy: *Gan Cao* clears heat, removes toxins and coordinates other herbs. It works in combination with *Jie Geng* to benefit the throat, and with *Xing Ren* to stop cough. {#envoy-gan-cao-clears-heat-removes-toxins-and-coordinates-other-herbs.-it-works-in-combination-with-jie-geng-to-benefit-the-throat-and-with-xing-ren-to-stop-cough. .unnumbered}

According to Wu Jutong, a well-known physician in the Qing dynasty
(1644-1911 AD), this formula consists of light herbs and is indicated
for mild wind heat.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Removes wind, clears heat, disperses lung qi and stops
cough

**\[Indications\]** An early stage of wind-warm: Cough, mild fever, mild
thirst and a superficial and rapid pulse

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for mild wind
heat in early stage of wind warm. Pathogenic warm affects the descending
and dispersing of lung qi via the mouth and nose, leading to cough. Body
fluids remain undamaged from mild wind heat causes mild fever and
thirst. A superficial, rapid pulse is a typical sign indicative of
exterior heat. As a result, the treatment strategies are to expel wind,
clear heat, disperse lung qi and stop cough.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough, mild fever ad mild thirst.

2\. Modifications

-   For high-grade fever, add *Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) to clear heat.

-   For cough with yellow sputum due to severe lung heat, add *Huang
    Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and *Zhu Ru* (Caulis Bambusae in Taenia)
    to clear lung heat and resolve phlegm.

-   For severe thirst, add *Bei Sha Shen* (Radix Glehniae) and *Tian Hua
    Fen* (Radix Trichosanthis) to clear heat and generate fluids.

-   For sore, swollen throat, add *Niu Bang Zi* (Fructus Arctii) to
    clear heat and relieve sore throat.

3\. Modern use: Upper respiratory tract infection, flu, acute
bronchitis, acute tonsillitis, acute conjunctivitis and keratitis that
can be diagnosed as wind heat attacking the lung or mild wind heat in
the liver meridian.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for cough due to
external contraction of wind cold. Considering the light, volatile
ingredients in this formula, it's advisable not to decoct for a long
time.

***Yin Qiao San* (Lonicera and Forsythia Powder)**

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (*Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| M      | >      | *jīn   | 15g    | Flos Lonicerae Japonicae       |
| onarch | 金银花 | yín    |        |                                |
|        |        | huā*   |        |                                |
+========+========+========+========+================================+
|        | > 连翘 | *lián  | 15g    | Fructus Forsythiae             |
|        |        | qiào*  |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| Mi     | > 薄荷 | *bò    | 6g     | Herba Menthae                  |
| nister |        | hé*    |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | >      | *niú   | 6g     | Fructus Arctii                 |
|        | 牛蒡子 | bàng   |        |                                |
|        |        | zĭ*    |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | >      | *jīng  | 4g     | Spica Schizonepetae            |
|        | 荆芥穗 | jiè    |        |                                |
|        |        | suì*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | >      | *dàn   | 5g     | Semen Sojae Praeparatum        |
|        | 淡豆豉 | dòu    |        |                                |
|        |        | chĭ*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 竹叶 | *zhú   | 4g     | Folium Phyllostachydis Henonis |
| stants |        | yè*    |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 桔梗 | *jié   | 6g     | Radix Platycodonis             |
|        |        | gĕng*  |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
|        | > 芦根 | *lú    | 15g    | Rhizoma Phragmitis             |
|        |        | gēn*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān   | 5g     | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae  |
|        |        | căo*   |        |                                |
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Jin Yin Hua* and *Lian Qiao* dispel wind,
clear heat, remove toxins and repel turbidity.

Minister: Pungent in taste and cool in property, *Bo He* and *Niu Bang
Zi* help to expel wind, clear heat in the upper *Jiao*, remove toxins
and benefit the throat. Pungent in taste and mildly warm in property,
*Jing Jie Sui* and *Dan Dou Chi* help to facilitate the effect of
releasing the exterior.

Assistants: *Zhu Ye* clears heat and relieves restlessness. Sweet in
taste and cold in property, *Lu Gen* generates fluids, relieves thirst
and helps to remove pathogenic factors. *Jie Geng* disperses lung qi and
relieves cough.

### Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates other herbs. It also works in combination with *Jie Geng* to relieve sore throat.  {#envoy-gan-cao-coordinates-other-herbs.-it-also-works-in-combination-with-jie-geng-to-relieve-sore-throat. .unnumbered}

With a combination of a small amount of cool, pungent herbs, this
formula drives pathogenic warm out of the body and moderates the cool
property of other herbs. In addition, it expels wind, clears heat and at
the same time removes toxins.

According to Wu Jutong, a well-known physician in the Qing dynasty
(1644-1911 AD), this formula consists of warm and cool pungent herbs and
is indicated for severe fever, mild chills, sore throat and thirst.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Releases the exterior, clears heat and removes toxins

**\[Indications\]** External contraction of wind heat or early stage of
wind-warm: Fever, absence of or inhibited sweating, mild wind/cold
intolerance, headache, thirst, cough, sore throat, a red tongue tip with
a thin, white or slightly yellow coating and a superficial, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for early stage
of febrile disease. Pathogenic warm affects the lung and binds the
Wei-defensive qi causes mild wind/cold intolerance. The struggle between
anti-pathogenic qi and pathogenic factors causes fever and headache.
Impaired opening and closing of the skin pores causes an absence of or
inhibited sweating. Wind heat affecting the lung causes sore throat.
Wind heat affecting the dispersing and descending of lung qi causes
cough. Pathogenic warm consuming body fluids causes thirst. A red tongue
tip with slightly yellow coating and a superficial, rapid pulse are
typical signs indicative of pathogenic warm affecting the exterior. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to release the exterior, clear heat
and remove toxins.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, mild wind/cold
intolerance, sore throat and thirst.

2\. Modifications

-   For high-grade fever, add *Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) and *Zhi Mu*
    (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae).

-   For severe thirst, add *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) and *Tian Hua
    Fen* (Radix Trichosanthis) to clear heat and generate fluids.

-   For severe sore throat, add *Ma Bo* (Lasiosphaera seu Calvatia) and
    *Xuan Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae) to clear heat and remove toxins.

-   For cough, add *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum) to bear down
    lung qi and resolve phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Flu, acute tonsillitis and parotitis that can be
diagnosed as wind heat in the *Wei*-defense phase.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for external
contraction of wind cold or early stage of damp heat. Considering the
light, volatile ingredients in this formula, it's advisable not to
decoct for a long time.

Both *Yin Qiao San* and *Sang Ju Yin* are first recorded in the *Wen
Bing Tiao Bian* (*Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases*) by Wu
Jutong. The two formulas that release the exterior with cool, pungent
herbs are indicated for the early stage of febrile diseases. They share
five herbs in common---*Lian Qiao*, *Jie Geng*, *Gan Cao*, *Bo He* and
*Lu Gen*. However, *Jin Yin Hua, Jing Jie, Dou Chi, Niu Bang Zi* and
*Zhu Ye* are combined in *Yin Qiao San* to clear heat and relieve sore
throat. *Yin Qiao San* is indicated for fever, mild cold intolerance and
sore throat due to toxic heat attacking the lung in the early stage of
febrile diseases. *Xing Ren* is combined in *Sang Ju Yin* to disperse
lung qi and stop cough. *Sang Ju Yin* has a stronger effect in stopping
cough but a weaker effect than *Yin Qiao San* in releasing the exterior
and clearing heat. It is indicated for cough, mild fever and mild thirst
due to wind heat attacking the lung.

***Ma Huang Xing Ren Gan Cao Shi Gao Tang* (Ephedra, Apricot Kernel,
Gypsum and Licorice Decoction)**

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-----------+--------+--------+---+------------------------------------+
| Monarch   | > 石膏 | *shí   | 1 | Gypsum Fibrosum                    |
|           |        | gāo*   | 8 |                                    |
|           |        |        | g |                                    |
+===========+========+========+===+====================================+
|           | > 麻黄 | *má    | 9 | Herba Ephedrae                     |
|           |        | huáng* | g |                                    |
+-----------+--------+--------+---+------------------------------------+
| Minister  | > 杏仁 | *xìng  | 9 | Semen Armeniacae Amarum            |
|           |        | rén*   | g |                                    |
+-----------+--------+--------+---+------------------------------------+
| Assista   | >      | *zhì   | 6 | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
| nts/envoy | 炙甘草 | gān    | g | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|           |        | căo*   |   |                                    |
+-----------+--------+--------+---+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: Pungent and sweet in taste and extremely cold in nature, *Shi
Gao* clears lung heat and generates fluids. *Ma Huang* disperses lung
qi, relieves dyspnea and expels interior heat. *Shi Gao* and *Ma Huang*
(a double dose of *Ma Huang*) mutually reinforce each other. They work
in combination to clear lung heat without affecting the body, disperse
lung qi and relieve dyspnea without aggravating heat.

Minister: *Xing Ren* bears down lung qi. It works in combination with
*Ma Huang* to relieve cough/dyspnea.

Assistants/envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* supplements qi and harmonizes the middle
*jiao*. It moderates the harsh property of *Ma Huang* in dispersing qi
and the strong effect of Shi Gao in clearing heat and damaging the
spleen/stomach.

This formula is modified *Ma Huang Tang* by removing *Gui Zhi* but
adding *Shi Gao*. With the function of clearing, dispersing and
descending, it acts to clear heat, disperse lung qi and relieve
cough/dyspnea.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Disperses lung qi, clears heat and relieves dyspnea

**\[Indications\]** Cough/dyspnea due to lung heat upon external
contraction of wind: unresolved fever, cough, shortness of breath, nasal
flaring, thirst, absence or presence of sweating, and a superficial,
rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for
cough/dyspnea due to lung heat upon external contraction of wind. Over
time, unresolved wind cold may transform into heat to affect the lung.
Alternatively, wind heat directly affecting the lung causes unresolved
fever. Excessive heat affecting the flow of qi causes cough/dyspnea or
nasal flaring in severe cases. Heat consuming body fluids causes thirst.
Sweating occurs when heat dispels fluids to the surface of the body.
Absence of sweating occurs when the *Wei*-defensive qi is bound and skin
pores are blocked. A thin, yellow tongue coating and a superficial,
rapid pulse are typical signs indicative of pathogenic heat entering and
accumulating in the lung. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
disperse lung qi, clear heat and relieve cough/dyspnea.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, cough, shortness of
breath, a tongue with a thin yellow coating, and a superficial, rapid
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For excessive heat in the lung with persistent high-grade fever upon
    sweating, increase the dose of *Shi Gao* and add *Zhi Mu* (Rhizoma
    Anemarrhenae) and *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) to clear lung
    heat.

-   For yellow, sticky phlegm, add *Gua Lou* (Fructus Trichosanthis) and
    *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) to clear heat and resolve phlegm.

-   For a cough producing phlegm with chest discomfort, add *Pi Pa Ye*
    (Folium Eriobotryae) and *Kuan Dong Hua* (Flos Farfarae) to resolve
    phlegm and stop coughs.

3\. Modern use: Common cold, upper respiratory tract infection, acute
bronchitis, bronchial pneumonia, lobar pneumonia, and bronchial asthma
that can be diagnosed as pathogenic heat accumulating in the lung.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for cough/dyspnea
due to wind cold or in deficiency syndrome.

**1.3 Release the exterior by supplementing anti-pathogenic qi**

Qi-supplementing exterior-releasing formulas are indicated for
constitutional deficiency coupled with external contraction. Because
deficiency can be subcategorized into qi deficiency, blood deficiency,
yin deficiency and yang deficiency, in addition to exterior-releasing
herbs, these formulas also contain herbs that supplement qi, warm yang,
nourish yin or tonify blood.

Typical formulas are *Bai Du San* (Toxin-Removing Powder), *Ma Huang Fu
Zi Xi Xin Tang* (Ephedra, Aconite and Asarum Decoction), and *Jia Jian
Wei Rui Tang* (Solomon\'s Seal Variant Decoction).

***Bai Du San* (Toxin-Resolving Powder)**

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (*Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
| M      | > 羌活 | *qiāng | 900g | Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii   |
| onarch |        | huó*   |      |                                 |
+========+========+========+======+=================================+
|        | > 独活 | *dú    | 900g | Radix Angelicae Pubescentis     |
|        |        | huó*   |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
| Mi     | > 川芎 | *chuān | 900g | Rhizoma Chuanxiong              |
| nister |        | xiōng* |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 柴胡 | *chái  | 900g | Radix Bupleuri                  |
|        |        | hú*    |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 桔梗 | *jié   | 900g | Radix Platycodonis              |
| stants |        | gĕng*  |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 枳壳 | *zhĭ   | 900g | Fructus Aurantii                |
|        |        | qiào*  |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 前胡 | *qián  | 900g | Radix Peucedani                 |
|        |        | hú*    |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 茯苓 | *fú    | 900g | Poria                           |
|        |        | líng*  |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 人参 | *rén   | 900g | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng        |
|        |        | shēn*  |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān   | 900g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae   |
|        |        | căo*   |      |                                 |
+--------+--------+--------+------+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Qiang Huo* is pungent and bitter in taste. It expels the
wind, cold and dampness of the upper body. *Du Huo* expels wind, cold
and dampness of the lower body.

Ministers: *Chuan Xiong* is pungent in taste, warm in property and has a
tendency to disperse and ascend to the head and eyes. It expels wind and
relieves pain. *Chai Hu* is pungent in taste and has a dispersing
capacity. It clears heat.

Assistants: *Jie Geng* ascends to unblock lung qi. *Zhi Qiao* descends
to soothe the chest. *Qian Hu* bears down qi and resolves phlegm. *Fu
Ling* strengthens the spleen and drains dampness. *Ren Shen* 1)
supplements anti-pathogenic qi to dispel pathogenic factors; 2) protects
*Yuan*-primordial qi from being damaged by herbs that have a dispersing
capacity; 3) prevents against re-invasion of external pathogenic
factors.

### Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates other herbs, benefits qi and harmonizes the spleen and stomach. During decocting, *Sheng Jiang* and *Bo He* are added to facilitate the effect of releasing the exterior and dispelling cold. {#envoy-gan-cao-coordinates-other-herbs-benefits-qi-and-harmonizes-the-spleen-and-stomach.-during-decocting-sheng-jiang-and-bo-he-are-added-to-facilitate-the-effect-of-releasing-the-exterior-and-dispelling-cold. .unnumbered}

Since *Ren Shen* is employed to supplement anti-pathogenic qi and drive
pathogenic factors out of the body, this formula is also known as *Ren
Shen Bai Du San* (Toxin-Removing Powder with Ginseng).

Yu Jiayan, a well-known physician in the Qing dynasty (1644-1911 AD)
used this formula for dysentery due to internal retention of cold
dampness. Because dysentery occurs when the spleen fails to transport
and transform, the treatment strategies are to release the exterior,
strengthen the spleen, resolve dampness and circulate qi. This method is
called "sailing a boat against the current".

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Dispels cold, resolves dampness, supplements qi and
releases the exterior

**\[Indications\]** Qi deficiency coupled with external contraction of
wind, cold and dampness: cold intolerance, high-grade fever, headache,
neck stiffness, general ache, absence of sweating, nasal congestion, and
chest fullness/discomfort, a pale tongue with a white and greasy
coating, and a superficial, weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for qi
deficiency coupled with external contraction of wind, cold and dampness.
Qi deficiency causes wind/cold intolerance. External contraction of
wind, cold and dampness has two consequences---anti-pathogenic qi
failing to dispel pathogenic factors and cold-dampness obstructing the
*Wei*-defensive qi. This may cause cold intolerance, high-grade fever
and absence of sweating. Obstructed flow of meridian qi may cause
headache, neck stiffness and general ache. Wind cold attacking the lung
causes nasal congestion. Lung qi failing to disperse and descend causes
cough producing phlegm and chest tightness/discomfort. Deficiency of
anti-pathogenic qi coupled with cold-dampness causes a white greasy
tongue coating and a superficial, weak pulse.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cold intolerance, high-grade
fever, general ache, absence of sweating and a superficial, weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For patients without deficiency, remove *Ren Shen* and *Jing Jie*
    (Herba Schizonepetae) and *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae) to
    facilitate the effect of dispelling wind/cold. In this case, the
    formula is known as *Jing Fang Bai Du San* (Toxin-Removing Powder
    with Schizonepetae and Saposhnikoviae).

-   For severe qi deficiency, add *Dang Shen* (Radix Codonopsis) and
    *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) to supplement qi.

-   For severe diarrhea, add *Shan Yao* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae), *Qian Shi*
    (Semen Euryales) and *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) to resuscitate
    yang.

3\. Modern use: Common cold, stomach flu, bronchitis and allergic
dermatitis that can be diagnosed as wind, cold and dampness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for wind-heat
exterior syndrome, yin deficiency coupled with exterior syndrome, and
dysentery coupled with tenesmus or blood/pus in stools.

***Ma Huang Fu Zi Xi Xin Tang* (Ephedra, Aconite and Asarum Decoction)**

*Shang Han Lun (Treatise on Cold Damage)*

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| M      | > 麻黄 | *má    | 6g | Herba Ephedrae                     |
| onarch |        | huáng* |    |                                    |
+========+========+========+====+====================================+
| Mi     | > 附子 | *fù    | 8g | Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata |
| nister |        | zĭ*    |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 细辛 | *xì    | 3g | Radix et Rhizoma Asari             |
| stants |        | xīn*   |    |                                    |
+--------+--------+--------+----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ma Huang* releases the exterior and dispels cold.

Ministers: *Fu Zi* warms meridians and warms yang.

Assistants: *Xi Xin* assists *Ma Huang* in releasing the exterior and
assists *Fu Zi* in warming the interior.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Warms yang and releases the exterior

**\[Indications\]** Yang deficiency coupled with exterior syndrome:
Chills, fever, absence of sweating, lassitude, and a deep pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for yang
deficiency coupled with external contraction of cold. Wind cold
affecting the exterior causes chills and fever. Yang qi deficiency
coupled with contraction of cold causes lassitude and a deep pulse. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to warm yang and release the
exterior.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, chills, absence of
sweating and a deep, weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For mild symptoms, remove *Xi Xin* and add *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et
    Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae Praeparata cum Melle) to moderate the pungent
    and dispersing property of other herbs and protect anti-pathogenic
    qi.

-   For severe yang deficiency, add *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and
    *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae Praeparata cum Melle)
    to resuscitate yang.

3\. Modern use: Common cold and rheumatic arthritis in the elderly that
can be diagnosed as yang qi deficiency coupled with external contraction
of wind cold.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for yin blood
deficiency coupled with external contraction of wind cold or exterior
syndrome without deficiency.

***Jia Jian Wei Rui Tang* (Solomon\'s Seal Variant Decoction)**

*Tong Su Shang Han* (*Popular Guide to the 'Treatise on Cold Damage'*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
| Monarch | > 葳蕤 | *wēi   | 9g | Rhizoma Polygonati Odorati        |
|         |        | ruí*   |    |                                   |
+=========+========+========+====+===================================+
| Mi      | > 葱白 | *cōng  | 6g | Bulbus Allii Fistulosi            |
| nisters |        | bái*   |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
|         | >      | *dàn   | 9g | Semen Sojae Praeparatum           |
|         | 淡豆豉 | dòu    |    |                                   |
|         |        | chĭ*   |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
|         | > 薄荷 | *bò    | 5g | Herba Menthae                     |
|         |        | hé*    |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
| Ass     | > 白薇 | *bái   | 3g | Radix et Rhizoma Cynanchi Atrati  |
| istants |        | wēi*   |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
|         | > 桔梗 | *jié   | 5g | Radix Platycodonis                |
|         |        | gĕng*  |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
|         | > 大枣 | *dà    | 2  | Fructus Jujubae                   |
|         |        | zăo*   |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy   | >      | *zhì   | 1. | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae     |
|         | 炙甘草 | gān    | 5g | Praeparata cum Melle              |
|         |        | căo*   |    |                                   |
+---------+--------+--------+----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Wei Rui* (also known as *Yu Zhu*) is sweet in taste and cold
in property. It nourishes yin to generate fluids, clears heat, moistens
the lung and benefits the stomach.

Ministers: *Cai Bai*, *Dan Dou Chi* and *Bo He* dispel pathogenic
factors and, at the same time, protect anti-pathogenic qi.

Assistants: *Bai Wei* is bitter, salty in taste and cold in property. It
clears lung heat without damaging yin and is therefore indicated for
heat due to yin deficiency. *Jie Geng* releases the exterior, disperses
lung qi, stops coughing and benefits the throat. *Da Zao* assists *Wei
Rui* in nourishing yin blood and moistening dryness.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* coordinates the other herbs.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin and releases the exterior

**\[Indications\]** Yin deficiency coupled with external contraction of
wind heat: headache, fever, mild wind/cold intolerance, absence of or
scanty sweating, coughing, restlessness, a red tongue and a rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for yin
deficiency coupled with external contraction of wind heat. Wind heat
affecting the exterior causes fever and mild wind/cold intolerance. Wind
heat affecting the upper body and impairing the descending of lung qi
causes headache and cough. Yin deficiency causes an absence of or scanty
sweating and thirst. Restlessness, a red tongue and a rapid pulse are
typical signs indicative of heat due to yin deficiency. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to nourish yin and release the exterior.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, mild cold intolerance, a
dry mouth and throat, a red tongue and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe wind-heat symptoms, add *Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae
    Japonicae) and *Lian Qiao* (Fructus Forsythiae) to release the
    exterior.

-   For cough, a dry throat and difficult expectoration, add *Bei Mu*
    (Bulbus Fritillaria) and *Gua Lou Pi* (Pericarpium Trichosanthis) to
    resolve phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Common cold, acute tonsillitis and pharyngitis that can
be diagnosed as yin deficiency coupled with exterior syndrome. It's
beneficial to the elderly, women after childbirth and those who
recovered from major or chronic diseases.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for exterior
syndrome without yin deficiency.

**Summary**

Formulas that release the exterior are indicated for exterior syndrome
due to contraction of six external pathogenic factors. There are a total
of 8 formulas and 3 annex formulas in this chapter.

**Release the exterior with warm, pungent herbs**

Formulas that use warm, pungent herbs to release the exterior are
indicated for wind-cold exterior syndrome. *Ma Huang Tang* has strong
diaphoretic action and is indicated for chills, fever, absence of
sweating and dyspnea in excessive exterior syndrome. *Gui Zhi Tang*
harmonizes the *Ying*-nutrients and *Wei*-defense and is a typical
formula for fever, chills, sweating, and wind intolerance in deficiency
exterior syndromes. It is also indicated for disharmony between the
*Ying*-nutrients and *Wei*-defense due to internal dysfunctions. *Jiu
Wei Qiang Huo Tang* releases the exterior, clears the interior and
guides qi to different meridians. It is indicated for external
contraction of wind, cold and dampness coupled with a bitter taste and
mild thirst due to interior heat. *Xiao Qing Long Tang* dispels cold and
resolves phlegm. It is indicated or internal cold retention coupled with
external contraction of wind cold. This formula removes pathogenic
factors out of the body and at the same time, protects anti-pathogenic
qi.

**Release the exterior with cool, pungent herbs**

Formulas that use cool, pungent herbs to release the exterior are
indicated for wind-heat exterior syndrome due to external contraction of
wind heat/warm. *Sang Ju Yin* is indicated for cough due to mild wind
heat attacking the lung meridian. *Yin Qiao San* is indicated for severe
fever, mild chills, sore throat and thirst due to wind heat attacking
the *Wei*-defense. *Ma Huang Xing Ren Gan Cao Shi Gao Tang* clears heat
and relieves cough/dyspnea and is indicated for cough/dyspnea due to
pathogenic heat accumulating in the lung.

**Release the exterior by supplementing anti-pathogenic qi**

Formulas that release the exterior by supplement anti-pathogenic qi are
indicated for deficiency of anti-pathogenic qi coupled with external
contraction. *Bai Du San* is a typical formula for qi deficiency coupled
with external contraction of wind, cold and dampness. *Ma Huang Fu Zi Xi
Xin Tang* is a typical formula for yang qi deficiency coupled with
external contraction of wind cold. *Jia Jian Wei Rui Tang* is a typical
formula for yin deficiency coupled with exterior syndrome. Since it
contains light, clear herbs that have a dispersing capacity and
yin-nourishing herbs, this formula induces sweat without damaging
anti-pathogenic qi and nourishes yin without retaining pathogenic
factors.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. Describe the indications, cautions and categories of              |
| exterior-releasing formulas.                                          |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. *Ma Huang Tang* and *Gui Zhi Tang* are both indicated for         |
| wind-cold exterior syndrome. What are the similarities and            |
| differences in ingredients, functions, indications and compatibility? |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. *Xiao Qing Long Tang* and *Ma Huang Xing Ren Shi Gao Gan Cao      |
| Tang* are both indicated for coughing and dyspnea. What are the       |
| differences in indications and ingredients?                           |
|                                                                       |
| 4\. *Xiao Qing Long Tang* is indicated for coughing and dyspnea due   |
| to external cold and internal fluid retention. Why are *Wu Wei Zi*    |
| and *Bai Shao* (both having an astringing property) included in the   |
| formula?                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| 5\. Describe the similarities and differences between *Sang Ju Yin*   |
| and *Yin Qiao San* in ingredients, functions, indications and         |
| compatibility?                                                        |
|                                                                       |
| 6\. Explain the significance of the role of *Ren Shen* in *Bai Du     |
| San*. Why is *Bai Du San* known as a typical formula of "sailing a    |
| boat against the current"?                                            |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 2 Formulas that Drain Downward**

Formulas that drain downward act to promote bowel movements, remove food
stagnation in the stomach and intestines, or resolve water retention.
They are indicated for interior excess syndrome. They formulas fall into
the category of the stategy of draining downwards (*Xia Fa*). This
strategy is based on the theory of "promoting bladder/bowel movements to
remove pathogenic factors and regulating and bearing qi down for
abdominal fullness and distension" recorded in the *Su Wen Yin Yang Ying
Xiang Da Lun* (Chapter 5 of the Basic Questions).

Contributing factors of interior excess syndrome include heat
accumulation, cold retention, dryness stagnation and water retention.
Considering the constitutional differences in cold, heat, deficiency and
excess, the treatment strategies are to clear heat with cold-property
ingredients, dispel cold, to moisten the intestines, reinforce
anti-pathogenic qi and resolve water retention. As a result, there are
five categories of formulas that drain downward, which respectively
contain cold-property ingredients, warm-property ingredients, moistening
ingredients, water retention-resolving ingredients and purgative and
qi/blood-nourishing ingredients. In addition, considering the severities
and duration, the purgation can be drastic or moderate.

Formulas that drain downward are indicated for interior excess syndrome
with resolved exterior syndrome. Otherwise, it's advisable to release
the exterior first or treat both the exterior and interior. It's
contraindicated for pregnant women, women after childbirth, women on
their period, people with a weakened immune system, and people with loss
of fluids or blood. If necessay, it's essential to combine tonics to
protect anti-pathogenic qi from being damaged while attacking pathogenic
factors.

Furthermore, formulas that drain downward easily damage stomach qi, it's
therefore important to discontinue when the symptoms are alleviated.
It's also necessary to modify dietary habits (stay away or limit the
intake of oily food) to protect stomach qi.

**2.1 Formulas that drain downward with cold-property ingredients**

Formulas that drain downward with cold-property ingredients are
indicated for interior excessive heat accumulation syndrome. Clinical
signs and symptoms include constipation, incomplete evacuation,
abdominal pain/tenderness, tidal fever, delirious speech, a red tongue
with a yellow, thick coating and a deep, excessive pulse. Common
ingredients include *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and *Mang Xiao*
(Natrii Sulfas). A classic formula is *Da Cheng Qi Tang* (Major
Purgative Decoction).

***Da Cheng Qi Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarch  | > 大黄  | *dà huáng*  | 12g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei      |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
| Minister | > 芒硝  | *máng xiāo* | 6g   | Natrii Sulfas              |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | > 厚朴  | *hòu pò*    | 24g  | Cortex Magnoliae           |
| sistants |         |             |      | Officinalis                |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 枳实  | *zhĭ shí*   | 12g  | Fructus Aurantii Immaturus |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Da Huang* is bitter and cold and acts to drain downward.

Minister: *Mang Xiao* is salty and cold and acts to clear heat, promote
bowel movements, soften hardness and moisten dryness. *Mang Xiao* and
*Da Huang* mutually reinforce in draining downward.

Assistants: *Hou Pu* bears qi down and relieves fullness. *Zhi Shi*
moves qi, and resolves masses.

The formula is called *Da Cheng Qi*, which literally means a stronger
purgative effect in draining stomach qi downward and unblocking the
stomach and intestines.

As for decoction method, *Zhi Shi* and *Hou Pu* are decocted first. *Da
Huang* is decocted later to preserve its efficacy in draining downward.
*Mang Xiao* is dissolved into the decoction.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct *Hou Pu* and *Zhi Shi* first;
decoct *Da Huang* later and dissolve *Mang Xiao* in the decoction.

**\[Functions\]** Drains downward and purges heat accumulation.

**\[Indications\]**

-   Yangming Fu-organ excess syndrome: Constipation, frequent
    flatulence, abdominal fullness, abdominal pain/tenderness, abdominal
    hardness upon palpation, tidal fever, delirious speech, profuse
    perspiration from the palms and soles, a dry, yellow, thorny or dry
    and black tongue coating, and a deep, excessive pulse.

-   Fecal impaction with green, watery and foul-smelling diarrhea,
    peri-umbilical pain, abdominal masses, a dry mouth and tongue, and a
    slippery, excessive pulse.

-   Syncope, seizures or mania due to interior excessive heat

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula to drain
downward with cold herbs. It is indicated for Yangming Fu-organ
excessive syndrome. After entering the interior, pathogenic heat
consumes body fluids and, intertwined with dry feces, it obstructs the
flow of intestinal qi thus resulting in constipation, abdominal
fullness, frequent flatulence, abdominal pain/tenderness and hardness
upon palpation. Exuberant interior heat may drive the fluids to the
surface of the body, causing tidal fever and profuse perspiration from
the palms and soles. Heat disturbing the mind may cause delirious speech
or mania. Heat consuming body fluids causes a yellow or dry, black,
thorny tongue coating and a deep, excessive pulse.

Clinical manifestations indicated by this formula were summarized into
four words by ancient physicians---*Pi* (abdominal stuffiness), *Man*
(fullness/distension due to qi stagnation, coupled with a sensation of
resistance upon pressure), *Zao* (dryness) and *Shi* (excess, referring
to abdominal pain/tenderness and constipation due to heat accumulation).
Qi stagnation and heat accumulation may interact as both cause and
effect. Exuberant heat in the intestines may dry feces to obstruct the
intestines and cause fecal impaction with green, watery and
foul-smelling diarrhea. Stagnant heat binding yang qi may cause heat
syncope (cold limbs). Exuberant heat consuming body fluids may cause
malnourishment of the sinews or muscles, leading to convulsions. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to purge heat, drain downward and
supplement yin fluids. In *Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage),
this method is described as "removing the firewood from under the
cauldron and preserving yin with urgent bowel purgation".

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal stuffiness, fullness,
dryness and pain/tenderness, a dry yellow tongue coating and a deep,
excessive pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a dry mouth due to damage to fluids, add *Xuan Shen* (Radix
    Scrophulariae), *Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae) and *Mai Dong* (Radix
    Ophiopogonis) to nourish yin and moisten dryness.

-   For severe qi stagnation, add *Lai Fu Zi* (Semen Raphani), *Chi
    Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Rubra) and *Tao Ren* (Semen Persicae) to move
    qi, relieve stagnation, circulate blood and clear heat.

3\. Modern use: Simple acute bowel obstruction, postoperative adhesive
bowel obstruction, acute cholecystitis, acute pancreatitis, and
high-grade fever coupled with constipation, a yellow tongue coating, and
an excessive pulse in febrile diseases that can be diagnosed as interior
heat accumulation.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Considering its strong purgative
efficacy, patients with deficiency of qi and yin, the aged and those
with a weakened immune system should use this formula with caution, as
is the case for pregnant women. Also, patients should discontinue this
formula when the constipation is relieved to protect anti-pathogenic qi
from being damaged.

**\[Annex formulas\]**

+------------+-------------+-----------+------------------------------+
| Formula    | Ingredients | Actions   | Indications                  |
+------------+-------------+-----------+------------------------------+
| #          | *Da Huang*  | M         | Yangming-Fu organ excessive  |
| ### *Xiao  | 12g, *Hou   | oderately | syndrome characterized by    |
| Cheng Qi T | Pu* 6g, and | purges    | stuffiness, fullness and     |
| ang* (*Sha | *Zhi Shi*   | heat      | pain/ tenderness             |
| ng Han Lun | 9g          | acc       |                              |
| *) {#xiao- |             | umulation |                              |
| cheng-qi-t |             |           |                              |
| ang-shang- |             |           |                              |
| han-lun .u |             |           |                              |
| nnumbered} |             |           |                              |
+------------+-------------+-----------+------------------------------+
| #### *Tia  | *Da Huang*  | Mildly    | Yangming-Fu organ excessive  |
| o Wei Chen | 12g, *Gan   | purges    | syndrome characterized by    |
| g Qi Tang* | Cao* 6g and | heat      | dryness and pain/tenderness  |
|  *(Shang H | *Mang Xiao* | acc       |                              |
| an Lun*) { | 15g         | umulation |                              |
| #tiao-wei- |             |           |                              |
| cheng-qi-t |             |           |                              |
| ang-shang- |             |           |                              |
| han-lun .u |             |           |                              |
| nnumbered} |             |           |                              |
+------------+-------------+-----------+------------------------------+

**\[Comparison\]**

As for compositions, *Xiao Cheng Qi Tan*g is modified from *Da Cheng Qi
Tang* by removing *Mang Xiao*. *Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang* is modified from
*Da Cheng Qi Tang* by removing *Zhi Shi* and *Hou Pu* but adding *Gan
Cao*.

As for doses, the dose of *Hou Pu* in *Da Cheng Qi Tang* is double the
dose of *Da Huang*, because it pays equal attention to draining downward
as it does moving qi. With a drastic purgative action, it is indicated
for Yangming Fu-organ excessive syndrome with severe heat accumulation
(abdominal stuffiness, fullness, dryness and pain/tenderness). *Xiao
Cheng Qi Tang* removes *Mang Xiao* and decreases the dose of *Zhi Shi*
and *Hou Pu*, and therefore has a mild effect in draining downward. It
is indicated for mild heat accumulation (abdominal stuffiness, fullness
and pain/distension). *Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang* removes *Zhi Shi* and
*Hou Pu*, increases the dose of *Mang Xiao* and adds *Gan Cao*. As a
result, it has milder effect in draining downward and is indicated for
internal dryness accumulation and abdominal pain/tenderness without
stuffiness and fullness.

As for decoction, in *Da Cheng Qi Tang,* *Zhi Shi* and *Hou Pu* are
decocted first, *Da Huang* later and *Mang Xiao* is dissolved in the
decoction. In *Xiao Cheng Qi Tang* the three ingredients are decocted
together. In *Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang*, *Da Huang* and *Gan Cao* are
decocted first and *Mang Xiao* is dissolved later.

**2.2 Formulas that drain downward with warm-property ingredients**

Formulas that drain downward with warm-property ingredients are
indicated for interior excessive cold accumulation syndrome. Clinical
signs and symptoms include constipation, abdominal distension/pain, cold
limbs, a pale tongue with a white coating and a deep, tight pulse.
Warm-property ingredients can dispel cold and unblock obstruction.
Common interior-warming ingredients include *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti
Lateralis Praeparata) and *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis). Common
purgative ingredients include *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and
*Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas). Qi-tonifying ingredients like *Ren Shen*
(Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) are often combined for interior cold
accumulation coupled with qi deficiency. Typical formulas include *Wen
Pi Tang* (Spleen-Warming Decoction) and *Da Huang Fu Zi Tang* (Rhubarb
and Aconite Decoction).

***Wei Pi Tang***

*Bei Ji Qian Jin Yao Fang* (*Important Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold
Pieces for Emergency*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarchs | > 附子  | *fù zĭ*     | 6g   | Radix Aconiti Lateralis    |
|          |         |             |      | Praeparata                 |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
|          | > 大黄  | *dà huáng*  | 15g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei      |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Minis    | > 干姜  | *gān jiāng* | 9g   | Rhizoma Zingiberis         |
| ter**s** |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 芒硝  | *máng xiāo* | 6g   | Natrii Sulfas              |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | > 人参  | *rén shēn*  | 6g   | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng   |
| sistants |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 当归  | *dāng guī*  | 9g   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis   |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| *        | > 甘草  | *gān căo*   | 6g   | Radix et Rhizoma           |
| *Envoy** |         |             |      | Glycyrrhizae               |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Fu Zi* is pungent and warm and acts to warm spleen yang,
dispel cold and alleviate pain. *Da Huang* is bitter and cold and acts
to drain downward and resolve stagnation.

Ministers: *Gan Jiang* warms the spleen and stomach, dispels cold and
assists Fu Zi in warming yang. *Mang Xiao* drains downward, softens
hardness and assists *Da Huang* in promoting bowel movements.

Assistants: *Ren Shen* and *Dang Gui* supplement anti-pathogenic qi,
nourish blood and remove pathogenic factors

Envoy: *Gan Cao* assists *Ren Shen* in supplementing qi and coordinating
the other ingredients.

The formula is modified from *Si Ni Tang* (Frigid Extremities Decoction)
(compositions: *Fu Zi,* *Gan Jiang* and *Gan Cao*), and *Tiao Wei Cheng
Qi Tang* (Stomach-Regulating and Purgative Decoction) (compositions: *Da
Huang*, *Mang Xiao* and *Gan Ca*o) plus *Ren Shen* and *Dang Gui*. As a
result, this formula warms yang, drains downward and supplements
anti-pathogenic qi.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Drains downward, dispels cold accumulation and warms
spleen yang.

**\[Indications\]** Internal cold retention due to spleen yang
deficiency: Constipation, abdominal pain, periumbilical colic, cold
hands and feet, absence of thirst, a white tongue coating, and a deep,
wiry and slow pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for internal
cold retention due to yang deficiency. Cold retention due to spleen yang
deficiency may obstruct the flow of intestinal qi and result in
constipation and periumbilical pain. Spleen yang failing to warm the
extremities causes cold hands and feet. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to warm spleen yang, drain downward and tonify qi.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal pain, constipation,
cold hands and feet, a white tongue coating and a deep, wiry and slow
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For abdominal distension/pain due to cold retention and qi
    stagnation, add *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) and *Zhi
    Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus) to move qi and alleviate pain.

-   For abdominal pain with a cold sensation, add *Wu Yao* (Radix
    Linderae) and *Xiao Hui Xiang* (Fructus Foeniculi) to warm the
    spleen and stomach and alleviate pain.

3\. Modern use: Simple acute bowel obstruction and pyloric obstruction
that can be diagnosed as internal cold retention due to spleen yang
deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Da Huang Fu Zi Tang* (Rhubarb and Aconite
Decoction) from the *Jin Kui Yao Lve* (*Essentials from the Golden
Cabinet*)

\[Ingredients\] *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) 9g, *Fu Zi* (Radix
Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata) 12g, and *Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari)
3g

\[Actions\] Warms the interior, dispels cold, promotes bowel movements
and alleviates pain

\[Indications\] Interior excessive cold syndrome: Abdominal pain,
constipation, fever, cold hands and feet, a white, greasy tongue coating
and a wiry, tight pulse.

Both *Wen Pi Tang* and *Da Huang Fu Zi Tang* are formulas that drain
downward with warm-property ingredients. They both contain *Da Huang*
and *Fu Zi* and are indicated for constipation due to cold accumulation.
However, *Da Huang Fu Zi Tang* is indicated for interior cold
accumulation without deficiency of anti-pathogenic qi, consequently *Fu
Zi* is used in combination with *Xi Xin* to dispel cold and alleviate
pain; and *Da Huang* is used to promote bowel movements. *Wen Pi Tang*
is indicated for interior cold retention due to spleen yang deficiency,
as a result, *Fu Zi, Gan Jiang, Gan Cao, Ren Shen* and *Dang Gui* are
used to warm the spleen, dispel cold and tonify anti-pathogenic qi; and
*Da Huang* and *Mang Xiao* are combined to drain downward and promote
bowel movements.

**2.3 Formulas that drain downward with moistening ingredients**

Formulas that drain downward with moistening ingredients are indicated
for constipation due to intestinal dryness or kidney yang deficiency.
Contributing factors may include heat consuming body fluids,
constitutional fire exuberance and dryness in the stomach and intestines
or impaired qi transformation due to kidney deficiency. Clinical signs
and symptoms include constipation, a dry tongue, or lower back
soreness/weakness. Common moistening ingredients include *Huo Ma Ren*
(Fructus Cannabis) and *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum). For kidney
deficiency, kidney yang-warming ingredients such as *Rou Cong Rong*
(Herba Cistanches) and blood-nourishing and intestine-moistening
ingredient such as *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis) are often used.
Typical formulas include *Ma Zi Ren Wan* (Cannabis Fruit Pill) and *Ji
Chuan Jian* (Fluid-Replenishing Decoction).

***Ma Zi Ren Wan (Aka Pi Ye Ma Ren Wan)***

*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarch  | >       | *má zĭ rén* | 500g | Fructus Cannabis           |
|          |  麻子仁 |             |      |                            |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
| Minis    | > 大黄  | *dà huáng*  | 500g | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei      |
| ter**s** |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 杏仁  | *xìng rén*  | 250g | Semen Armeniacae Amarum    |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 白芍  | *bái sháo*  | 250g | Radix Paeoniae Alba        |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | > 枳实  | *zhĭ shí*   | 250g | Fructus Aurantii Immaturus |
| sistants |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 厚朴  | *hòu pò*    | 250g | Cortex Magnoliae           |
|          |         |             |      | Officinalis                |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Envoy    | > 蜂蜜  | *fēng mì*   |      | Mel                        |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ma Zi Ren* is sweet in taste and neutral in property. It is
rich in oil and acts to moisten the intestines and promotes bowel
movement.

Ministers: *Da Huang* is bitter and cold and acts to clear heat and
promote bowel movements. *Xing Ren* moistens dryness, promotes bowel
movements and bears down lung qi. Because the lung is
interior-exteriorly connected with the large intestine, bearing down
lung qi benefits unblocking the large intestine. *Bai Shao* nourishes
yin, astringes fluids, harmonizes the interior, and alleviates spasms.

Assistant: *Zhi Shi* bears qi down and breaks stagnation. *Hou Pu* moves
qi and relieves fullness.

Envoy: *Feng Mi* reinforces the spleen and stomach, moistens the
intestines, promotes bowel movements and coordinates the other
ingredients.

The formula is also known as *Pi Yue Ma Ren Wan* (Spleen-benefiting
Cannabis Fruit Pill) and modified from *Xiao Cheng Qi Tang* (Minor
Purgative Decoction, ingredients: *Da Huang*, *Zhi Shi* and *Hou Pu*)
coupled with *Ma Zi Ren*, *Bai Shao*, *Xing Ren* and *Feng Mi*. *Xiao
Cheng Qi Tang* has a mild effect in clearing heat and removing food
stagnation in the stomach and intestines; however, a smaller dose is
adopted in actual administration. Bigger doses of *Ma Zi Ren*, *Xing
Ren*, *Feng Mi* and *Bai Shao* are used to nourish yin, moisten dryness,
promote bowel movements and protect anti-pathogenic qi from being
damaged by the harsh property of *Xiao Cheng Qi Tang*. The initial dose
is 10 small pills and gradually increased afterwards to moderately
moisten the intestines and clear heat.

The compatibility characteristics of this formula are draining downward
without damaging anti-pathogenic qi and moistening the intestines
without causing greasiness. As a result, it clears heat, moistens
dryness, nourishes yin fluids, and promotes bowel movements.

**\[Method of administration\]** 9g honey pills for each dose, 2-3 doses
a day. Alternatively, decoct with water (decoct *Da Huang* later).

**\[Functions\]** Moistens the intestines, clears heat, moves qi and
promotes bowel movements.

**\[Indications\]** Constipation due to dry heat in the stomach and
intestines: Constipation, frequent, urgent urination, a slightly yellow
tongue coating and a thready, hesitant pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for constipation
due to dry heat in the stomach and intestines. The stomach receives and
preliminarily decomposes food. The spleen transports and transforms
water and food. Dry heat in the stomach affects the functions of the
spleen in transportation and transformation, leading to frequent, urgent
urination and constipation. This is called *Pi Yue* (spleen restraint)
in the *Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*). As a result, the
treatment strategies are to moisten the intestines, clear heat, move qi
and promote bowel movements.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Constipation, mild abdominal
fullness and distension, and a slightly yellow tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For hemorrhoids and constipation, add *Tao Ren* (Semen Persicae) and
    *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis) to nourish and harmonize
    blood, moisten the intestines and promote bowel movements.

-   For hemorrhoids coupled with bleeding, add *Huai Hua* (Flos
    Sophorae) and *Ce Bai Ye* (Cacumen Platycladi) to cool blood and
    stop bleeding.

-   For loss of intestinal fluids and a dry tongue and mouth, add *Zeng
    Ye Tang* (Humor-Increasing Decoction) to supplement fluids and
    promote bowel movements.

3\. Modern use: Chronic constipation, constipation in people with qi
deficiency, after childbirth or postoperative hemorrhoids that can be
diagnosed as dry heat in the stomach and intestines.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula moistens the intestines;
however, *Xiao Cheng Qi Tang* breaks stagnation and bears qi down.
Consequently, people with insufficient fluids and blood should use this
formula with caution or only use it for a short period of time.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Wu Ren Wan* (Five Kernels Pill) from the *Shi Yi
De Xiao Fang* (*Effective Formulas from Generations of Physicians*)

\[Ingredients\] *Tao Ren* (Semen Persicae), *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae
Amarum), *Song Zi Ren* (Semen Pini Koraiensis), *Bai Zi Ren* (Semen
Platycladi), *Yu Li Ren* (Semen Pruni) and *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri
Reticulatae).

\[Actions\] Moistens the intestines and promotes bowel movements.

\[Indications\] Intestinal dryness due to exhaustion of fluids:
Difficulty in defecation, constipation due to blood deficiency (the
elderly or women after childbirth), a dry tongue and a thready, hesitant
pulse.

This formula contains five types of kernels to moisten the intestines
and *Chen Pi* to regulate qi. As a result, it is indicated for
constipation due to intestinal dryness.

***Ji Chuan Jian***

*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (*The Complete Works of \[Zhang\] Jing-yue*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+-------+---------------------------+
| Monarch  | >       | *ròu cōng   | 6-9g  | Herba Cistanches          |
|          |  肉苁蓉 | róng*       |       |                           |
+==========+=========+=============+=======+===========================+
| Minis    | > 当归  | *dāng guī*  | 9-15g | Radix Angelicae Sinensis  |
| ter**s** |         |             |       |                           |
+----------+---------+-------------+-------+---------------------------+
|          | > 牛膝  | *niú xī*    | 6g    | Radix Achyranthis         |
|          |         |             |       | Bidentatae                |
+----------+---------+-------------+-------+---------------------------+
| As       | > 枳壳  | *zhĭ qiào*  | 3g    | Fructus Aurantii          |
| sistants |         |             |       |                           |
+----------+---------+-------------+-------+---------------------------+
|          | > 泽泻  | *zé xiè*    | 4.5g  | Rhizoma Alismatis         |
+----------+---------+-------------+-------+---------------------------+
|          | > 升麻  | *shēng má*  | 1     | Rhizoma Cimicifugae       |
|          |         |             | .5-3g |                           |
+----------+---------+-------------+-------+---------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Rou Cong Rong* is salty and warm and acts to tonify kidney
yang, supplement essence-blood, moisten the intestines and promote bowel
movements. It is a key ingredient for constipation due to yang
deficiency.

Ministers: *Dang Gui* nourishes blood, moistens dryness and promotes
bowel movements. *Niu Xi* tonifies the kidney, strengthens the lower
back and helps to promote bowel movements.

Assistants: *Zhi Qiao* moves qi and resolves stagnation. *Ze Xie* enters
the kidney, removes turbidity and assists *Niu Xi* to bear down and *Zhi
Qiao* promotes bowel movements.

The formula warms the kidney, supplements essence, moistens the
intestines and promotes bowel movements. Its name *Ji Chuan Jian*
literally means to nourish the river to allow for the smooth passing of
boats. The first word *Ji* means to tonify. The second word *Chuan*
means the fluids. The name *Ji Chuan Jian* means to promote bowel
movements by distributing body fluids through tonifying the kidney.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms the kidney, supplements essence, moistens the
intestines and promotes bowel movements.

**\[Indications\]** Constipation due to deficiency of kidney yang and
essence: Difficulty in defecation, difficult passing of stools, profuse,
clear urine, lower back and knee soreness, dizziness, blurred vision,
cold intolerance, cold limbs, a pale tongue with a white coating, and a
deep, slow pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for constipation
due to deficiency of kidney yang and essence. The kidney dominates
fluids and governs opening and closing of the bladder. Kidney yang
failing to transform qi may cause profuse, clear urine. Insufficient
fluids in the intestines may cause difficulty in defecation and
difficult passing of stools. Since the lumbus is the house of the
kidney, kidney essence deficiency may cause lower back and knee
soreness. Kidney essence failing to nourish the brain may cause
dizziness and blurred vision. Kidney yang deficiency may cause internal
cold and subsequently, cold intolerance, cold limbs, a pale tongue with
a white coating and a deep, thready pulse. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to warm the kidney, supplement essence, moisten the
intestines and promote bowel movements.

This formula promotes bowel movements through warming the kidney and
moistening the intestines (without using purgative ingredients). The
compatibility characteristics of this formula are combining unblocking
with reinforcing and combining bearing down with lifting up.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Difficulty in defecation,
difficult passing of stools, lower back and knee soreness, a pale tongue
with a white coating, and a deep, slow pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For qi deficiency, add *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) to
    tonify qi.

-   For severe kidney essence deficiency, add *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae
    Praeparata) and *Shan Zhu Yu* (Fructus Corni) to nourish kidney
    essence.

-   For insufficient fluids, combine *Zeng Ye Tang* (Humor-Increasing
    Decoction: *Mai Dong*, *Xuan Shen* and *Sheng Di*) to moisten the
    intestines.

3\. Modern use: Senile constipation, chronic constipation or
constipation after childbirth that can be diagnosed as kidney essence
deficiency coupled with intestinal dryness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
constipation in an excess syndrome or constipation due to (yin)
deficiency heat.

**2.3 Formulas that drain downward with water retention-resolving
ingredients**

Formulas that drain downward with water retention-resolving ingredients
are indicated for interior water retention. Signs and symptoms include
cough-induced chest pain, edema, abdominal distension, difficulty in
urination and defecation, and a deep, excessive pulse. Common herbs to
resolve water retention include *Da Ji* (Radix Knoxiae), *Yuan Hua*
(Flos Genkwa) and *Gan Sui* (Radix Kansui). Because some ingredients are
toxic, it's essential to combine with stomach-nourishing and
qi-supplementing ingredients such as *Da Zao* (Fructus Jujubae). A
typical formula is *Shi Zao Tang* (Ten Jujubes Decoction).

***Shi Zao Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+------------+------------------+--------------------------------------+
| > 甘遂     | *gān suì*        | Radix Kansui                         |
+============+==================+======================================+
| > 大戟     | *dà jĭ*          | Radix Knoxiae                        |
+------------+------------------+--------------------------------------+
| > 芫花     | *yuán huā*       | Flos Genkwa                          |
+------------+------------------+--------------------------------------+
| > 大枣     | *dà zăo*         | Fructus Jujubae                      |
+------------+------------------+--------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

*Gan Sui* is bitter and cold and acts to resolve water dampness in
meridians. *Da Ji* is pungent, bitter and cold and acts to take water
retention out of the zang-fu organs. *Yuan Hua* is pungent, bitter and
warm and acts to resolve hidden fluid out of the thoracic and
hypochondriac regions. Because these three ingredients are harsh and
toxic, 10 pieces of *Da Zao* are combined to strengthen the spleen,
supplement qi, protect the stomach and moderate the harsh and toxic
properties of the other three ingredients.

The original method by Zhang Zhongjing: 1) Grind *Gan Sui*, *Da Ji* and
*Yuan Hua* into a powder and mix with the decoction of *Da Zao* and
water. The dosage form of powder is adopted because the active component
of *Gan Sui* is difficult to dissolve in water. 2) It's advisable to
start with a small dose in the morning with an empty stomach to protect
anti-pathogenic qi. For slow response, increase the dose the next day.
3) In case of fast response, eat porridge to protect the spleen and
stomach.

**\[Method of administration\]** Grind equal parts of the three
ingredients into a powder or capsule. Take 0.5-1g for each dose with a
decoction of *Da Zao* and water. For slow response, increase the dose.
For fast response, ask the patient to eat porridge to protect the spleen
and stomach.

**\[Functions\]** Resolves water retention.

**\[Indications\]** 1) Pleural effusion: Pain in the chest and rib area
that is aggrevated by cough and rolling over the body or breathing,
epigastric stuffiness, retching, shortness of breath, headache, blurred
vision, a slippery tongue coating and a deep, wiry pulse. Alternatively,
the chest pain may radiate to the back, coupled with shortness of
breath. 2) Edema: general edema, especially in the lower body, abdominal
distension, panting and difficulty in defecation and urination.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for pleural
effusion or edema that can be diagnosed as excessive syndrome. Water
retention in the thoracic area affecting the flow of qi may cause pain
in the chest and rib area that is aggravated by cough, rolling over the
body or breathing. In severe cases, the chest pain may radiate to the
back. Water retention affecting the descending of lung qi may cause
cough and shortness of breath. Water retention below the heart may cause
epigastric stuffiness. Failure of stomach qi to descend may cause
retching. Water retention affecting the ascending of clean yang may
cause headache and blurred vision. Water retention affecting the water
passage in the San*jiao* may cause edema, abdominal distension and
difficulty in defecation and urination. Water retention affecting the
skin may cause general edema. A slippery tongue coating and a deep pulse
are typical signs indicative of excessive water retention. As a result,
the treatment strategy is to drastically purge water retention.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough-induced pain in the chest
and rib area, edema, abdominal distension, difficulty in defecation and
urination and a deep, wiry pulse.

2\. Modifications

For patients with a weak constitution, use *Ting Li Zi* (Semen Lepidii)
and *Dao Zao* to descend lung qi and protect the spleen.

3\. Modern use: Exudative pleurisy, tuberculous pleurisy, ascites due to
liver cirrhosis and chronic renal edema that can be diagnosed as
excessive water retention.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Due to the harsh and toxic properties
of three ingredients, this formula can only be used for a short period
of time. It is contraindicated for pregnant women or in combination with
*Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae).

**2.5 Formulas that drain downward with purgative and
qi/blood-nourishing ingredients**

Formulas that drain downward with purgative and qi/blood nourishing
ingredients are indicated for constipation due to combined excess and
deficiency. Patients may present with abdominal fullness, constipation
and symptoms due to deficiency of qi/blood or yin fluids. Common
purgative ingredients include *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and
*Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas). Tonic ingredients include *Ren Shen* (Radix
et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Sheng Di
Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae) and *Xuan Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae). A
typical formula is *Huang Long Tang* (Yellow Dragon Decoction).

***Huang Long Tang***

*Shang Han Liu Shu* (*Six Texts on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+
| > 大黄   | *dà huáng*   | 9-15g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei            |
+==========+==============+========+==================================+
| > 芒硝   | *máng xiāo*  | 6-9g   | Natrii Sulfas                    |
+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+
| > 枳实   | *zhĭ shí*    | 6-9g   | Fructus Aurantii Immaturus       |
+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+
| > 厚朴   | *hòu pò*     | 9-12g  | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis     |
+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+
| > 甘草   | *gān căo*    | 3-6g   | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+
| > 人参   | *rén shēn*   | 6-9g   | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng         |
+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+
| > 当归   | *dāng guī*   | 6-9g   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
+----------+--------------+--------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

*Da Huang*, *Mang Xiao*, *Zhi Shi* and *Hou Pu* (*Da Cheng Qi Tang*)
have drastic effect in purging heat accumulation in the stomach and
intestines, *Ren Shen* supplements qi, and *Dang Gui* nourishes blood.
Since the lung is internal-externally connected with the large
intestine, *Jie Geng* is combined to disperse lung qi. *Sheng Jiang*,
*Gan Cao* and *Da Zao* harmonize the spleen and stomach and assist *Ren
Shen* and *Dang Gui* to tonify qi and blood. In addition, *Gan Cao*
coordinates the other ingredients.

This is a typical formula that drains downward with purgative and
qi/blood-nourishing ingredients. *Da Cheng Qi Tang* is used to purge
heat (descending), and *Jie Geng* is used to disperse lung qi
(ascending). This formula purges heat without damaging anti-pathogenic
qi and tonifies qi and blood without retaining pathogenic factors.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with 3-6g of *Jie Geng*, 3-5
pieces of fresh ginger, 2 Chinese dates (jujube fruits) and dissolve
*Mang Xiao* in the decoction.

**\[Functions\]** Purges heat, promotes bowel movements, supplements qi
and nourishes blood.

**\[Indications\]** Yangming Fu-organ excessive syndrome coupled with
deficiency of qi and blood: Watery diarrhea, constipation, abdominal
distension/pain, fever, thirst, mental fatigue, shallow breathing,
delirious speech, carphology, mental unconsciousness, a dry, yellow or
black tongue coating, and a deficient pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for Yangming
Fu-organ excessive syndrome coupled with deficiency of qi and blood.
Interior heat intertwined with feces may obstruct the flow of intestinal
qi, resulting in constipation, and abdominal pain/distension. Heat
consuming body fluids may cause fever, thirst, a dry, yellow or black
tongue coating as well as fecal impaction with watery diarrhea. Heat
disturbing the mind and affecting anti-pathogenic qi may cause
life-threatening signs such as mental unconsciousness, delirious speech,
and carphology. Deficiency of qi and blood or interior heat consuming qi
and blood may cause mental fatigue, shallow breathing and a deficient
pulse. As a result, the treatment strategies are to purge heat, promote
bowel movements, supplement qi, and nourish blood.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Constipation, watery,
foul-smelling diarrhea, abdominal distension/pain, fever, thirst, mental
fatigue, shallow breathing, a dry, yellow tongue coating and a deficient
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For qi and blood deficiency in the elderly, remove *Mang Xiao*,
    decrease the dose of *Da Huang* and add *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma
    Ginseng) and *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis) to moderate its
    drastic purgation and reinforce deficiency. .

-   For a dry, yellow tongue coating and a thready pulse, add *Mai Dong*
    (Radix Ophiopogonis), *Xuan Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae) and *Sheng
    Di* (Radix Rehmanniae) \[Known as *Zeng Ye Tang* (Humor-Increasing
    Decoction)\] to nourish yin fluids.

3\. Modern use: Typhoid, paratyphoid and senile bowel obstruction that
can be diagnosed as Yangming Fu-organ excessive syndrome coupled with
deficiency of qi and blood.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula contains *Da Cheng
Qi Tang*, it's contraindicated for pregnant women. Considering its
drastic purgation, patients should discontinue when constipation is
relieved.

**Summary**

There are a total of 8 formulas and 5 annex formulas in this chapter.
These formulas are classified into five categories: formulas to drain
downward with cold-property ingredients, formulas that drain downward
with warm-property ingredients, formulas that drain downward with
moistening ingredients, formulas that drain downward with water
retention-resolving ingredients and formulas that drain downward with
purgative and qi/blood-nourishing ingredients.

**Formulas that drain downward with cold-property ingredients**

These formulas drain downward and clear heat and are indicated for
interior excessive heat accumulation. For example, *Mang Xiao*, *Da
Huang*, *Zhi Shi* and *Hou Pu* are combined in *Da Cheng Qi Tang*. With
their drastic purgative efficacy, this formula is indicated for Yangming
Fu-organ excessive syndrome characterized by abdominal stuffiness,
fullness, dryness and pain/tenderness.

**Formulas that drain downward with warm-property ingredients**

These formulas drain downward and promote bowel movements and are
indicated for interior cold accumulation. *Wen Pi Tang* warms and
tonifies spleen yang and is indicated for constipation and abdominal
