*Long Dan Xie Gan Tang*, *Xie Qing Wan* and *Dang Gui Long Hui Wan* can
all be used to reduce excess fire in the liver meridian. *Long Dan Xie
Gan Tang* clears fire and resolves damp heat. It's therefore indicated
for ascending of liver fire and downward flow of damp heat. *Xie Qing
Wan* reduces fire and relieves stagnation in the liver meridian. It's
therefore indicated for infantile convulsions due to high fever. *Dang
Gui Long Hui Wan* has a stronger fire-reducing efficacy. It is indicated
for restlessness, delirium and mania due to excess fire in the liver
meridian.

***Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Monarch  | >     | *gé     | 15g | Radix Puerariae Lobatae          |
|          |  葛根 | gēn*    |     |                                  |
+==========+=======+=========+=====+==================================+
| M        | >     | *huáng  | 9g  | Radix Scutellariae               |
| inisters |  黄芩 | qín*    |     |                                  |
+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|          | >     | *huáng  | 9g  | Rhizoma Coptidis                 |
|          |  黄连 | lián*   |     |                                  |
+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Assista  | > 炙  | *zhì    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
| nt/Envoy | 甘草  | gān     |     | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|          |       | căo*    |     |                                  |
+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ge Gen* clears exterior heat, ascends clear spleen qi and
stops diarrhea.

Ministers: *Huang Qin* and *Huang Lian* clear heat and dry dampness in
the stomach and intestines.

Assistant/envoy: *Gan Cao* harmonizes the middle *jiao* and coordinates
the other ingredients.

This formula clears the interior and releases the exterior. It is
indicated for "70% pathogenic factors entering the interior, whereas 30%
still remaining in the exterior".

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Releases the exterior and clears the interior.

**\[Indications\]** Unresolved exterior heat entering the interior:
Fever, diarrhea of strong-odored stools, a burning sensation of the
anus, a feverish sensation in the chest, a dry mouth, panting, sweating,
a yellow tongue coating and a rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for unresolved
exterior heat entering Yangming. Exuberant interior heat may cause
fever, thirst, a feverish sensation in the chest, a yellow tongue
coating and a rapid pulse. Interior heat affecting the lung may cause
panting. Interior heat affecting the skin may cause sweating. Yangming
heat forces the turbid intestinal qi to move downward and result in
diarrhea with strong-odored stools and a burning sensation of the anus.
As a result, the treatment strategies are to release the exterior and
clear heat in the stomach and intestines.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, diarrhea, a yellow tongue
coating and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For abdominal pain, add *Chao Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba
    Praeparata) to alleviate spasms and stop pain.

-   For tenesmus, add *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) and *Bing Lang*
    (Semen Arecae) to move qi and relieve tenesmus.

3\. Modern use: Acute enteritis, bacillary dysentery, enteric typhoid
and stomach flu that can be diagnosed as interior Yangming heat.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with diarrhea, no fever, and a deep, slow or weak pulse due to deficiency cold.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-diarrhea-no-fever-and-a-deep-slow-or-weak-pulse-due-to-deficiency-cold. .unnumbered}

***Zuo Jin Wan***

*Dan Xi Xin Fa* (*Teachings of Zhu Dan-xi*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+-------+---------+-------+--------------------------------+
| Monarch  | >     | *huáng  | 180g  | Rhizoma Coptidis               |
|          |  黄连 | lián*   |       |                                |
+==========+=======+=========+=======+================================+
| Assista  | > 吴  | *wú zhū | 30g   | Fructus Evodiae                |
| nt/Envoy | 茱萸  | yú*     |       |                                |
+----------+-------+---------+-------+--------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Huang Lian* clears heart fire to clear fire in the liver and
stomach.

Assistant/envoy: *Wu Zhu Yu* is pungent and warm. It soothes liver qi,
harmonizes the stomach and bears qi down, protects the stomach from
being damaged by cold-property *Huang Lian* and induces *Huang Lian* to
the liver meridian to clear liver fire.

The combination of *Huang Lian* and *Wu Zhu Yu* clears heart fire
(mother) to clear liver fire (son), and subsequently, reduces stomach
fire and relieves such symptoms as acid reflux, vomiting and a bitter
mouth.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 3-6g water pill or 2-4 capsules
(0.35g/capsule) after meal for each dose, 2 doses a day. Alternatively,
decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears liver fire, bears qi down and stops vomiting.

**\[Indications\]** Liver fire affecting the stomach: Distension and
pain in the hypochondriac region, acid reflux, gastric upset, vomiting,
a bitter mouth, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a wiry, rapid
pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for liver fire
affecting the stomach and disharmony between the liver and stomach.
Liver fire and qi stagnation may cause distension/pain in the
hypochondriac region. Liver fire affecting the descending of stomach qi
may cause gastric upset, acid reflux, vomiting and a bitter mouth. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to clear liver fire, harmonize the
stomach and bear qi down.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Vomiting, acid reflux, pain in
the hypochondriac region, a bitter mouth, a red tongue with a yellow
coating and a wiry, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe acid reflux, add *Wu Zei Gu* (Endoconcha Sepiae) and
    *Duan Wa Leng* (Concha Arcae Praeparatum) to inhibit acid.

-   For severe pain in the hypochondriac region, combine with *Si Ni
    San* to soothe the liver and move qi.

3\. Modern use: Acute or chronic gastritis, esophagitis and gastric
ulcer that can be diagnosed as liver fire affecting the stomach.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: It's worth noting the ratio between *Huang Lian* and *Wu Zhu Yu* is 6:1. This formula is contraindicated for patients with vomiting and acid reflux due to deficiency cold.  {#cautions-contraindications-its-worth-noting-the-ratio-between-huang-lian-and-wu-zhu-yu-is-61.-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-vomiting-and-acid-reflux-due-to-deficiency-cold. .unnumbered}

### **\[Annex formulas\]**  {#annex-formulas-1 .unnumbered}

+--------------+-----------+------------------+------------------------+
| **Formula**  | **Ingr    | **Actions**      | **Indications**        |
|              | edients** |                  |                        |
+--------------+-----------+------------------+------------------------+
| #### Wu J    | Huang     | Soothes the      | Disharmony between the |
| i Wan (*Tai  | Lian, Wu  | liver, regulates | liver and spleen:      |
| Ping Hui Min | Zhu Yu    | the spleen,      | gastric pain, acid     |
|  He Ji Ju Fa | and Bai   | clears heat and  | reflux, abdominal pain |
| ng*) {#wu-ji | Shao      | harmonizes the   | and diarrhea           |
| -wan-tai-pin |           | stomach          |                        |
| g-hui-min-he |           |                  |                        |
| -ji-ju-fang  |           |                  |                        |
| .unnumbered} |           |                  |                        |
+--------------+-----------+------------------+------------------------+
| #### Xi      | Huang     | Clears heat,     | Dysentery due to damp  |
| ang Lian Wan | Lian and  | dries dampness,  | heat: Diarrhea with    |
|  (*Tai Ping  | Mu Xiang  | moves qi and     | blood, abdominal pain  |
| Hui Min He J |           | relieves         | and tenesmus           |
| i Ju Fang*)  |           | stagnation       |                        |
| {#xiang-lian |           |                  |                        |
| -wan-tai-pin |           |                  |                        |
| g-hui-min-he |           |                  |                        |
| -ji-ju-fang  |           |                  |                        |
| .unnumbered} |           |                  |                        |
+--------------+-----------+------------------+------------------------+

*Zuo Jin Wan*, *Wu Ji Wan* and *Xiang Lian Wan* all contain *Huang Lian*
and *Wu Zhu Yu*. With a ratio of 6: 1 between *Huang Lian* and *Wu Zhu
Yu*, *Zuo Jin Wan* focuses on clearing the liver and bearing qi down. It
is indicated for vomiting and acid reflux due to liver fire affecting
the stomach. *Wu Ji Wan* uses *Bai Shao* to harmonize the liver and
spleen, relieve spasms and alleviate pain. It is indicated for gastric
pain, acid reflux, abdominal and diarrhea due to disharmony between the
liver and spleen. *Xiang Lian Wan* uses fried *Huang Lian* with *Wu Zhu
Yu* to clear heat and dry dampness and *Mu Xiang* is added to move qi
and stop pain. It is indicated for dysentery with pus and blood in
stools, abdominal pain and tenesmus due to damp heat.

***Xie Bai San***

*Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue* (*Key to Diagnosis and Treatment of
Children's Diseases*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Monarch  | > 桑  | *sāng   | 12g | Cortex Mori                      |
|          | 白皮  | bái pí* |     |                                  |
+==========+=======+=========+=====+==================================+
| Minister | > 地  | *dì gŭ  | 12g | Cortex Lycii                     |
|          | 骨皮  | pí*     |     |                                  |
+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Assista  | > 炙  | *zhì    | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
| nt/Envoy | 甘草  | gān     |     | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|          |       | căo*    |     |                                  |
+----------+-------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Sang Bai Pi* is sweet, cold and descending. It enters the lung
meridian, clears lung heat, bears lung qi down and stops
coughing/panting.

Minister: *Di Gu Pi* is sweet and cold. It enters the lung, clears lung
heat, nourishes yin and assists the lung in purging fire.

Assistant/envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* and *Jing Mi* benefit the stomach,
generate fluids, and coordinate the other ingredients.

The formula combines purging with moistening and reducing with
reinforcing. It works well for lung heat consuming fluids.

**\[Method of administration\]** Add 6g of *Jing Mi* (Oryza Sativa L.)
and decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears lung heat and stops coughing/panting.

**\[Indications\]** Coughing/panting due to lung heat: Cough, panting,
skin-steaming feverish sensation, a red tongue with a yellow coating and
a thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for cough due to
hidden fire in the lung. Hidden fire in the lung affecting the
descending of lung qi may cause cough. The lung dominates the skin and
skin hair. Hidden fire in the lung may cause skin-steaming feverish
sensation, especially in the afternoon. The hidden fire may damage yin
and lead to a red tongue with a yellow coating and a thready, rapid
pulse. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear lung heat and
stop coughing/panting.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough, panting, skin-steaming
feverish sensation, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a thready,
rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe lung heat, add *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and *Zhi
    Mu* (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae) to enhance the efficacy of purging lung
    heat.

-   For severe coughing/panting, add *Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae), *Xing
    Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum) and *Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) to
    enhance the efficacy of clearing the lung and alleviating
    coughing/panting.

-   For restlessness, feverish sensation and thirst, add *Tian Hua Fen*
    (Radix Trichosanthis) and *Lu Gen* (Rhizoma Phragmitis) to clear
    heat and generate fluids.

3\. Modern use: Bronchitis, early stage pneumonia, whooping cough and
early stage measles that can be diagnosed as hidden fire in the lung.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with coughing due to wind cold or phlegm turbidity or panting in a deficiency syndrome.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-coughing-due-to-wind-cold-or-phlegm-turbidity-or-panting-in-a-deficiency-syndrome. .unnumbered}

***Qing Wei San***

*Pi Wei Lun* (*Treatise on the Spleen and Stomach*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 黄连 | *huáng  | 3g  | Rhizoma Coptidis                   |
| narch |        | lián*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 升麻 | *shēng  | 9g  | Rhizoma Cimicifugae                |
| sters |        | má*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 生地 | *shēng  | 9g  | Radix Rehmanniae                   |
| tants |        | dì*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 丹皮 | *dān    | 9g  | Cortex Moutan                      |
|       |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng   | 6g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis           |
|       |        | guī*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Huang Lian* clears stomach fire.

Minister/envoy: *Sheng Ma* is pungent, sweet and mildly cold. It enters
the stomach and large intestine meridians, ascends yang, purges
fire/heat and removes toxins. In addition, it guides the other
ingredients into the stomach meridian.

When used in combination with *Sheng Ma*, *Huang Lian* purges fire
without causing cold retention. When used in combination with *Huang
Lian*, *Sheng Ma* disperses fire without causing adverse ascending.

Assistants: As stomach heat may consume yin blood, *Sheng Di* is used to
cool blood and nourish yin. *Dan Pi* clears heat, cools blood and
resolve stagnation. *Dang Gui* nourishes and circulates blood and helps
to resolve swelling and alleviate pain.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears the stomach and cools blood.

**\[Indications\]** Toothache due to stomach fire: Toothache radiating
to the head, a feverish sensation on the cheeks, toothache that is
alleviated with cold and aggrivated with heat, gum swelling, redness or
ulceration, lip or facial pain and swelling, foul breath, a dry mouth, a
dry, red tongue with a yellow coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for toothache due
to stomach fire. The stomach meridian enters the upper teeth and the
large intestine meridian enters the lower teeth. Accumulated heat in the
stomach may, over time, transform into fire. The ascending of stomach
fire may cause toothache that radiate to the head and a feverish
sensation on the cheeks. The stomach is full of qi and blood. Stomach
heat may affect the capillaries and lead to bleeding gums. Qi stagnation
and blood stasis may gradually cause decay of blood and flesh hence the
swollen, red and ulcerative gums and lips or facial pain and swelling.
Foul breath and a dry mouth/tongue are signs indicative of stomach heat
damaging fluids. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear
stomach fire and cool/circulate blood.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Toothache, foul breath, a red
tongue with a yellow coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For constipation, add *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) to purge
    heat and promote bowel movements.

-   For bleeding gums due to exuberant stomach fire, add *Niu Xi* (Radix
    Achyranthis Bidentatae) to induce blood to flow downward.

-   For severe thirst due to damage to fluids, add *Xuan Shen* (Radix
    Scrophulariae) and *Tian Hua Fen* (Radix Trichosanthis) to clear
    heat and generate fluids.

3\. Modern use: Stomatitis, periodontitis and trigeminal neuralgia that
can be diagnosed as stomach heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula clears the stomach and
cools blood. It is contraindicated for patients with toothache due to
wind cold or kidney deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Xie Huang San* (Yellow-Draining Powder) from the
*Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue* (Key to Diagnosis and Treatment of
Children's Diseases)

\[Ingredients\] *Huo Xiang Ye* (Herba Agastachis), *Shan Zhi Ren*
(Fructus Gardeniae), *Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum), *Gan Cao* (Radix et
Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), and *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae).

\[Actions\] Clears the hidden fire in the spleen and stomach.

\[Indications\] Hidden fire in the spleen and stomach: Foul breath,
mouth ulceration, thirst, fast hunger after eating food, a dry mouth and
lips, a red tongue and a rapid pulse. It's also used to treat waggling
tongue due to spleen heat.

Both *Xie Huang San* and *Qing Wei San* combine clearing heat with
ascending and dispersing. *Xie Huang San* also acts to resolve dampness
and is often used to treat wagging tongue, foul breath and mouth
ulceration due to spleen heat. Qing Wei San clears the stomach, cools
blood and nourishes yin. It is often used to treat toothache, bleeding
gums or facial swelling and pain due to stomach heat.

***Yu Nǚ Jian***

*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (*The Complete Works of Zhang Jing-yue*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 石膏 | *shí    | 9    | Gypsum Fibrosum                  |
| narch |        | gāo*    | -15g |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Min   | > 熟地 | *shú    | 9    | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata      |
| ister |        | dì*     | -30g |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 知母 | *zhī    | 4.5g | Rhizoma Anemarrhenae             |
| tants |        | mŭ*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 6g   | Radix Ophiopogonis               |
|       |        | dōng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 牛膝 | *niú    | 4.5g | Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae     |
|       |        | xī*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Shi Gao* purges stomach fire, relieves feverish sensations and
stops thirst.

Minister: *Shu Di* nourishes kidney yin and supplements kidney essence.

Assistants: *Zhi Mu* assists *Shi Gao* in clearing stomach heat and
assists *Shu Di* in nourishing kidney yin. *Mai Dong* nourishes the
kidney and benefits the stomach.

Assistant/envoy: *Niu Xi* induces heat downward and tonifies the liver
and kidney.

Both *Yu* *Nǚ Jian* and *Qing Wei San* are indicated for toothache due
to stomach heat. *Qing Wei San* clears the stomach and cools and
circulates blood. It is indicated for toothache, loose teeth and red,
swollen and ulcerative gums due to exuberant stomach fire affecting the
blood. *Yu Nǚ Jian* clears the stomach and nourishes yin. It is
indicated for toothache, loose teeth and bleeding gums due to stomach
fire and kidney yin deficiency.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears stomach heat and nourishes kidney yin.

**\[Indications\]** Stomach heat and kidney yin deficiency: Headache,
toothache, loose teeth, bleeding gums, feverish sensations, thirst, a
red tongue with a dry, scanty coating and a slippery, rapid pulse. It's
also used to treat fast hunger after eating food due to wasting &
thirsting disorder.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for stomach heat
and kidney yin deficiency. Ascending of stomach heat along the pathway
of the stomach meridian may cause headache and toothache. Heat damaging
capillaries may cause the gums to bleed. Because the kidney dominates
bones and the teeth are an extension of bone, kidney yin deficiency may
cause loose teeth. Feverish sensations, thirst, and a red tongue with a
dry, yellow coating are signs indicative of stomach heat and yin
deficiency. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear stomach
heat and nourish kidney yin.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Loose teeth, feverish sensations,
thirst, and a dry, deep-red tongue.

2\. Modifications

-   For exuberant fire, add *Shan Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae) and *Di Gu
    Pi* (Cortex Lycii) to clear heat and purge fire.

-   For bleeding gums due to exuberant blood heat, remove *Shu Di* add
    *Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae) and *Xuan Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae)
    to enhance the efficacy of clearing heat and cooling blood.

3\. Modern use: Gingivitis, acute stomatitis, glossitis and diabetes
that can be diagnosed as stomach heat and kidney yin deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with deficiency cold in the spleen and stomach.

***Shao Yao Tang***

*Su Wen Bing Ji Qi Yi Bao Ming Ji* (*Collection of Writings on the
Mechanism of Disease, Suitability of Qi, and the Safeguarding of Life as
Discussed in the 'Basic Questions'*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 黄连 | *huáng  | 15g | Rhizoma Coptidis                   |
| archs |        | lián*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 黄芩 | *huáng  | 15g | Radix Scutellariae                 |
|       |        | qín*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 木香 | *mù     | 6g  | Radix Aucklandiae                  |
| sters |        | xiāng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 槟榔 | *bīng   | 6g  | Semen Arecae                       |
|       |        | láng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng   | 15g | Radix Angelicae Sinensis           |
|       |        | guī*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 白芍 | *bái    | 30g | Radix Paeoniae Alba                |
|       |        | sháo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 大黄 | *dà     | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei              |
| tants |        | huáng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 肉桂 | *ròu    | 3g  | Cortex Cinnamomi                   |
|       |        | guì*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy | >      | *zhì    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       | 炙甘草 | gān     |     | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Huang Lian* and *Huang Qi*n are bitter and cold. They clear
heat, dry dampness, benefit the stomach and intestines and stop
diarrhea.

Ministers: *Mu Xiang* and *Bing Lang* move qi and relieve stagnation.
*Dang Gui* and *Bai Shao* nourish and circulate blood. A large dose of
*Bai Shao* is used to alleviate spasms and pain.

Assistants: *Da Huang* is bitter, cold and descending. The combination
of *Da Huang*, *Huang Qin* and *Huang Lian* clears heat and dries
dampness. The combination of *Da Huang* and *Dang Gui* circulates blood.
It also purges damp heat through promoting bowel movements, known as
"treating excessive diarrhea with purgatives". A small dose of *Rou Gui*
is used to assist *Dang Gui* in moving blood and moderates the harsh
property of bitter, cold ingredients.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* benefits qi, harmonizes the middle *jiao*, and
coordinates the other ingredients.

Compatibility characteristics: This formula regulates qi and blood, and
treats excessive diarrhea with purgatives. It also addresses both cold
and heat, especially using cold to counteract heat.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, dries dampness and harmonizes qi and
blood.

**\[Indications\]** Dysentery due to damp heat: Abdominal pain, pus and
blood in stools, tenesmus, a burning sensation in the anus, dark yellow
urine, a yellow and greasy tongue coating and a wiry, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for dysentery due
to damp heat. Downward flow of damp heat to the large intestine may
obstruct the flow of qi and cause abdominal pain and tenesmus. Damp heat
scorching the capillaries of the intestine may cause pus and blood in
stools. A burning sensation in the anus, dark yellow urine, a red tongue
with a yellow, greasy coating and a wiry, rapid pulse are signs
indicative of internal damp heat disturbing qi and blood. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to clear heat, dry dampness and harmonize
qi and blood.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Pus and blood in stools,
abdominal pain, tenesmus, a yellow and slightly greasy tongue coating
and a wiry, rapid or slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a dry, yellow tongue coating due to heat consuming fluids,
    remove *Rou Gui* and add *Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae) to cool blood
    and nourish yin.

-   For a greasy tongue coating and a slippery pulse, add *Sha Ren*
    (Fructus Amomi) and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) to
    resolve dampness and move qi.

-   For more blood than pus in stools or bloody stools, add *Bai Tou
    Weng* (Radix Pulsatillae) and *Di Yu* (Radix Sanguisorbae) to cool
    blood and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Bacillary dysentery, amoebic dysentery, allergic colitis
and acute enteritis that can be diagnosed as damp heat accumulating in
the large intestine.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with early-stage dysentery coupled with exterior syndrome or diarrhea due to deficiency cold.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-early-stage-dysentery-coupled-with-exterior-syndrome-or-diarrhea-due-to-deficiency-cold. .unnumbered}

**\[Annex Formula\]** *Huang Qin Tang* (Scutellaria Decoction) from the
*Shang Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

\[Ingredients\] *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae), *Bai Shao* (Radix
Paeoniae Alba), *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae Praeparata
cum Melle) and *Da Zao* (Fructus Jujubae)

\[Actions\] Clears heat, stops diarrhea, harmonizes the middle *jiao*
and alleviates pain

\[Indications\] Dysentery due to heat: Fever, a bitter taste in the
mouth, abdominal pain, diarrhea, a red tongue with a yellow coating and
a rapid pulse.

Both *Huang Qin Tang* and *Shao Yao Tang* are indicated for heat-related
dysentery. *Huang Qin Tang* has a mild heat-clearing efficacy. It is
often used to treat diarrhea due to damp heat with incomplete bowel
evacuation, a bitter taste in the mouth and fever. *Shao Yao Tang* has a
stronger efficacy in clearing heat and drying dampness. In addition, it
also moves qi and regulates blood. It is often used to treat diarrhea
due to damp heat with pus and blood in stools, abdominal pain, tenesmus
and a burning sensation in the anus.

***Bai Tou Weng Tang***

*Shan Han Lun* (*Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *bái    | 15g  | Radix Pulsatillae                |
| narch | 白头翁 | tóu     |      |                                  |
|       |        | wēng*   |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Mini  | > 黄连 | *huáng  | 5g   | Rhizoma Coptidis                 |
| sters |        | lián*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 黄柏 | *huáng  | 12g  | Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis    |
|       |        | băi*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 秦皮 | *qín    | 12g  | Cortex Fraxini                   |
| stant |        | pí*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Bai Tou Weng* is bitter and cold and enters blood. It clears
heat, removes toxins, cools blood and stops dysentery.

Ministers: *Huang Lian* clears heat, removes toxins and is an essential
herb for dysentery. *Huang Bai* clears heat and dries dampness. *Huang
Lian* and *Huang Bai* assist *Bai Tou Weng* in clearing toxic heat in
the intestines.

Assistant: *Qin Pi* is bitter and cold. It clears heat, removes toxins,
astringes the intestines and stops dysentery.

Assistant/envoy: *Niu Xi* induces heat downward and tonifies the liver
and kidney.

Both *Bai Tou Weng Tang* and *Shao Yao Tang* are indicated for
dysentery. *Bai Tou Weng Tang* is indicated for dysentery due to toxic
heat entering the blood phase. Clinical manifestations include bloody
stools, abdominal pain, tenesmus, a red tongue with a yellow coating and
a wiry, rapid pulse. *Shao Yao Tang* is indicated for dysentery due to
damp heat accumulating in the intestines. Clinical manifestations
include blood and pus in stools, abdominal pain, tenesmus, a yellow and
slightly greasy tongue coating and a wiry, rapid or slippery pulse. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat, dry dampness and
harmonize qi and blood, known as "treating excessive diarrhea with
purgatives".

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, removes toxins, cools blood and stops
dysentery.

**\[Indications\]** Dysentery due to toxic heat: Abdominal pain,
tenesmus, a burning sensation in the anus, bloody stools (more blood
than pus), thirst with a desire to drink water, a red tongue with a
yellow coating and a wiry, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for dysentery due
to toxic heat. Toxic heat damaging the capillaries of the intestines may
cause bloody stools (more blood than pus). Toxic heat obstructing the qi
of intestines may cause abdominal pain and tenesmus. Thirst with a
desire to drink water, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a wiry,
rapid pulse are signs indicative of internal exuberant toxic heat. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat, remove toxins, cool
blood and stop dysentery.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: More blood than pus in stools,
abdominal pain, tenesmus, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a wiry,
rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe tenesmus, add *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae), *Bing Lang*
    (Semen Arecae) and *Zhi Qiao* (Fructus Aurantii) to regulate qi.

-   For profuse pus and blood in stools, add *Chi Shao* (Radix Paeoniae
    Rubra), *Dan Pi* (Cortex Moutan) and *Di Yu* (Radix Sanguisorbae) to
    cool and harmonize blood.

3\. Modern use: Bacillary dysentery and amoebic dysentery that can be
diagnosed as toxic heat accumulating in the intestines.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with spleen/stomach weakness should use this formula with caution.  {#cautions-contraindications-patients-with-spleenstomach-weakness-should-use-this-formula-with-caution. .unnumbered}

**4.6 Formulas that clear deficiency heat**

Formulas that clear deficiency heat are indicated for night fever with
morning coolness and a red tongue with a scanty coating due to
incompletely resolved heat and yin damage in late stage febrile disease
as well as bone-steaming tidal fever, night sweats and a red face due to
internal heat from yin deficiency. Common deficiency heat-clearing
ingredients like *Qing Hao* (Herba Artemisiae Annuae), *Qin* *Jiao*
(Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae) and *Yin Chai Hu* (Radix Stellariae) are
often used in combination with yin-nourishing ingredients like *Bie Jia*
(Carapax Trionycis) and *Sheng Di Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae). Typical
formulas are *Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang* (Sweet Wormwood and Turtle Shell
Decoction), *Qing Gu San* (Bone-Clearing Powder) and *Dang Gui Liu Huang
Tan*g (Chinese Angelica Six Yellow Decoction).

***Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (*Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 青蒿 | *qīng   | 6g  | Herba Artemisiae Annuae            |
| archs |        | hāo*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 鳖甲 | *biē    | 15g | Carapax Trionycis                  |
|       |        | jiă*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 生地 | *shēng  | 12g | Radix Rehmanniae                   |
| sters |        | dì*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 知母 | *zhī    | 6g  | Rhizoma Anemarrhenae               |
|       |        | mŭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 丹皮 | *dān    | 9g  | Cortex Moutan                      |
| stant |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Qing Hao* is bitter, pungent, cold and aromatic. It clears
heat and dispels pathogenic factors. *Bie Jia* is salty and cold. It
nourishes yin and clears heat.

Ministers: *Sheng Di* and *Zhi Mu* assist *Qing Hao* in clearing heat
and assist *Bie Jia* in nourishing yin.

Assistant: *Dan Pi* is bitter, pungent and mildly cold. It enters the
blood phase to clear hidden heat.

This formula nourishes yin without retaining pathogenic factors and
removes pathogenic factors without damaging anti-pathogenic qi.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin and clears heat.

**\[Indications\]** Late stage febrile disease (heat damaging yin):
Night fever with morning coolness, absence of sweating, a red tongue
with a scanty coating and a thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for heat damaging
yin in late stage febrile diseases. *Wei*-defensive yang (qi) circulates
on the exterior during the day and enters the interior at night. The
struggle between *Wei*-defensive yang and unresolved pathogenic factors
in the interior may cause night fever. In the morning, *Wei*-defensive
yang (qi) travels to the exterior, resulting in morning coolness. Heat
severely damaging yin in late stage febrile disease may cause an absence
of sweating. A red tongue with a scanty coating and a thready, rapid
pulse are signs indicative of internal heat from yin deficiency. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to nourish yin and remove
pathogenic factors.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Night fever with morning
coolness, absence of sweating, a red tongue with a scanty coating and a
thready, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For night fever with morning coolness and thirst with a desire to
    drink water, add *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) and *Shi Hu*
    (Caulis Dendrobii) to clear heat, generate fluids, and alleviate
    thirst.

-   For fatigue and thirst due to deficiency of qi and yin, add *Ren
    Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Wu Wei Zi* (Fructus Schisandrae
    Chinensis) and *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) to supplement qi and
    nourish yin.

-   For pediatric summer fever, add *He Gen* (Petiolus Nelmbinis), *Zhu
    Ye* (Folium Phyllostachydis Henonis) and *Lu Gen* (Rhizoma
    Phragmitis) to clear summer heat.

3\. Modern use: Fever for an unknown reason, low-grade fever in
remissive contagious diseases, pediatric summer fever, chronic
nephropyelitis and renal tuberculosis that can be diagnosed as fever due
to yin deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with wind stirring due to yin deficiency. In addition, it's
advisable to wrap *Qing Hao* in a piece of cloth and decoct later,
because it is not resistant to high temperature.

***Qing Gu San***

*Zheng Zhi Zhun Sheng* (*Standards for Diagnosis and Treatment*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *yín    | 5g  | Radix Stellariae                   |
| narch | 银柴胡 | chái    |     |                                    |
|       |        | hú*     |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 知母 | *zhī    | 3g  | Rhizoma Anemarrhenae               |
| sters |        | mŭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *hú     | 3g  | Rhizoma Picrorhizae                |
|       | 胡黄连 | huáng   |     |                                    |
|       |        | lián*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *dì gŭ  | 3g  | Cortex Lycii                       |
|       | 地骨皮 | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 秦艽 | *qín    | 3g  | Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae       |
| tants |        | jiāo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 青蒿 | *qīng   | 3g  | Herba Artemisiae Annuae            |
|       |        | hāo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 鳖甲 | *biē    | 3g  | Carapax Trionycis                  |
|       |        | jiă*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy | > 甘草 | *gān    | 2g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Yin Chai Hu* is sweet, bitter and mildly cold. It clears
deficiency heat and eliminates the bone-steaming feverish sensation.

Ministers: *Zhi Mu, Hu Huang Lian* and *Di Gu Pi* clear deficiency heat
and eliminate feverish sensations.

Assistants: *Qin* *Jiao* and *Qing Hao* are both pungent and cold. They
clear internal deficiency heat. *Bie Jia* nourishes yin and submerges
yang. The combination of *Bie Jia* and *Zhi Mu* has a stronger efficacy
in nourishing yin.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients and protects stomach
qi from being damaged by bitter, cold ingredients.

This formula clears interior heat (symptoms) and nourishes yin fluids
(the root cause).

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears deficiency heat and eliminates bone-steaming
feverish sensations.

**\[Indications\]** Internal heat due to yin deficiency: Bone-steaming
tidal fever or persistent low-grade fever, weight loss, red lips, malar
flush, drowsiness, night sweats, thirst, restlessness, a red tongue with
a scanty coating and a thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for bone-steaming
tidal fever due to yin deficiency. Yin deficiency of the liver and
kidney may cause internal heat. Accumulation of deficiency heat may
cause bone-steaming tidal fever, restlessness and thirst. Ascending of
deficiency fire may cause red lips and malar flush. Deficiency fire
dispelling fluids may cause night sweats. Over time, failure of yin to
nourish the skin and body may cause weight loss. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to clear deficiency heat, eliminate
bone-steaming feverish sensation and nourish yin.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Bone-steaming tidal fever, night
sweats, weight loss, a red tongue with a scanty coating and a thready,
rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe yin deficiency with internal heat, remove *Hu Huang Lian*
    and add *Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae) to enhance the yin-nourishing
    efficacy.

-   For cough due to deficiency fire scorching the lung, add *E* *Jiao*
    (Colla Corii Asini), *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) and *Chuan Bei
    Mu* (Bulbus Fritillariae Cirrhosae) to moisten the lung and stop
    coughing.

3\. Modern use: Tuberculosis or consumptive conditions that can be
diagnosed as internal heat due to yin deficiency.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: It's advisable to wrap *Qing Hao* in a piece of cloth and decoct later, because it is not resistant to high temperature. {#cautions-contraindications-its-advisable-to-wrap-qing-hao-in-a-piece-of-cloth-and-decoct-later-because-it-is-not-resistant-to-high-temperature. .unnumbered}

***Dang Gui Liu Huang Tang***

*Lan Shi Mi Cang* (*Secrets from the Orchid Chamber*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 当归 | *dāng   | 6g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis           |
| archs |        | guī*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 生地 | *shēng  | 6g  | Radix Rehmanniae                   |
|       |        | dì*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 熟地 | *shú    | 6g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata        |
|       |        | dì*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 黄连 | *huáng  | 6g  | Rhizoma Coptidis                   |
| sters |        | lián*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 黄芩 | *huáng  | 6g  | Radix Scutellariae                 |
|       |        | qín*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 黄柏 | *huáng  | 6g  | Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis      |
|       |        | băi*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 黄芪 | *huáng  | 12g | Radix Astragali                    |
| stant |        | qí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Dang Gui, Sheng Di* and *Shu Di* nourish yin and blood.

Ministers: *Huang Lian*, *Huang Qin* and *Huang Bai* clear heat, reduce
fire and protect yin.

Assistant: *Huang Qi* supplements qi and secures the exterior.

Compatibility characteristics: 1) combination of nourishing yin/blood
and clearing fire/heat; 2) combination of supplement qi to secure the
exterior and nourishing yin to reduce fire.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin, reduces fire, secures the exterior and
stops sweating.

**\[Indications\]** Fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency: Fever,
night sweats, malar flush, restlessness, dry mouth and lips, dry stools,
dark-yellow urine, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for night sweats
due to fire hyperactivity from yin deficiency. Deficiency fire
disturbing the yin fluids may cause fever and night sweats. Ascending of
deficiency fire may cause malar flush and restlessness. Dry mouth and
lips, dry stools and dark-yellow urine indicate fire hyperactivity due
to yin deficiency. As a result, the treatment strategies are to nourish
yin, reduce fire, secure the exterior and stop sweating.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Night sweats, malar flush,
restlessness, dark-yellow urine, a red tongue and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For mild fire/heat, remove *Huang Lian* and *Huang Qin* and add *Zhi
    Mu* (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae) to clear heat and nourish yin.

-   For severe sweating, add *Fu Xiao Mai* (Fructus Tritici Levis) and
    *Shan Yu Rou* (Fructus Corni) to enhance the efficacy of stopping
    sweating.

-   For severe hot flush due to yang hyperactivity from yin deficiency,
    add *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba) and *Gui Ban* (Plastrum
    Testudinis) to nourish yin and submerge yang.

3\. Modern use: Hyperthyroidism, tuberculosis, diabetes and menopausal
syndrome that can be diagnosed as fire hyperactivity due to yin
deficiency.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with a poor appetite and loose stools due to spleen/stomach weakness.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-a-poor-appetite-and-loose-stools-due-to-spleenstomach-weakness. .unnumbered}

**Summary**

There are a total of 19 formulas and 10 annex formulas in this chapter.
These formulas are classified into six categories: formulas to clear
heat in the qi phase, formulas that clear heat in the *Ying*-nutrients
phase and cool blood, formulas that clear heat and remove toxins,
formulas that clear heat in qi and blood phases, formulas that clear
heat in zang-fu organs and formulas that clear deficiency heat.

**Formulas that clear heat in the qi phase**

Both *Bai Hu Tang* and *Zhu Ye Shi Gao Tang* clear heat in the qi phase.
*Bai Hu Tang* has a stronger efficacy in clearing heat in the qi phase.
It is indicated for high fever, sweating, thirst and a large, surging
pulse. With a mild efficacy in clearing heat, *Zhu Ye Shi Gao Tang* also
supplements qi, nourishes yin and harmonizes the stomach. It is
indicated for fever, profuse sweating, chest tightness, restlessness,
and nausea due to damage to qi and yin in late stage febrile disease and
ascending of stomach qi.

**Formulas that clear heat in the *Ying*-nutrients phase and cool
blood**

Both *Qing Ying Tang* and *Qing Re Di Huang Tang* (originally known as
*Xi* *Jiao Di Huang Tang*) are indicated for heat entering the
*Ying*-nutrients and blood. *Qing Ying Tang* clears heat in the
*Ying*-nutrients phase, nourishes yin and harmonizes blood. It is
indicated for fever that is aggravated at night, delirium or indistinct
purpura and erythema in the early stage of heat entering the
*Ying*-nutrients phase. *Qing Re Di Huang Tang* clears heat, removes
toxins, cools blood and resolves stasis. It is indicated for
unconsciousness, delirium, purple or black skin rashes, bleeding or
macular eruption due to heat entering the blood phase.

**Formulas that clear heat and remove toxins**

*Huang Lian Jie Du Tang, Liang Ge San* and *Pu Ji Xiao Du Yin* can all
be used to clear heat and remove toxins. Consisting of bitter, cold,
fire-reducing and toxin-removing ingredients, *Huang Lian Jie Du Tang*
is indicated for fever, restlessness, incoherent speech, hematemesis and
boils/carbuncles due to excessive heat/fire. *Liang Ge San* reduces fire
and promotes bowel movements. It is indicated for fever, a red face,
feverish sensations in the chest, mouth or tongue ulceration,
constipation and dark-yellow urine. *Pu Ji Xiao Du Yin* removes wind,
clears heat and removes toxins. It is indicated for fever with a swollen
head due to pandemic wind-heat toxin.

**Formulas that clear heat in the qi and blood phases**

*Qing Wen Bai Du Yin* is modified from *Bai Hu Tan*g, *Qing Re Di Huang
Tang* and *Huang Lian Jie Du Tang*. It is indicated for high fever,
strong thirst, splitting headache, extreme restlessness, hematemesis,
maculae, a deep red tongue, chapped lips and a deep, rapid pulse.

**Formulas that clear heat in zang-fu organs**

*Dao Chi San* clears the heart, promotes urination and nourishes yin. It
is indicated for feverish sensations in the chest, mouth or tongue
ulceration and dysuria due to heat in the heart and small intestine
meridians. Both *Long Dan Xie Gan Tang* and *Zuo Jin Wan* can clear
liver fire. *Long Dan Xie Gan Tang* has a stronger efficacy in clearing
excess fire. It also resolves damp heat in the lower *jiao* and is
therefore indicated for excess fire in the liver and gallbladder and
downward flow of damp heat. *Zuo Jin Wan* bears qi down and stops
vomiting. It is indicated for vomiting or gastric upset due to liver
fire affecting the stomach. *Xie Bai San* clears lung heat, stops
coughing and alleviates panting. It is indicated for cough or panting
due to hidden fire in the lung. Both *Qing Wei San* and *Yu* *Nǚ Jian*
can treat toothache due to stomach fire. *Qing Wei San* clears the
stomach, cools blood and at the same time, reduces hidden fire. It is
indicated for toothache, headache, gum atrophy, bleeding gums and
swollen, painful cheeks due to exuberant stomach fire. *Yu* *Nǚ Jian*
clears stomach heat and nourishes kidney yin. It is indicated for
restlessness, feverish sensations, a headache and bleeding gums due to
stomach fire and kidney deficiency. *Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang*,
*Shao Yao Tang* and *Bai Tou Weng Tang* can all be used to treat
dysentery. *Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang* releases the exterior and
clears the interior. It is indicated for fever and diarrhea due to
unresolved heat entering the interior. *Shao Yao Tang* clears heat,
dries dampness and harmonizes qi and blood. It is indicated for
dysentery due to damp heat. *Bai Tou Weng Tang* clears heat, removes
toxins, cools blood and stops dysentery. It is indicated for dysentery
with bloody stools due to toxic heat.

**Formulas that clear deficiency heat**

*Qing Hao Bie* *Jiao Tang, Qing Gu San* and *Dang Gui Liu Huang Tang*
can all be used to nourish yin and clear heat. *Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang*
nourishes yin and clears heat. It is indicated for night fever with
morning coolness and absence of sweating due to yin damage in late stage
febrile disease. *Qing Gu San* primarily clears deficiency heat, as well
as nourishing yin. It is indicated for bone-steaming tidal fever, red
lips, and malar flush resulting from internal heat due to yin
deficiency. *Dang Gui Liu Huang Tang* nourishes yin, reduces fire,
secures the exterior and stops sweating. It is indicated for night
sweats, a red face, restlessness, a red tongue and a rapid pulse due to
deficiency fire.

**Review Questions**

| compatibility of *Zhi Mu* and *Shi Gao*.                              |

| them in clinical practice (based on indications, treatment strategy   |
| and ingredient compatibility)?                                        |
|                                                                       |

| Tang* can all nourish yin and clear heat. What are their similarities |
| and differences in indications and ingredients?                       |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 5 Formulas that Resolve Summer** **heat**

Since formulas that resolve summer heat clear summer heat and resolve
dampness, they are indicated for summer heat-related problems.

Summer heat is a yang pathogen and hot in nature. As a result, its
clinical manifestations include body fever, a red face, restlessness,
dark-yellow urine, a red tongue, and a rapid pulse. As people often turn
to cold drinks in hot summer, summer heat may also cause fever and
chills. In addition, summer heat is liable to associate itself with
dampness, causing a heavy sensation in the body, gastric stuffiness, a
poor appetite, and diarrhea. Summer heat tends to ascend and disperse
and results in sweating which may, in turn, consume qi and body fluids,
leading to fatigue, shallow breathing, and thirst with a desire to drink
water.

Common summerheat-clearing ingredients include *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos
Lonicerae Japonicae), *Hua Shi* (Talcum), *Xi Gua Pi* (Exocarpium
Citrulli) and *Zhu Ye* (Folium Phyllostachydis Henonis). Typical
formulas that resolve summer heat are *Qing Luo Yin* (Meridian-Clearing
Beverage), *Xiang Ru San* (Mosla Powder), *Liu Yi San* (Six-to-One
Powder), *Gui Ling Gan Lu Yin* (Cinnamon and Poria Sweet Dew Beverage)
and *Qing Shu Yi Qi Tang* (Summer heat-Clearing and Qi-Supplementing
Decoction).

Because summer heat is a seasonal pathogenic factor, formulas that
resolve summer heat are generally used in summer only. Considering its
hot nature, heat-clearing formulas are often used in combination. It's
also important to modify formulas according to its associated patterns,
severities and interactions between anti-pathogenic qi and pathogenic
factors.

***Qing Luo Yin***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (*Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarchs               金银花    *jīn yín   6g     Flos Lonicerae Japonicae
                                   huā*              
  ---------------------- --------- ---------- ------ -------------------------
                         西瓜皮    *xī guā    6g     Exocarpium Citrulli
                                   pí*               

  Ministers/assistants   扁豆花    *biăn dòu* 6g     Flos Lablab Album
                                   *huā*             

                         荷叶      *hé yè*    6g     Folium Nelumbinis

                         丝瓜皮    *sī guā    6g     Exocarpium Luffae Fructus
                                   pí*               

                         竹叶      *zhú yè*   6g     Folium Phyllostachydis
                                                     Henonis
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Jin Yin Hua* is cool, pungent and aromatic; it acts to clear
and resolve summer heat. *Xi Gua Pi* clears heat and generates fluids.

Ministers/assistants: *Bian Dou Hua* is aromatic and tends to disperse;
it acts to resolve summer dampness. *He Ye* is cool and tends to
disperse; it acts to clear summer heat. *Si Gua Pi* clears summer heat
in the lung meridian. *Zhu Ye* clears the heart, resolves water
retention and helps to clear summer heat and dampness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears summer heat and resolves dampness.

**\[Indications\]** Mild summer heat syndrome: fever, mild thirst, foggy
mind, dizziness, and a pale-red tongue with a thin white coating.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for summer
heat affecting the exterior (mild condition). Mild and superficial
summer heat causes fever and mild thirst. Ascending of summer heat
causes a foggy mind and dizziness. A pale-red tongue with a thin, white
coating indicates mild, superficial retention of pathogenic factors.
Because summer heat affects the exterior (*We*i-defense phase), it's
advisable to choose cool, pungent and aromatic herbs to clear heat and
resolve dampness.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, mild thirst, a foggy mind,
dizziness and a thin, white tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For unproductive cough with a high-pitched coughing sound due to
    summer heat affecting the lung, add *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae
    Amarum), *Jie Geng* (Radix Platycodonis), *Sang Bai Pi* (Cortex
    Mori) and *Sheng Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae) to
    disperse lung qi, harmonize the lung and stop cough.

-   For fever and severe thirst, add *Sheng Shi Ga*o (Gypsum Fibrosum)
    and *Lu Gen* (Rhizoma Phragmitis) to clear summer heat.

3\. Modern use: Sunstroke or pediatric summer fever that can be
diagnosed as summer heat affecting the qi phase (mild condition).
Furthermore, this decoction can be used as a tea-like beverage in summer
to prevent summer heat-related problems.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is indicated for mild
summer heat problems and not for severe summer heat or summer heat
affecting the middle and lower *jiao*.

***Xiang Ru San***

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (*Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch       香薷          *xiāng rú* 10g    Herba Moslae
  ------------- ------------- ---------- ------ -------------------------
  Minister      厚朴          *hòu pò*   5g     Cortex Magnoliae
                                                Officinalis

  Assistant     白扁豆        *bái biăn  5g     Semen Lablab Album
                              dòu*              
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Xiang Ru* is warm, pungent and aromatic; it acts to release
the exterior and dispel cold.

Minister: *Hou Pu* is bitter, warm, pungent and aromatic; it acts to
circulate qi, resolve dampness, harmonize the spleen and stomach, and
relieve stuffiness.

Assistant: *Bai Bian Dou* strengthens the spleen, harmonizes the spleen
and stomach, resolves dampness, and clears summer heat.

A small amount of liquor is added to decoct with other ingredients to
enhance the medicinal efficacy.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears summer heat, releases the exterior, resolves
dampness and harmonizes the spleen and stomach.

**\[Indications\]** Yin summer heat syndrome: chills, fever, a heavy
sensation in the head, general ache, absence of sweating, abdominal
pain, diarrhea, chest/gastric fullness/discomfort, a white, greasy
tongue coating, and a superficial pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for
contraction of external cold coupled with internal cold dampness in
summer. In hot summer, failing to stay warm during sleep may cause one
to contract external cold; over ingestion of cold food/drink may cause
internal cold dampness. This is known as yin summer heat syndrome. Cold
binding the *Wei*-defensive yang causes chills, fever, general aches,
absence of sweating, and a superficial pulse. Cold dampness affecting
the ascending of spleen qi and descending of stomach qi causes abdominal
pain, diarrhea, chest/gastric fullness/discomfort, and a white, greasy
tongue coating. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear
summer heat, release the exterior, resolve dampness and harmonize the
spleen and stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chills, fever, no sweating,
gastric stuffiness, a white, greasy tongue coating, and a superficial
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For restlessness due to internal heat, add *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos
    Lonicerae Japonicae) and *Lian Qiao* (Fructus Forsythiae) to clear
    heat.

-   For severe diarrhea due to internal dampness, add *Fu Ling* (Poria),
    *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis) and *Rou Dou Kou* (Semen
    Myristicae) to drain dampness and stop diarrhea.

-   For fever, headache, chills, no sweating, thirst, a red face, chest
    stuffiness/discomfort, a white and greasy tongue coating, and a
    superficial, rapid pulse due to summer warm dampness combined with
    external contraction of cold, change *Bai Bian Dou* to *Xian Bian
    Dou Hua* (Flos Lablab Album) and add *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae
    Japonicae) and *Lian Qiao* (Fructus Forsythiae) to enhance the
    summer heat-clearing effect. The modified formula is known as *Xin
    Jia Xiang Ru Yin* (Newly Supplemented Mosla Beverage, recorded in
    the *Wen Bing Tiao Bian*).

3\. Modern use: Common cold or acute gastroenteritis that can be
diagnosed as contraction of external cold coupled with internal cold
dampness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is warm and dry in
property and therefore not indicated for summer heat syndrome.

***Liu Yi San***

*Huang Di Su Wen Xuan Ming Lun Fang* (*Formulas from the Discussion
Illuminating the Yellow Emperor\'s Basic Questions*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch              滑石        *huá shí*  180g   Talcum
  -------------------- ----------- ---------- ------ -------------------------
  Minister/assistant   甘草        *gān căo*  30g    Radix et Rhizoma
                                                     Glycyrrhizae

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Hua Shi* is bland in taste and cold in property; it acts to
drain dampness and clear damp-heat out of the three *Jiaojiao* through
urination.

Minister/assistant: *Gan Cao* clears heat, drains fire, supplements qi
and harmonizes the spleen and stomach. It enhances the effect of *Hua
Shi* in clearing summer heat and, at the same time, protects the stomach
from being damaged by the cold property of *Hua Shi*.

The formula clears heat without damaging the stomach and promotes
urination without damaging yin.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct 9-18g of fine powder or mix with
warm water for each dose, 2-3 doses a day.

**\[Functions\]** Clears summer heat and drains dampness.

**\[Indications\]** Summer heat and dampness: Fever, thirst, and
dysuria. Alternatively, diarrhea may be present. It can also be used for
stranguria due to damp heat in the urinary bladder.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for summer
heat and dampness. Summer heat is hot in nature. When summer heat
affects the heart, fever and restlessness may occur. Summer heat
consuming fluids causes thirst. Summer heat combined with dampness and
downward flow of damp heat to the urinary bladder may cause dysuria.
Dampness affecting the intestines may cause diarrhea. Considering summer
heat and dampness affecting the qi transformation of the urinary
bladder, the treatment strategies are to clear summer heat and drain
dampness. As a result, it can also be used for stranguria due to damp
heat in the urinary bladder.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, thirst and dysuria.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe summer heat, add *Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) and *Jin Yin
    Hua* (Flos Lonicerae Japonicae) to clear heat.

-   For severe diarrhea, add *Bai Bian Dou* (Semen Lablab Album), *Cang
    Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis) and *Fu Ling* (Poria) to strengthen the
    spleen, drain dampness and stop diarrhea.

-   For heat or stones due to downward flow of damp heat, add *Che Qian
    Zi* (Semen Plantaginis), *Qu Mai* (Herba Dianthi) and *Zhi Zi*
    (Fructus Gardeniae) to promote urination and relieve stranguria.

3\. Modern use: Sunstroke, pediatric summer fever and acute enteritis
that can be diagnosed as summer heat and dampness. It can also be used
for urinary infection or stones due to damp heat in the urinary bladder.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is indicated for mild
summer heat and dampness.

***Gui Ling Gan Lu San***

*Huang Di Su Wen Xuan Ming Lun Fang* (*Formulas from the Discussion
Illuminating the Yellow Emperor\'s Basic Questions*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | 滑石   | *huá   | 120g | Talcum                             |
| narch |        | shí*   |      |                                    |
+=======+========+========+======+====================================+
| Mini  | 石膏   | *shí   | 60g  | Gypsum Fibrosum                    |
| sters |        | gāo*   |      |                                    |
+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+
|       | 寒水石 | *hán   | 60g  | Glauberitum                        |
|       |        | shuĭ   |      |                                    |
|       |        | shí*   |      |                                    |
+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 泽泻 | *zé    | 30g  | Rhizoma Alismatis                  |
| tants |        | xiè*   |      |                                    |
+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú    | 30g  | Poria                              |
|       |        | líng*  |      |                                    |
+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+

|       |        | líng*  |      |                                    |
+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+

|       |        | zhú*   |      |                                    |

|       |        | guì*   |      |                                    |

|       | 炙甘草 | gān    |      | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|       |        | căo*   |      |                                    |
+-------+--------+--------+------+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Hua Shi* is bland in taste and cold in property; it acts to
drain dampness and clear summer heat.

Ministers: *Shi Ga*o and *Han Shui Shi* are both very cold in property
and assist *Hua Shi* in clearing summer heat.

Assistants*: Ze Xie, Fu Lin*g and *Zhu Ling* promote urination and drain
dampness. *Bai Zhu* strengthens the spleen and resolves dampness. *Rou
Gui* unblocks yang, transforms qi and enhances the effect of promoting
urination.

Envoy: *Zhi Gao Cao* supplements qi, harmonizes the spleen and stomach,
and coordinates the other ingredients.

The formula consists of *Liu Yi San* (Six-to-One Powder), *Wu Ling San*
(Five Ingredients Powder with Poria) and addition of *Shi Gao* (Gypsum
Fibrosum) and *Han Shui Shi* (Glauberitum). It clears summer heat,
promotes urination and drains dampness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears summer heat, transforms qi and drains dampness.

**\[Indications\]** Contraction of external summer heat coupled with
internal retention of water dampness: Fever, headache, restlessness and
thirst with a desire to drink water, as well as dysuria. It can also be
used for diarrhea and vomiting in cholera.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for summer
heat coupled with internal retention of water dampness. External
contraction of summer heat causes fever, headache, restlessness and
thirst with a desire to drink water. Internal water dampness affecting
the qi transformation of the urinary bladder causes dysuria. Water
dampness affecting the transportation and transformation of the spleen
and stomach causes diarrhea and vomiting in cholera. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to clear summer heat, transform qi, promote
urination and drain dampness.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, headache, restlessness,
thirst with a desire to drink water, and dysuria.

2\. Modifications

-   For mild summer heat, decrease the dose of *Shi Gao* (Gypsum
    Fibrosum) and *Han Shui Shi* (Glauberitum) or replace the two with
    *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae Japonicae) and *Xi Gua Pi* (Exocarpium
    Citrulli).

-   For severe summer heat, remove *Rou Gui* and *Bai Zhu* and replace
    *Zhi Gan Cao* with *Sheng Gan Cao*.

-   For water dampness affecting the spleen and stomach, add *Huo Xiang*
    (Herba Agastachis) and *Pei Lan* (Herba Eupatorii) to resolve
    dampness and harmonize the middle *jiao*.

3\. Modern use: Sunstroke, pediatric summer fever, acute gastroenteritis
and acute urinary infection that can be diagnosed as summer heat and
dampness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula mainly acts to
clear summer heat, it's not indicated for summer (heat) dampness (more
dampness than heat). For more dampness than summer heat, it's advisable
to use dampness-resolving formulas such as *San Ren Tang* (Three Kernels
Decoction).

***Qing Shu Yi Qi Tang***

*Wen Re Jing Wei* (*Warp and Woof of Warm-Heat Diseases*)

| archs |         | guā    |       |                                  |

|       |         | căo*   |       |                                  |
