|       | >      | *jīng   | 1.5g | Spica Schizonepetae              |
|       | 黑芥穗 | jiè     |      |                                  |
|       |        | suì*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Envoy | > 甘草 | *gān    | 3g   | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
|       |        | căo*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Bai Zhu* and *Shan Yao* tonify spleen qi, resolve dampness and
stop vaginal discharges.

Ministers: *Ren Shen* tonifies qi and fortifies the spleen. *Cang Zhu*
dries dampness and benefits the spleen.

Assistants: *Chai Hu* soothes the liver and regulates qi. *Bai Shao*
nourishes blood and softens the liver. *Chen Qian Zi* clears heat and
resolves dampness through urination. *Chen Pi* moves qi, dries dampness
and prevents stagnation. *Hei Jie Sui* removes wind and resolves
dampness.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients.

This formula primarily tonifies qi and also lifts yang. It is a basic
formula for spleen qi sinking and deficiency heat.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Tonifies the spleen, soothes the liver, resolves
dampness and stops vaginal discharges.

**\[Indications\]** Vaginal discharges due to liver qi stagnation
coupled with dampness (resulting from spleen deficiency): White, thin,
clear and odorless vaginal discharges, lassitude, loose stools, a pale
tongue with a white coating and a delayed or soft, weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for vaginal
discharge due to liver qi stagnation coupled with dampness (resulting
from spleen deficiency). Spleen deficiency and liver qi stagnation may
cause internal dampness, which in turn flow downward and result in
vaginal discharges. Spleen qi deficiency may cause lassitude and loose
stools. As a result, the treatment strategies are to tonify spleen qi,
resolve dampness, stop vaginal discharge, soothe the liver and relieve
qi stagnation.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: White, thin, clear and odorless
vaginal discharge, lassitude, a pale tongue with a white coating and a
soft, delayed pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For yellow vaginal discharge, add *Huang Bai* (Cortex Phellodendri
    Chinensis), *Tu Fu Ling* (Rhizoma Smilacis Glabrae) and *Yi Yi Ren*
    (Semen Coicis) to clear heat and resolve dampness.

-   For severe pain in the chest and hypochondriac region, add *Xiang
    Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi), *Yu Jin* (Radix Curcumae) and *Qing Pi*
    (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae Viride) to soothe the liver and
    alleviate pain.

-   For persistent vaginal discharges, add *Duan Long Gu* (Os Draconis
    Praeparatum), *Duan Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae Praeparatum) and *Qian
    Shi* (Semen Euryales) to stop astringe and stop leucorrhea.

### 3. Modern use: Leucorrhea due to female reproductive inflammation such as adnexitis, cervicitis and vaginitis that can be diagnosed as downward flow of dampness resulting from liver qi stagnation with spleen deficiency.  {#modern-use-leucorrhea-due-to-female-reproductive-inflammation-such-as-adnexitis-cervicitis-and-vaginitis-that-can-be-diagnosed-as-downward-flow-of-dampness-resulting-from-liver-qi-stagnation-with-spleen-deficiency. .unnumbered}

**7.2 Formulas that tonify blood**

Formulas that tonify blood are indicated for blood deficiency. Clinical
manifestations may include dizziness, blurred vision, a sallow
complexion, pale lips and finger nails, palpitations, insomnia, a pale
tongue and a thready pulse. Blood-nourishing ingredients like *Dang Gui*
(Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata), *Bai
Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba), *E* *Jiao* (Colla Corii Asini), and *Long
Yan Rou* (Arillus Longan) are often used in combination with
qi-tonifying and blood-circulating ingredients. Typical formulas are *Si
Wu Tang* (Four Ingredients Decoction), *Gui Pi Tang* (Spleen-Restoring
Decoction) and *Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang* (Chinese Angelica
Blood-Supplementing Decoction).

***Si Wu Tang***

*Xian Shou Li Shang Xu Duan Mi Fang* (Immortal Secret Formulas for
Traumatology)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *shú dì | 10g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata      |
| narch | 熟地黄 | huáng*  |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Min   | > 当归 | *dāng   | 10g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
| ister |        | guī*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 白芍 | *bái    | 10g  | Radix Paeoniae Alba              |
| tants |        | sháo*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 川芎 | *chuān  | 10g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong               |
|       |        | xiōng*  |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Shu Di* tonifies blood and nourishes yin.

Minister: *Dang Gui* nourishes liver blood, harmonizes blood and
regulates menstruation.

Assistants: *Bai Shao* enters the liver to nourish blood and astringe
yin. *Chuan Xiong* unblocks the liver meridian. It ascends to the head
and eyes and descends to the sea of blood.

Compatibility characteristics: 1) *Shu Di*, *Dang Gui* and *Bai Shao*
tonify blood and *Chuan Xiong* circulates blood, enabling this formula
to tonify and harmonize blood; 2) *Shu Di* and *Bai Shao* are cold in
property, whereas *Dang Gui* and *Chuan Xiong* are warm in property,
enabling this formula to be neutral (neither cold nor warm); 3) *Shu Di*
and *Bai Shao* are known as "blood-nourishing ingredients", whereas Dang
Gui and Chuan Xiong are known as "qi-moving and blood-regulating
ingredients", enabling this formula to tonify blood without causing
stagnation and circulate blood without causing bleeding.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 10-15ml oral liquid for each dose,
3 doses a day. Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Tonifies and harmonizes blood.

**\[Indications\]** Blood deficiency and stagnation: Palpitations,
insomnia, dizziness, blurred vision, a lusterless complexion, irregular
menstruation, scanty menstruation, amenorrhea, peri-umbilical pain, a
pale tongue and a thready, wiry or thready, hesitant pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for blood
deficiency and stagnation. The liver stores blood. Failure of blood to
nourish the head may cause dizziness and blurred vision. Liver blood
failing to nourish the heart may cause palpitations, insomnia and a
lusterless complexion. Deficiency and stagnation of blood may affect the
*Chong* and *Ren* meridians, causing irregular menstruation, scanty
menstruation, amenorrhea, or peri-umbilical pain. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to tonify and harmonize blood and regulate
*Chong* and *Ren*.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Palpitations, dizziness, a
lusterless complexion, a pale tongue and a wiry, thready pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For blood deficiency coupled with cold, add *Rou Gui* (Cortex
    Cinnamomi), *Pao Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Praeparatum) and *Ai Ye*
    (Folium Artemisiae Argyi) to warm and unblock blood vessels.

-   For blood deficiency coupled with heat, add *Huang Qin* (Radix
    Scutellariae), *Dan Pi* (Cortex Moutan) and *Shu Di* (Radix
    Rehmanniae Praeparata) to clear heat and cool blood.

3\. Modern use: Irregular menstruation, pregnancy/childbirth problems,
chronic skin conditions such as urticaria and traumatological conditions
that can be diagnosed as blood deficiency and stagnation.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with loose stools due to spleen deficiency and those with qi
collapse following blood loss.

**\[Annex formula\]**

+----------+-----------+------------------+----------------------------+
| **F      | **Ingr    | **Actions**      | **Indications**            |
| ormula** | edients** |                  |                            |
+----------+-----------+------------------+----------------------------+
| ###      | Si Wu     | Nourishes blood, | Deficiency of *Chong* and  |
| # *Jiao* | Tang + E  | stops bleeding,  | *Ren*: Uterine bleeding,   |
|  Ai Tang | *Jiao*,   | regulates        | heavy menstruation,        |

| ui Yao L | Gan Cao   | benefit the      | blood; persistent bleeding |

| lüè .unn |           |                  |                            |
| umbered} |           |                  |                            |

| eng Yu T | Tang +    | regulates        | blood within the vessels:  |
| ang (*Ma | Ren Shen, | menstruation,    | early periods, heavy       |
| i Yin Zh | Huang Qi  | supplements qi   | menstruation with a pale   |
| eng Zhi* |           | and controls     | color, limb weakness and   |
| ) {#shen |           | blood within     | general fatigue            |
| g-yu-tan |           | vessels          |                            |
| g-mai-yi |           |                  |                            |

| zhi .unn |           |                  |                            |

| ### Tao  | Tang +    | circulates blood | stagnation: early periods, |
| Hong Si  | Tao Ren,  |                  | heavy menstruation with    |
| Wu Tang  | Hong Hua  |                  | purple clots and abdominal |
| (*Yu Ji  |           |                  | pain/distension.           |
| Wei Yi*) |           |                  |                            |

The above three formulas are all modified from *Si Wu Tang*. *Jiao* Ai
Tang has a stronger efficacy in tonifying blood, calming the fetus and
stop bleeding. It is indicated for all kinds of bleeding due to
deficiency of the *Chong* and *Ren*. *Sheng Yu Tang* tonifies qi and
blood and is indicated for deficiency of both qi and blood. *Tao Hong Si
Wu Tang* has a stronger efficacy in circulating blood and resolving
stasis. It is indicated for blood deficiency coupled with stagnant
blood.

***Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang***

*Nei Wai Shang Bian Huo Lun* (Clarifying Doubts about Damage from
Internal and External Causes)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 黄芪 | *huáng  | 30g  | Radix Astragali                  |
| narch |        | qí*     |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Min   | > 当归 | *dāng   | 6g   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
| ister |        | guī*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Huang Qi* tonifies qi, secures the exterior, restores floating
yang qi and clears deficient heat. In addition, it greatly reinforces
the *Yuan*-primordial qi.

Minister: *Dang Gui* nourishes and harmonizes blood and reinforces
deficiency.

This formula is indicated for fever and headache due to blood deficiency
during period or after childbirth. It also reinforces anti-pathogenic
qi, removes toxins, and thus helps to heal ulcers or wounds.

Compatibility characteristics: The dose ratio between *Huang Qi* and
*Dang Gui* is 5: 1. This formula reinforces qi and nourishes blood to
secure the exterior and astringe yang.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Tonifies qi and generates blood.

**\[Indications\]** Fever due to blood deficiency: Hot sensation in the
skin and muscles, a red face, restlessness, thirst with a desire to
drink water, and a surging, large and deficient pulse (weak upon heavy
pressure). It can also be used to treat fever and headache due to blood
deficiency during periods or after childbirth and unhealed wounds or
ulcerations.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for fever due to
blood deficiency. The blood is the mother of qi. Yin blood deficiency
may cause floating of yang and subsequently, hot sensation in the skin
and muscle, a red face, restlessness, thirst with a desire to drink
water, fever, headache, and a surging, large and deficient pulse. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to reinforce intangible qi to
regenerate tangible blood, and clear deficiency heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Hot sensation in the skin and
muscles, a red face, thirst with a desire to drink water, a large,
deficient and weak pulse upon heavy pressure.

2\. Modifications

-   For persistent bleeding due to qi/blood deficiency, add *Duan Long
    Gu* (Os Draconis Praeparatum), *Duan Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae
    Praeparatum) and *Shan Yu Rou* (Fructus Corni) to astringe blood and
    stop bleeding.

-   For unhealed ulcers due to toxic heat, add *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos
    Lonicerae Japonicae) and *Sheng Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma
    Glycyrrhizae) to clear heat and remove toxins.

3\. Modern use: Anemia and allergic purpura that can be diagnosed as
deficiency of qi and blood.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with fever due to yin deficiency and heat in the qi phase.

***Gui Pi Tang***

*Zheng Ti Lei Yao* ()

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 人参 | *rén    | **1  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng         |
| narch |        | shēn*   | 0g** |                                  |
| **s** |        |         |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
|       | >      | *lóng   | 10g  | Arillus Longan                   |
|       | 龙眼肉 | yăn     |      |                                  |
|       |        | ròu*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Min   | > 黄芪 | *huáng  | 10g  | Radix Astragali                  |
| ister |        | qí*     |      |                                  |
| **s** |        |         |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 白术 | *bái    | 6g   | Rhizoma Atractylodis             |
|       |        | zhú*    |      | Macrocephalae                    |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng   | 9g   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
|       |        | guī*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| **As  | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 6g   | Poria                            |
| sista |        | líng*   |      |                                  |
| nts** |        |         |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 远志 | *yuăn   | 6g   | Radix Polygalae                  |
|       |        | zhì*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *suān   | 6g   | Semen Ziziphi Spinosae           |
|       | 酸枣仁 | zăo     |      |                                  |
|       |        | rén*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 木香 | *mù     | 3g   | Radix Aucklandiae                |
|       |        | xiāng*  |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| **En  | >      | *zhì    | 5g   | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
| voy** | 炙甘草 | gān     |      | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|       |        | căo*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ren Shen* supplements qi, regenerates blood and tonifies the
heart and spleen. *Long Yan Rou* nourishes blood, calms the mind and
tonifies the heart.

Minister: *Huang Qi* and *Bai Zhu* assist *Ren Shen* in supplementing
qi. *Dang Gui* assists *Long Yan Rou* in tonifying blood.

Assistants: *Fu Ling*, *Yuan Zhi* and *Suan Zao Ren* calm the heart
mind. *Mu Xiang* regulates qi and harmonizes the spleen and stomach. *Da
Zao* and *Sheng Jiang* are combined to fortify the spleen and benefit
the stomach.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* supplements qi and coordinates the other
ingredients.

This formula tonifies spleen qi and heart blood, and at the same time,
regulates qi and prevents stagnation, enabling the blood to be returned
to and controlled by the spleen. That's why it's called *Gui Pi Tang*
(Spleen-Restoring Decoction).

**\[Method of administration\]** Add fresh ginger and Chinese dates and
decoct with water. Alternatively, take 9g honeyed pills for each dose, 3
doses a day.

**\[Functions\]** Supplements qi, tonifies blood, fortifies the spleen
and nourishes the heart.

**\[Indications\]**

-   Deficiency of heart blood and spleen qi: Mild or severe
    palpitations, poor memory, insomnia, night sweats, fatigue, a poor
    appetite, a sallow complexion, a pale tongue with a white coating
    and a thready, weak pulse.

-   Spleen failing to control blood within the vessels: Bloody stools,
    subcutaneous purpura, metrorrhagia and metrostaxis, early period,
    heavy menstruation with a pale color, dribbling of blood, a pale
    tongue with a thin, white coating and a thready, weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for deficiency
of heart blood and spleen qi. Heart blood failing to nourish the heart
mind may cause mild or severe palpitations, poor memory, insomnia and a
sallow complexion. Spleen qi failing to transport and transform may
cause fatigue and a poor appetite. Spleen failing to control blood
within the vessels may cause bloody stools, subcutaneous purpura,
metrorrhagia and metrostaxis, early period, heavy menstruation with a
pale color, or dribbling of blood. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to supplement spleen qi to control blood within the vessels and
nourish heart blood to calm the mind.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Palpitations, insomnia, fatigue,
a poor appetite, bloody stools, heavy menstruation, a pale tongue and a
thready, weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For uterine bleeding due to cold, add *Ai Ye Tan* (Folium Artemisiae
    Argyi Carbonisatum) and *Pao Jiang Tan* (Rhizoma Zingiberis
    Carbonisatum) to warm meridians and stop bleeding.

-   For uterine bleeding due to heat, add *Sheng Di Tan* (Radix
    Rehmanniae Carbonisatum), *E* *Jiao Zhu* (Colla Corii Asini Pilula)
    and *Zong Lǘ Tan* (Petiolus Trachycarpi Carbonisatus) to clear heat
    and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Insomnia, gastric or duodenal ulcer, dysfunctional
uterine bleeding, aplastic anemia, and thrombocytopenic purpura that can
be diagnosed as deficiency of heart blood and spleen qi.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with bleeding due to deficiency heat. {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-bleeding-due-to-deficiency-heat. .unnumbered}

**7.3 Formulas that tonify qi and blood**

Formulas that tonify qi and blood are indicated for deficiency of qi and
blood. Clinical manifestations may include a lusterless complexion,
dizziness, blurred vision, palpitations, a poor appetite, shortness of
breath, reluctance to talk, a pale tongue and a deficient, weak pulse.
Qi-tonifying herbs like *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Huang
Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae)
are often used in combination with blood-nourishing ingredients like
*Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae
Praeparata) and *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba). A typical formula is
*Zhi Gan Cao Tang* (Honey-Fried Licorice Decoction).

***Zhi Gan Cao Tang* (*Fu Mai Tang*)**

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mon   | >      | *zhì    | 12g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
| archs | 炙甘草 | gān     |      | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|       |        | căo*    |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
|       | > 生地 | *shēng  | 50g  | Radix Rehmanniae                 |
|       |        | dì*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 人参 | *rén    | 6g   | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng         |
| sters |        | shēn*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 大枣 | *dà     | 10 P | Fructus Jujubae                  |
|       |        | zăo*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 阿胶 | *ē      | 6g   | Colla Corii Asini                |
|       |        | jiāo*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 10g  | Radix Ophiopogonis               |
|       |        | dōng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *má zĭ  | 10g  | Fructus Cannabis                 |
|       | 麻子仁 | rén*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 桂枝 | *guì    | 9g   | Ramulus Cinnamomi                |
| tants |        | zhī*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 生姜 | *shēng  | 9g   | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens        |
|       |        | jiāng*  |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Zhi Gan Cao* tonifies the spleen and stomach. *Sheng Di
Huang* nourishes yin and blood.

Ministers: *Ren Shen* and *Da Zao* supplement qi, fortify the spleen,
nourish blood and harmonize the Ying-nutrients. *E* *Jiao*, *Mai Dong*
and *Ma Zi Ren* nourish yin blood.

Assistants: *Gui Zhi* and *Sheng Jiang* warm heart yang, unblock blood
vessels, and enhance the medicinal potency. In addition, the combination
of *Gui Zhi* and *Gan Cao* activates heart yang.

This formula nourishes heart yin/blood, activates heart yang/qi, and
alleviates palpitations.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water, melt *E* *Jiao* and
mix with the decoction in 3 times.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin/blood, supplements qi, warms yang, and
alleviates palpitations.

**\[Indications\]**

-   Yin/blood insufficiency and yang qi weakness: Palpitations,
    weakness, a dry, small tongue with a scanty coating, and a knotted,
    intermittent pulse.

-   Lung atrophy: Cough with profuse salivation, weight loss, shortness
    of breath, restlessness, insomnia, spontaneous sweating, night
    sweats, a dry throat and tongue, dry stools, and a deficient, rapid
    pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for yin/blood
insufficiency and yang qi weakness. Yang qi failing to pump blood may
cause a knotted, intermittent pulse. Yin blood failing to nourish the
heart may cause palpitations. Yin blood failing to nourish the body may
cause weakness, shortness of breath, and a dry, small tongue with a
scanty coating. As a result, the treatment strategies are to nourish
yin/blood, supplement qi and warm yang.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: A knotted, intermittent pulse,
palpitations, weakness, shortness of breath, and a scanty coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For insomnia and dream-disturbed sleep, add *Suan Zao Ren* (Semen
    Ziziphi Spinosae) and *Bai Zi Ren* (Semen Platycladi) to nourish the
    heart and calm the mind.

-   For severe palpitations, add *Long Gu* (Os Draconis) and *Mu Li*
    (Concha Ostreae) to tranquilize the mind.

3\. Modern use: Viral myocarditis, cardiac arrhythmias, coronary artery
disease, rheumatic heart disease, hyperthyroidism and silicosis that can
be diagnosed as yin/blood insufficiency and yang qi weakness. .

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency and with dampness
affecting the flow of qi due to spleen deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Jia Jian Fu Mai Tang* (Pulse-Restoring Variant
Decoction) from the *Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of
Warm Diseases)

\[Ingredients\] *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae Praeparata
cum Melle), *Gan Di Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata), *Bai Shao*
(Radix Paeoniae Alba), *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis), *E* *Jiao*
(Colla Corii Asini) and *Ma Ren* (Fructus Cannabis)

\[Actions\] Nourishes yin/blood, regenerates fluids and moistens
dryness.

\[Indications\] Late stage febrile disease with retention of pathogenic
heat and yin fluid deficiency: Fever, a red face, a dry mouth and
tongue, a deficient, large pulse, and more feverish sensations in the
palms and soles than in the hands/feet.

**7.4 Formulas that tonify yin**

Formulas that tonify yin are indicated for kidney yin deficiency.
Clinical manifestations may include weight loss, dizziness, tinnitus,
hot flushes, rosy cheeks, feverish sensations in the palms, soles and
chest, night sweats, insomnia, low back soreness, nocturnal emissions, a
red tongue with a scanty coating and a thready, rapid pulse.
Yin-nourishing ingredients like *Di Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae), *Mai
Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis), *Gui Ban* (Plastrum Testudinis), *Bie*
*Jiao* (Carapax Trionycis) and *Bei Sha Shen* (Radix Glehniae) are often
used in combination with heat-clearing ingredients. Typical formulas are
*Liu Wei Di Huang Wan* (Six Ingredients Rehmannia Pill), *Zuo Gui Wan*
(Left-Restoring Pill), *Da Bu Yin Wan* (Major Yin-Supplementing Pill)
and *Yi Guan Jian* (Effective Integration Decoction).

***Liu Wei Di Huang Wan* (*Di Huang Wan*)**

*Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue* (Key to Diagnosis and Treatment of
Children's Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *shú dì | 24g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata      |
| narch | 熟地黄 | huáng*  |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Mini  | >      | *shān   | 12g  | Fructus Corni                    |
| sters | 山茱萸 | zhū yú* |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 山药 | *shān   | 12g  | Rhizoma Dioscoreae               |
|       |        | yào*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 泽泻 | *zé     | 9g   | Rhizoma Alismatis                |
| tants |        | xiè*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 丹皮 | *dān    | 9g   | Cortex Moutan                    |
|       |        | pí*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 9g   | Poria                            |
|       |        | líng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Shu Di Huang* nourishes kidney yin, supplements kidney
essence, and benefits the marrow.

Ministers: *Shan Zhu Yu* tonifies the liver and kidney and astringes
essence. *Shao Yao* fortifies the spleen, supplements qi and astringes
kidney essence.

The combination of *Shu Di Huang*, *Shan Zhu Yu* and *Shan Yao* tonifies
yin of the kidney, liver and spleen, especially the kidney.

Assistants: *Ze Xie* drains turbid dampness and moderates the greasiness
of *Shu Di Huang*. *Dan Pi* clears deficiency heat, reduces deficiency
fire and moderates the warm/astringe-property of *Shan Zhu Yu*. *Fu
Ling* resolves water dampness and assists *Shan Yao* in fortifying the
spleen and moving qi.

Compatibility characteristics: 1) This formula tonifies yin of the
kidney, liver and spleen, especially the kidney; 2) This formula
combines "three reinforcing ingredients" with "three reducing
ingredients", focusing on the "three reinforcing ones".

This is an essential formula for yin deficiency of the liver and kidney.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g honeyed pill for each dose, 2
doses a day with warm water. Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes the liver and kidney.

**\[Indications\]** Yin deficiency of the liver and kidney: Low
back/knee soreness, dizziness, blurred vision, tinnitus, deafness, night
sweats, nocturnal emissions, wasting & thirsting disorder, bone-steaming
tidal fever, feverish sensations in the palms and soles, a dry mouth,
sore throat, loose teeth, heel pain, dribbling of urine, incomplete
fontanel closure, a red tongue with a scanty coating and a deep, thready
and rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for yin
deficiency of the liver and kidney. The kidney dominates bones and
generates marrow. Kidney yin deficiency may cause malnourishment of
bones and brain marrows. Liver yin/blood deficiency may cause
malnourishment of the head and eyes. Yin deficiency of the liver and
kidney may cause heel pain, low back/knee soreness, loose teeth,
dizziness and blurred vision. Kidney essence deficiency may cause
tinnitus or deafness. Deficiency fire may cause bone-steaming tidal
fever, feverish sensations in the palms and soles, a dry mouth and sore
throat. Deficiency heat expelling fluids may cause night sweats.
Deficiency fire disturbing the essence chamber may cause nocturnal
emissions. Congenital kidney deficiency may cause incomplete fontanel
closure. As a result, the treatment strategies are to tonify the liver
and kidney.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Low back/knee soreness,
dizziness, blurred vision, a dry mouth and throat, a red tongue with a
scanty coating and a deep, thready and rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency, add *Zhi Mu* (Rhizoma
    Anemarrhenae) and *Huang Bai* (Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis) to
    enhance the effect of clearing heat and reducing fire.

-   For spleen deficiency and qi stagnation, add *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma
    Atractylodis Macrocephalae), *Sha Ren* (Fructus Amomi) and *Chen Pi*
    (Fructus Amomi) to prevent qi stagnation affecting the spleen.

3\. Modern use: Chronic nephritis, hypertension, diabetes,
hyperthyroidism, central retinitis, and menopausal syndrome that can be
diagnosed as kidney yin deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with loose stools due to spleen deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]**

+------------+------------+-------------+-----------------------------+
| *          | **Ing      | **Actions** | **Indications**             |
| *Formula** | redients** |             |                             |
+------------+------------+-------------+-----------------------------+

| Bai Di Hua | Huang      | yin and     | yin deficiency:             |

| nnumbered} |            |             |                             |
+------------+------------+-------------+-----------------------------+

| uang Wan ( | Wan + Gou  | the liver   | dry eyes or lacrimation     |

| uan-shu .u |            |             |                             |
| nnumbered} |            |             |                             |

| i Zhi*) {# | Wei Zi     | alleviates  | depth of breathing; hiccups |

| mai-zhi .u |            |             |                             |

| an (*Yi Bu | Wei Zi,    | and kidney  |                             |
|  Quan Lu*) | Mai Dong   |             |                             |

+------------+------------+-------------+-----------------------------+

*Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan* tonifies kidney yin and reduces deficiency fire.
It is indicated for fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency. *Qi Ju Di
Huang Wan* nourishes the liver and kidney and benefits the eyes. It is
indicated for dry eyes and blurred vision due to yin deficiency of the
liver and kidney. *Du Qi Wan* tonifies kidney yin and maintains the
depth of breathing. It is indicated for coughing, panting or hiccups due
to kidney yin deficiency and kidney failing to absorb qi. *Mai Wei Di
Huang Wan* tonifies eth lung and kidney, astringes the lung and stops
coughing. It is indicated for coughing or panting due to yin deficiency
of the lung and kidney.

***Zuo Gui Wan***

*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (The Complete Works of Zhang Jing-yue)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *shú dì | 24g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata      |
| narch | 熟地黄 | huáng*  |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Mini  | > 龟胶 | *guī    | 12g  | Colla Testudinis Plastri         |
| sters |        | jiāo*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 鹿胶 | *lù     | 12g  | Colla Cornus Cervi               |
|       |        | jiāo*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 枸杞 | *gŏu    | 12g  | Fructus Lycii                    |
|       |        | qĭ*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 山药 | *shān   | 12g  | Rhizoma Dioscoreae               |
|       |        | yào*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shān   | 12g  | Fructus Corni                    |
|       | 山茱萸 | zhū yú* |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | >      | *tù sī  | 12g  | Semen Cuscutae                   |
| tants | 菟丝子 | zĭ*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 牛膝 | *niú    | 9g   | Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae     |
|       |        | xī*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Shu Di Huang* tonifies kidney essence, supplements blood and
nourishes yin.

Ministers: *Gui* *Jiao* and *Lu* *Jiao* supplement essence/marrow. *Gui*
*Jiao* tonifies yin, whereas *Lu* *Jiao* tonifies yang (pursuing yin
from yang). *Gou Qi Zi* nourishes the liver and kidney. *Shan Yao*
fortifies the spleen and tonifies kidney essence. *Shan Zhu Yu*
nourishes the liver and astringes essence.

Assistant: *Tu Si Zi* and *Niu Xi* tonify the liver and kidney,
strengthen the bones/sinews and benefit the low back/knees.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g honeyed pill for each dose, 2
doses a day. Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes kidney yin, supplements kidney essence and
benefits the marrow.

**\[Indications\]** Kidney yin deficiency: Dizziness, blurred vision,
low back and leg soreness, nocturnal emissions, spontaneous sweating,
night sweats, a dry mouth and throat, a red tongue with a scanty coating
and a thready pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for kidney yin
deficiency. Kidney failing to nourish the lumbus, bones/sinews and brain
may cause dizziness, blurred vision and low back/leg soreness. Yin
fluids failing to moisten and nourish the throat may cause a dry, mouth
and throat and thirst with a desire to drink water. Deficiency heat
disturbing the essence chamber may cause nocturnal emissions. Deficiency
fire expelling the fluids may cause night sweats. Yin deficiency
affecting the function of yang in astringing the fluids may cause
spontaneous sweating. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
nourish kidney yin, tonify the kidney and benefit the marrow.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Dizziness, blurred vision, low
back/leg soreness, spontaneous sweating, night sweats, a scanty tongue
coating and a thready pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For up-flaming of deficiency fire, remove *Gou Qi Zi* and *Lu*
    *Jiao* and add *Nǚ Zhen Zi* (Fructus Ligustri Lucidi) and *Mai Dong*
    (Radix Ophiopogonis) to nourish yin and clear heat.

-   For bone-steaming fever at night, add *Di Gu Pi* (Cortex Lycii) to
    clear deficiency heat.

3\. Modern use: Senile osteoporosis, dementia, menopausal syndrome,
chronic nephritis and infertility that can be diagnosed as kidney yin
deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with a poor appetite and loose stools due to spleen deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Zuo Gui Yin* (Left-Restoring Beverage) from the
*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (The Complete Works of Zhang Jing-yue)

\[Ingredients\] *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata), *Shan Yao*
(Rhizoma Dioscoreae), *Gou Qi* (Fructus Lycii), *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et
Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae Praeparata cum Melle), *Fu Ling* (Poria) and S*han
Zhu Yu* (Fructus Corni)

\[Actions\] Tonifies kidney yin

\[Indications\] Kidney yin deficiency: Low back soreness, nocturnal
emissions, night sweats, a dry mouth and throat, thirst with a desire to
drink water, redness in the tongue tip and a thready, rapid pulse.

Both *Zuo Gui Yin* and *Zuo Gui Wan* are indicated for kidney yin
deficiency. As the efficacy of *Zuo Gui Yi*n in nourishing kidney yin is
not as strong as *Zuo Gui Wan*, it is indicated for mild kidney yin
deficiency.

***Yi Guan Jian***

*Xu Ming Yi Lei An* (Supplement to 'Classified Case Records of Famous
Physicians')

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+----------+---------+------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *shēng   | 18-30g  | Radix Rehmanniae             |
| narch | 生地黄 | dì       |         |                              |
|       |        | huáng*   |         |                              |
+=======+========+==========+=========+==============================+
| Mini  | > 枸杞 | *gŏu qĭ* | 9-18g   | Fructus Lycii                |
| sters |        |          |         |                              |
+-------+--------+----------+---------+------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng    | 9g      | Radix Angelicae Sinensis     |
|       |        | guī*     |         |                              |
+-------+--------+----------+---------+------------------------------+
|       | > 麦冬 | *mài     | 9g      | Radix Ophiopogonis           |
|       |        | dōng*    |         |                              |
+-------+--------+----------+---------+------------------------------+
|       | >      | *bĕi shā | 9g      | Radix Glehniae               |
|       | 北沙参 | shēn*    |         |                              |
+-------+--------+----------+---------+------------------------------+
| Assi  | >      | *chuān   | 4.5g    | Fructus Toosendan            |
| stant | 川楝子 | liàn zĭ* |         |                              |
+-------+--------+----------+---------+------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Sheng Di Huang* tonifies kidney yin to nourish the liver
(nourishing water to support wood).

Ministers: *Gou Qi*, *Dang Gui*, *Mai Dong* and *Bei Sha Shen* nourish
yin/blood and soften the liver.

Assistant: *Chuan Lian Zi* soothes the liver, clear heat, move qi and
alleviate pain.

This formula mainly consists of yin-nourishing ingredients coupled with
a small amount of bitter, liver-soothing ingredients. As a result, it
nourishes yin without causing stagnation and soothes the liver without
damaging yin.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin and soothes the liver.

**\[Indications\]** Liver qi stagnation due to yin deficiency of the
liver and kidney: Pain in the chest and hypochondriac region, acid
reflux, a dry mouth and throat, a dry, red tongue and a thready, weak or
deficient, wiry pulse. It can also be used to treat hernia or abdominal
masses.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for liver qi
stagnation due to yin deficiency. The liver is a yin organ but its
functions are more yang. Liver yin deficiency may affect the free flow
of liver qi. Liver qi stagnation may, in turn, cause pain in the chest
and hypochondriac region. Yin failing to moisten the body may cause a
dry mouth and throat and a dry, red tongue. Liver qi affecting the
stomach may cause acid reflux. As a result, the treatment strategies are
to nourish yin and soothe the liver.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Pain in the chest and
hypochondriac region, acid reflux, a dry, red tongue and a deficient,
wiry pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For constipation, add *Jue Ming Zi* (Semen Cassiae) and *Huo Ma Ren*
    (Fructus Cannabis) to moisten the intestines and promote bowel
    movements.

-   For a dry mouth and throat, add *Chi Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Rubra)
    and *Tian Hua Fen* (Radix Trichosanthis) to clear heat and
    regenerate fluids.

3\. Modern use: Chronic hepatitis, chronic gastritis, gastric and
duodenal ulcers, intercostal neuralgia, and neurosis that can be
diagnosed as qi stagnation due to yin deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with phlegm dampness due to spleen deficiency.

**7.5 Formulas that tonify yang**

Formulas that tonify yang are indicated for kidney yang deficiency.
Clinical manifestations may include a bright pale complexion, cold
intolerance, cold limbs, low back/knee soreness, dysuria or frequent
urination, impotence, premature ejaculation, edema, a pale tongue with a
white coating and a deep, slow and weak pulse. Yang-warming ingredients
like *Lu* *Jiao* (Colla Cornus Cervi), *Rou Cong Rong* (Herba
Cistanches), *Tu Si Zi* (Semen Cuscutae) and *Ba Ji Tian* (Radix
Morindae Officinalis) are often used in combination with
interior-warming ingredients like *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis
Praeparata) and *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi) as well as yin-nourishing
and water retention-resolving ingredients. Typical formulas are *Shen Qi
Wan* (Kidney Qi Pill) and *You Gui Wan* (Right-Restoring Pill).

***Shen Qi Wan***

*Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *shú dì | 24g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata      |
| narch | 熟地黄 | huáng*  |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
| Mini  | >      | *shān   | 12g  | Fructus Corni                    |
| sters | 山茱萸 | zhū yú* |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 山药 | *shān   | 12g  | Rhizoma Dioscoreae               |
|       |        | yào*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 附子 | *fù zĭ* | 5g   | Radix Aconiti Lateralis          |
|       |        |         |      | Praeparata                       |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 桂枝 | *guì    | 5g   | Ramulus Cinnamomi                |
|       |        | zhī*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 9g   | Poria                            |
| tants |        | líng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 泽泻 | *zé     | 9g   | Rhizoma Alismatis                |
|       |        | xiè*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 丹皮 | *dān    | 9g   | Cortex Moutan                    |
|       |        | pí*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Shu Di Huang* nourishes kidney yin, supplements kidney
essence, and benefits the marrow.

Ministers: *Shan Zhu Yu* and *Shan Yao* tonify the liver and spleen and
astringe essential qi. Small doses of *Fu Zi* and *Gui Zhi* warm yang
and transform qi.

Assistants: *Fu Ling* and *Ze Xie* drain dampness. *Dan Pi* circulates
blood, resolves water retention and prevents retention of pathogenic
factors.

Compatibility characteristics: 1) This formula is composed of large
doses of yin-nourishing ingredients and a small amount of yang-warming
ingredients; 2) this formula combines reinforcing with reducing to
prevent stagnation; 3) this formula treats both the root cause (by
warming yang and transforming qi) and symptoms (by resolving water
dampness).

This formula is indicated for edema, phlegm fluid retention, wasting &
thirsting disorder, beriberi and pregnancy complicated with urinary
retention resulting from metabolic water disorder due to kidney yang
deficiency.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g honeyed pill for each dose, 2
doses a day with warm water. Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Tonifies kidney yang.

> **\[Indications\]** Kidney yang deficiency: Low back pain, leg
> weakness, a cold sensation below the low back, lower abdominal
> contracture, dysuria or profuse urine, especially at night, impotence,
> premature ejaculation, a pale, swollen tongue and a weak pulse (deep,
> thready in the *Chi* region). This formula can be used to treat phlegm
> fluid retention, edema, wasting & thirsting disorder, beriberi and
> pregnancy complicated with urinary retention.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for kidney yang
deficiency. The lumbus is the house of kidney. Kidney yang deficiency
may cause low back pain and a cold sensation below the low back.
Impaired qi transformation of the urinary bladder may cause dysuria,
edema or pregnancy complicated with urinary retention. Water retention
due to yang deficiency may cause phlegm fluid retention. Kidney yang
failing to control the urinary bladder may cause profuse urine,
especially at night. Yang deficiency may cause abnormal distribution of
body fluids, and subsequently, wasting & thirsting disorder. Phlegm
fluid retention, edema and beriberi all result from metabolic water
disorder. Kidney yang deficiency may cause impotence and premature
ejaculation. A pale, swollen tongue and a weak pulse (deep and thread in
the *Chi* region) are signs indicative of kidney yang deficiency. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to tonify kidney yang, transform qi
and resolve water retention.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Low back pain, leg weakness,
dysuria or profuse urine, a pale, swollen tongue and a weak pulse (deep
and thready in the *Chi* region).

2\. Modifications

-   For severe deficiency cold, replace *Gui Zhi* with *Rou Gui* (Cortex
    Cinnamomi) and increase the doses of *Fu Zi* and *Rou Gui* to warm
    the kidney and dispel cold.

-   For impotence, add *Yin Yang Huo* (Herba Epimedii) and *Ba Ji Tian*
    (Radix Morindae Officinalis) to warm yang.

-   For low back/knee soreness and weakness, add *Du Zhong* (Cortex
    Eucommiae), *Xu Duan* (Radix Dipsaci) and *Gou Ji* (Rhizoma Cibotii)
    to tonify the kidney and strengthen the bones/tendons.

3\. Modern use: Chronic nephritis, diabetes, hyperaldosteronism,
hypothyroidism, neurasthenia, chronic prostatitis and
hypoadrenocorticism that can be diagnosed as kidney yang deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with deficiency fire/heat (due to kidney deficiency).

**\[Annex formula\]**

+---------+---------------------+-------------+------------------------+

| hen Qi  | Shan Yao, Che Qian  | kidney yang | deficiency: edema, low |
| Wan (*J | Zi, Mou Dan Pi, Rou | and         | back heaviness,        |
| i Sheng | Gui, Chuan Niu Xi   | resolves    | swollen legs, and      |
|  Fang*) | and Shu Di Huang    | water       | dysuria                |

| *Ji She | Shu Di Huang, Rou   | and         | deficiency: A dark     |
| ng Fang | Gui, Ze Xie, Fu     | supplements | complexion, cold,      |
| *) {#sh | Ling and Lu Rong    | es          | swollen feet,          |
| i-bu-wa |                     | sence/blood | tinnitus, deafness,    |

| eng-fan |                     |             | soreness, dysuria and  |

*Jia Wei Shen Qi Wan* and *Shi Bu Wan* are both modified from *Shen Qi
Wan*. The former warms kidney, resolves water retention, and eliminates
edema. It is indicated for edema and dysuria due to kidney yang
deficiency. The latter warms kidney yang and supplements essence/blood.
It is indicated for kidney yang deficiency and insufficient
essence/blood.

***You Gui Wan***

*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (The Complete Works of Zhang Jing-yue)

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shān   | 9g   | Fructus Corni                    |
|       | 山茱萸 | zhū yú* |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *gŏu qĭ | 9g   | Fructus Lycii                    |
|       | 枸杞子 | zĭ*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 山药 | *shān   | 12g  | Rhizoma Dioscoreae               |
|       |        | yào*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 杜仲 | *dù     | 12g  | Cortex Eucommiae                 |
| tants |        | zhòng*  |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *tù sī  | 12g  | Semen Cuscutae                   |
|       | 菟丝子 | zĭ*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng   | 9g   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
|       |        | guī*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Lu* *Jiao* tonifies kidney yang and supplements essence/blood.
*Fu Zi* and *Rou Gui* warm kidney yang.

Ministers: *Shu Di*, *Shan Zhu Yu, Gou Qi* and *Shan Yao* tonify kidney
yin, supplement essence, and benefit the marrow. They are combined to
"pursue yang from yin".

Assistants: *Du Zhong* and *Tu Si Zi* tonify the liver and kidney and
strengthen the low back/knees. *Dang Gui* nourishes and harmonizes blood
to tonify the kidney (essence) and liver (blood).

This formula aims to reinforce the Yuan-primordial yang of the
right-sided (*You* in Chinese Pinyin) kidney. It is therefore called
*You Gui Wan*.

Compatibility characteristics: This formula contains yin-nourishing
ingredients to "pursue yang from yin". In addition, it purely reinforces
kidney yang and essence.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g honeyed pill for each dose, 2
doses a day with warm water. Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms kidney yang and supplements essence and benefits
the marrow

**\[Indications\]** Fire debilitation of the life gate due to kidney
yang deficiency: Fatigue due to aging or qi deficiency, cold
intolerance, cold limbs, low back/knee soreness, impotence, nocturnal
emissions, infertility/sterility, a poor appetite, loose stools or
urinary incontinence, a pale tongue with a white coating and a deep,
slow pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for fire
debilitation of the life gate due to kidney yang deficiency. Yang qi
failing to warm the body may cause fatigue, and low back/knee soreness.
Yang deficiency may cause internal cold and subsequently, cold
intolerance and cold limbs. Kidney failing to secure/store the essence
may cause impotence and nocturnal emissions. Cold retention in the
essence chamber or uterus may cause sterility/infertility. Fire (kidney
yang) failing to regenerate earth (the spleen/stomach) may cause a poor
appetite and loose stools. Kidney yang failing to control the urinary
bladder may cause urinary incontinence. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to warm kidney yang and supplement essence and benefit
the marrow.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fatigue, cold intolerance, cold
limbs, low back/knee soreness and a deep, slow pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe yang qi deficiency, add *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma
    Ginseng) to tonify the *Yuan*-primordial qi.

-   For seminal emissions, turbid vaginal discharge or loose stools due
    to yang deficiency, add *Bu Gu Zhi* (Fructus Psoraleae) to tonify
    the kidney.

-   For diarrhea due to kidney yang deficiency, add *Wu Wei Zi* (Fructus
    Schisandrae Chinensis) and *Rou Dou Kou* (Semen Myristicae) to
    astringe the intestine and stop diarrhea.

3\. Modern use: Nephrotic syndrome, senile osteoporosis and sterility
due to oligospermia that can be diagnosed as kidney yang deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with phlegm dampness and fire hyperactivity.

**\[Annex formula\]** *You Gui Yin* (Right-Restoring Drink) from the
*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (The Complete Works of Zhang Jing-yue)

\[Ingredients\] *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata), *Shan Yao*
(Rhizoma Dioscoreae), *Gou Qi Zi* (Fructus Lycii), *Shan Zhu Yu*
(Fructus Corni), *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), *Rou Gui*
(Cortex Cinnamomi), *Du Zhong* (Cortex Eucommiae) and *Zhi Fu Zi* (Radix
Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata).

\[Actions\] Warms kidney yang and supplements essence/blood

\[Indications\] Kidney yang insufficiency: Fatigue, abdominal/lumbar
pain, cold limbs, a thready pulse and a pale tongue with a white
coating. Alternatively, it can also be used to treat true cold and false
heat due to yin rejecting yang.

Both *You Gui Yin* and *You Gui Wan* can warm kidney essence and are
indicated for kidney yang deficiency. *You Gui Wan* has a stronger
efficacy in warming kidney yang and supplementing essence/blood. *You
Gui Yin* is indicated for mild kidney yang deficiency, whereas *You Gui
Wan* is indicated for severe kidney yang deficiency.

**7.6 Formulas that tonify yin and yang**

Formulas that tonify yin and yang are indicated for deficiency of kidney
yin and yang. Clinical manifestations may include dizziness, blurred
vision, low back/knee soreness, impotence, nocturnal emissions, cold
intolerance, cold limbs, spontaneous sweating, night sweats, and
afternoon fever. Common yang-tonifying ingredients like *Fu Zi* (Radix
Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata), *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi), *Rou Cong
Ron*g (Herba Cistanches) and *Lu* *Jiao* (Colla Cornus Cervi) are often
used in combination with yin-nourishing ingredients like *Shu Di* (Radix
Rehmanniae Praeparata), *Shan Zhu Yu* (Fructus Corni) and *Gui* *Jiao*
(Colla Testudinis Plastri). It's necessary to increase the number or
doses of yin-nourishing or yang-tonifying ingredients according to the
severity of yin/yang deficiency. Typical formulas are *Di Huang Yin Zi*
(Rehmannia Drink), *Gui Lu Er Xian* *Jiao* (Tortoise Shell and Deer Horn
Two Immortals Glue) and *Qi Bao Mei Ran Dan* (Seven Treasures
Beard-Blackening Elixir).

***Di Huang Yin Zi***

*Sheng Ji Zong Lu* (Comprehensive Recording of Divine Assistance)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mon   | >      | *shú dì | 12g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata      |
| archs | 熟地黄 | huáng*  |      |                                  |
+=======+========+=========+======+==================================+
|       | >      | *shān   | 6g   | Fructus Corni                    |
|       | 山茱萸 | zhū yú* |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *ròu    | 6g   | Herba Cistanches                 |
|       | 肉苁蓉 | cōng    |      |                                  |
|       |        | róng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *bā jĭ  | 6g   | Radix Morindae Officinalis       |
|       | 巴戟天 | tiān*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 附子 | *fù zĭ* | 6g   | Radix Aconiti Lateralis          |
| sters |        |         |      | Praeparata                       |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 肉桂 | *ròu    | 6g   | Cortex Cinnamomi                 |
|       |        | guì*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 石斛 | *shí    | 6g   | Caulis Dendrobii                 |
|       |        | hú*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 6g   | Radix Ophiopogonis               |
|       |        | dōng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *wŭ wèi | 6g   | Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis    |
|       | 五味子 | zĭ*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | >      | *shí    | 6g   | Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii        |
| tants | 石菖蒲 | chāng   |      |                                  |
|       |        | pú*     |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 远志 | *yuăn   | 6g   | Radix Polygalae                  |
|       |        | zhì*    |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 6g   | Poria                            |
|       |        | líng*   |      |                                  |
+-------+--------+---------+------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Shu Di Huang* and *Shan Zhu Yu* nourish kidney yin. *Rou Cong
Rong* and *Ba Ji Tian* warm kidney yang.

Ministers: *Fu Zi* and *Rou Gui* warm yang, dispel cold, and assist *Rou
Cong Rong* and *Ba Ji Tian* in warming the kidney and astringing yang.
*Shi Hu*, *Mai Dong* and *Wu Wei Zi* astringe yin fluids.

Assistants: *Shi Chang Pu*, *Yuan Zhi* and *Fu Ling* resolve phlegm and
open the orifice. *Sheng Jiang* and *Da Zao* are combined to benefit the
stomach and harmonize the middle *jiao*.

The formula nourishes kidney yin, tonifies kidney yang, resolves phlegm
and opens orifices.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water plus fresh ginger and
Chinese dates.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes kidney yin, tonifies kidney yang, resolves
phlegm and opens orifices.

**\[Indications\]** Tongue stiffness and paralysis due to kidney
deficiency and ascending of phlegm-turbidity: Tongue stiffness with an
inability to speak, leg paralysis, a dry mouth with no desire to drink
water, cold feet, a red face and a deep, thready, and weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for tongue
stiffness and leg paralysis. Deficiency of yin and yang may cause
malnourishment of the bones and muscles, leading to leg paralysis with
an inability to walk. Kidney deficiency may cause phlegm, which in turn
ascends to affect the tongue, leading to tongue stiffness and an
inability to speak. Up-floating of deficient yang (due to kidney yang
deficiency) may cause a dry mouth and red face. Yang deficiency also
causes cold feet. As a result, the treatment strategies are to nourish
yin, tonify yang, resolve phlegm and open orifices.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Tongue stiffness with an
inability to speak, leg paralysis, cold feet, a red face and a deep,
thready and weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For leg weakness/paralysis due to kidney deficiency, remove *Shi
    Chang Pu* and *Yuan Zhi* that unblock and open the orifices.

-   For qi deficiency, add *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Ren Shen*
    (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) to supplement qi.

-   For blood deficiency, add *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis) and
    *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba) to tonify blood and harmonize the
    *Ying*-nutrients.

3\. Modern use: Late stage hypertension, cerebral arteriosclerosis,
sequela of apoplexy, myelitis, syringomyelia and Parkinson's disease
that can be diagnosed as deficiency of kidney yin and yang.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with hyperactivity of yang, blood heat and phlegm fire.

**Summary**

There are a total of 19 formulas and 19 annex formulas in this chapter.
These formulas are classified into six categories: formulas to tonify
qi, formulas to tonify blood, formulas that tonify qi and blood,
formulas that tonify yin, formulas that tonify yang and formulas that
tonify yin and yang.

**Formulas that tonify qi**

*Si Jun Zi Tang*, *Shen Ling Bai Zhu San*, *Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang*, *Sheng
Mai San*, *Wan Dai Tang* and *Yu Ping Feng San* can all tonify qi. *Si
Jun Zi Tang* is an essential formula to supplement qi and fortify the
spleen. It is indicated for mild qi deficiency of the spleen and
stomach. *Shen Ling Bai Zhu San* supplements qi, fortifies the spleen,
drains dampness and stops diarrhea. It is indicated for spleen/stomach
qi deficiency coupled with dampness, especially for diarrhea due to
spleen deficiency coupled with dampness. *Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang*
supplements qi and lifts yang. It is indicated for severe spleen qi
deficiency, dysfunctions of the spleen and stomach, fever due to qi
deficiency, and prolapsed rectum or uterus due to qi sinking. *Sheng Mai
San* tonifies qi, nourishes/astringes yin and stops sweating. It is
indicated for deficiency of qi and yin. *Wan Dai Tang* tonifies qi,
fortifies the spleen, resolves dampness, stops vaginal discharges, and
soothes the liver. It is indicated for white vaginal discharges due to
downward flow of dampness resulting from spleen deficiency coupled with
liver qi stagnation. *Yu Ping Feng San* supplements qi, secures the
exterior and stops sweating. It is indicated for spontaneous sweating
(due to qi deficiency) and frequent common colds.

**Formulas that tonify blood**

*Si Wu Tang*, *Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang* and *Gui Pi Tang* can all tonify
blood and are indicated for blood deficiency. *Si Wu Tang* is an
essential formula to regulate menstruation. It tonifies and harmonizes
blood and is indicated for deficiency and stagnation of *Ying*-nutrients
blood and irregular menstruation/dysmenorrhea due to deficiency of the
*Chong* and *Ren* meridians. *Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang* tonifies qi and
regenerates blood. It is indicated for overexertion or fever due to
blood deficiency. Gui Pi Tang supplements qi, tonifies blood, fortifies
the spleen and nourishes the heart. It is indicated for deficiency of
heart blood and spleen qi and spleen failing to control blood within the
vessels.

**Formulas that tonify qi and blood**

*Zhi Gan Cao Tang* nourishes yin and blood, supplements qi and warms
yang. It is indicated for knotted or and moves qi. It is indicated for
knotted, intermittent pulses and palpitations due to insufficiency of
yin blood and deficiency of yang qi.

**Formulas that tonify yin**

*Liu Wei Di Huang Wan*, *Zuo Gui Wan*, *Da Bu Yin Wan* and *Yi Guan
Jian* can all nourish yin and are indicated for yin deficiency. *Liu Wei
Di Huang Wan* tonifies the liver, spleen and kidney, especially the
kidney. It is a common formula for nourish kidney yin. *Zuo Gui Wan*
nourishes kidney yin, supplements essence and benefits the marrow. It is
indicated for insufficiency of kidney yin and essence, especially kidney
yin. *Ba Bu Yin Wan* nourishes yin and reduces fire. It is indicated for
hyperactivity of deficient fire due to yin deficiency of the liver and
kidney. *Yi Guan Jian* nourishes yin and soothes the liver. It is
indicated for liver qi stagnation and yin deficiency of the liver and
kidney.

**Formulas that tonify yang**

Shen Qi Wan and You Gui Wan can both warm and tonify kidney yang and are
indicated for kidney yang deficiency. *Shen Qi Wan* is indicated for all
kinds of kidney yang deficiency. *You Gui Wan* warms kidney yang and
supplements essence and blood. It is indicated for kidney yang
deficiency, fire debilitation of the life gate and fire failing to
generate earth. It has stronger efficacy in warming kidney yang than
*Shen Qi Wan*.

**Formulas that tonify yin and yang**

*Di Huang Yin Zi* tonifies yin and yang and is indicated for deficiency
of yin and yang. It nourishes yin, tonifies yang, opens orifices and
resolves phlegm. As a result, it is used to treat tongue stiffness and
paralysis.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. Describe the meaning and category of formulas that tonify or      |
| reinforce and list the representative formulas.                       |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. List the names, compositions, actions, indications and            |
| modifications of essential formulas that tonify qi and blood.         |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Both *Gui Zhi Tang* and *Yu Ping Feng San* are used to treat      |
| spontaneous sweating. How do you choose them in clinical practice?    |
|                                                                       |
| 4\. Explain (with an example) why qi-tonifying ingredients are        |
| combined in formulas that tonify blood.                               |
|                                                                       |
| 5\. *Liu Wei Di Huang Wan* is indicated for kidney yin deficiency.    |
| Explain why there are "three reinforcing ingredients" and "three      |
| reducing ingredients" in this formula.                                |
|                                                                       |
| 6\. Describe the similarities and differences between *Da Bu Yin Wan* |
| and *Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan* in actions and indications.                |
|                                                                       |
| 7\. Describe the similarities and differences between *Zuo Gui Wan*   |
| and *You Gui Wan* and between *Liu Wei Di Huang Wan* and *Shen Qi     |
| Wan* in actions and indications.                                      |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 8 Formulas that Secure and Astringe**

As the name suggests, formulas that secure and astringe are indicated
for loss of or abnormal leakage/discharge of qi, blood, essence and body
fluids.

Failure of qi to hold or control blood, essence and body fluids may
cause spontaneous sweating, night sweats, persistent cough, chronic
diarrhea/dysentery, nocturnal emissions/spermatorrhea, enuresis,
frequent urination, uterine bleeding or leucorrhea. In terms of loss or
leakage/discharge pathways of qi, blood, essence and body fluids,
formulas that secure and astringe are categorized into five
types---formulas to consolidate the exterior and stop sweating, formulas
to reinforce the lung and stop coughing, formulas to astringe the large
intestine and stop diarrhea, secure the essence gate and stop nocturnal
emissions/urinary emissions and secure *Chong* and *Ren* meridians and
stop uterine bleeding/leucorrhea.

It's essential to be aware of the symptoms (loss of or abnormal
leakage/discharge of qi, blood, essence and body fluids) and underlying
cause (deficiency of anti-pathogenic qi) prior to treatment.
Deficiency-reinforcing ingredients are needed to tonify qi, nourish yin
and blood, warm yang, refill essence and supplement body fluids. In case
of profuse sweating and bowel and bladder incontinence due to severe
damage to *yuan*-primordial qi and collapse of yin and yang, a large
dose of *Shen Fu Tan*g (Ginseng and Aconite Decoction) or *Sheng Mai
San* (Pulse-Engendering Powder) is used to resuscitate yang, supplement
qi, rescue yin and prevent collapse. Since formulas that secure and
astringe are indicated for anti-pathogenic qi deficiency alone without
pathogenic factors, they are contraindicated for sweating in febrile
diseases, coughing due to phlegm-fluid retention, dysentery due to damp
heat, diarrhea due to improper diet, nocturnal emissions due to fire
stirring and uterine bleeding due to excess heat. Otherwise, above
pathogenic factors may reside in the body.

## 8.1 Formulas that consolidate the exterior and stop sweating {#formulas-that-consolidate-the-exterior-and-stop-sweating .unnumbered}

Formulas that consolidate the exterior and stop sweating are indicated
for spontaneous sweating that aggravates upon physical exertion, night
sweats and general fatigue. Common ingredients include *Duan Mu Li*
(Concha Ostreae Praeparatum), *Fu Xiao Mai* (Fructus Tritici Levis) and
*Ma Huang Gen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ephedrae) to consolidate the exterior
and stop sweating, coupled with qi-supplement ingredients including
*Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis
Macrocephalae). A classic formula is *Mu Li San* (Oyster Shell Powder).

***Mu Li San***

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarch  | >       | *duàn mŭ    | 30g  | Concha Ostreae Praeparatum |
|          |  煅牡蛎 | lì*         |      |                            |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
| Minister | > 黄芪  | *huáng qí*  | 30g  | Radix Astragali            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | >       | *má huáng   | 30g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ephedrae  |
| sistants |  麻黄根 | gēn*        |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | >       | *fú xiăo    | 30g  | Fructus Tritici Levis      |
|          |  浮小麦 | mài*        |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Duan Mu Li* is weighty and slightly cold in property. It acts
to nourish yin and submerge yang. Calcined oyster shell can stop
sweating.

Minister: *Huang Qi* supplements qi and consolidates the exterior. When
used in combination, *Duan Mu Li* and *Huang Qi* work for both
spontaneous sweating and night sweats.

Assistants: *Ma Huang Gen* astringes spontaneous sweating; *Fu Xiao Mai*
nourishes heart yin and stops night sweats.

Both *Mu Li San* and *Yu Ping Feng San* (Jade Wind-Barrier Powder) can
be used for spontaneous sweating due to weakness of *wei-*defensive qi.
The former stops sweating and reinforces qi and is indicated for both
spontaneous sweating and night sweats. The latter supplements qi and
removes wind and is indicated for spontaneous sweating due to qi
deficiency or qi deficiency coupled with external contraction of wind.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water (decrease the dose
according to the original proportion)

**\[Functions\]** Supplements qi, consolidates the exterior, astringes
yin fluids and stops sweating.

**\[Indications\]** Persistent spontaneous sweating that aggravates upon
physical exertion, night sweats, palpitations, easily startled,
shortness of breath, restlessness, fatigue, a pale red tongue and a
thready weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for spontaneous
sweating and night sweats. *Wei*-defensive qi failing to hold and
control *ying*-nutrients and yin may cause spontaneous sweating. Profuse
sweating may damage yin. Yin deficiency may cause deficient fire to
disturb yin fluids, resulting in night sweats. As sweat is the liquid of
the heart, persistent sweating may cause deficiency of heart qi and yin,
leading to palpitations, easily startled, shortness of breath and
fatigue. As a result, the treatment strategies are to supplement qi,
consolidate the exterior, astringe yin fluids and stop sweating.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Spontaneous sweating, night
sweats, palpitations, shortness of breath and a thready weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe spontaneous sweating due to qi deficiency, increase the
    dose of *Huang Qi* and combine with *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma
    Ginseng) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae) to
    supplement qi and consolidate the exterior.

-   For severe night sweats due to yin deficiency, combine with *Bai
    Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba), *Shan Yu Rou* (Fructus Corni) and *Nuo
    Dao Gen* (Radix Oryzae Glutinosae) to nourish yin and stop night
    sweats.

-   For severe palpitations and easily startled, combine with *Suan Zao
    Ren* (Semen Ziziphi Spinosae), *Bai Zi Ren* (Semen Platycladi) and
    *Duan Long Gu* (Os Draconis Praeparatum) to nourish the heart and
    calm the mind.

3\. Modern use: Profuse sweating in children or adults after illnesses,
childbirth or surgery, autonomic nerve dysfunction and menopause that
can be diagnosed as *wei*-defensive qi deficiency and heart yang
hyperactivity.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for night sweats
from fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency.

## 8.2 Formulas that reinforce the lung and stop coughing {#formulas-that-reinforce-the-lung-and-stop-coughing .unnumbered}

Formulas that reinforce the lung and stop coughing are indicated for
chronic cough due to deficiency of qi and yin: Cough with scanty sticky
phlegm, panting, spontaneous sweating, and a deficient rapid pulse.
Common ingredients include *Yin Su Ke* (Pericarpium Papaveris), *Wu Wei
Zi* (Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis) and *Wu Mei* (Fructus Mume) to
astringe the lung and stop coughing, coupled with qi-supplementing and
yin-nourishing ingredients such as *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng)
and *E* *Jiao* (Colla Corii Asini) as well as phlegm-resolving and
qi-descending ingredients such as *Sang Bai Pi* (Cortex Mori), *Kuan
Dong Hua* (Flos Farfarae) and *Bei Mu* (Bulbus Fritillaria). A classic
formula is *Jiu Xian Sa*n (Nine Immortals Powder).

***Jiu Xian San***

*Wei Sheng Bao Jian* (Precious Mirror of Health)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Monarch | >      | *yīng sù  | 240g  | Pericarpium Papaveris       |
|         | 罂粟壳 | ké*       |       |                             |
+=========+========+===========+=======+=============================+
| Mi      | >      | *wŭ wèi   | 30g   | Fructus Schisandrae         |
| nisters | 五味子 | zĭ*       |       | Chinensis                   |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 乌梅 | *wū méi*  | 30g   | Fructus Mume                |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 人参 | *rén      | 30g   | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng    |
|         |        | shēn*     |       |                             |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 阿胶 | *ē jiāo*  | 30    | Colla Corii Asini           |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Ass     | >      | *kuăn     | 30g   | Flos Farfarae               |
| istants | 款冬花 | dōng huā* |       |                             |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | >      | *sāng bái | 30g   | Cortex Mori                 |
|         | 桑白皮 | pí*       |       |                             |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 贝母 | *bèi mŭ*  | 15g   | Bulbus Fritillaria          |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 桔梗 | *jié      | 30g   | Radix Platycodonis          |
|         |        | gĕng*     |       |                             |
+---------+--------+-----------+-------+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Ying Su Ke* is used to astringe the lung and
stop coughing.

Ministers: *Wu Wei Zi* and *Wu Mei* are sour in taste and act to
astringe lung qi and generate fluids to moisten the lung. *Wu Wei Zi*
also helps to stop sweating; *Ren Shen* supplements lung qi and
generates fluids; *E* *Jiao* nourishes yin blood and moistens the lung;

Assistants: *Kuan Dong Hua* resolves phlegm, stops coughing,
down-regulates lung qi and alleviates panting; *Sang Bai Pi* is sweet in
taste and cold in property and acts to down-regulate qi and alleviate
panting; Bei Mu moistens the lung, resolves phlegm and alleviates
coughing and panting; *Jie Geng* disperses lung qi (to counterbalance
the astringent actions of Monarch and Minister ingredients), resolves
phlegm and stops coughing.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water (decrease the dose
according to the original proportion)

**\[Functions\]** Astringes the lung, stops coughing, supplements qi and
nourishes yin

**\[Indications\]** Chronic coughing due to deficiency of qi and yin:
Cough with scanty sticky phlegm, panting, spontaneous sweating and a
deficient rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for chronic
cough due to deficiency of qi and yin. Chronic coughs may damage lung qi
and affect its descending and dispersing, resulting in panting. Lung qi
failing to consolidate the exterior may cause spontaneous sweating.
Chronic coughs may damage lung yin and cause internal deficiency heat.
The deficiency heat may scorch fluids into scanty sticky phlegm and
result in a deficiency rapid pulse. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to astringe and supplement lung qi, nourish yin, resolve
phlegm and stop coughing.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chronic coughing, panting,
spontaneous sweating and a deficient rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For spontaneous sweating and fatigue due to severe qi deficiency,
    combine with *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Bai Zhu* (Radix
    Astragali) to supplement qi and consolidate the exterior.

-   For a dry mouth/throat and a red tongue with a scanty coating due to
    severe lung yin deficiency, combine with *Mai Dong* (Radix
    Ophiopogonis) and *Bei Sha Shen* (Radix Glehniae) to nourish yin and
    moisten the lung.

-   For panting upon physical exertion and more exhalation than
