    inhalation due to kidney deficiency, combine with *Ge Jie* (Gecko)
    and *Hu Tao Rou* (Gecko) to tonify kidney qi.

3\. Modern use: Bronchitis or whooping cough that can be diagnosed as
chronic coughing due to deficiency of qi and yin in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula contains *Ying Su
Ke*, it should not be taken for a long period of time.

## 8.3 Formulas that astringes the large intestine and stop diarrhea {#formulas-that-astringes-the-large-intestine-and-stop-diarrhea .unnumbered}

Formulas that astringe the large intestine and stop diarrhea are
indicated for chronic diarrhea or dysentery, bowel incontinence,
abdominal pain that alleviates upon warmth and pressure, general
fatigue, a poor appetite and a deficient pulse due to deficiency cold of
the spleen and kidney. Common ingredients include *Ying Su Ke*
(Pericarpium Papaveris), *He Zi* (Fructus Chebulae) and *Wu Wei Zi*
(Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis) to astringe the large intestine and stop
diarrhea. Herbs that warm the spleen and stomach such as *Gan Jiang*
(Rhizoma Zingiberis), *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) and *Bai
Zhu* (Bulbus Fritillaria) as well as herbs that warm kidney yang such as
*Bu Gu Zhi* (Fructus Psoraleae) and *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi) are
often used in combination. In case of chronic diarrhea or dysentery
damaging yin blood, *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis) and *Bai Shao*
(Radix Paeoniae Alba) can be combined to nourish yin blood. Classic
formulas include *Zhen Ren Yang Zang Tang* (True Man's Decoction to
Nourish the Organs) and *Si Shen Wan* (Four Spirits Pill).

### ***Zhen Ren Yang Zang Tang*** {#zhen-ren-yang-zang-tang .unnumbered}

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *yīng  | 1   | Pericarpium Papaveris               |
| narch | 罂粟壳 | sù ké* | 08g |                                     |
+=======+========+========+=====+=====================================+
| Mini  | >      | *ròu   | 15g | Semen Myristicae                    |
| sters | 肉豆蔻 | dòu    |     |                                     |
|       |        | kòu*   |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|       | > 诃子 | *hē    | 36g | Fructus Chebulae                    |
|       |        | zĭ*    |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 人参 | *rén   | 18g | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng            |
| tants |        | shēn*  |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|       | > 白术 | *bái   | 18g | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae  |
|       |        | zhú*   |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|       | > 肉桂 | *ròu   | 24g | Cortex Cinnamomi                    |
|       |        | guì*   |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng  | 18g | Radix Angelicae Sinensis            |
|       |        | guī*   |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|       | > 白芍 | *bái   | 48g | Radix Paeoniae Alba                 |
|       |        | sháo*  |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|       | > 木香 | *mù    | 42g | Radix Aucklandiae                   |
|       |        | xiāng* |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+
| Envoy | >      | *zhì   | 60g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae       |
|       | 炙甘草 | gān    |     | Praeparata cum Melle                |
|       |        | căo*   |     |                                     |
+-------+--------+--------+-----+-------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Ying Su Ke* is used to astringe the large
intestine and stop diarrhea.

Ministers: *Rou Dou Kou* and *He Zi* warm the spleen and stomach,
dissipate cold, astringe the large intestine and stop diarrhea.

Assistants: *Ren Shen* strongly reinforces yuan-primordial qi,
strengthens the spleen and benefits the stomach; *Bai Zhu* supplements
qi, strengthens the spleen and resolves dampness; *Rou Gui* warms the
kidney and spleen, dissipates cold and alleviates pain; *Dang Gui* and
*Bai Shao* nourish blood and astringe yin; and *Mu Xiang* regulates qi
and awakens the spleen.

Envoy: When used in combination with *Ren Shen* and *Bai Zhu*, *Zhi Gan
Cao* helps to supplement qi and strengthens the spleen; when used in
combination with *Bai Shao*, *Zhi Gan Cao* helps to alleviate pain. It
also harmonizes the actions of other ingredients.

This formula warms the spleen and stomach and reinforces the spleen and
kidney. It is therefore known as a decoction to nourish the organs. It's
characterized by 1) astringing and reinforcement, especially astringe;
2) tonifying both the spleen and kidney, especially the spleen; and 3)
reinforcement, astringing and at the same time regulating qi.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water (decrease the dose
according to the original proportion)

**\[Functions\]** Astringes the large intestine, warms and reinforces
the spleen and kidney and stops diarrhea

**\[Indications\]** Chronic diarrhea or dysentery due to deficiency cold
of the spleen and kidney: persistent diarrhea or dysentery, bowel
incontinence or even rectal prolapse, peri-umbilical pain that
alleviates upon warmth and pressure, general fatigue, a poor appetite, a
pale tongue with a white coating and a deep thready pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for chronic
diarrhea or dysentery due to deficiency cold of the spleen and kidney.
Chronic diarrhea or dysentery may damage the spleen and kidney and cause
spleen qi to sink and result in kidney yang deficiency, thus leading to
severe diarrhea or dysentery, bowel incontinence and rectal prolapse.
Yang deficiency of the spleen and kidney may cause internal cold and
result in peri-umbilical pain that alleviates upon warmth and pressure.
As a result, the treatment strategies are to astringe the large
intestine, stop diarrhea, warm and reinforce the spleen and kidney, and
nourish yin/blood.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chronic diarrhea or dysentery,
bowel incontinence, abdominal pain that alleviates upon warmth and
pressure, general fatigue, a poor appetite, a pale tongue with a white
coating and a deep thready pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For diarrhea containing undigested food and cold limbs due to severe
    deficiency cold of the spleen and kidney, combine with *Fu Zi*
    (Radix Aconiti Lateralis), *Bu Gu Zhi* (Fructus Psoraleae) and *Gan
    Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) to warm the spleen and kidney.

-   For rectal prolapse, combine with *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and
    *Sheng Ma* (Rhizoma Cimicifugae) to lift yang qi.

-   For severe abdominal pain, increase the dose of *Bai Shao* and *Gan
    Cao* to alleviate pain and combine with *Yan Hu Suo* (Rhizoma
    Corydalis) to strengthen the pain-relief effect.

3\. Modern use: Chronic enteritis, chronic dysentery or intestinal
tuberculosis that can be diagnosed as failure to astringe the large
intestine due to deficiency cold of the spleen and kidney.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's advisable to ask the patient to
stay away from cold, greasy food during the treatment. Since this
formula contains *Ying Su Ke*, it should not be taken for a long period
of time.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Tao Hua Tang* (Peach Blossom Decoction) from
*Shan Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

\[Ingredients\] *Chi Shi Zhi* (Halloysitum Rubrum), *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma
Zingiberis) and *Jing Mi* (non-glutinous rice)

\[Functions\] Warms the spleen and stomach, astringes the large
intestine and stops dysentery

\[Indications\] Chronic dysentery due to deficiency cold, dysentery with
pus and dark blood in stools, abdominal pain that alleviates upon warmth
and pressure, a pale tongue with a white coating and a deep weak or
thready pulse.

### ***Si Shen Wan*** {#si-shen-wan .unnumbered}

*Nei Ke Zhai Yao (*Summary of Internal Medicine)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+---------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| M      | >       | *bŭ gŭ  | 1   | Fructus Psoraleae               |
| onarch |  补骨脂 | zhī*    | 20g |                                 |
+========+=========+=========+=====+=================================+
| Min    | >       | *ròu    | 6g  | Semen Myristicae                |
| isters |  肉豆蔻 | dòu     |     |                                 |
|        |         | kòu*    |     |                                 |
+--------+---------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Assi   | >       | *wŭ wèi | 60g | Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis   |
| stants |  五味子 | zĭ*     |     |                                 |
+--------+---------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | >       | *wú zhū | 30g | Fructus Evodiae                 |
|        |  吴茱萸 | yú*     |     |                                 |
+--------+---------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Bu Gu Zhi* is pungent and bitter in taste and warm in
property. A large dose is used to warm kidney yang and nourish spleen
earth.

Minister: *Rou Dou Kou* astringes the large intestine, assists *Bu Gu
Zhi* to warm the kidney and spleen, and stops diarrhea.

Assistants: *Wu Zhu Yu* is pungent and warm in property and acts to warm
the liver, spleen and kidney and disperse yin cold; *Wu Wei Zi* is sour
in taste and warm in property and acts to reinforce kidney qi, astringe
the large intestine and stop diarrhea.

Envoys: Ginger and Chinese dates are mixed in decoction as envoys. The
former warms the stomach and dissipates cold. The latter tonifies the
spleen and nourishes the stomach. The two also coordinate the actions of
other ingredients.

Both *Si Shen Wan* and *Zhen Ren Yang Zang Tang* are indicated for
chronic diarrhea. With a large dose of *Bu Gu Zhi* (supplemented by *Rou
Dou Kou*, *Wu Wei Zi* and *Wu Zhu Yu*), *Si Shen Wan* is better in
warming and reinforcing the spleen and kidney than astringing the large
intestine. It is mainly indicated for early morning diarrhea due to yang
deficiency of the spleen and kidney. With a large dose of *Ying Su Ke*
(supplemented by *He Zi*, *Rou Dou Kou*, *Ren Shen*, *Bai Zhu* and *Rou
Gui*, etc.), *Zhen Ren Yang Zang Tang* is better in astringing the large
intestine than warming and reinforcing the spleen and kidney (especially
the spleen). It is mainly indicated for chronic diarrhea or dysentery
and bowel incontinence due to deficiency cold of the spleen and stomach.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g pills for each dose, 2 doses a
day; or decoct with water (decrease the dose according to the original
proportion).

**\[Functions\]** Warms the kidney and spleen, astringes the large
intestine and stops diarrhea

**\[Indications\]** Early morning diarrhea: Abdominal pain and diarrhea
in early morning, the pain alleviates after bowel movements, a poor
appetite, undigested food in stools, a pale tongue with a thin white
coating and a deep slow and weak pulse; alternatively, chronic diarrhea
with abdominal pain that alleviates upon warmth, low back soreness, cold
limbs and general fatigue may be present.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for early
morning diarrhea due to yang deficiency of the spleen and kidney. Early
morning diarrhea is also known as kidney-related diarrhea. In early
morning, yin qi reaches its peak and yang qi starts to activate. Failure
of yang qi activation due to kidney yang deficiency (fire decline of the
vital gate) and descending of yin qi may cause sleep-interrupted
abdominal pain. Yang deficiency of the spleen and kidney (fire failing
to promote earth) may cause a poor appetite. Failure of yang to warm the
spleen and stomach may cause an abdominal pain that alleviates upon
warmth, low back soreness, cold limbs and general fatigue. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to warm the kidney and spleen, astringe the
large intestine and stop diarrhea.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Early morning diarrhea, a poor
appetite, general fatigue, cold limbs, a pale tongue with a white
coating and a deep slow and weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For rectal prolapse due to spleen qi sinking from chronic diarrhea,
    combine with *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali), *Sheng Ma* (Rhizoma
    Cimicifugae) and *Chai Hu* (Radix Bupleuri) to lift yang qi.

-   For severe low back soreness and cold limbs, combine with *Fu Zi*
    (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata) and *Rou Gui* (Cortex
    Cinnamomi) to warm kidney yang.

-   For diarrhea containing undigested food, combine with *Jiao Shan
    Zh*a (Fructus Crataegi Praeparata) and *Chao Ji Nei Jin* (fried
    Endothelium Corneum Gigeriae Galli) to strengthen the spleen and
    promote digestion.

3\. Modern use: Chronic enteritis, irritable bowel syndrome, allergic
colitis, diabetes-related refractory diarrhea and intestinal
tuberculosis that can be diagnosed as yang deficiency of the spleen and
kidney.

## 8.4 Formulas that secure the essence gate and stop nocturnal emissions/urinary incontinence {#formulas-that-secure-the-essence-gate-and-stop-nocturnal-emissionsurinary-incontinence .unnumbered}

Formulas that secure the essence gate and stop nocturnal emissions are
indicated for nocturnal emissions, spermatorrhea, urinary incontinence,
frequent urination, tinnitus and low back soreness due to kidney
deficiency and kidney failing to control urinary bladder. Common
kidney-tonifying ingredients include *Sha Yuan Ji Li* (Semen Astragali
Complanati), *Sang Piao Xiao* (Oötheca Mantidis) and *Qian Shi* (Semen
Euryales). Ingredients that astringe are often combined, such as *Duan
Long Gu* (Os Draconis Praeparata), *Duan Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae
Praeparata) and *Lian Xu* (Stamen Nelumbinis). Classic formulas include
*Jin Suo Gu Jing Wan* (Golden Lock Essence-Securing Pill) and *Sang Piao
Xiao San* (Mantis Egg Shell Powder)

### ***Jin Suo Gu Jing Wan*** {#jin-suo-gu-jing-wan .unnumbered}

*Yi Fang Ji Jie* (Collections and Explanations of Medical Formulas)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+----------+---------+------+--------------------------------+
| Mo    | >        | *shā    | 60g  | Semen Astragali Complanati     |
| narch | 沙苑蒺藜 | yuàn jí |      |                                |
|       |          | lí*     |      |                                |
+=======+==========+=========+======+================================+
| Mini  | > 芡实   | *qiàn   | 60g  | Semen Euryales                 |
| sters |          | shí*    |      |                                |
+-------+----------+---------+------+--------------------------------+
|       | > 莲须   | *lián   | 60g  | Stamen Nelumbinis              |
|       |          | xū*     |      |                                |
+-------+----------+---------+------+--------------------------------+
| Assis | > 煅龙骨 | *duàn   | 30g  | Os Draconis Praeparata         |
| tants |          | lóng    |      |                                |
|       |          | gŭ*     |      |                                |
+-------+----------+---------+------+--------------------------------+
|       | > 煅牡蛎 | *duàn   | 30g  | Concha Ostreae Praeparata      |
|       |          | mŭ lì*  |      |                                |
+-------+----------+---------+------+--------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Sha Yuan Ji Li* is sweet in taste and warm in property and
acts to tonify the kidney and secure the essence.

Ministers: *Qian Shi* and *Lian Xu* are sweet and astringent in taste
and neutral in property. They act to reinforce kidney essence and tonify
spleen qi; in addition, *Lian Xu* coordinates the heart and kidney.

Assistants: *Duan Long G*u is sweet and astringent in taste and neutral
in property and *Duan Mu Li* is salty in taste and neutral with slightly
cold in property. They act to stop nocturnal emissions, submerge yang,
and calm the mind.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g pills for each dose with light
salted soup, 2-3 doses a day; or decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Tonifies the kidney and secures the essence

**\[Indications\]** Nocturnal emissions due to kidney deficiency,
spermatorrhea, low back soreness, tinnitus, weakness of the limbs,
general fatigue, a pale tongue with a white coating and a thready weak
pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for nocturnal
emission due to kidney deficiency and insecurity of essence gate. Kidney
failing to control the essence gate may cause nocturnal emissions or
spermatorrhea. Kidney essence failing to nourish the spirit and
transform essence into qi may cause general fatigue. Kidney essence
deficiency may also cause low back soreness and tinnitus. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to tonify the kidney and secure the essence
gate.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Nocturnal emissions,
spermatorrhea, low back soreness, tinnitus, a pale tongue with a white
coating and a thready weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

For cold pain in the low back and knee joints and impotence due to
kidney yang deficiency, combine with *Yin Yang Huo* (Herba Epimedii),
*Suo Yang* (Herba Cynomorii) and *Ba Ji Tian* (Radix Morindae
Officinalis) to warm kidney yang.

For nocturnal emissions (wet dreams), feverish sensations in the palms
and soles and night sweats due to kidney yin deficiency, combine with
*Gui Jia* (Carapax et Plastrum Testudinis), *Sheng Di* (Radix
Rehmanniae) and *Shan Yu Rou* (Fructus Corni) to nourish kidney yin.

3\. Modern use: Neurasthenia, sterility, neurosis, chronic prostatitis,
and chylous urine that can be diagnosed as insecure essence gate due to
kidney deficiency in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for nocturnal
emissions due to downward flow of damp heat or reckless stirring of
ministerial fire due to yin deficiency of the liver and kidney.

### ***Sang Piao Xiao San*** {#sang-piao-xiao-san .unnumbered}

*Ben Cao Yan Yi* (Extension of the Materia Medica)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *sāng    | 30g | Oötheca Mantidis                  |
| narch | 桑螵蛸 | piāo     |     |                                   |
|       |        | xiāo*    |     |                                   |
+=======+========+==========+=====+===================================+
| Mini  | > 龙骨 | *lóng    | 30g | Os Draconis                       |
| sters |        | gŭ*      |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|       | > 龟甲 | *guī     | 30g | Carapax et Plastrum Testudinis    |
|       |        | jiă*     |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 人参 | *rén     | 30g | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng          |
| tants |        | shēn*    |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|       | > 茯神 | *fú      | 30g | Sclerotium Poriae Pararadicis     |
|       |        | shén*    |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shí     | 30g | Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii         |
|       | 石菖蒲 | chāng    |     |                                   |
|       |        | pú*      |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|       | > 远志 | *yuăn    | 30g | Radix Polygalae                   |
|       |        | zhì*     |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng    | 30g | Radix Angelicae Sinensis          |
|       |        | guī*     |     |                                   |
+-------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Sang Piao Xiao* is sweet and salty in taste and neutral in
property. It tonifies the kidney, astringes the essence and stops
nocturnal emissions or urinary incontinence.

Ministers: *Long Gu* astringes and secures the essence and calms the
mind; *Gui Jia* nourishes kidney yin. Combined *Sang Piao Xiao* and
*Long Gu* astringe the essence and stop nocturnal emissions or urinary
incontinence; combined *Sang Piao Xia*o and *Gui Jia* nourish yin,
submerge yang and calm the mind.

Assistants: *Ren Shen* tonifies yuan-primordial qi and calms the heart
mind; *Fu Shen* calms the mind; *Shi Chang Pu* opens the orifices and
calms the mind; *Yuan Zhi* calms the mind and harmonizes the heart and
kidney; and *Dang Gui* nourishes heart blood.

Both *Sang Piao Xiao San* and *Jin Suo Gu Jing Wan* can be used for
nocturnal emissions due to kidney deficiency. The former tonifies the
heart and kidney and is indicated for frequent urination, urinary
incontinence and nocturnal emissions due to deficiency of the heart and
kidney and incoordination between the kidney water and heart fire. The
latter tonifies kidney essence and is indicated for nocturnal emissions
or spermatorrhea due to insecurity of the essence gate.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water and take the
decoction before going to bed.

**\[Functions\]** Regulates and reinforces the heart and kidney,
astringe essence and stops nocturnal emissions and urinary incontinence.

**\[Indications\]** Deficiency of the heart and kidney: Frequent
urination, urinary incontinence, cloudy urine, nocturnal emissions, mind
wandering, forgetfulness, a pale tongue with a white coating and a
thready weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for deficiency of
the heart and kidney. Failure of the kidney to control the urinary
bladder may cause frequent urination and urinary incontinence. Failure
of the kidney to store essence may cause nocturnal emissions or cloudy
urine. Heart deficiency may cause mind wandering and forgetfulness. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to reinforce the heart and kidney
to secure essence and stop nocturnal emissions/urinary incontinence.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Frequent urination, urinary
incontinence, cloudy urine, forgetfulness and mind wandering.

2\. Modifications

For kidney yang deficiency, combine with *Suo Yang* (Herba Cynomorii)
and *Tu Si Zi* (Semen Cuscutae) to warm kidney yang.

For severe cloudy urine, combine with *Bi Xie* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae
Hypoglaucae) and *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae) to separate turbid from
clear.

3\. Modern use: Urinary incontinence, frequent urination, cloudy urine
and neurasthenia due to deficiency of the heart and kidney in Chinese
medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Psychological counselling should be
combined for children with enuresis.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Suo Quan Wan* (Stream-Reducing Pill) from the
*Wei Shi Jia Cang Fang* (Wei's Family Prescriptions)

\[Ingredients\] *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae), *Yi Zhi Ren* (Fructus
Alpiniae Oxyphyllae) and *Shan Yao* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae)

\[Functions\] Warms the kidney, dissipates cold, astringes urine and
stops enuresis

\[Indications\] Deficiency cold of the urinary bladder: Frequent
urination, enuresis, cold intolerance of the lower abdomen, a pale
tongue, and a deep weak pulse.

Both *Sang Piao Xiao San* and *Suo Quan Fang* can be used for frequent
urination or enuresis. The former is indicated for enuresis or frequent
urination due to deficiency of the heart and kidney. The latter is
indicated for enuresis due to deficiency cold of the urinary bladder.

## 8.5 Formulas that secure the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians and stop uterine bleeding/leucorrhea {#formulas-that-secure-the-chong-and-ren-meridians-and-stop-uterine-bleedingleucorrhea .unnumbered}

Formulas that secure the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians are indicated for
uterine bleeding and leucorrhea due to deficiency of the spleen and
kidney and insecurity of the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians. Patients may
present with chronic uterine bleeding, dripping of leucorrhea,
palpitations, shortness of breath, low back soreness, and general
fatigue. Common ingredients include *Chun Gen Pi* (Cortex Ailanthi),
*Duan Long Gu* (Os Draconis Praeparata), *Duan Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae
Praeparata), *Bai Guo* (Semen Ginkgo), *Wu Bei Zi* (Galla Chinensis) and
*Chi Shi Zhi* (Halloysitum Rubrum). For formulas that stop uterine
bleeding, ingredients that tonify spleen qi are often combined such as
*Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis
Macrocephalae), coupled with ingredients that nourish yin and clear heat
such as *Gui Jia* (Carapax et Plastrum Testudinis) and *Huang Bai*
(Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis). For formulas that stop leucorrhea,
ingredients that strengthen the spleen and resolve dampness are often
combined such as *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae), *Cang
Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis) and *Shan Yao* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae) or
ingredients that clear heat and resolve dampness such as *Huang Bai*
(Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis) and *Che Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis).
Classic formulas include *Gu Chong Tang* (Penetrating Vessel-Securing
Decoction), *Gu Jing Wan* (Menses-Securing Pill), *Wan Dai Tang*
(Discharge-Ceasing Decoction) and *Yi Huang Tang* (Yellow-Transforming
Decoction).

### ***Gu Chong Tang*** {#gu-chong-tang .unnumbered}

*Yi Xue Zhong Zhong Can Xi Lu* (Records of Chinese Medicine with
Reference to Western Medicine)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 白术  | *bái    | 30g | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae |
| archs |         | zhú*    |     |                                    |
+=======+=========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 黄芪  | *huáng  | 18g | Radix Astragali                    |
|       |         | qí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | >       | *shān   | 24g | Fructus Corni                      |
| sters |  山茱萸 | zhū yú* |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 白芍  | *bái    | 12g | Radix Paeoniae Alba                |
|       |         | sháo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | >       | *duàn   | 24g | Os Draconis Praeparata             |
| tants |  煅龙骨 | lóng    |     |                                    |
|       |         | gŭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >       | *duàn   | 24g | Concha Ostreae Praeparata          |
|       |  煅牡蛎 | mŭ lì*  |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >       | *zōng   | 6g  | Petiolus Trachycarpi Carbonisatus  |
|       |  棕榈炭 | lǚ tàn* |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >       | *wŭ bèi | 1   | Galla Chinensis                    |
|       |  五倍子 | zĭ*     | .5g |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茜草  | *qiàn   | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rubiae            |
|       |         | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >       | *hăi    | 12g | Endoconcha Sepiae                  |
|       |  海螵蛸 | piāo    |     |                                    |
|       |         | xiāo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+---------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Bai Zhu* and *Huang Qi* tonify qi and strengthen the spleen.

Ministers: *Shan Zhu Yu* and *Bai Shao* tonify and reinforce the liver
and kidney to nourish blood and yin and secure the *Chong* meridian.

Assistants: *Duan Long Gu*, *Duan Mu Li*, *Zong Lǚ Tan* and *Wu Bei Zi*
stop bleeding; Qian Cao and Hai Piao Xiao resolve stasis and stop
bleeding.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Supplements qi, strengthens the spleen, secure the
*Chong* meridian and stops bleeding

**\[Indications\]** Spleen qi deficiency with insecure *Chong* meridian:
Sudden or chronic uterine bleeding with thin blood in pale color,
dizziness, palpitations, shortness of breath, fatigue, soreness and
weakness of the low back and knee joints, a pale tongue and a thready
weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for spleen qi
deficiency and insecurity of the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians. Spleen qi
failing to control blood within the vessels coupled with insecurity of
the *Chon*g and *Ren* meridians may cause uterine bleeding with thin
clear blood in pale color. Spleen qi deficiency may cause shortness of
breath and fatigue. Kidney deficiency coupled with insecurity of the
*Chong* and *Ren* meridians may cause soreness and weakness of the low
back and knee joints. Loss of blood may cause dizziness and
palpitations. As a result, the treatment strategies are to supplement
qi, strengthen the spleen, secure the *Chong* meridian and stop
bleeding.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Uterine bleeding with thin blood
in pale color, shortness of breath, fatigue, soreness and weakness of
the low back and knee joints, a pale tongue and a thready weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

For complication of internal heat, combine with *Sheng Di* (Radix
Rehmanniae) and *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) to clear heat and stop
bleeding.

For uterine bleeding after anger, combine with *Chai Hu* (Radix
Bupleuri), *Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae) and *Dan Pi* (Cortex Moutan) to
soothe the liver to maintain free flow of qi.

3\. Modern use: Dysfunctional uterine bleeding or heavy bleeding after
childbirth that can be diagnosed as spleen qi deficiency with insecurity
of the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for patients with
uterine bleeding due to reckless flow of blood or yin deficiency with
blood heat.

### ***Gu Jing Wan*** {#gu-jing-wan .unnumbered}

*Dan Xi Xin Fa* (Teachings of \[Zhu\] Dan-xi)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+--------+-------+-----------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 龟甲 | *guī   | 30g   | Carapax et Plastrum Testudinis    |
| narch |        | jiă*   |       |                                   |
+=======+========+========+=======+===================================+
|       | > 白芍 | *bái   | 30g   | Radix Paeoniae Alba               |
|       |        | sháo*  |       |                                   |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+-----------------------------------+
|       | > 黄芩 | *huáng | 30g   | Radix Scutellariae                |
|       |        | qín*   |       |                                   |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+-----------------------------------+
| Min   | > 黄柏 | *huáng | 9g    | Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis     |
| ister |        | băi*   |       |                                   |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+-----------------------------------+
| Assis | >      | *chūn  | 22.5g | Cortex Ailanthi                   |
| tants | 椿根皮 | gēn    |       |                                   |
|       |        | pí*    |       |                                   |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+-----------------------------------+
|       | > 香附 | *xiāng | 7.5g  | Rhizoma Cyperi                    |
|       |        | fù*    |       |                                   |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Gui Jia* is salty and sweet in taste and neutral in property.
It nourishes kidney yin and reduces fire; *Bai Shao* is bitter and sour
in taste and cold in property. It secures yin and nourishes (liver)
blood; and *Huang Qin* is bitter in taste and cold in property. It
clears heat and stops bleeding.

Minister: *Huang Bai* is bitter in taste and cold in property. It
assists *Huang Qin* to clear heat and assist *Gui Jia* to reduce fire.

Assistants: *Chun Gen Pi* is bitter and astringent in taste and cool in
property. It secures menses and stops bleeding; *Xiang Fu* soothes liver
qi and regulates menstruation.

The formula clears heat, reduces fire and stops uterine bleeding and is
therefore known as *Gu Jing Wan* (Menses-Securing Pill).

Both *Gu Jing Wan* and *Gu Chong Tang* can be used to treat uterine
bleeding. *Gu Jing Wan* targets the underlying cause by nourishing yin
and clearing heat. It has a mild effect in stopping hemorrhage and
indicated for uterine bleeding due to deficiency fire (yin deficiency)
impairing the *Chon*g and *Ren* meridians. *Gu Chong Tang* is indicated
for uterine bleeding due to spleen qi deficiency and insecurity of
*Chong* and *Ren* meridians.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g pills for each dose, 2 doses a
day; or decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin, clears heat, secure menses and stops
bleeding

**\[Indications\]** Uterine bleeding due to yin deficiency and blood
heat: prolonged period, heavy menstruation, earlier period with deep red
or dark purple blood, feverish sensations in the palms and soles,
soreness and weakness in the low back and knee joints, a red tongue and
a wiry rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for uterine
bleeding due to yin deficiency and blood heat. Yin deficiency of the
liver and kidney may lead to deficiency fire to impair the *Chong* and
*Ren* meridians and cause blood to move recklessly, resulting in
prolonged period, heavy menstruation or earlier period. Deep red or dark
purple blood is a sign indicative of reckless flow of blood. Yin
deficiency of the liver and kidney may cause soreness and weakness of
the low back and knee joints and feverish sensations in the palms and
soles. As a result, the treatment strategies are to nourish yin, clear
heat, secure menses, and stop bleeding.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Uterine bleeding with deep red or
dark purple blood, a red tongue and a wiry rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

For heavy or chronic bleeding, combine with *Qian Cao Gen* (Radix et
Rhizoma Rubiae), *Shen Can Qi* (Radix et Rhizoma Notoginseng) and *Zong
lǚ Tan* (Petiolus Trachycarpi Carbonisatus) to stop bleeding (branch
symptoms).

For mild deficiency heat, remove Huang Bai and combine with *Nǚ Zhen Zi*
(Fructus Ligustri Lucidi) and *Han Lian Cao* (Herba Ecliptae) to nourish
yin and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Dysfunctional uterine bleeding, heavy menstruation after
artificial abortion, heavy menstruation, persistent dripping of
menstruation or earlier period due to inflammation in reproductive
disorders that can be diagnosed as internal heat due to yin deficiency
in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with uterine bleeding due to
spleen deficiency should use this formula with caution.

### ***Yi Huang Tang*** {#yi-huang-tang .unnumbered}

*Fu Qing Zhu Nǚ Ke* (Fu Qing-zhu\'s \[Treatise on\] Gynecology)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------+
| Monarchs       | >      | *chǎo    | 30g | Rhizoma Dioscoreae      |
|                | 炒山药 | shān     |     | Praeparata              |
|                |        | yào*     |     |                         |
+================+========+==========+=====+=========================+
|                | >      | *chǎo*   | 30g | Semen Euryales          |
|                | 炒芡实 | *qiàn    |     | Praeparata              |
|                |        | shí*     |     |                         |
+----------------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------+
| Ministers &    | > 黄柏 | *huáng   | 6g  | Cortex Phellodendri     |
| assistants     |        | băi*     |     | Chinensis               |
+----------------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------+
|                | >      | *chē     | 3g  | Semen Plantaginis       |
|                | 车前子 | qián zĭ* |     |                         |
+----------------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------+
|                | > 白果 | *bái     | 10g | Semen Ginkgo            |
|                |        | guŏ*     |     |                         |
+----------------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Chao Shan Yao* and *Chao Qian Shi* tonify the spleen and
kidney and astringe or stop leucorrhea.

Ministers & assistants: *Huang Bai* is bitter in taste and cold in
property and enters the kidney meridian. It clears heat and resolves
dampness; *Che Qian Zi* is sweet and tasteless and cold in property. It
resolves dampness and clears heat; and *Bai Guo* astringes and stops
leucorrhea, clears heat and resolves dampness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Tonifies the spleen and kidney, clears heat and
resolves dampness

**\[Indications\]** Leucorrhea due to deficiency of the spleen and
kidney coupled with downward flow of damp heat: Sticky yellow and white
leucorrhea with a strong odor, general fatigue, a poor appetite,
soreness and weakness of the low back and knee joints, a yellow greasy
tongue coating and a soft pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for leucorrhea
due to deficiency of the spleen and kidney coupled with downward flow of
damp heat. Failure of spleen qi to transport and transform may cause
water dampness, which in turn transform into heat. Failure of kidney qi
to transform bodily fluids may generate damp, which in turn also
generate heat. Downward flow of damp heat may cause sticky, yellow and
white leucorrhea with a strong odor. Spleen deficiency may cause general
fatigue and a poor appetite. Kidney deficiency may cause soreness and
weakness of the low back and knee joints. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to tonify the spleen and kidney, clear heat, resolve
dampness and stop leucorrhea.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Yellow, sticky, smelly
leucorrhea, general fatigue, low back soreness and a yellow greasy
tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For persistent leucorrhea, combine with *Ji Guan Hua* (Flos Celosiae
    Cristatae) and *Duan Hai Piao Xiao* (Endoconcha Sepiae Praeparata).

-   For low back pain, combine with *Du Zhong* (Cortex Eucommiae) and
    *Gou Ji* (Rhizoma Cibotii) to tonify the kidney.

-   For abdominal pain due to qi stagnation, combine with *Xiang Fu*
    (Rhizoma Cyperi) and *Chuan Lian Zi* (Fructus Toosendan) to soothe
    liver qi.

3\. Modern use: Vaginitis, cervicitis or pelvic inflammation that can be
diagnosed as deficiency of the spleen and kidney coupled with downward
flow of damp heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with uterine bleeding due to
(yin) deficiency heat should use this formula with caution.

**Summary**

Formulas that secure and astringe are indicated for loss of or abnormal
leakage/discharge of qi, blood, essence and body fluids. There are a
total of 14 formulas and 2 annex formulas in this chapter. These
formulas are classified into five categories: formulas to consolidate
the exterior and stop sweating, formulas to astringe the lung and stop
coughing, formulas to astringe the large intestine and stop diarrhea,
formulas to secure the essence gate and stop nocturnal emissions and
formulas to secure the Chong and Ren meridians and stop uterine
bleeding/leucorrhea.

**Consolidating the exterior to stop sweating**

*Mu Li San* is a common formula for spontaneous sweating and night
sweats. It acts to consolidate the exterior, stop sweating, secure yin
and nourish qi.

**Astringing the lung to stop coughing**

*Jiu Xian San* is indicated for chronic coughing due to deficiency of
both qi and yin. It acts to astringe the lung, stop coughing, supplement
qi and nourish yin.

**Astringing the large intestine to stop diarrhea**

Both *Zhen Ren Yang Zang Tang* and *Si Shen Wan* can warm and reinforce
the spleen and kidney, astringe the large intestine and stop chronic
diarrhea (due to deficiency cold). The former is better in warming
spleen yang, astringing the large intestine, circulating blood and
regulating qi and is more indicated for chronic diarrhea or dysentery
due to deficiency cold of the spleen and kidney. The latter is better in
warming kidney yang and has a mild effect in astringing the large
intestine and is more indicated for early morning diarrhea due to kidney
yang deficiency.

**Securing the essence gate to stop nocturnal emissions**

Both *Jin Suo Gu Jing Wan* and *Sang Piao Xiao San* are indicated for
nocturnal emissions or urinary incontinence due to kidney deficiency.
The former secures kidney essence and is indicated for nocturnal
emissions or spermatorrhea. The latter reinforces the heart and kidney
and secures kidney essence and is indicated for frequent urination,
urinary incontinence, seminal emissions and cloudy urine.

**Securing the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians to stop uterine
bleeding/leucorrhea**

Both *Gu Chong Tang* and *Gu Jing Wan* are used for uterine bleeding.
*Gu Chong Tang* supplements qi, consolidates Chong and Ren meridians and
stops bleeding. It is more indicated for uterine bleeding due to
deficiency of the spleen and kidney and insecurity of the *Chong* and
*Ren* meridians. *Gu Jing Wan* nourishes yin and clears heat and is more
indicated for uterine bleeding due to uterine bleeding due to (yin)
deficiency heat. Yi Huang Tang tonifies the spleen and kidney, clears
heat and resolves dampness and is indicated for yellow leucorrhea due to
deficiency of the spleen and kidney coupled with downward flow of damp
heat.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. Why are tonifying ingredients often combined in formulas that     |
| secure and astringe?                                                  |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. *Yu Ping Feng San*, *Mu Li San* and *Gui Zhi Tang* can all be     |
| used for spontaneous sweating. How do you choose them in clinical     |
| practice?                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Describe the similarities and differences between *Zhen Ren Yang  |
| Zang Tang* and *Si Shen Wan* in terms of ingredients, functions and   |
| indications.                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| 4\. Describe the similarities and differences between *Jin Suo Gu     |
| Jing Wan* and *Sang Piao Xiao San* in terms of their functions and    |
| indications.                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| 5\. Both *Gu Chong Tang* and *Gui Pi Tang* can be used for uterine    |
| bleeding. How do you choose them in clinical practice?                |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

> **Chapter 9 Formulas that Tranquilize the Mind**

As the name suggests, formulas that tranquilize the mind are indicated
for mental restlessness.

Multiple factors may contribute to mental restlessness. Excess patterns
are often associated with being frightened or fire heat disturbing the
heart mind. Patients may present with panic, anger, restlessness and
mania. The treatment strategies for excess patterns are to tranquilize
the mind with heavy (weighty) materials. Deficiency patterns are
associated with malnourishment of the heart mind due to consumption of
yin and blood by too much thinking or worries. Patients may present with
palpitations, forgetfulness, insomnia and dream-disturbed sleep. The
treatment strategies for deficiency patterns are to nourish yin and
blood to tranquilize the mind. As a result, this chapter focuses on two
categories of formula: one to tranquilize the mind with heavy materials
and the other to tranquilize the mind by nourishing yin and blood.

Formulas that tranquilize the mind with heavy materials consist of heavy
metals or minerals. These ingredients are difficult to digest and, over
time, may damage stomach qi. It's important for patients with weak
spleen and stomach to combine herbs that strengthen the spleen and
harmonize the stomach. In addition, some ingredients are toxic, such as
*Zhu Sha* (Cinnabaris) and should not be taken for a long period of
time.

**9.1 Formulas that Tranquilize the Mind with Heavy Materials**

Formulas that tranquilize the mind with heavy materials are indicated
for mental restlessness due to yang hyperactivity of the heart and liver
and heat disturbing the mind. Patients may present with agitation,
insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep and mild or severe palpitations. Common
ingredients include heavy metals or minerals such as *Zhu Sha*
(Cinnabaris) and *Ci Shi* (Magnetitum), coupled with heart-clearing
herbs such as *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis) and *Zhi Zi* (Fructus
Gardeniae) as well as yin-nourishing herbs such as *Mai Dong* (Radix
Ophiopogonis) and *Sheng Di Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae). Classic formula
to tranquilize the mind is *Zhu Sha An Shen Wan* (Cinnabar Mind-Calming
Pill).

***Zhu Sha An Shen Wan***

*Nei Wai Shang Bian Huo Lun* (Clarifying Doubts about Damage from
Internal and External Causes)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+--------+------+-----------------------------------+
| M      | > 朱砂 | *zhū   | 15g  | Cinnabaris (powder and wrapped    |
| onarch |        | shā*   |      | up)                               |
+========+========+========+======+===================================+
| Mi     | > 黄连 | *huáng | 18g  | Rhizoma Coptidis                  |
| nister |        | lián*  |      |                                   |
+--------+--------+--------+------+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 生地 | *shēng | 4.5g | Radix Rehmanniae                  |
| stants |        | dì*    |      |                                   |
+--------+--------+--------+------+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 当归 | *dāng  | 7.5g | Radix Angelicae Sinensis          |
|        |        | guī*   |      |                                   |
+--------+--------+--------+------+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | >      | *zhì*  | 1    | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae     |
|        | 炙甘草 | *gān   | 6.5g | Praeparata cum Melle              |
|        |        | căo*   |      |                                   |
+--------+--------+--------+------+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Zhu Sha* is weighty and enters the heart meridian. It is cold
in property and acts to tranquilize the mind and clear heart fire.

Minister: *Huang Lian* is bitter in taste and cold in property and acts
to clear heart fire. When used in combination, *Zhu Sha* and *Huang
Lian* can tranquilize the mind, clear fire and relieve mental
restlessness.

Assistants: *Sheng Di* nourishes yin and clears heat; and *Dang Gui*
nourishes blood.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* coordinates and harmonizes the actions of other
ingredients. It moderates the bitter and cold of *Huang Lian* and
prevents the stomach from being damaged by *Zhu Shao*.

This formula clears heart fire (clinical symptoms) and nourishes yin and
blood (root cause).

**\[Method of administration\]** Honey bolus, 6-9g for each dose and
take with warm water before going to bed at night

**\[Functions\]** Tranquilizes the mind, clears heart fire and nourishes
yin

**\[Indications\]** Hyperactivity of heart fire due to deficiency of yin
and blood: agitation, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, mild or severe
palpitations, vexation, a red tongue and a thready rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for mental
restlessness due to hyperactivity of heart fire and deficiency of yin
and blood. Hyperactive heart fire may disturb the mind and cause
agitation, insomnia and dream-disturbed sleep. Hyperactive fire
consuming yin and blood may cause malnourishment of heart mind, leading
to mild or severe palpitations. A red tongue and a thready rapid pulse
are typical signs indicative of fire hyperactivity due to yin
deficiency. As a result, the treatment strategies are to tranquilize the
mind, clear fire and nourish yin.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Palpitations, insomnia,
restlessness, a red tongue and a thready rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a severe feverish sensation in the chest due to hyperactivity of
    heart fire, combine with *Shan Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae) and *Lian
    Zi Xin* (Plumula Nelumbinis) to enhance the effect of clearing heart
    fire and alleviating restlessness.

-   For severe insomnia and dream-disturbed sleep, combine with *Suan
    Zao Ren* (Semen Ziziphi Spinosae) and *Ye Tiao Teng* (Caulis
    Polygoni Multiflori) to nourish the heart and calm the mind.

3\. Modern use: Neurasthenia, depression and tachycardia that can be
diagnosed as hyperactivity of heart fire due to deficiency of yin and
blood.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since *Zhu Sha* is toxic, this formula
should be taken in a small dose and only for a short period of time to
prevent mercury poisoning. In addition, this formula cannot be taken in
combination with iodide or bromide to prevent iatrogenic enteritis.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Zi Zhu Wan* (Loadstones and Cinnabar Pill),
originally known as *Shen Qu Wan* (Medicated Leaven Pill) from the *Bei
Ji Qian Jin Yao Fang* (Important Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold Pieces
for Emergency)

\[Ingredients\] *Shen Qu* (Massa Medicata Fermentata), *Ci Shi*
(Magnetitum) and *Zhu Sha* (Cinnabaris)

\[Functions\] Tranquilizes the mind and benefits the ear and eyes.

\[Indications\] Disharmony between the heart and kidney: blurred vision,
tinnitus, deafness, palpitations, insomnia and epilepsy.

Both *Zi Zhu Wan* and *Zhu Sha An Shen Wan* use *Zhu Sha* for
palpitations and insomnia. The former is more indicated for blurred
vision, palpations, insomnia, tinnitus and deafness due to disharmony
between heart (hyperactivity of heart yang) and kidney (kidney yin
deficiency). The latter is more indicated for palpitations and insomnia
due to hyperactivity of heart fire and deficiency of yin and blood.

**9.2 Formulas that Tranquilize the Mind by Nourishing Yin and Blood**

Formulas that tranquilize the mind by nourish yin and blood are
indicated for mental restlessness due to failure of yin and blood to
nourish the heart mind. Patients may present with a feverish sensation
in the chest, poor sleep, palpitations, forgetfulness, and a red tongue
with a scanty coating. Common ingredients that nourish the heart and
calm the mind include *Suan Zao Ren* (Semen Ziziphi Spinosae), *Bai Zi
Ren* (Semen Platycladi) and *Wu Wei Zi* (Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis),
coupled with herbs that nourish yin and blood, such as *Sheng Di* (Radix
Rehmanniae), *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) and *Dang Gui* (Radix
Angelicae Sinensis). Classic formulas include *Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan*
(Celestial Emperor Heart-Supplementing Elixir) and *Suan Zao Ren Tang*
(Wild Jujube Decoction).

***Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan***

*Jiao Zhe Fu Ren Liang Fang* (Corrections and Annotations to Fine
Formulas for Women)

**\[Indications\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | > 生地 | *shēng  | 1   | Radix Rehmanniae                  |
| onarch |        | dì*     | 20g |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
| Min    | > 玄参 | *xuán   | 15g | Radix Scrophulariae               |
| isters |        | shēn*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 天冬 | *tiān   | 30g | Radix Asparagi                    |
|        |        | dōng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 30g | Radix Ophiopogonis                |
|        |        | dōng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *suān   | 30g | Semen Ziziphi Spinosae            |
|        | 酸枣仁 | zăo     |     |                                   |
|        |        | rén*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *băi zĭ | 30g | Semen Platycladi                  |
|        | 柏子仁 | rén*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 当归 | *dāng   | 30g | Radix Angelicae Sinensis          |
|        |        | guī*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 人参 | *rén    | 15g | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng          |
| stants |        | shēn*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 15g | Poria                             |
|        |        | líng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *wŭ wèi | 30g | Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis     |
|        | 五味子 | zĭ*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 远志 | *yuăn   | 15g | Radix Polygalae                   |
|        |        | zhì*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 丹参 | *dān    | 15g | Radix et Rhizoma Salviae          |
|        |        | shēn*   |     | Miltiorrhizae                     |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 桔梗 | *jié    | 15g | Radix Platycodonis                |
|        |        | gĕng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Sheng Di* is used to nourish kidney yin
(water) to control deficiency heart fire.

Ministers: *Xuan Shen*, *Tian Dong* and *Mai Dong* nourish yin and clear
heat; *Suan Zao Ren* and *Bai Zi Ren* nourish the heart and calm the
mind; and *Dang Gui* nourishes blood.

Assistants: *Ren Shen* and *Fu Ling* calm the mind; Wu Wei Zi astringes
heart qi to calm the mind; *Yuan Zhi* coordinates the heart and kidney,
calms the mind and relieves palpitations; *Dan Shen* clears heart fire,
circulates heart blood and calms the mind.

Envoy: Jie Gen harmonizes and ascends the actions of other ingredients.

This formula nourishes yin and blood (root cause) and calms the heart
mind (clinical symptoms), especially the latter.

Both *Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan* and *Gui Pi Tang* can nourish blood and calm
the mind and are indicated for palpitations, forgetfulness and insomnia
due to malnourishment of the heart. The former is more indicated for
deficiency heat due to yin deficiency and blood failing to nourish the
heart, especially heart blood deficiency. The latter is more indicated
for deficiency of the heart and spleen, especially spleen qi deficiency.

**\[Method of administration\]** Honey bolus, take 6-9g for each dose
with warm water; or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin and blood and tranquilizes the heart
mind.

**\[Indications\]** Mental restlessness due to deficiency of yin and
blood: palpitations, insomnia, agitation, mental fatigue, nocturnal
emissions (wet dreams), forgetfulness, feverish sensations in the palms
and soles, mouth or tongue ulcerations, constipation, a red tongue with
a scanty coating and a wiry thready pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for mental
restlessness due to deficiency fire disturbing the mind resulting from
deficiency of yin and blood (of the heart and kidney). Failure of yin
and blood to nourish the heart mind may cause agitation, palpitations,
insomnia and forgetfulness. Ascending of deficiency heat/fire may cause
mouth or tongue ulcerations and feverish sensations in the palms and
soles. Deficiency fire disturbing the essence chamber may cause
nocturnal emissions (wet dreams). A red tongue with a scanty coating and
a thready rapid pulse are typical signs indicative of internal heat due
to yin deficiency. As a result, the treat strategies are to nourish yin
and blood and calm the heart mind.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristics signs and symptoms: Palpitations, insomnia, feverish
sensations in the palms and soles, a red tongue with a scanty coating
and a thready rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe insomnia, combine with *Long Chi* (Dens Draconis) and
    *Ye* *Jiao Teng* (Caulis Polygoni Multiflori) to enhance the effect
    of calming the mind.

-   For forgetfulness and mind-wandering, combine with *Yuan Zhi* (Radix
    Polygalae) and *Shi Chang Pu* (Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii) to calm
    and stabilize the mind.

3\. Modern use: Neurasthenia, heart disease and hyperthyroidism that can
be diagnosed as mental restlessness due to deficiency of yin and blood.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula contains yin-nourishing
herbs and is therefore not recommended for patients with weak spleen and
stomach, loose stools and a greasy tongue coating.

**\[Annex formula\]**

+---------------------+------------+--------------+-------------------+

Both *Bai Zi Yang Xin Wan* and *Kong Sheng Zheng Zhong Dan* are
indicated for insomnia and forgetfulness. The former has a mild effect
of clearing deficiency fire and is more indicated for mental
restlessness due to deficiency of the heart and kidney coupled with mild
deficiency heat. The latter is better in supplementing kidney essence
and more indicated for mental restlessness due to insufficiency of
kidney essence and disharmony between the heart and kidney.

### ***Suan Zao Ren Tang*** {#suan-zao-ren-tang .unnumbered}

### *Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)  {#jin-gui-yao-lüè-essentials-from-the-golden-cabinet .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | >      | *suān   | 18g | Semen Ziziphi Spinosae            |
| onarch | 酸枣仁 | zăo     |     |                                   |
|        |        | rén*    |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
| Min    | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 6g  | Poria                             |
| isters |        | líng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 知母 | *zhī    | 6g  | Rhizoma Anemarrhenae              |
|        |        | mŭ*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Ass    | > 川芎 | *chuān  | 6g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong                |
| istant |        | xiōng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān    | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae     |
|        |        | căo*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Suan Zao Ren* is used to nourish liver blood
and calm the heart mind.

Ministers: *Fu Ling* tranquilizes the heart mind; *Zhi Mu* nourishes yin
and clears heat.

Assistant: Chuan Xiong is pungent and dispersing and acts to regulate
liver blood and soothe liver qi. When used in combination with a large
dose of *Suan Zao Ren*, it acts to reinforce the liver. When used in
combination with *Zhi Mu*, it acts to prevent blood from being consumed
by warm-dry herbs.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula reinforces the liver and soothes liver qi.

*Suan Zao Ren Tang* and *Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan* are both indicated for
restlessness and insomnia due to deficiency of yin and blood and
deficiency heat disturbing the mind. The former is more indicated for
insomnia due to liver blood deficiency and associated symptoms including
dizziness, blurred vision and a dry mouth and throat. The latter is more
indicated for palpitations and insomnia due to yin-blood deficiency of
the heart and kidney (especially the heart) and associated symptoms
include feverish sensations in the palms and soles and mouth or tongue
ulcerations.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes blood, calms the mind, clears heat and
relieves restlessness.

**\[Indications\]** Deficiency heat disturbing the mind due to liver
blood deficiency: restlessness, insomnia, dizziness, blurred vision, a
dry mouth and throat, a red tongue and a wiry thready pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for nourishing
blood, calming the mind and soothing the liver. The liver stores blood
and houses ethereal soul (*Hun* in Chinese pinyin). Liver blood
deficiency may cause internal heat. Deficiency heat disturbing the heart
mind and malnourishment of the heart may cause restlessness and
insomnia. Failure of liver blood to ascend may cause dizziness and
blurred vision. A red tongue and a wiry thready pulse are typical signs
indicative of internal heat due to liver blood deficiency. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to nourish blood, calm the mind, clear heat
and relieve restlessness.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Restlessness, insomnia, a dry
mouth and throat, a red tongue and a wiry thready pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe restlessness, combine with *Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae)
    and *Lian Xin* (Plumula Nelumbinis) to clear heat and relieve
    restlessness.

-   For severe deficiency heat coupled with night sweats, combine with
    *Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae), *Fu Xiao Mai* (Fructus Tritici Levis) and
    *Wu Wei Zi* (Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis) to calm the mind and
    astringe sweating.

-   For waking up startled, combine with *Long Chi* (Dens Draconis) and
    *Zhen Zhu Mu* (Concha Margaritiferae Usta) to relieve fright and
    tranquilize the mind.

3\. Modern use: Neurasthenia, depression and menopausal syndrome that
can be diagnosed as internal heat disturbing the mind due to liver blood
deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Gan Mai Da Zao Tang* (Licorice, Wheat and Jujube
Decoction) from the *Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden
Cabinet)

\[Ingredients\] *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae) 9g, *Xiao Mai*
(Fructus Tritici) 15-30g and *Da Zao* (Fructus Jujubae) (5-10)

\[Method of administration\] Decoct with water and take in 3 times

\[Functions\] Nourishes the heart, calms the mind and moderates the
spleen and stomach

\[Indications\] Hysteria with associated symptoms including
mind-wandering, sadness, crying for no reason, restlessness, insomnia,
abnormal behaviors, frequent yawning, a pale red tongue and a thready
rapid pulse

*Gan Mai Da Zao Tang* and *Suan Zao Ren Tang* are both indicated for
poor sleep due to yin and blood deficiency of the heart and liver. The
former is more indicated for hysteria due to liver/heart qi stagnation
with associated symptoms including mind-wandering, disturbed sleep,
sadness and crying for no reason. The latter is more indicated for
restlessness and insomnia due to deficiency of liver blood.

**Summary**

There are a total of 3 formulas and 4 annex formulas in this chapter. In
terms of functions, these formulas are categorized into two types: one
to calm the mind with heavy materials and the other to calm the mind by
nourishing yin and blood.

**Calm the mind with heavy materials**

*Zhu Sha An Shen Wan* acts to calm the mind, clear fire and nourish yin
and is therefore indicated for mental restlessness, insomnia and
dream-disturbed sleep due to hyperactive heart fire damaging yin and
blood.

**Calm the mind by nourishing yin and blood**

*Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan* acts to nourish yin, clear heat, reinforce the
heart and calm the mind and is therefore indicated for palpitations,
insomnia, feverish sensations in the palms and soles, forgetfulness and
nocturnal emissions due to yin deficiency of the heart and kidney. *Suan
Zao Ren Tang* acts to nourish blood, calm the mind, clear heat and
relieve restlessness and is therefore indicated for restlessness,
insomnia, dizziness, blurred vision and a dry mouth and throat due to
liver blood deficiency and deficiency heat disturbing the mind.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. How do you choose between 'calming the mind with heavy materials' |
| and 'calming the mind by nourishing yin and blood in clinical         |
| practice?                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. Describe the similarities and differences of *Tian Wang Bu Xin    |
| Dan* and *Zhu Sha An Shen Wan* in terms of their ingredients,         |
| functions and indications.                                            |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Describe the similarities and differences of *Tian Wang Bu Xin    |
| Dan* and *Suan Zao Ren Tang* in terms of their functions and          |
| indications.                                                          |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 10 Formulas that Open the Orifices**

As the name suggests, the formulas that open the orifices are indicated
for loss of consciousness and blockage of orifices. These formulas often
contain aromatic herbs. Blockage of orifices can be categorized into
cold blockage and heat blockage. Heat blockage results from toxic heat
in febrile disease entering the pericardium or phlegm heat affecting the
pericardium. The treatment strategies are to clear heat and open the
orifices. Cold blockage results from cold, dampness and phlegm or turbid
qi misting the mind. The treatment strategies are to warm the interior
and unblock the orifices. As a result, there are two categories of
formulas: one to clear heat and the other to warm the interior and open
the orifices. The procedures of using formulas that open the orifices
are as follows:

First, distinguish (tense) closed syndrome from (flaccid) collapse
syndrome: closed syndrome is characterized by loss of consciousness,
lockjaw, clenching of fists and an excessive forceful pulse, whereas
collapse syndrome is characterized by flaccidity of the hands, urinary
incontinence, closed eyes, an open mouth, cold limbs upon sweating and a
deficient weak pulse. Formulas that open orifices are indicated for
closed syndrome but not for collapsed syndrome.

Second, differentiate between cold and heat of a closed syndrome prior
to treatment. The treatment strategies for an unreleased exterior
syndrome with loss of consciousness due to exuberant heat are to release
the exterior and clear heat.

Third, the treatment strategies for Yangming *F*u-organ syndrome with
loss of consciousness and delirium are to clear heat and promote bowel
movements; the treatment strategies for Yangming *Fu*-organ syndrome
coupled with heat entering the pericardium are to open the orifices
first and then clear heat and promote bowel movements or do both
simultaneously.

Fourth, since most orifice-opening herbs are pungent and dispersing,
they can only be used for a short period of time and patients are
supposed to discontinue once they become better.

In addition, orifice-opening herbs such as *She Xiang* (Moschus) and
*Bing Pian* (Borneolum Syntheticum) may affect fetal development and are
therefore contraindicated for pregnant women. Formulas that open the
orifices are often in the form of pills and powder, because decoction
may affect their potency.

## 10.1 Formulas that Open the Orifices with Cold-Property Ingredients  {#formulas-that-open-the-orifices-with-cold-property-ingredients .unnumbered}

Formulas that open the orifices with cold-property ingredients are
indicated for closed syndrome due to toxic heat entering the pericardium
in febrile disease. Patients may present with high-grade fever,
restlessness, loss of consciousness and delirium. In severe cases,
spasms or convulsions may also be present. These formulas are also
indicated for wind stroke, phlegm syncope and sudden faint with
unconsciousness due to contact with filthy turbidity. Principal
ingredients in these formulas are aromatic such as *She Xiang* (Moschus)
and *Bing Pian* (Borneolum Syntheticum). Herbs that clear the heart,
cool blood and remove toxins such as *Niu Huang* (Calculus Bovis) and
*Shui Niu* *Jiao* (Cornu Bubali) are often used in combination. Classic
formulas include *An Gong Niu Huang Wan* (Palace-Calming Bovine Bezoar
Pill), *Zi Xue San* (Purple Snow Powder) and *Zhi Bao Dan* (Supreme
Jewel Elixir).

***An Gong Niu Huang Wan* (*Niu Huang Wang*)**

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Mo     | > 牛黄 | *niú    | 1   | Calculus Bovis                    |
| narchs |        | huáng*  | 00g |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
|        | >      | *shuĭ   | 2   | Cornu Bubali                      |
|        | 水牛角 | niú     | 00g |                                   |
|        |        | jiăo*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 麝香 | *shè    | 25g | Moschus                           |
|        |        | xiāng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Min    | > 黄连 | *huáng  | 1   | Rhizoma Coptidis                  |
| isters |        | lián*   | 00g |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 黄芩 | *huáng  | 1   | Radix Scutellariae                |
|        |        | qín*    | 00g |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 山栀 | *shān   | 1   | Fructus Gardeniae                 |
|        |        | zhī*    | 00g |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 冰片 | *bīng   | 25g | Borneolum Syntheticum             |
|        |        | piàn*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 郁金 | *yù     | 1   | Radix Curcumae                    |
|        |        | jīn*    | 00g |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 珍珠 | *zhēn   | 50g | Margarita                         |
| stants |        | zhū*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 朱砂 | *zhū    | 1   | Cinnabaris                        |
|        |        | shā*    | 00g |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 雄黄 | *xióng  | 1   | Realgar                           |
|        |        | huáng*  | 00g |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 蜂蜜 | *fēng   |     | Mel (honey)                       |
|        |        | mì*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Niu Huang* is bitter in taste and cold in property and acts
to clear the heart, remove toxins, resolve phlegm and open the orifices;
*Shui Niu* *Jiao* is salty in taste and cold in property and acts to
clear the heart, cool blood and remove toxins; and *She Xiang* is
aromatic and acts to open the orifices and refresh the mind.

Ministers: *Huang Lian*, *Huang Qin* and *Shan Zhi* are bitter in taste
and cold in property and act to clear fire, remove toxins and assist
*Niu Huang* and *Shui Niu* *Jiao* to clear heat and remove toxins of the
pericardium; *Bing Pian* and *Yu Jin* act to dispel filthy turbidity and
open the orifices and assist *She Xiang* to refresh the mind.

Assistants: *Zhen Zhu* and *Zhu Sha* act to calm the mind and clear
heat; Xiong Huang acts to resolve phlegm and remove toxins.

Envoy: Honey harmonizes the stomach and coordinates the actions of other
ingredients.

This formula clears heat and fire, cools blood, removes toxins and opens
the orifices; however, it's better in clearing heat and removing toxins.

According to the original text, 'combine with *Ren Shen Tang* (Ginseng
Decoction) for a deficient pulse and combine with *Yin Hua* and *Bo He*
Tang (Lygodium and Mint Decoction) for an excessive pulse'. *Ren Shen*
is supplemented to reinforce qi and open the orifices; whereas Yin Hua
and Bo He are supplemented to clear heat.

**\[Method of Administration\]** 3g for each honey bolus. Adults: 1
bolus for each dose, 1 dose a days; Children ≤ 3 years old: 1/4 bolus
for each dose, 1 dose a day; Children aged between 4 and 6: 1/2 bolus
for each dose, 1 dose a day; alternatively, follow the doctor's advice.
For unconscious patients, melt the bolus with water and perform nasal
delivery.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, removes toxins, resolves phlegm and opens
the orifices

**\[Indications\]** Pathogenic heat entering the pericardium or phlegm
heat misting the pericardium: High-grade fever, restlessness, loss of
consciousness, delirium, a dry mouth and tongue, tongue stiffness, cold
limbs, a red or deep red tongue and a rapid pulse; loss of consciousness
after stroke and infantile convulsions due to heat blocking the
pericardium.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for pathogenic
heat entering the pericardium. Heat entering the pericardium and
disturbing the heart mind may cause a high-grade fever, restlessness,
loss of consciousness and delirium. Exuberant interior heat consuming
body fluids may cause a dry mouth and tongue. Phlegm heat closing the
orifices may cause tongue stiffness. Heat entering the pericardium may
cause cold limbs (the deeper the heat, the colder the limbs); loss of
consciousness after stroke and infantile convulsions due to high-grade
fever are also signs of heat blockage. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to clear heat, remove toxins, open the orifices and
refresh the mind.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Loss of consciousness, delirium,
high-grade fever, restlessness, a red or deep red tongue and a rapid
pulse.

2\. Modifications: For loss of consciousness, a shortened tongue and
constipation due to heat entering the pericardium, melt 2 boluses (*An
Gong Niu Huang Wan*) and mix with a powder of *Da Huang* (Radix et
Rhizoma Rhei) and take half of the drink first; then take the other half
if the patient still remains unconscious, which is known as *Niu Huang
Cheng Qi Tang* (Bovine Bezoar Purgative Decoction).

3\. Modern use: Encephalitis B, epidemic cerebrospinal meningitis, viral
encephalitis, cerebrovascular accidents, conscious disturbance after
brain injury, epilepsy, hepatic encephalopathy, toxic dysentery, uremia
and septicemia that can be diagnosed as phlegm heat blocking the
pericardium.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula should not be taken for a
large dose for a long period of time. It is contraindicated for pregnant
women.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Niu Huang Qing Xin Wan* (Bovine Bezoar
Heart-Clearing Pill) from the *Dou Zhen Shi Yi Xin Fa* (Teachings on the
Treatment of Pox from a Family Lineage of Doctors)

\[Ingredients\] *Niu Huang* (Calculus Bovis), *Zhu Sha* (Cinnabaris),
*Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis), *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae),
*Shang Zhi* (Fructus Gardeniae) and *Yu Jin* (Radix Curcumae)

\[Functions\] Clears heat, removes toxins, opens the orifices and calms
the mind.

\[Indications\] Pathogenic heat (in febrile disease) entering the
pericardium: fever, loss of consciousness, delirium, restlessness,
stroke (closed syndrome) and infantile convulsions due to high-grade
fever.

This formula is not as strong as *An Gong Niu Huang Wan* in clearing
heat and opening the orifices and is indicated for mild heat block
syndrome. *An Gong Niu Huang Wan* has strong effect in clearing heat,
removing toxins and opening the orifices and is often used for emergency
conditions due to pathogenic heat entering the pericardium or phlegm
heat misting the pericardium.

***Zi Xue San***

(*Su Gong Fang*) from the *Wai Tai Mi Yao* (Arcane Essentials from the

|        |        | xiān*   |       |                                 |
