|        | > 丁香 | *dīng   | 30g   | Flos Caryophylli                |
|        |        | xiāng*  |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 沉香 | *chén   | 150g  | Lignum Aquilariae Resinatum     |
|        |        | xiāng*  |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 朱砂 | *zhū    | 90g   | Cinnabaris                      |
|        |        | shā*    |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 磁石 | *cí     | 1440g | Magnetitum                      |
|        |        | shí*    |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 硝石 | *xiāo*  | 960g  | Nitrokalite                     |
|        |        | *shí*   |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+
|        | > 芒硝 | *máng   | 4800g | Natrii Sulfas                   |
|        |        | xiāo*   |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān    | 240g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae   |
|        |        | căo*    |       |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-------+---------------------------------+

Note: The original formula also contains 3000g of gold to tranquilize
the heart mind.

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Shui Niu* *Jiao* acts to clear the heart, cool blood and
remove toxins; *Ling Yang* *Jiao* acts to soothe the liver, stop wind
and relieve spasm; and *She Xiang* acts to open the orifices and refresh
the mind.

Ministers: *Shi Gao* and *Han Shui Shi* act to clear heat/fire, relieve
vexation and stop thirst; *Hua Shi* acts to clear heat, open the
orifices and induce diuresis; *Xuan Shen* acts to nourish yin, clear
heat and cool blood; and *Sheng Ma* acts to clear heat and remove
toxins.

Assistants: Like *She Xiang*, *Qing Mu Xiang*, *Ding Xiang* and *Chen
Xiang* are aromatic and act to circulate qi, unblock the orifices and
enhance the effect of refreshing the mind; *Zhu Sha* and *Ci Shi*
tranquilize the mind: *Zhu Sha* clears the heart and remove toxins and
*Ci Shi* restrains liver yang; and *Xiao Shi* and *Mang Xiao* act to
clear heat and promote bowel movements.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula opens the upper orifices and unblocks the lower orifices.
It targets the heart and liver by using heavy metals or minerals,
aromatic herbs and herbs that are sweet and salty in taste and cold and
cool in property to clear heat, open the orifices, stop wind, relieve
spasm and protect yin fluids.

**\[Method of Administration\]** For adults: take 1.5-3g of the powder
for each dose, 2 doses a day; for infants after 1 year old: 0.3 g for
each dose, 1 dose a day; for children ≤ 5, increase by 0.3g for each
additional year, 1 dose a day; and use appropriate dose for children
above the age of 5.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, opens the orifices, stops wind and
relieves convulsion.

**\[Indications\]** Heat blocking the pericardium or extreme heat
generating wind: High-grade fever, restlessness, loss of consciousness,
delirium, convulsive syncope, thirst, dry withered lips, dark-yellow
urine, constipation, a deep red tongue with a dry yellow coating and a
rapid, forceful or wiry rapid pulse; infantile convulsions due to
high-grade fever.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for heat
blocking the pericardium and extreme heat generating wind. Heat blocking
the pericardium and disturbing the heart mind may cause high-grade
fever, restlessness, loss of consciousness and delirium. Heat consuming
body fluids may cause thirst, dry withered lips, dark-yellow urine and
constipation. Heat generating wind may cause convulsive syncope.
Pathogenic heat entering the pericardium may trigger liver wind and
convulsions in children. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
clear heat, open the orifices, stop wind and relieve convulsions.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: High-grade fever, restlessness,
loss of consciousness, delirium, convulsive syncope, constipation, a
deep red tongue with a dry yellow coating and a rapid forceful pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For damage to both qi and yin, combine with the decoction of *Shen
    Mai San* (Pulse-Engendering Powder) to supplement qi and nourish
    yin.

-   For heat entering the ying-nutrients blood, combine with *Xi* *Jiao
    Di Huang Tang* (Rhinoceros Horn and Rehmannia Decoction) to cool
    blood and remove toxins.

3\. Modern use: Epidemic encephalitis B, epidemic cerebrospinal
meningitis, viral encephalitis, severe pneumonia, scarlet fever and
septicemia due to purulent infection, hepatic coma, infantile
convulsions due to high fever and measles that can be diagnosed as heat
entering the pericardium and extreme heat generating wind in Chinese
medicine.

4\. Cautions & treatment: This formula is contraindicated to collapse
syndrome, deficiency syndrome, chronic infantile convulsions and
pregnant women.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Xiao Er Hui Chun Dan* (Children\'s
Return-of-Spring Elixir) from the *Jing Xiu Tang Yao Shuo* (Medicine
Instructions of Jingxiutang)

\[Ingredients\] *Chuan Bei Mu* (Bulbus Fritillariae Cirrhosae), *Chen
Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aristolochiae),
*Bai Dou Kou* (Fructus Amomi Kravanh), *Zhi Qiao* (Fructus Aurantii),
*Fa Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae Praeparatum), *Chen Xiang* (Lignum
Aquilariae Resinatum), *Tian Zhu Huang* (Concretio Silicea Bambusae),
*Jiang Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus), *Quan Xie* (Scorpio), *Tan Xiang*
(Lignum Santali Albi), *Niu Huang* (Calculus Bovis), *She Xiang*
(Moschus), *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile), *Gou Teng* (Ramulus
Uncariae Cum Uncis), *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei), *Tian Ma*
(Rhizoma Gastrodiae), *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae) and *Zhu
Sha* (Cinnabaris).

\[Functions\] Opens the orifices, relieves convulsions, clears heat and
resolves phlegm

\[Indications\] Infantile convulsions due to phlegm heat misting the
heart mind: fever, restlessness, loss of consciousness, convulsive
syncope, vomiting, night cry, vomiting of milk, phlegm-related
cough/asthma, abdominal pain and diarrhea.

Both *Zi Xue San* and *Xiao Er Hui Chun Dan* can clear heat, open the
orifices, stop wind and relieve convulsions. The former is better in
clearing heat and opening the orifices. The latter is better in
resolving phlegm and relieving convulsions.

***Zhi Bao Dan***

(*Ling Yuan Fang*) from the *Su Shen Liang Fang* (Fine Formulas of *Su*
and *Shen*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

| Mo     | >      | *shuĭ   | 60g     | Cornu Bubali (powder)         |
| narchs | 水牛角 | niú     |         |                               |
|        |        | jiăo*   |         |                               |

|        |        | huáng*  |         |                               |

| isters | 安息香 | xiāng*  |         |                               |

|        |        | piàn*   |         |                               |

|        |        | mào*    |         |                               |

| stants |        | huáng*  |         |                               |

|        |        | shā*    |         |                               |

|        | > 琥珀 | *hŭ pò* | 30g     | Succinum                      |
+--------+--------+---------+---------+-------------------------------+
|        | > 金箔 | *jīn*   | 50      | Gold foil                     |
|        |        | *bó*    | pieces  |                               |
+--------+--------+---------+---------+-------------------------------+
|        | > 银箔 | *yín    | 50      | Silver foil                   |
|        |        | bó*     | pieces  |                               |
+--------+--------+---------+---------+-------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Shui Niu* *Jiao* acts to clear the heart, cool blood and
remove toxins; *Niu Huang* acts to clear the heart, remove toxins,
resolves phlegm and opens the orifices; and *She Xiang* is aromatic and
acts to open the orifices.

Ministers: *An Xi Xiang* is aromatic and acts to open the orifices;
*Bing Pian* is pungent and fragrant and acts to open the orifices, clear
heat and dispel filthy turbidity; *Dai Mao* acts to tranquilize the
heart mind, clear heat, remove toxins, stop wind, relieve convulsions
and assist Shui Niu *Jiao* to clear heat, cool blood and remove toxins.

Assistants: *Xiong Huang* acts to resolve phlegm and remove toxins; *Zhu
Sha*, *Hu Po*, *Jin Bo* and *Yin Bo* act to tranquilize the heart mind
and relieve convulsions.

*Zhi Bao Dan*, *An Gong Niu Huang Wan* and *Zi Xue San a*re known as
'three treasures to open the orifices with cold-property ingredients'.
*An Gong Niu Huang Wan* is coldest in property, followed by *Zi Xue San*
and *Zhi Bao Dan* (from *Wen Bing Tiao Bian*). *An Gong Niu Huang Wan*
is better in clearing heat and removing toxins and especially indicated
for a high-grade fever due to excess of pathogenic heat. *Zi Xue San* is
better in stopping wind and relieving convulsions and especially
indicated for convulsions due to high-grade fever. *Zhi Bao Dan*
contains more aromatic herbs and is better in opening the orifices and
dispelling turbidity. Since it only has a mild effect of clearing heat,
it's indicated for loss of consciousness due to turbid phlegm.

**\[Method of Administration\]** Take 1 bolus (3g) for each dose, 1 dose
a day. Use a smaller dose for children.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, opens the orifices, resolves turbidity
and removes toxins

**\[Indications\]** Phlegm heat blocking the pericardium: Loss of
consciousness, delirium, fever, restlessness, panting, a deep red tongue
with a yellow turbid greasy coating and a slippery rapid pulse; wind
stroke, sun stroke and infantile convulsions due to phlegm heat
affecting the pericardium.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classical formula for phlegm
heat blocking the pericardium. Phlegm heat affecting the pericardium may
cause loss of consciousness, delirium and panting. A deep red tongue
with a yellow turbid coating and a slippery rapid pulse are typical
signs indicative of phlegm heat. Wind stroke, sun stroke and infantile
convulsions due to internal phlegm heat may also cause loss of
consciousness and convulsions. As a result, the treatment strategies are
to resolve turbidity, open the orifices, clear heat and remove toxins.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Loss of consciousness, delirium,
fever, restlessness and panting.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe conditions with a weak pulse and constitution, use *Ren
    Shen Tang* (Ginseng Decoction) first to prevent further progression.

-   For severe heat with an excessive pulse, combine with decoction of
    *Lian Qiao* (Fructus Forsythiae) and *Xuan Shen* (Radix
    Scrophulariae) to enhance the effect of clearing the heart and
    removing toxins.

3\. Modern use: Epidemic encephalitis B, epidemic cerebrospinal
meningitis, cerebrovascular accidents, hepatic coma, toxic dysentery,
sunstroke and infantile convulsions that can be diagnosed as phlegm heat
blocking the pericardium.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since ingredients in this formula are
aromatic, pungent and dry, it may consume yin fluids and is therefore
contraindicated for patients with yin deficiency and yang excess.
Pregnant women should use this formula with caution.

## 10.1 Formulas that Open the Orifices with Warm-Property Ingredients {#formulas-that-open-the-orifices-with-warm-property-ingredients .unnumbered}

## Formulas that open the orifices with warm-property ingredients are indicated for cold blockage syndrome---cold, dampness and turbid phlegm blocking the mind or filthy turbidity obstructing the flow of qi. Common conditions are wind stroke, cold stroke, qi stagnation and phlegm syncope. Patients may present with sudden collapse, lockjaw, loss of consciousness, a white tongue coating and a slow pulse. These formulas often contain aromatic herbs to open the orifices such as *She Xiang* (Moschus), *Bing Pian* (Borneolum Syntheticum), *Su He Xiang* (Styrax) and *An Xi Xiang* (Benzoinum). In addition, herbs that warm the interior and circulate qi such as *Ding Xiang* (Flos Caryophylli), *Tan Xiang* (Lignum Santali Albi) and *Bi Ba* (Fructus Piperis Longi) are used in combination. Classic formula is *Su He Xiang Wan* (Storax Pill). {#formulas-that-open-the-orifices-with-warm-property-ingredients-are-indicated-for-cold-blockage-syndromecold-dampness-and-turbid-phlegm-blocking-the-mind-or-filthy-turbidity-obstructing-the-flow-of-qi.-common-conditions-are-wind-stroke-cold-stroke-qi-stagnation-and-phlegm-syncope.-patients-may-present-with-sudden-collapse-lockjaw-loss-of-consciousness-a-white-tongue-coating-and-a-slow-pulse.-these-formulas-often-contain-aromatic-herbs-to-open-the-orifices-such-as-she-xiang-moschus-bing-pian-borneolum-syntheticum-su-he-xiang-styrax-and-an-xi-xiang-benzoinum.-in-addition-herbs-that-warm-the-interior-and-circulate-qi-such-as-ding-xiang-flos-caryophylli-tan-xiang-lignum-santali-albi-and-bi-ba-fructus-piperis-longi-are-used-in-combination.-classic-formula-is-su-he-xiang-wan-storax-pill. .unnumbered}

***Su He Xiang Wan (*originally known as *Chi Li Ga Wan)***

(*Guang Ji Fang*) from the *Wai Tai Mi Ya*o (Arcane Essentials from the
Imperial Library)

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Su He Xian*g, *An Xi Xiang*, *She Xiang* and *Bing Pian* are
all aromatic and act to open the orifices, refresh the mind, and dispel
filthy turbidity.

Ministers: *Xiang Fu*, *Mu Xiang*, *Chen Xiang*, *Tan Xiang*, *Ru Xiang*
and *Ding Xiang* are all aromatic and act to circulate qi, dissipate
cold, alleviate pain, regulate qi and move blood.

Assistants: *Shui Niu* *Jiao* acts to clear the heart and remove toxins;
*Zhu Sha* strongly calms the mind; *Bai Zhu* reinforces spleen qi, dries
dampness and resolves turbidity; and *He Zi* astringes qi.

This formula contains aromatic, warm and dispersing ingredients. It acts
to warm and unblocks the orifices, dispel filthy turbidity, circulate qi
and alleviate pain. This formula is originally called *Chi Li Ga (Bai
Zhu) Wan* in *Guan Ji Fang*, volume 13, *Wai Tai Mi Yao* (Arcane
Essentials from the Imperial Library) and changed into *Su He Xiang Wan*
in *Su Shen Liang Fang* (Fine Formulas of Su and Shen). The original
formula is named after *Bai Zhu*, indicating that this formula acts to
open the orifices, circulate qi and at the same time reinforce
anti-pathogenic qi.

**\[Method of Administration\]** Take 1 honey bolus with warm water for
each dose, 1-3 doses a day; decrease the dose for children; and for
unconscious patients, melt the bolus with water and perform nasal
delivery.

**\[Functions\]** Opens the orifices, circulates qi and alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Cold blockage syndrome or qi stagnation due to cold
retention: Sudden collapse, lockjaw, loss of unconsciousness, a white
tongue coating, a slow pulse; sudden cardiac or abdominal pain, syncope,
wind stroke or contraction of seasonal epidemic qi.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for cold
blockage syndrome. Cold and filthy turbid qi misting the heart mind may
cause a sudden collapse, loss of unconsciousness and lockjaw. Cold
retention may obstruct the flow of qi and cause sudden cardiac or
abdominal pain or even syncope. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to warm and unblock the orifices, circulate qi and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: sudden collapse, loss of
consciousness, lockjaw, a white tongue coating and a slow pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For wind stroke with profuse phlegm, combine with ginger juice and
    *Zhu Li* (Succus Bambusae).

-   For epilepsy due to phlegm misting the heart mind, combine with the
    decoction of *Shi Chang Pu* (Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii) and *Yu Jin*
    (Radix Curcumae).

3\. Modern use: Epidemic encephalitis B, cerebrovascular accidents,
epilepsy, hepatic coma, angina pectoris and cardiac infarction that can
be diagnosed as cold blockage or qi stagnation due to cold retention.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula should be taken in a
small dose and only for a short period of time. Since some ingredients
in this formula may affect fetal development, pregnant women should use
this formula with caution. It's contraindicated for patients with
collapse syndrome and heat blockage.

**\[Annex formula\]**

*Guan Xin Su He Xiang Wan* is simplified *Su He Xiang Wan* and has a
moderate effect in opening the orifices and circulating qi. *Zi Jin
Ding* is commonly used for seasonal epidemic in summer. It has a strong
effect in resolving phlegm, dispelling filthy turbidity, removing
toxins, resolving swelling and alleviating pain; however, it only has a
mild effect in opening the orifices. By oral administration, it acts to
alleviate seasonal epidemic symptoms in summer including abdominal
stuffiness/pain, vomiting and diarrhea; by external application, it
works for boils, carbuncles and ulcerations.

**Summary**

There are a total of 4 formulas and four annex formulas in this chapter.
In terms of functions, these formulas are categorized into two types:
one to open the orifices with cold-property ingredients and the other to
open the orifices with warm-property ingredients.

**Open the orifices with cold-property ingredients**

Known as the 'three treasures in opening the orifices with cold-property
ingredients', *An Gong Niu Huang Wan*, *Zi Xue San* and *Zhi Bao Dan*
act to clear heat and open the orifices. *An Gong Niu Huang Wan* is
better in clearing heat, removing toxins and resolving phlegm and more
indicated for unconsciousness and delirium due to heat entering the
pericardium. *Zhi Bao Dan* is better in opening the orifices and
dispelling filthy turbidity and more indicated for unconsciousness due
to phlegm-turbidity affecting the pericardium. Although not as strong as
*An Gong Niu Huang Wan* in removing toxins and as *Zhi Bao Dan* in
opening the orifices, *Zi Xue San* is better in stopping wind and
relieving convulsions and more indicated for unconsciousness and
convulsive syncope due to extreme heat generating wind.

**Open the orifices with warm-property ingredients**

*Su He Xiang Wan* acts to open the orifices, circulate qi, dispel filthy
turbidity, warm the spleen and stomach and alleviate pain. It is
indicated for cold blockage syndrome. Because of its effect in warming
the spleen and stomach and alleviating pain, it's also used for cardiac
or abdominal pain due to qi stagnation and cold retention.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. Describe the procedures of using formulas that open the orifices  |
| in clinical practice.                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. What are the 'three treasures that open the orifices with         |
| cold-property ingredients'? Describe their similarities and           |
| differences in functions and indications.                             |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Analyze the formula of *Su He Xiang Wan* and explain the roles of |
| *Bai Zhu* and *He Zi* in the formula.                                 |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 11 Formulas that Regulate Qi**

As the name suggests, formulas that regulate qi act to circulate or
down-regulate qi and are indicated for qi stagnation or adverse
ascending of qi.

Common patterns of qi stagnation are liver qi or spleen/stomach qi
stagnation. The treatment strategies are to circulate qi. Common
patterns of adverse qi ascending are ascending of stomach qi or lung qi.
The treatment strategies are to down-regulate qi. As a result, this
chapter focuses on two categories of formula: one to circulate qi and
the other to down-regulate qi.

It's essential to distinguish deficiency from excess prior to treatment.
For excess qi stagnation, the treatment strategy is to circulate qi;
reinforcing qi may aggravate the stagnation; and for deficiency qi
stagnation, the treatment strategy is to reinforce qi; circulating or
breaking qi may further damage qi. Since pungent, warm and dry-property
ingredients in formulas that regulate qi may consume qi and fluids, and
subsequently, generate heat and fire, these formulas should only be
taken for a short period of time. Alternatively, ingredients that
supplement qi and nourish yin can be used in combination. The elderly
patients or patients with a weak constitution, constitutional deficiency
of qi and yin or severe internal heat should use these formulas with
caution or combine with other medicine. In addition, some ingredients in
these formulas may move blood or affect fetal development, patients with
bleeding tendency and women during period or pregnancy should use these
formulas with caution.

## 11.1 Formulas that Circulate Qi  {#formulas-that-circulate-qi .unnumbered}

Formulas that circulate qi act to unblock qi activities and are often
indicated for liver or spleen/stomach qi stagnation. Herbs that regulate
qi such as *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Hou Pu* (Cortex
Magnoliae Officinalis) and *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) or herbs that
soothe liver qi such as *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi), *Chuan Lian Zi*
(Fructus Toosendan) and *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae) are often used in
these formulas. Classic formulas include *Yue Ju Wan* (Chuanxiong and
Atractylodis Pill), *Zhi Shi Xie Bai Gui Zhi Tang* (Immature Bitter
Orange, Macrostem Onion and Cinnamon Twigs Decoction), *Gua Lou Xie Bai
Bai Jiu Tang* (Trichosanthes, Chinese Chive and Liquor Decoction), *Ban
Xiu Hou Pu Tang* (Pinellia and Officinal Magnolia Bark Decoction), *Hou
Pu Wen Zhong Tang* (Officinal Magnolia Bark Center-Warming Decoction),
*Jing Ling Zi San* (Toosendan Powder), *Tian Tai Wu Yao San* (Linderae
Powder), and *Nuan Gan Jian* (Liver-Warming Decoction).

***Yue Ju Wan* (*Xiong Qu Wan*)**

*Dan Xi Xin Fa* (Teachings of \[Zhu\] Dan-xi)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | > 香附 | *xiāng  | 6g  | Rhizoma Cyperi                    |
| onarch |        | fù*     |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
| Mi     | > 川芎 | *chuān  | 6g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong                |
| nister |        | xiōng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 苍术 | *cāng   | 6g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis              |
| stants |        | zhú*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 山栀 | *shān   | 6g  | Fructus Gardeniae                 |
|        |        | zhī*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 神曲 | *shén   | 6g  | Massa Medicata Fermentata         |
|        |        | qū*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Xiang Fu* is a key herb for qi disorders and acts to circulate
qi.

Minister: *Chuan Xiong* acts to move blood and circulate qi. It resolves
stasis and enhances the effect of *Xiang Fu* in relieving qi stagnation.

Assistants: *Can Zhu* acts to strengthen the spleen and dry dampness;
*Shan Zhi* acts to clear heat/fire; and *Shen Qu* acts to promote
digestion and harmonize the stomach.

Phlegm stagnation may result from qi stagnation, damp accumulation or
fire consuming body fluids. This formula relieves five types of
stagnation, although six types of stagnation are mentioned in
*Indications*.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water; or take 6-9g pills
for one dose, 2 doses a day.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi and relieves stagnation

**\[Indications\]** Six types of stagnation: chest stuffiness, abdominal
distension/pain, belching, acid reflux, nausea, vomiting, indigestion, a
red tongue with a yellow greasy coating and a wiry rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** Many conditions are associated with
stagnation. This is a typical formula for six types of stagnation,
namely, stagnation of qi, blood, phlegm, fire, dampness and food. Qi
stagnation is the most common one. Contributing factors of qi stagnation
include emotional fluctuation, too much worries, failure to adapt to
sudden weather changes and improper diet. Qi stagnation may cause blood
to stagnate. Qi failing to circulate body fluids may cause damp
stagnation. Accumulation of dampness may transform into phlegm. Qi
stagnation of the spleen and stomach may cause food retention. Qi
stagnation may, over time, transform into fire. Six types of stagnation
are mainly associated with the liver, gallbladder, spleen and stomach,
leading to symptoms such as chest stuffiness, abdominal distension/pain,
vomiting, acid reflux and indigestion. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to circulate qi and relieve stagnation. When qi flows
smoothly, stagnations of blood, phlegm, fire, dampness and food
accordingly alleviate.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chest stuffiness, abdominal
distension/pain, indigestion, a red tongue with a yellow greasy coating
and a wiry rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe qi stagnation, increase the dose of *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma
    Cyperi) and combine with *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) and *Yu Jin*
    (Radix Curcumae) to circulate qi.

-   For severe blood stagnation, increase the dose of *Chuan Xiong*
    (Rhizoma Chuanxiong) and combine with *Tao Ren* (Semen Persicae) and
    *Hong Hua* (Flos Carthami) to circulate blood.

-   For severe dampness, increase the dose of *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma
    Atractylodis) and combine with *Fu Ling* (Poria) and *Ze Xie*
    (Rhizoma Alismatis) to resolve dampness.

-   For severe fire, increase the dose of *Shan Zhi* (Fructus Gardeniae)
    and combine with *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and *Huang Lian*
    (Rhizoma Coptidis) to clear heat and reduce fire.

-   For severe indigestion, increase the dose of *Shen Qu* (Massa
    Medicata Fermentata) and combine with *Shan Zha* (Fructus Crataegi)
    and *Mai Ya* (Fructus Hordei Germinatus) to promote digestion.

-   For severe phlegm stagnation, combine with *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma
    Pinelliae) and *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae) to resolve
    phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Gastrointestinal neurosis, functional gastrointestinal
disorders, peptic ulcer, chronic gastritis, cholecystitis, gallstones,
chronic hepatitis, intercostal neuralgia, dysmenorrhea and irregular
menstruation that can be diagnosed as qi stagnation in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with yin deficiency should
use this formula with caution, because its ingredients are warm and dry
in property.

***Zhi Shi Xie Bai Gui Zhi Tang***

*Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Mo     | > 瓜蒌 | *guā    | 12g | Fructus Trichosanthis             |
| narchs |        | lóu*    |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
|        | > 薤白 | *xiè    | 9g  | Bulbus Allii Macrostemi           |
|        |        | bái*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Min    | > 枳实 | *zhĭ    | 12g | Fructus Aurantii Immaturus        |
| isters |        | shí*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 12g | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis      |
|        |        | pò*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Ass    | > 桂枝 | *guì    | 6g  | Ramulus Cinnamomi                 |
| istant |        | zhī*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Gua Lou* is sweet in taste and cold in property and acts to
resolve phlegm, regulate qi and soothe the chest; *Xie Bai* is pungent
in taste and warm in property and acts to resolve phlegm and dissipate
cold. When used in combination, the two herbs dissipate yin cold in the
chest, resolve turbid phlegm in upper *jiao* and regulate qi activities
in the chest.

Ministers: *Zhi Shi* induces qi to flow downward and relieves
stuffiness; and *Hou Pu* dries dampness, resolves phlegm and relieves
stuffiness.

Assistant: *Gui Zhi* warms yang and dissipates cold.

This formula circulates and down-regulates qi, and at the same time,
dissipates cold and resolves phlegm.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Warms yang, relieves stuffiness, resolves phlegm and
down-regulates qi.

**\[Indications\]** Chest-*Bi* (impediment): Chest stuffiness/pain that
may radiate towards the back, panting, coughing, shortness of breath,
upward qi rushing to the chest from the hypochondriac region, a white
greasy tongue coating and a deep wiry or tense pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for chest-*Bi*
(impediment syndrome) due to phlegm-qi stagnation obstructing chest
yang. Phlegm-qi stagnation in the chest may cause chest stuffiness/pain
that may radiate towards the back. Turbid phlegm obstructing the
dispersing and descending of lung qi may cause panting, coughing and
shortness of breath. Obstructed chest yang may cause yin-cold qi to rush
upward to the chest from the hypochondriac region. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to unblock yang qi, relieve stagnation, resolve
phlegm and induce qi to flow downward.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chest fullness, upward qi rushing
to the chest from the hypochondriac region, a white greasy tongue
coating and a deep wiry or tense pulse.

2\. Modifications:

-   For severe cold, combine with *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and
    *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata) to warm yang and
    dissipate cold.

-   For severe turbid-phlegm, combine with *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae)
    and *Fu Ling* (Poria) to resolve phlegm and soothe the chest.

-   For blood stasis, combine with *Dan Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Salviae
    Miltiorrhizae) and *Hong Hua* (Flos Carthami) to circulate blood and
    resolve stasis.

3\. Modern use: Angina pectoris, intercostal neuralgia, non-suppurative
costochondritis and chronic bronchitis that can be diagnosed as
phlegm-qi obstructing chest yang.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula unblocks yang qi,
relieves stagnation, resolves phlegm and induces qi to flow downward,
patients with chest-*Bi* (impediment) due to yang qi deficiency should
avoid taking it alone.

**\[Annex formula\]**

+--------------------+--------+-------------+-------------------------+
| Formula            | Ingre  | Functions   | Indications             |
|                    | dients |             |                         |
+--------------------+--------+-------------+-------------------------+
| *Gua Lou Xie Bai   | *Gua   | Unblocks    | Mild chest-*Bi*         |
| Bai Jiu Tang* from | Lou,   | yang        | (impediment) due to     |
| the *Jin Gui Yao   | Xie    |             | phlegm-qi obstructing   |
| Lüè* (Essentials   | Bai,   | Relieves    | chest yang: chest       |
| from the Golden    | L      | stagnation  | fullness/pain that may  |
| Cabinet)           | iquor* |             | radiate towards the     |
|                    |        | Circulates  | back, panting,          |
|                    |        | qi          | coughing, shortness of  |
|                    |        |             | breath, a white greasy  |
|                    |        | Resolves    | tongue coating and a    |
|                    |        | phlegm      | deep wiry or tense      |
|                    |        |             | pulse                   |
+--------------------+--------+-------------+-------------------------+
| *Gua Lou Xie Bai   | *Gua   | Unblocks    | Chest*-Bi* (impediment) |
| Ban Xia Tang*      | Lou,   | yang        | with severe             |
| (Trichosanthes,    | Xie    |             | turbid-phlegm, chest    |
| Chinese Chive and  | Bai,   | Relieves    | pain that radiates      |
| Pinellia           | Ban    | stagnation  | towards the back and an |
| Decoction) from    | Xia,   |             | inability to lie flat   |
| *Jin Gui Yao Lüè*  | L      | Resolves    |                         |
| (Essentials from   | iquor* | phlegm      |                         |
| the Golden         |        |             |                         |
| Cabinet)           |        | Soothes the |                         |
|                    |        | chest       |                         |
+--------------------+--------+-------------+-------------------------+

*Gua Lou Xie Bai Bai Jiu Tang*, *Gua Lou Xie Bai Ban Xia Tang* and *Zhi
Shi Xie Bai Gui Zhi Tang* can all be used for chest-*Bi* (impediment).
*Gua Lou Xie Bai Bai Jiu Tang* is better in unblocking yang, relieving
stagnation, circulating qi and resolving phlegm and indicated for
chest-*Bi* (impediment) with mild turbid-phlegm. *Gua Lou Xie Bai Ban
Xia Tang* is better in resolving phlegm and relieving stagnation and
indicated for chest-*Bi* (impediment) with severe turbid-phlegm. *Zhi
Shi Xie Bai Gui Zhi Tang* is better in unblocking yang, relieving
stagnation, inducing qi to flow downward, resolving phlegm, stop globus
sensation and indicated for chest-*Bi* (impediment) with severe
phlegm-qi stagnation, chest fullness and upward qi rushing to the chest.

### ***Ban Xia Hou Pu Tang*** {#ban-xia-hou-pu-tang .unnumbered}

### *Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet) {#jin-gui-yao-lüè-essentials-from-the-golden-cabinet-1 .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | > 半夏 | *bàn    | 12g | Rhizoma Pinelliae                 |
| onarch |        | xià*    |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
| Mi     | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 9g  | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis      |
| nister |        | pò*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 生姜 | *shēng  | 15g | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens         |
| stants |        | jiāng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 12g | Poria                             |
|        |        | líng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 苏叶 | *sū yè* | 6g  | Folium Perillae                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ban Xia* resolves phlegm, stop globus sensation, and
down-regulate stomach qi.

Minister: *Hou Pu* circulates qi, relieves stagnation, induces qi to
flow downward and resolves stuffiness.

Assistants: *Sheng Jiang* is pungent in taste and warm in property and
acts to stop globus sensation, down-regulate qi and resolve phlegm. It
assists *Ban Xia* to harmonize the stomach and stop vomiting, and at the
same time moderates the poison of *Ban Xia*. *Fu Ling* is sweet and
tasteless and acts to strengthen the spleen and resolve dampness.

Envoy: *Su Ye* is aromatic and dispersing and assists *Hou Pu* to
circulate qi and relieve stagnation. With its light weight, *Su Ye*
enters the lung and coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula is characterized by combination of pungent and bitterness.
Pungent ingredients act to circulate qi and stop globus sensation. The
bitter ingredients act to dry dampness and down-regulate qi.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi, resolves globus sensation,
down-regulates qi and resolves phlegm

**\[Indications\]** Plum-pit qi: a foreign body sensation in the throat
that cannot be swallowed or expectorated, a pale tongue with a white
moist coating and a wiry or wiry slippery pulse; coughing and vomiting
may also be present.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for plum-pit qi.
Plum-pit qi results from phlegm-qi stagnation. Failure of the liver to
maintain free flow of qi may cause liver qi stagnation. Failure of lung
and stomach qi to descend may cause phlegm. Phlegm-qi may ascend along
the pathways of meridians to the throat, leading to a foreign body
sensation that cannot be swallowed or expectorated, coughing or
vomiting. As a result, the treatment strategies are to circulate qi and
resolve phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: a foreign body sensation in the
throat, a white greasy tongue coating and a wiry slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe qi stagnation, combine with *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi)
    and *Yu Jin* (Radix Curcumae) to circulate qi and relieve
    stagnation.

-   For pain in the rib area, combine with *Chuan Lian Zi* (Fructus
    Toosendan) and *Yan Hu Suo* (Rhizoma Corydalis) to soothe the liver
    and stop pain.

-   For sore throat, combine with *Xuan Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae) and
    *Jie Geng* (Radix Platycodonis) to clear the throat.

-   3\. Modern use: Globus hystericus, a foreign body sensation in the
    throat, anxiety, depression, chronic pharyngitis, chronic
    bronchitis, chronic gastritis and esophageal spasm that can be
    diagnosed as phlegm-qi stagnation in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since ingredients in this formula are
pungent and warm-dry in property, it's contraindicated for patients with
insufficiency of fluids due to yin deficiency or hyperactivity of fire.

***Hou Pu Wen Zhong Tang***

*Nei Wai Shang Bian Huo Lun* (Clarifying Doubts about Damage from
Internal and External Causes)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 30g | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis      |
| onarch |        | pò*     |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
| Mi     | >      | *căo    | 15g | Semen Alpiniae Katsumadai         |
| nister | 草豆蔻 | dòu     |     |                                   |
|        |        | kòu*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 橘皮 | *jú pí* | 30g | Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae     |
| stants |        |         |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 木香 | *mù     | 15g | Radix Aucklandiae                 |
|        |        | xiāng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 干姜 | *gān    | 2g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis                |
|        |        | jiāng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 15g | Poria                             |
|        |        | líng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | >      | *zhì    | 15g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae     |
|        | 炙甘草 | gān     |     | Praeparata cum Melle              |
|        |        | căo*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Hou Pu* is bitter and pungent in taste and warm in property
and acts to circulate qi and relieve distension.

Minister: *Cao Dou Kou* acts to dry dampness, circulate qi, warm the
spleen and stomach and dissipate cold.

Assistants: *Ju Pi* and *Mu Xiang* circulate qi, harmonize the spleen
and stomach, dissipate cold and assist *Hou Pu* to circulate qi and dry
dampness. Both dry and fresh ginger warm the spleen and stomach,
dissipate cold, and assist *Cao Dou Kou* to dissipate cold and alleviate
pain. *Fu Ling* acts to strengthen the spleen and resolve dampness.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* supplements qi, harmonizes the stomach and
coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

**\[Method of administration\]** Add 3 fresh ginger pieces and decoct
with water.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi, relieves fullness, warms the spleen and
stomach and dries dampness.

**\[Indications\]** Cold dampness obstructing the qi flow of the spleen
and stomach: Abdominal distension, fullness or pain, a poor appetite, a
white greasy tongue coating and a deep wiry pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for cold dampness
obstructing the qi flow of the spleen and stomach. Cold dampness
affecting qi flow of the spleen and stomach may cause abdominal
distension, fullness or pain. Failure of the stomach to receive and
decompose food and failure of the spleen to transport and transform
water and food may cause a poor appetite. A white greasy tongue coating
and a deep wiry pulse are typical signs indicative of cold dampness
affecting qi flow of the spleen and stomach. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to circulate qi, relieve stagnation, warm the
spleen/stomach, and dry dampness.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal distension, fullness or
pain, a white greasy tongue coating and a deep wiry pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For abdominal pain due to severe cold, combine with *Liang Jiang*
    (Rhizoma Alpiniae Officinarum) and *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi) to
    warm the spleen and stomach, dissipate cold and alleviate pain.

-   For nausea and vomiting due to adverse ascending of stomach qi,
    combine with *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) and *Sha Ren* (Fructus
    Amomi) to down-regulate stomach qi.

3\. Modern use: Acute/chronic gastritis, enteritis, gastric ulcer and
functional gastrointestinal disorder that can be diagnosed as
cold-dampness affecting the qi flow of the spleen and stomach.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since ingredients in this formula are
bitter and pungent in taste and warm-dry in property, it's
contraindicated for patients with stomach yin deficiency to prevent
further damage to qi and yin.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Liang Fu Wan* (Lesser Galangal and Cyperus Pill)
from the *Liang Fang Ji Ye* (Collections of Fine Formulas)

\[Ingredients\] *Gao Liang Jiang* (Rhizoma Alpiniae Officinarum) and
*Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi)

\[Functions\] Circulates qi, soothe the liver, dissipate cold and
alleviate pain

\[Indications\] Qi stagnation due to cold retention: gastric pain,
distension and fullness in the hypochondriac region, cold intolerance,
preference for warmth, a white tongue coating, a wiry pulse and
dysmenorrhea.

Both *Liang Fu Wan* and *Hou Pu Wen Zhong Tang* can circulate qi and
alleviate pain. *Hou Pu Wen Zhong Tang* is more indicated for abdominal
distension, fullness and pain and a white greasy tongue coating due to
cold dampness obstructing the qi of the spleen and stomach. *Liang Fu
Wan* harmonizes the stomach and soothes the liver and is more indicated
for pain in the chest and hypochondriac region, cold intolerance and
preference for warmth due to cold obstructing the flow of qi.

***Jin Ling Zi San***

*Tai Ping Sheng Hui Fang* (Formulas from Benevolent Sages Compiled
during the Taiping Era) recorded in *Xiu Zhen Fang* (Pocket
Prescriptions)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------------+---------+---------+-----+---------------------------+
| Monarch       | >       | *chuān  | 30g | Fructus Toosendan         |
|               |  金铃子 | liàn    |     |                           |
|               |         | zĭ*     |     |                           |
+===============+=========+=========+=====+===========================+
| Minister &    | >       | *yán hú | 30g | Rhizoma Corydalis         |
| Assistant     |  延胡索 | suŏ*    |     |                           |
+---------------+---------+---------+-----+---------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Jin Ling Zi* is bitter in taste and cold in property and acts
to soothe liver qi, clear liver fire and alleviate pain.

Minister and assistant: *Yan Hu Suo* is bitter and pungent in taste and
warm in property and acts to circulate qi, move blood and alleviate
pain.

Liquor is used as an envoy to coordinate the above two herbs.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6-9g powder with liquor or water
for each dose; alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Soothes the liver, clears heat, circulates blood and
alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Liver qi stagnation transforming into fire:
Intermittent pain in the chest, abdomen and hypochondriac region, a
bitter taste, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a wiry pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a basic formula for pain due to
liver qi stagnation transforming into fire. The liver maintains free
flow of qi and stores blood. Liver qi stagnation may cause blood to
stagnate, resulting in intermittent pain in the chest, abdomen and
hypochondriac region. Liver qi stagnation transforming into fire may
cause a bitter taste, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a wiry
rapid pulse. As a result, the treatment strategies are to soothe the
liver, clear heat, circulate blood and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Pain in the chest, abdomen and
hypochondriac region, a bitter taste, a red tongue with a yellow coating
and a wiry pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For pain in the chest and hypochondriac region, combine with *Yu
    Jin* (Radix Curcumae) and *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi) to soothe the
    liver and alleviate pain.

-   For abdominal distension and pain, combine with *Mu Xiang* (Radix
    Aucklandiae) and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) to regulate
    qi and alleviate pain.

-   For dysmenorrhea, combine with *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis)
    and *Yi Mu Cao* (Herba Leonuri) to regulate menstruation and
    alleviate pain.

-   For swelling and pain in external genitalia, combine with *Wu Yao*
    (Radix Linderae), *Ju He* (Semen Citri Reticulatae) and *Li Zhi He*
    (Semen Litchi) to circulate qi and resolve masses.

3\. Modern use: Chronic hepatitis, chronic cholecystitis, gallstones,
chronic gastritis, peptic ulcer and dysmenorrhea that can be diagnosed
as liver qi stagnation transforming into fire.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Pregnant women should use this formula
with caution.

***Tian Tai Wu Yao San***

*Sheng Ji Zong Lu* (Comprehensive Recording of Divine Assistance)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | > 乌药 | *wū yào* | 15g | Radix Linderae                    |
| onarch |        |          |     |                                   |
+========+========+==========+=====+===================================+
| Min    | > 青皮 | *qīng    | 15g | Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae     |
| isters |        | pí*      |     | Viride                            |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 木香 | *mù      | 15g | Radix Aucklandiae                 |
|        |        | xiāng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *xiăo    | 15g | Fructus Foeniculi                 |
|        | 小茴香 | huí      |     |                                   |
|        |        | xiāng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *gāo     | 15g | Rhizoma Zingiberis                |
|        | 高良姜 | liáng    |     |                                   |
|        |        | jiāng*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 槟榔 | *bīng    | 9g  | Semen Arecae                      |
| stants |        | láng*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 川   | *chuān   | 15g | Fructus Toosendan                 |
|        | 楝子\* | liàn zĭ* |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *bā dòu* | 12g | Fructus Crotonis                  |
|        | 巴豆\* |          |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+----------+-----+-----------------------------------+

Note: \* *Ba Dou* and *Chuan Lian Zi* should be dry fried for 5 to 10
minutes. Discard Ba Dou and then decoct with water.

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Wu Yao* soothes liver qi, dissipates cold and alleviates pain.

Ministers: *Qing Pi* and *Mu Xiang* soothe liver qi and alleviate pain;
*Xiao Hui Xiang* and *Gao Liang Jiang* warm the liver, dissipate cold
and alleviate pain. These four herbs are aromatic, pungent and warm and
enhance the effect of *Wu Yao* in soothing liver qi, dissipating cold
and alleviating pain.

Assistants: *Bing Lang* induces qi to flow downward to the lower *jiao*
and break masses; *Chuan Lian Zi* regulates qi, alleviates pain,
moderates the bitterness and coldness of *Ba Dou* and at the same time,
enhance its effect in circulating qi and resolving masses.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 3-5g powder before meals for each
dose; alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi, soothes the liver, dissipates cold and
alleviates pain.

**\[Indications\]** Inguinal hernia due to cold obstructing the flow of
qi: lateral lower abdominal pain that radiates to the testes, swelling
and bearing-down sensation of the testes, a pale tongue with a white
coating and a deep wiry pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for inguinal
hernia due to cold obstructing the flow of qi. Cold dwelling in liver
meridian may cause liver qi stagnation, resulting in lateral lower
abdominal pain that radiates to the testes and swelling and bearing-down
sensation of the testes. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
circulate qi, soothe the liver, dissipate cold and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: lateral lower abdominal pain that
radiates to the testes, a pale tongue with a white coating and a deep
wiry pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For swelling and bearing-down sensation of the testes, combine with
    *Li Zhi He* (Semen Litchi) and *Ju He* (Semen Citri Reticulatae) to
    circulate qi and resolve masses.

-   For severe cold with preference for warmth, combine with *Rou Gui*
    (Cortex Cinnamomi) and *Wu Zhu Yu* (Fructus Evodiae) to dissipate
    cold and alleviate pain.

3\. Modern use: Indirect and direct inguinal hernia, testitis,
epididymitis, chronic gastritis, functional gastrointestinal disorder,
intestinal spasm and dysmenorrhea that can be diagnosed as qi stagnation
due to cold retention in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for patients with
yin deficiency of the liver and kidney or damp-heat.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Ju He Wan* (Tangerine Seed Pill) from the *Ji
Sheng Fang* (Formulas to Aid the Living)

\[Ingredients\] *Hai Zao* (Sargassum), *Kun Bu* (Thallus Laminaria),
*Hai Dai* (Herba Zosterae Marinae), *Chao Chuan Lian Zi* (Fried Fructus
Toosendan), *Chao Tao Ren* (Fried Semen Persicae), *Mu Tong* (Caulis
Akebiae), *Chao Zhi Shi* (Fried Fructus Aurantii Immaturus), *Chao Yan
Hu Suo* (Fried Rhizoma Corydalis), *Gui Xin* (Ramulus Cinnamomi), *Mu
Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae), and *Chao Ju He* (Fried Semen Citri
Reticulatae)

\[Functions\] Circulates qi, alleviates pain and softens and resolves
masses.

\[Indications\] Inguinal hernia: Swelling, bearing-down sensation or
hardness of the testes, periumbilical pain and scrotal swelling that may
contain yellow pus or ulceration.

Both *Ju He Wan* and *Tian Tai Wu Yao San* can circulate qi and
alleviate pain due to inguinal hernia. The former circulates blood and
softens/resolves masses and is more indicated for testes swelling,
hardness and pain due to qi stagnation and blood stasis along the
pathway of liver meridian that results from cold dampness dwelling in
liver meridian. the latter circulates qi, dissipates cold and alleviates
pain and is more indicated for inguinal hernia characterized by lateral
lower abdominal pain that radiates to the testes and bearing-down
sensation of the testes due to cold obstructing the flow of qi.

***Nuan Gan Jian***

*Jing Yue Quan Shu* (The Complete Works of \[Zhang\] Jing-yue)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
| Monarchs     | > 肉桂  | *ròu    | 3   | Cortex Cinnamomi           |
|              |         | guì*    | -6g |                            |
+==============+=========+=========+=====+============================+
|              | >       | *xiăo   | 6g  | Fructus Foeniculi          |
|              |  小茴香 | huí     |     |                            |
|              |         | xiāng*  |     |                            |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
| Ministers    | > 当归  | *dāng   | 6   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis   |
|              |         | guī*    | -9g |                            |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
|              | >       | *gŏu qĭ | 9g  | Fructus Lycii              |
|              |  枸杞子 | zĭ*     |     |                            |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
|              | > 乌药  | *wū     | 6g  | Radix Linderae             |
|              |         | yào*    |     |                            |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
|              | > 沉香  | *chén   | 3g  | Lignum Aquilariae          |
|              |         | xiāng*  |     | Resinatum                  |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
| Assistant &  | > 茯苓  | *fú     | 6g  | Poria                      |
| Envoy        |         | líng*   |     |                            |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
|              | > 生姜  | *shēng  | 3g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens  |
|              |         | jiāng*  |     |                            |
+--------------+---------+---------+-----+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Rou Gui* is pungent and sweet in taste and heat in property
and acts to warm the kidney and liver, dissipate cold, and alleviate
pain; *Xiao Hui Xian*g is pungent in taste and warm in property and acts
to warm the liver, dissipate cold, regulate qi, and alleviate pain.

Ministers: *Dang Gui* nourishes blood and reinforces the liver; *Gou Qi
Zi* nourishes the liver and kidney; *Wu Yao* and *Chen Xiang* circulate
qi, dissipate cold (in liver meridian) and alleviate pain.

Assistant and Envoy: *Fu Ling* strengthens the spleen and resolves
dampness; *Sheng Jiang* is warm in property and dissipates cold.

This formula warms and reinforces the liver and kidney (root cause),
dissipates cold and circulates qi (symptoms).

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Warms and reinforces the liver and kidney, circulates
qi and alleviates pain.

**\[Indications\]** Inguinal hernia due to deficiency cold of the liver
and kidney: Cold pain in the testes, lateral lower abdominal pain, cold
intolerance, preference for warmth, a pale tongue with a white coating
and a deep slow pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for inguinal
hernia due to deficiency cold of the liver and kidney. Deficiency cold
in the liver and kidney may affect the flow of qi in liver meridian,
resulting in cold pain in the testes, lateral lower abdominal pain, cold
intolerance and preference for warmth. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to warm the liver and kidney, circulate qi and alleviate
pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Pain in the testes or lateral
lower abdomen, cold intolerance, preference for warmth, a pale tongue
with a white coating and a deep slow pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe cold, combine with *Wu Zhu Yu* (Fructus Evodiae), *Gan
    Jian*g (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Fu Zi* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) to warm
    the interior and dispel cold.

-   For severe abdominal pain, combine with *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi)
    and *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae) to circulate qi and alleviate pain.

-   For severe pain in the testes, combine with *Qing Pi* (Pericarpium
    Citri Reticulatae Viride) and *Ju He* (Semen Citri Reticulatae) to
    soothe the liver and regulate qi.

3\. Modern use: Varicocele, testitis, epididymitis, inguinal hernia and
hydrocele that can be diagnosed as deficiency cold of the liver and
kidney in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for scrotal
redness, swelling and pain in heat syndrome.

## 11.2 Formulas that Down-regulate Qi  {#formulas-that-down-regulate-qi .unnumbered}

Formulas that down-regulate qi alleviate asthma or stop vomiting and are
indicated for adverse qi ascending of the lung and stomach. Herbs such
as *Zi Su Zi* (Fructus Perillae), *Sang Bai Pi* (Cortex Mori) and *Xing
Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum) are often used to down-regulate qi and
alleviate coughing or asthma due to ascending of lung qi. Ingredients
such as *Xuan Fu Hua* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum), *Dai Zhe Shi*
(Haematitum), *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae), *Ding Xiang* (Flos
Caryophylli) and *Shi Di* (Calyx Kaki) are often used to that
down-regulate stomach qi and stop hiccups or vomiting due to ascending
of stomach qi. Classic formulas include *Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang* (Perilla
Fruit Qi-Descending Decoction), *Ding Chuan Tang* (Asthma-Alleviating
Decoction), *Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang* (Inula and Hematite Decoction), *Ju
Pi Zhu Ru Tang* (Tangerine Peel and Bamboo Shavings Decoction) and *Ding
Xiang Shi Di Tang* (Clove and Persimmon Decoction).

### ***Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang*** {#su-zi-jiang-qi-tang .unnumbered}

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Monarch | >       | *zĭ sū   | 75g   | Fructus Perillae            |
|         |  紫苏子 | zĭ*      |       |                             |
+=========+=========+==========+=======+=============================+
| Mi      | > 半夏  | *bàn     | 75g   | Rhizoma Pinelliae           |
| nisters |         | xià*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 厚朴  | *hòu pò* | 30g   | Cortex Magnoliae            |
|         |         |          |       | Officinalis                 |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Ass     | > 肉桂  | *ròu     | 45g   | Cortex Cinnamomi            |
| istants |         | guì*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 当归  | *dāng    | 45g   | Radix Angelicae Sinensis    |
|         |         | guī*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 生姜  | *shēng   | 2     | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens   |
|         |         | jiāng*   | p     |                             |
|         |         |          | ieces |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 大枣  | *dà zăo* | 1     | Fructus Jujubae             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 苏叶  | *sū yè*  | 2g    | Folium Perillae             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Envoy   | > 甘草  | *gān     | 60g   | Radix et Rhizoma            |
|         |         | căo*     |       | Glycyrrhizae                |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Zi Su Zi* down-regulates lung qi and resolves phlegm.

Ministers: *Ban Xia* resolves phlegm and alleviates coughing; *Hou Pu*
down-regulates qi, alleviates panting, soothes the chest and relieves
the sensation of fullness.

Assistants: *Rou Gui* warms kidney yang, assists the kidney in keeping
the breathing in a certain depth and alleviate panting; *Dang Gui* is
pungent and sweet in taste and warm and moist in property. It nourishes
blood and assists *Rou Gui* to warm and reinforce the kidney. *Sheng
Jiang, Da Zao* and *Zi Su Ye* release the exterior and dissipate cold

Envoy: *Gan Cao* harmonizes the spleen and stomach, supplements qi and
coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula is recorded in the *Yi Fang Ji Jie* (Collections and
Analyses on Medical Formulas), which states, 'If *Rou Gui* is replaced
with *Chen Xiang*, then this formula has a mild effect of warming the
kidney but a strong effect in keeping the breathing in a certain depth'.

In addition, this formula targets both the root cause (by warming the
kidney and assisting its function in keeping the breath in a certain
depth) and clinical symptoms (by down-regulating qi and resolving
phlegm).

**\[Method of administration\]** Add 2 pieces of fresh ginger, 1 Chinese
date and 2g of perilla leaf and decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Down-regulates qi, alleviate asthma, resolves phlegm
and stops coughing

**\[Indications\]** Coughing and panting in combined deficiency (root
cause) and excess (clinical symptoms) syndrome: Panting or coughing with
profuse phlegm, shortness of breath, chest fullness and tightness, a
white slippery or white greasy tongue coating and a wiry slippery pulse;
alternatively, low back pain, leg weakness, edema in the lower limbs may
be present.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for coughing and
panting in combined deficiency (root cause) and excess (clinical
symptoms) syndrome. Phlegm accumulating in the lung may affect
descending of lung qi and result in coughing or panting and chest
fullness or tightness. Kidney failing to keep the breath in a certain
depth may cause shortness of breath. Kidney deficiency may cause water
retention and subsequently, edema in the lower limbs. Kidney deficiency
may also cause low back pain or leg weakness. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to down-regulate qi, resolve phlegm and warm/reinforce
the kidney.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Coughing or panting, shortness of
breath, chest fullness and tightness, a white slippery or white greasy
tongue coating and a wiry slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe coughing or panting with an inability to lie flat,
    combine with *Ting Li Zi* (Semen Lepidii) and *Da Zao* (Fructus
    Jujubae) to down-regulate qi and alleviate asthma.

-   For complication of an exterior syndrome, combine with *Ma Huang*
    (Herba Ephedrae) and *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum) to
    disperse lung qi and dispel external pathogenic factors.

-   For severe kidney yang deficiency, combine with *Fu Zi* (Radix
    Aconiti Lateralis) and *Chen Xiang* (Lignum Aquilariae Resinatum) to
    warm the kidney and assist its function in keeping the breath in a
    certain depth.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis, emphysema and bronchial asthma that
can be diagnosed as phlegm accumulation.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since ingredients in this formula are
warm and dry in property, it is contraindicated for patients with yin
deficiency of the lung and kidney or lung heat.

### ***Ding Chuan Tang*** {#ding-chuan-tang .unnumbered}

### *She Sheng Zhong Miao Fan*g (Numerous Miraculous Prescriptions for Health Cultivation)  {#she-sheng-zhong-miao-fang-numerous-miraculous-prescriptions-for-health-cultivation .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| M       | > 麻黄  | *má      | 9g    | Herba Ephedrae              |
| onarchs |         | huáng*   |       |                             |
+=========+=========+==========+=======+=============================+
|         | > 白果  | *bái     | 9g    | Semen Ginkgo                |
|         |         | guŏ*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Mi      | > 杏仁  | *xìng    | 4.5g  | Semen Armeniacae Amarum     |
| nisters |         | rén*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 苏子  | *sū zĭ*  | 6g    | Fructus Perillae            |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 半夏  | *bàn     | 9g    | Rhizoma Pinelliae           |
|         |         | xià*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | >       | *kuăn    | 9g    | Flos Farfarae               |
|         |  款冬花 | dōng     |       |                             |
|         |         | huā*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Ass     | >       | *sāng    | 9g    | Cortex Mori                 |
| istants |  桑白皮 | bái pí*  |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
|         | > 黄芩  | *huáng   | 4.5g  | Radix Scutellariae          |
|         |         | qín*     |       |                             |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+
| Envoy   | > 甘草  | *gān     | 3g    | Radix et Rhizoma            |
|         |         | căo*     |       | Glycyrrhizae                |
+---------+---------+----------+-------+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ma Huang* disperses lung qi, dissipates cold and alleviates
panting; *Bai Guo* astringes the lung, resolves phlegm and alleviates
panting. When used in combination, these two ingredients have strong
effect in alleviating panting and, at the same time, dispersing but not
consuming lung qi, in other words, astringing lung qi removing
pathogenic factors.

Ministers: *Xing Ren*, *Su Zi*, *Ban Xia* and *Kuan Dong Hua*
down-regulate qi, alleviate panting, resolve phlegm and stop coughing.

Assistants: *Sang Bai Pi* clears the lung and alleviates panting; *Huang
Qin* clears heat and resolves phlegm.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula disperses, down-regulates, clears and astringes lung qi and
therefore has a strong effect in alleviating panting and coughing.

Compared with *Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang* can down-regulate qi and alleviate
panting, *Ding Chuan Tang* contains *Ma Huang* to disperse lung qi, *Bai
Guo* to astringes lung qi and other ingredients to clear heat and
resolve phlegm. It is more indicated for asthma due to phlegm heat
accumulating in the lung or wind cold attacking the lung. *Su Zi Jiang
Qi Tang* contains *Su Zi* to down-regulate qi and resolve phlegm and
*Rou Gui* and *Dang Gui* to warm the kidney and facilitate the kidney's
function in keep the breathing in a certain depth. It is more indicated
for coughing or panting in a combined deficiency and excess syndrome.

Compared with *Xiao Qing Long Tang* in the effect of alleviating
coughing or panting due to external contraction of wind cold and
internal phlegm-turbidity, *Ding Chuan Tang* disperses and
down-regulates lung qi and releases the exterior and is more indicated
for asthma due to internal accumulation of phlegm heat or wind cold
attacking the exterior. *Xiao Qing Long Tang* releases the exterior,
dissipates cold and resolves retention of cold fluids and is more
indicated for asthma due to both internal and external cold retention.

Compared with *Ma Huang Xing Ren Gan Cao Tang* in alleviating coughing
or panting due to lung heat coupled with external contraction of
pathogenic factors, *Ding Chuan Tang* is better in astringing,
down-regulating lung qi, clearing heat and resolving phlegm. *Ma Huang
Xing Ren Gan Cao Shi Gao Tang*, using *Shi Gao* and *Ma Huang* (2:1) as
the monarchs, has a strong effect in clearing and dispersing lung qi;
however, it has no effect in clearing heat and resolving phlegm.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Disperses and down-regulates lung qi, clears heat and
resolves phlegm.

**\[Indications\]** Asthma due to internal accumulation of phlegm heat
and external contraction of wind cold: Coughing or panting with profuse
yellow thick phlegm, chest distension and tightness, mild aversion to
wind cold, a yellow greasy tongue coating and a rapid slippery pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for asthma due to
internal accumulation of phlegm heat and external contraction of wind
cold. Phlegm heat may obstruct the descending of lung qi. External
contraction of wind cold may further block the dispersing and descending
of lung qi, resulting in asthma, coughing with yellow thick phlegm,
panting, chest distension and tightness and mild aversion to wind cold.
As a result, the treatment strategies are to disperse and down-regulate
lung qi, clear heat and resolve phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Panting, coughing with profuse
yellow phlegm, a yellow greasy tongue coating and a rapid slippery
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe lung heat, combine with *Sheng Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum)
    and *Yu Xing Cao* (Herba Houttuyniae) to clear lung heat.

-   For sticky phlegm, combine with *Quan Gua Lou* (Fructus
    Trichosanthis) and *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) to clear heat
    and resolve phlegm.

-   For severe chest tightness, combine with *Zhi Qiao* (Fructus
    Aurantii) and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) to regulate qi
    and soothe the chest.

3\. Modern use: Bronchial asthma and chronic bronchitis that can be
diagnosed as phlegm heat accumulating in the lung and wind cold
attacking the lung in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for patients with
acute external contraction of wind cold (no internal phlegm heat) or
chronic asthma with a weak pulse due to yin deficiency of the lung and
kidney or qi deficiency.

### ***Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang*** {#xuan-fu-dai-zhe-tang .unnumbered}

### *Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)  {#shang-han-lun-treatise-on-cold-damage .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+
| Monarch    | >      | *xuán  | 9g | Flos Inulae                      |
|            | 旋覆花 | fù     |    |                                  |
|            |        | huā*   |    |                                  |
+============+========+========+====+==================================+
| Ministers  | >      | *dài   | 3g | Haematitum                       |
|            | 代赭石 | zhĕ    |    |                                  |
|            |        | shí*   |    |                                  |
+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+
|            | > 生姜 | *shēng | 1  | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens        |
|            |        | jiāng* | 5g |                                  |
+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+
|            | > 半夏 | *bàn   | 9g | Rhizoma Pinelliae                |
|            |        | xià*   |    |                                  |
+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+
| Assistants | > 人参 | *rén   | 6g | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng         |
|            |        | shēn*  |    |                                  |
+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+
|            | > 大枣 | *dà    | 4  | Fructus Jujubae                  |
|            |        | zăo*   |    |                                  |
+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+
| Assistant  | >      | *zhì   | 9g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
| & envoy    | 炙甘草 | gān    |    | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|            |        | căo*   |    |                                  |
+------------+--------+--------+----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Xuan Fu Hua* induces qi to flow downward, resolves phlegm,
down-regulates qi and alleviates belching.

Ministers: *Dai Zhe Shi* is weighty and acts to down-regulate qi of the
lung and stomach. A small dose of *Dai Zhe Shi* enhances the effect of
*Xuan Fu Hua* in down-regulating qi, stopping vomiting and resolving
phlegm; A large dose of *Sheng Jiang* acts to 1) harmonize the stomach,
down-regulates stomach qi and stops vomiting; 2) disperses qi to resolve
phlegm; and 3) moderates the toxicity of *Ban Xia*. *Ban Xia* resolves
phlegm and down-regulates stomach qi.

Assistants: *Ren Shen*, *Da Zao* and *Zhi Gan Cao* are sweet in taste
and warm in property and act to supplement qi, strengthen the spleen and
benefit the stomach.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

Both *Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang* and *Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang* contain
ingredients such as *Ban Xia*, *Ren She*n, *Gan Cao* and *Da Zao* and
are indicated for fullness/distension due to combined deficiency and
excess. The former down-regulates qi, resolves phlegm, supplements qi
and harmonizes the stomach and is indicated for fullness/distension due
to stomach deficiency coupled with phlegm obstructing the descending of
stomach qi. The latter opens the orifices with pungent herbs and
descends with bitter herbs and is indicated for fullness/distension due
to combine cold and heat.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Down-regulates qi, resolves phlegm, supplements qi and
harmonizes the stomach.

**\[Indications\]** Stomach deficiency with phlegm obstructing the
descending of stomach qi: epigastric fullness, belching, acid reflux,
vomiting, vomiting of salivation, a pale tongue with a white slippery
coating and a wiry weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for stomach
deficiency with phlegm obstructing the descending of stomach qi.
Originally, this formula is indicated belching and epigastric fullness
due to damage to spleen/stomach qi by vomiting and internal
turbid-phlegm affecting the spleen/stomach. Internal turbid-phlegm
obstructing the qi activities of the spleen and stomach may cause
epigastric fullness, frequent belching, acid reflux, vomiting, vomiting
of salivation and a white slippery tongue coating. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to down-regulate stomach qi, resolve phlegm,
supplement qi and harmonize the stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Epigastric fullness, frequent
belching, vomiting, a white slipper tongue coating and a wiry weak
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe adverse qi flow with mild stomach deficiency, increase
    the dose of *Dai Zhe Shi* to enhance its effect in down-regulating
    stomach qi.

-   For profuse phlegm with a greasy tongue coating, combine with *Fu
    Ling* (Poria) and *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae) to
    resolve phlegm and harmonize the stomach.

-   For severe abdominal distension, combine with *Zhi Shi* (Fructus
    Aurantii Immaturus) and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) to
    circulate qi and relieve fullness.

3\. Modern use: Gastroneurosis, chronic gastritis, stomach dilation,
gastric or duodenal ulcers, incomplete pyloric obstruction and
neurogenic hiccup that can be diagnosed as stomach deficiency with
phlegm obstructing the descending of stomach qi.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since *Dai Zhe Shi* is cold in
property and may affect the function of stomach qi in decomposing food,
patients with a severe stomach deficiency should take with a small dose.

**\[Annex formula\]**
