### ***Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang*** {#ju-pi-zhu-ru-tang .unnumbered}

### *Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)  {#jin-gui-yao-lüè-essentials-from-the-golden-cabinet-2 .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| *           | > *     | *jú pí* | *   | Pericarpium Citri           |
| *Monarchs** | *橘皮** |         | *12 | Reticulatae                 |
|             |         |         | g** |                             |
+=============+=========+=========+=====+=============================+
|             | > 竹茹  | *zhú    | 12g | Caulis Bambusae in Taenia   |
|             |         | rú*     |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| **          | > 生姜  | *shēng  | 9g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens   |
| Ministers** |         | jiāng*  |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
|             | > 人参  | *rén    | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng    |
|             |         | shēn*   |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| **          | > 大枣  | *dà     | 5   | Fructus Jujubae             |
| Assistant** |         | zăo*    |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| **Envoy**   | > 甘草  | *zhì    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma            |
|             |         | gān     |     | Glycyrrhizae                |
|             |         | căo*    |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ju Pi* is pungent and bitter in taste and warm in property
and acts to circulate qi, harmonize the stomach and stop hiccups; *Zhu
Ru* is sweet in taste and slightly cold in property and acts to clear
heat, harmonize the stomach and stop vomiting.

Ministers: *Sheng Jiang* harmonizes the stomach and stops vomiting.
Combined *Sheng Jiang* and *Zhu Ru* warm the stomach and clear heat.
*Ren Shen* supplements qi, reinforces the spleen and stomach, nourishes
yin, and harmonizes ying-nutrients. Combined *Ren Shen* and *Ju Pi*
circulate qi and reinforce the spleen and stomach.

Assistant: Combined *Gan Cao* and *Da Zao* nourish qi, reinforce the
spleen and benefit the stomach. Combined *Da Zao* and *Ren Shen* restore
stomach qi.

Envoy: In addition, *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other
ingredients.

This formula reinforces the stomach, clears stomach heat and
down-regulates stomach qi. It clears heat and at the same time, warms
the stomach; it reinforces the stomach and at the same time, circulates
qi. It is especially indicated for hiccups and retch due to deficiency
heat of the stomach.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Down-regulates qi, stops hiccups, supplements qi and
clears heat

**\[Indications\]** Hiccups due to deficiency heat of the stomach:
Hiccups or retch, feverish sensations, shallow breathing, a dry mouth, a
red tender tongue and a deficient rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for hiccups due
to deficiency heat of the stomach. Stomach qi deficiency may cause
shallow breathing. Yin deficiency may generate heat. This coupled with
adverse ascending of stomach qi may cause feverish sensations, a dry
mouth, hiccups and retch. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
supplement qi, clear heat, down-regulate qi and stop hiccups.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Hiccups or vomiting and a red
tender tongue.

2\. Modifications

-   For a dry mouth and a red tongue with a scanty coating due to
    stomach yin deficiency, combine with *Shi Hu* (Caulis Dendrobii) and
    *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) to nourish stomach yin.

-   For thirst with a desire to drink water, a red tongue with a yellow
    coating due to severe stomach heat, combine with *Huang Lian*
    (Rhizoma Coptidis) to clear stomach heat.

3\. Modern use: Morning sickness, vomiting due to incomplete pyloric
obstruction and persistent hiccups following abdominal surgery that can
be diagnosed as deficiency heat of the stomach.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for hiccups and
vomiting due to deficiency cold or excess heat.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Xin Zhi Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang* (Newly Modified
Tangerine Peel and Bamboo Shavings Decoction) from the *Wen Bing Tiao
Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

\[Ingredients\] *Ju Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Zhu Ru*
(Caulis Bambusae in Taenia), *Shi Di* (Calyx Kaki) and *Jiang Zhi*
(Ginger Juice)

\[Functions\] Down-regulates stomach qi

\[Indications\] Hiccups due to damp warm and qi stagnation

Both *Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang* and *Xin Zhi Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang* can clear heat,
down-regulate qi and stop hiccups/vomiting (due to stomach heat and
adverse ascending of stomach qi). The former down-regulates and
supplements qi and clears heat and is indicated for hiccups due to
stomach heat and stomach qi deficiency. The latter harmonizes the
stomach and down-regulates qi and is indicated for hiccups due to
stomach heat and qi stagnation (without stomach qi deficiency).

### ***Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang*** {#ding-xiang-shi-di-tang .unnumbered}

### *Zheng Yin Mai Zhi* (Symptoms, Causes, Pulses, and Treatment)  {#zheng-yin-mai-zhi-symptoms-causes-pulses-and-treatment .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| Monarchs    | > 丁香  | *dīng   | 6g  | Flos Caryophylli            |
|             |         | xiāng*  |     |                             |
+=============+=========+=========+=====+=============================+
|             | > 柿蒂  | *shì    | 9g  | Calyx Kaki                  |
|             |         | dì*     |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| Minister    | > 生姜  | *shēng  | 6   | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens   |
|             |         | jiāng*  | g\* |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+
| Assistant   | > 人参  | *rén    | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng    |
|             |         | shēn*   |     |                             |
+-------------+---------+---------+-----+-----------------------------+

Note: \* There was no recorded dosage of *Sheng Jiang* in the original
formula.

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: Aromatic and pungent, *Ding Xiang* is warm in property and
acts to warm the spleen and stomach, dissipate cold, down-regulate qi
and stop vomiting; *Shi Di* down-regulates stomach qi.

Minister: *Sheng Jiang* warms the stomach, down-regulates stomach qi and
stops vomiting.

Assistant: *Ren Shen* supplements qi and nourishes the stomach.

*Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang*, *Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang* and *Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang*
can all down-regulate qi and reinforce the stomach. *Ding Xiang Shi Di
Tang* warms the stomach and down-regulates stomach qi and is more
indicated for hiccups due to deficiency cold of the stomach. *Xuan Fu
Dai Zhe Tang* down-regulates qi and resolves phlegm and is more
indicated for epigastric masses, acid regurgitation, vomiting and
belching due to stomach deficiency and phlegm obstructing the flow of
qi. *Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang* clears heat and down-regulates qi and is more
indicated for hiccups due to deficiency heat of the stomach.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Down-regulates qi, stops hiccups, warms the spleen and
stomach and supplements qi

**\[Indications\]** Hiccups due to deficiency cold of the stomach qi:
Persistent hiccups, chest and stomach stuffiness, a pale tongue with a
white coating and a deep slow pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for hiccups due
to deficiency cold of the stomach. Failure of stomach qi to descend may
cause chest and stomach stuffiness and persistent hiccups. A pale tongue
with a white coating and a deep slow pulse are typical signs indicative
of deficiency cold of the stomach. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to warm the stomach, reinforce and down-regulate stomach qi and stop
hiccups.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Hiccups, a pale tongue with a
white coating and a deep slow pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe stomach cold, combine with *Wu Zhu Yu* (Fructus Evodiae)
    and *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) to warm the spleen and stomach
    and dissipate cold.

-   For severe chest and gastric stuffiness and distension, combine with
    *Chen Mi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulate) and *Mu Xiang* (Radix
    Aucklandiae) to circulate qi and relieve stagnation.

-   For complication of qi stagnation and phlegm, combine with *Chen Pi*
    (Pericarpium Citri Reticulate) and *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) to
    circulate qi and resolve phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Neurogenic hiccups and diaphragmatic spasm that can be
diagnosed as deficiency cold of the stomach in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for patients with
a dry mouth, a red tender tongue and a deficient rapid pulse due to
deficiency heat of the stomach.

**Summary**

There are a total of 12 formulas and 7 annex formulas in this chapter.
In terms of functions, these formulas are categorized into two types:
one to circulate qi and the other to down-regulate qi. Formulas that
**circulate qi** are listed in the following table:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Formula**   **Functions**                    **Indications**
  ------------- -------------------------------- -----------------------------
  *Yue Ju Wan*  Circulates qi, relieves          Six stagnations of qi, blood,
                stagnation, transforms dampness, phlegm, fire, food and
                resolves phlegm, clears heat and dampness
                promotes digestion               

  *Zhi Shi Xie  Unblocks yang qi, relieves       Chest pain and upward qi
  Bai Gui Zhi   stagnation, resolves phlegm and  rushing to the chest from the
  Tang*         induces qi to flow downward      hypochondriac region due to
                                                 phlegm-qi obstructing chest
                                                 yang

  *Ban Xia Hou  Circulates qi, relieves the      Plum-pit qi due to phlegm qi
  Pu Tang*      foreign body sensation in the    stagnation
                throat, down-regulates qi and    
                resolves phlegm                  

  *Hou Pu Wen   Circulates qi, relieves          Gastric/abdominal fullness,
  Zhong Tang*   distension, warms the spleen and distension and pain due to
                stomach and resolves dampness    cold-dampness obstructing the
                                                 flow of qi

  *Jin Ling Zi  circulates qi/blood, alleviates  Pain in the chest and
  San*          pain and soothe the liver        hypochondriac region due to
                                                 liver qi stagnation
                                                 transforming into fire

  *Tian Tai Wu  circulates qi and dissipates     Lateral lower abdominal pain
  Yao San*      cold                             that radiates to testes and
                                                 swelling and bearing-down
                                                 sensation of the testes due
                                                 to cold-induced qi stagnation

  *Nuan Gan     Warms and reinforces the liver   cold pain in the testes or
  Jian*         and kidney (root cause),         lateral lower abdominal pain,
                dissipates cold, circulates qi   cold intolerance and
                and alleviates pain (symptoms)   preference for warmth due to
                                                 deficiency of the liver and
                                                 kidney and cold retention in
                                                 the liver meridian
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Down-regulating qi**

Formulas that down-regulates qi are indicated for adverse ascending of
the lung and stomach. Both *Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang* and *Ding Chuan Tang*
can down-regulate qi, resolve phlegm and alleviate panting. *Su Zi Jiang
Qi Tang* also warms and reinforces the kidney and is indicated for
panting, coughing, shortness of breath, and chest tightness due to
phlegm retention and kidney yang deficiency. *Ding Chuan Tang* also
clears heat and resolves phlegm and is indicated for asthma due to wind
cold attacking the exterior and internal accumulation of phlegm heat.
*Xuan Fu Da Zhe Tang* resolves phlegm, down-regulates and supplements qi
and reinforce the spleen and stomach and is indicated for epigastric
masses and persistent belching due to stomach deficiency and phlegm
obstructing the descending of stomach qi. *Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang* is
indicated for hiccups and retch due to deficiency heat of the stomach.
*Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang* is indicated for hiccups due to deficiency cold
of the stomach.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. Explain why *Yue Ju Wan* (indicated for six types of stagnation)  |
| does not contain any ingredients that resolve phlegm and relieve      |
| stagnation and why *Xiang Fu* is used as the Monarch in the formula.  |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. Explain why *Ban Xia Hou Pu Tang* is indicated for plum-pit qi    |
| due to phlegm qi stagnation in the throat.                            |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Describe the roles of *Hou Pu* in *Ban Xia Hou Pu Tang* and *Hou  |
| Pu Wen Zhong Tang*.                                                   |
|                                                                       |
| 4\. Explain the significance of fried *Chuan Lian Zi* with *Ba Dou*   |
| in *Tian Tai Wu Yao San*.                                             |
|                                                                       |
| 5\. Describe the similarities and differences of *Ding Chuan Tang*    |
| and *Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang* regarding their ingredients, function and   |
| indications.                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| 6\. Which ingredient has a big dose in *Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang*?        |
| Explain the combination of the formula ingredients.                   |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 12 Formulas that Regulate Blood**

As the name suggests, formulas that regulate blood are indicated for
blood stasis or hemorrhage. These formulas fall under the category of
reducing (*Xiao*) strategy of the eight strategies in Chinese medicine
from the perspective of herbal formulary.

Blood moistens and nourishes the body. Physiologically, blood circulates
within the vessels to irrigate five-zang and six-fu organs and nurture
four limbs, muscles, bones and tissues. Obstructed flow of blood may
result in two consequences: blood stasis or bleeding. The treatment
strategies are to circulate blood and resolve stasis for blood stasis
and to stop bleeding for hemorrhage. As a result, this chapter focuses
on two categories of formula: one to circulate blood and resolve stasis
and the other to stop bleeding.

It's essential to differentiate the root cause of blood stasis or
bleeding prior to treatment. In acute conditions, it's important to
address the symptoms first. In remissive stages, it's important to
address the root cause first. In some cases, however, the root cause and
symptoms may need to be addressed simultaneously. Since aggressive or
prolonged period of stasis-resolving may consume blood and damage
anti-pathogenic qi, these formulas should be taken in a short period of
time. In addition, ingredients that nourish blood and supplement qi are
often combined to protect qi from being damaged. What's more, because
these formulas may move blood and affect fetal development, pregnant
women or women during period or having heavy menstruation should avoid
or use these formulas with caution. Because formulas that stop bleeding
may leave stasis behind, ingredients that circulate blood are often
combined to facilitate the effect in stopping hemorrhage but prevent
blood stagnation. For bleeding due to stagnant blood, the treatment
strategies are to resolve stasis and stop bleeding.

**12.1 Formulas that Circulate Blood and Resolve Stasis**

Formulas that circulate blood and resolve stasis are used for blood
stasis. *Tao Ren* (Semen Persicae), *Hong Hua* (Flos Carthami), *Chuan
Xiong* (Rhizoma Chuanxiong), *Chi Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Rubra) and *Dan
Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Salviae Miltiorrhizae) are often used in the
formula to circulate blood and resolve stasis; *Zhi Shi* (Fructus
Aurantii Immaturus) and *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi) are also used in the
formula to regulate qi. Alternatively, *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma
Rhei) and *Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas) are used to clear heat and resolve
stasis; *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae
Sinensis) are used to supplement qi and nourish blood. Classic formulas
include *Tao He Cheng Qi Tang* (Peach Kernel Qi-Guiding Decoction), *Xue
Fu Zhu Yu Tang* (Blood Stasis Expelling Decoction), *Bu Yang Huan Wu
Tang* (Yang-Supplementing and Five-Returning Decoction), *Fu Yuan Huo
Xue Tang* (Original Qi-Restoring and Blood-Moving Decoction), *Wen Jing
Tang* (Meridian-Warming Decoction), *Sheng Hua Tang* (Engendering and
Transforming Decoction), *Shi Xiao San* (Sudden Smile Powder), *Dan Shen
Yin* (Salvia Beverage), *Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wa*n (Cinnamon Twig and Poria
Pill) and *Bie Jia Jian Wan* (Turtle Shell Decocted Pill).

### ***Tao He Cheng Qi Tang*** {#tao-he-cheng-qi-tang .unnumbered}

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarchs       桃仁     *táo rén* 12g   Semen Persicae
  -------------- -------- --------- ----- -----------------------------------
                 大黄     *dà       12g   Radix et Rhizoma Rhei
                          huáng*          

  Ministers      芒硝     *máng     6g    Natrii Sulfas
                          xiāo*           

                 桂枝     *guì zhī* 6g    Ramulus Cinnamomi

  Assistant &    炙甘草   *zhì gān  6g    Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
  Envoy                   căo*            Praeparata cum Melle
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Tao Ren* is bitter and sweet in taste and neutral in property
and acts to break blood and resolve stasis; *Da Huang* clears heat and
promotes bowel movements.

Ministers: *Mang Xiao* is salty and bitter in taste and cold in property
and acts to clear heat, soft masses and assist *Da Huang* to clear heat;
*Gui Zhi* is pungent and sweet in taste and warm in property and assists
*Tao Ren* to circulate blood and resolve stasis, and at the same time,
prevent the cold/cool property of *Mang Xiao* and *Da Huang*.

Assistant & envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* protects the stomach and moderates the
strong potency of other ingredients.

This formula resolves stasis, clears heat, circulates blood and at the
same time, prevents cold/cool.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water except for *Mang
Xiao* (mix with water and drink)

**\[Functions\]** Breaks blood and resolves stasis

**\[Indications\]** Blood accumulating in the lower *jiao*: Acute lower
abdominal pain, Normal urination, fever at night, maniac behaviors, a
deep red tongue and a deep hesitant pulse. It can also be used for
amenorrhea and dysmenorrhea.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for stasis-heat
accumulating in the lower *jiao*. Stasis-heat in the lower *jiao* may
cause acute lower abdominal pain. Patients have Normal urination but
fever at night because stasis heat in blood phase does not impair the qi
transformation of the urinary bladder. The heart dominates blood and
blood vessels and stores mind. Stasis-heat disturbing the heart mind may
cause maniac behaviors. Blood stasis in the uterus may cause
dysmenorrhea or amenorrhea. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
break blood, resolve stasis and clear heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Acute lower abdominal pain,
Normal urination, a deep excessive or hesitant pulse.

2\. Modification*s*

-   For pain due to stagnant blood from traumatic injuries, combine with
    *Chi Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Rubra)*, Dang Gui Wei* (Radix Angelicae
    Sinensis) and *Hong Hua* (Flos Carthami) to circulate blood, resolve
    stasis and alleviate pain.

-   For amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea or retention of lochia, combine with
    *Si Wu Tang* (Four Substances Decoction).

-   For complication of qi stagnation, combine with *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma
    Cyperi), *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae) and *Mu Xiang* (Radix
    Aucklandiae) to regulate qi and alleviate pain.

3\. Modern use: Acute pelvic inflammation, retention of placenta,
adnexitis, intestinal obstruction, endometriosis and acute cerebral
hemorrhage that can be diagnosed as stasis-heat accumulating in the
lower *jiao*.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula breaks blood and
resolves stasis, it's contraindicated for pregnant women, the elderly
and people with a weak constitution.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Da Huang Zhe Chong Wan* (Rhubarb and Eupolyphaga
Pill) from *Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

\[Ingredients\] *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei), *Huang Qin* (Radix
Scutellariae), *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), *Tao Ren*
(Semen Persicae), *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum), *Shao Yao*
(Radix Paeoniae), *Gan Di Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae Recens), *Gan Qi*
(Resina Toxicodendri), *Mang Chon*g (Tabanus), *Shui Zhi* (Hirudo), *Qi
Cao* (Holotrichia) and *Zhe Chong* (Eupolyphaga seu Steleophaga)

\[Functions\] Resolves stasis and regenerates new blood

\[Indications\] Extreme deficiency: Emaciation, abdominal fullness, loss
of appetite, dry rough and scaly skin, and a dull, dark appearance of
the eyes.

Both *Da Huang Zhe Chong Wan* and *Tao He Cheng Qi Tang* are used for
retention of stagnant blood. The former is indicated for extreme
deficiency, emaciation and dry rough scaly skin. It has strong effect of
breaking stasis and regenerating new blood by adding *Shui Zhi*, *Mang
Chong*, *Di Huang* and *Shao Yao*. The latter is indicated for acute
lower abdominal pain, fever at night and amenorrhea due to stasis-heat
accumulating in the lower *jiao*. *Gui Zhi* is combined to warm and
unblock blood vessels.

### ***Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang***  {#xue-fu-zhu-yu-tang .unnumbered}

*Yi Lin Gai Cuo* (Correction of Errors in Medical Works)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Mo     | > 桃仁 | *táo    | 12g | Semen Persicae                   |
| narchs |        | rén*    |     |                                  |
+========+========+=========+=====+==================================+
|        | > 红花 | *hóng   | 9g  | Flos Carthami                    |
|        |        | huā*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Min    | > 赤芍 | *chì    | 6g  | Radix Paeoniae Rubra             |
| isters |        | sháo*   |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 川芎 | *chuān  | 4   | Rhizoma Chuanxiong               |
|        |        | xiōng*  | .5g |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 牛膝 | *niú    | 9g  | Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae     |
|        |        | xī*     |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 生地 | *shēng  | 9g  | Radix Rehmanniae                 |
| stants |        | dì*     |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 当归 | *dāng   | 9g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
|        |        | guī*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 桔梗 | *jié    | 4   | Radix Platycodonis               |
|        |        | gĕng*   | .5g |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 枳壳 | *zhĭ    | 6g  | Fructus Aurantii                 |
|        |        | qiào*   |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 柴胡 | *chái   | 3g  | Radix Bupleuri                   |
|        |        | hú*     |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān    | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
|        |        | căo*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Tao Ren* breaks blood, resolves stasis and moistens dryness;
*Hong Hua* circulates blood, resolves stasis and alleviates pain.

Ministers: *Chi Shao* and *Chuan Xiong* circulate blood and resolve
stasis; *Niu Xi* circulates blood, unblocks meridians and induces blood
to flow downward, promotes diuresis and resolves stasis.

Assistants: *Sheng Di* and *Dang Gui* nourish yin and blood and protect
yin/blood from damage by stasis-resolving ingredients; *Jie Geng*
(ascends) and *Zhi Qiao* (descends) regulate qi activities in the chest
and *Jie Geng* also ascends other ingredients; *Chai Hu* soothes the
liver and relieves qi stagnation.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula is characterized by the combination of 1) invigorating
blood and circulating qi; 2) resolving stasis and nourishing blood; and
3) ascending and descending to harmonize qi and blood.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Circulates blood, resolves stasis, moves qi and
alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Blood stasis in the chest: Persistent stabbing chest
pain or headache with a fixed location, chronic hiccoughs, mild or
severe palpitations, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, restlessness,
irritability, tidal fever in the evening, dark lips, a dark appearance
of the eyes, a deep red tongue or a tongue with petechiae or ecchymosis
and a wiry or wiry tense pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for blood stasis
in the chest. Chest and hypochondriac region are on the pathways of
liver meridian. Blood stasis may obstruct the flow of qi and result in a
stabbing chest pain. Stagnant blood disturbing the head may cause a
stabbing headache with a fixed location. Stasis heat affecting the
diaphragm may cause hiccoughs. Stasis heat disturbing the heart may
cause mild or severe palpitations, insomnia and dream-disturbed sleep.
Blood stasis in the chest and liver qi stagnation may cause restlessness
and irritability. Dark lips, a dark appearance of the eyes, a deep red
tongue and a wiry pulse are all typical signs indicative of blood
stasis. As a result, the treatment strategies are to circulate blood,
resolve stasis, move qi and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chest pain or headache with a
fixed location, a deep red tongue or a tongue with ecchymosis and a wiry
or wiry tense pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For chronic pain entering the collaterals, combine with *Chuan Shan
    Jia* (Squama Manitis), *Di Long* (Pheretima) and *San Leng* (Rhizoma
    Sparganii) to break blood, unblock collaterals and alleviate pain.

-   For severe qi stagnation, combine with *Chuan Lian Zi* (Fructus
    Toosendan), *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi) and *Qing Pi* (Pericarpium
    Citri Reticulatae Viride) to soothe the liver, regulate qi and
    alleviate pain.

-   For amenorrhea and dysmenorrhea, combine with *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma
    Cyperi), *Yi Mu Cao* (Herba Leonuri) and *Ze Lan* (Herba Lycopi) to
    circulate blood and regulate menstruation.

3\. Modern use: Angina pectoris due to coronary artery disease,
rheumatic heart disease, chest pain due to physical injury to the chest
or costochondritis and headache or dizziness due to cerebral thrombosis,
hypertension, hyperlipidemia, thromboangitis obliterans, neurosis and
post-concussion syndrome that can be diagnosed as blood stasis
obstructing the flow of qi in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since many ingredients in this formula
break blood and resolve stasis, it's only indicated for patients with
blood stasis. It's contraindicated for pregnant women.

**\[Annex formula\]**

Like *Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang*, the above formulas all contain *Tao Ren*,
*Hong Hua*, *Chuan Xiong*, *Chi Shao* and *Dang Gui* and are indicated
for conditions related to blood stasis. Specifically, *Xue Fu Zhu Yu
Tang* is indicated for blood stasis in the chest---*Zhi Qiao*, *Jie
Gen*g, *Chai Hu* and *Niu Xi* are combined in to relieve qi stagnation
and induce blood to flow downward; *Tong Qiao Huo Xue Tang* is indicated
for blood stasis in the head and face---*She Xiang* and *Lao Cong* are
combined to circulate blood and unblock the orifices; *Ge Xia Zhu Yu
Tang* is indicated for blood stasis below the diaphragm (pain in the rib
side and abdominal pain with masses due to liver qi stagnation)---*Xiang
Fu*, *Wu Yao* and *Zhi Qiao* are combined to circulate qi and alleviate
pain; Shao Fu Zhu Yu Tang is indicated for abdominal masses, irregular
menstruation and dysmenorrhea due to blood stasis in the lower
abdomen---*Xiao Hui Xiang*, *Gui Zhi* and *Gan Jiang* are combined to
warm meridians and alleviate pain; and *Shen Tong Zhu Yu Tang* is
indicated for pain in the limbs or joints due to stagnant blood or
wind-cold-dampness obstructing meridians---*Qin* *Jiao*, *Qiang Huo* and
*Di Long* are combined to circulate blood, dispel wind, unblock
meridians and alleviate pain.

### ***Bu Yang Huang Wu Tang*** {#bu-yang-huang-wu-tang .unnumbered}

*Yi Lin Gai Cuo* (Correction of Errors in Medical Works)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| M      | > 黄芪 | *huáng  | 1   | Radix Astragali                  |
| onarch |        | qí*     | 20g |                                  |
+========+========+=========+=====+==================================+
| Mi     | > 当归 | *dāng   | 6g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis         |
| nister |        | guī*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 赤芍 | *chì    | 5g  | Radix Paeoniae Rubra             |
| stants |        | sháo*   |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 川芎 | *chuān  | 3g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong               |
|        |        | xiōng*  |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 桃仁 | *táo    | 3g  | Semen Persicae                   |
|        |        | rén*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 红花 | *hóng   | 3g  | Flos Carthami                    |
|        |        | huā*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 地龙 | *dì     | 3g  | Pheretima                        |
|        |        | lóng*   |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Huang Qi* is used to reinforce yuan-primordial
qi, circulate blood, resolve stasis and unblock meridians.

Minister: *Dang Gui* circulates blood and resolves stasis (without
damaging blood).

Assistants: *Chi Shao*, *Chuan Xiong*, *Tao Ren* and *Hong Hua* assist
*Dang Gui* to circulate blood and resolve stasis; *Di Long* unblocks
meridians.

This formula uses a large dose of qi-reinforcing herb, small doses of
blood-circulating ingredients and worms/insects to unblock meridians. It
reinforces qi without causing stagnation and circulates blood without
damaging anti-pathogenic qi.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Reinforces qi, circulates blood and unblocks meridians

**\[Indications\]** Wind stroke due to qi deficiency and blood stasis:
Hemiplegia, deviation of the eye and mouth, slurred speech, salivation
(drooling), frequent, urgent urination or urinary incontinence, a dark
tongue with a white coating and a slow weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for sequelae of
wind stroke due to qi deficiency and blood stasis. Qi failing to
circulate blood may cause malnourishment of muscles or tendons,
resulting in hemiplegia and deviation of the eye and mouth.
Malnourishment of the tongue may cause slurred speech. Qi failing to
hold body fluids may cause salivation (drooling), frequent or urgent
urination and urinary incontinence. A dark tongue with a white coating
and a slow weak pulse are typical signs indicative of qi deficiency and
blood stasis. As a result, the treatment strategies are to reinforce qi,
circulate blood and unblock meridians.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Hemiplegia, deviation of the eye
and mouth, a dark tongue with a white coating and a slow weak pulse.

2\. Modifications: Increase the dose of blood-circulating ingredients
appropriately in clinical practice.

-   For predominance of cold, combine with *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus Cinnamomi)
    to warm meridians and dissipate cold.

-   For weakness of the spleen and stomach, combine with *Dang Shen*
    (Radix Codonopsis) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis
    Macrocephalae) to reinforce qi and strengthen the spleen.

-   For profuse phlegm, combine with *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) and
    *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) to resolve phlegm.

-   For slurred speech, combine with *Shi Chang Pu* (Rhizoma Acori
    Tatarinowii), *Yuan Zhi* (Radix Polygalae) and *Yu Jin* (Radix
    Curcumae) to resolve phlegm and open the orifices.

3\. Modern use: Sequelae of cerebrovascular accidents, coronary heart
disease, sequelae of Polio, hemiplegia, paraplegia or flaccidity of
unilateral upper/lower limb that can be diagnosed as qi deficiency and
blood stasis in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients need to take this formula for
some time to obtain marked effect and continued to take after recovery
to consolidate the effect and prevent relapse. It's worth noting that a
large dose of *Huang Qi* is needed (start from 30-60g and gradually
increase the dose in case of insignificant effect).

It's contraindicated for stroke (hemiplegia, a red tongue with a yellow
coating and a surging large pulse) due to yin deficiency-induced yang
hyperactivity and stasis-phlegm obstructing meridians.

### ***Fu Yuan Huo Xue Tang*** {#fu-yuan-huo-xue-tang .unnumbered}

*Yi Xue Fa Ming* (New Medical Perspectives)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Mo     | >      | *dà       | 30g | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei          |
| narchs | 大黄\* | huáng*    |     |                                |
+========+========+===========+=====+================================+
|        | > 柴胡 | *chái hú* | 15g | Radix Bupleuri                 |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Min    | >      | *táo rén* | 12g | Semen Persicae                 |
| isters | 桃仁\* |           |     |                                |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|        | > 红花 | *hóng     | 6g  | Flos Carthami                  |
|        |        | huā*      |     |                                |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|        | >      | *chuān    | 6g  | Squama Manitis                 |
|        | 穿山甲 | shān jiă* |     |                                |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 当归 | *dāng     | 9g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis       |
| stants |        | guī*      |     |                                |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|        | >      | *guā lóu  | 9g  | Radix Trichosanthis            |
|        | 瓜蒌根 | gēn*      |     |                                |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Envoy  | > 甘草 | *gān căo* | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae  |
+--------+--------+-----------+-----+--------------------------------+

Note: \* *Da Huan*g and *Tao Ren* are soaked in liquor

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: A large dose of liquor-soaked *Da Huang* is used to resolve
stasis and break blood; *Chai Hu* soothes liver qi and guides other
ingredients to enter liver meridian. The two herbs (one ascending and
one descending) work together to resolve stasis in the rib side.

Ministers: *Tao Ren* and *Hong Hua* circulate blood, resolve stasis,
reduce swelling and alleviate pain; *Chuan Shan Jia* resolves stasis,
unblocks meridians and reduces swelling.

Assistants: *Dang Gui* nourishes and circulates blood; *Gua Lou Gen*
(also known as *Tian Hua Fen*) clears heat, reduces swelling and
alleviates pain. According to *Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing* (Shen Nong's
Classic of the Materia Medica), *Gua Lou Gen* is used for traumatic
injuries or fracture; and according to *Ri Hua Zi Ben Cao* (Ri Hua-zi's
Materia Medica), *Gua Lou Gen* is used for retention of stagnant blood
after traumatic injuries.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula is characterized by 1) regulating qi activities by using
ingredients that ascend and descend; and 2) circulating blood without
consuming yin blood.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water and drink with an
empty stomach (adjust the dose according to the proportions of the
original formula)

**\[Functions\]** Circulates blood, resolves stasis, soothes the liver
and unblocks meridians

**\[Indications\]** Swelling and pain in the rib side due to retention
of stagnant blood after traumatic injuries

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for retention of
stagnant blood following traumatic injuries. Retention of stagnant blood
after traumatic injuries may obstruct the flow of qi and result in
swelling and pain (can be intolerable) in the rib side. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to circulate blood, resolve stasis, soothe the
liver and unblock meridians.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Swelling and pain in the rib side
following traumatic injuries.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe stasis and pain, combine with *San Qi* (Radix et Rhizoma
    Notoginseng), *Ru Xiang* (Olibanum), *Mo Yao* (Myrrha) and *Yuan Hu*
    (Rhizoma Corydalis) to circulate blood, resolve stasis, reduce
    swelling and alleviate pain.

-   For severe qi stagnation and pain, combine with *Chuan Xiong*
    (Rhizoma Chuanxiong), *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi), *Yu Jin* (Radix
    Curcumae) and *Qing Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae Viride) to
    circulate qi and alleviate pain.

3\. Modern use: Physical trauma to the chest and rib side, intercostal
neuralgia, costochondritis and mammary gland hyperplasia that can be
diagnosed as retention of stagnant blood.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula helps with bowel
movements. It's contraindicated for pregnant women. The elderly and
patients with a weak constitution should take this formula with caution.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Qi Li San* (Stasis-Resolving and Pain-Alleviating
Powder) from the *Tong Shou Lu* (Longevity Formulas by Xiang Tian-rui)

\[Ingredients\] *Zhu Sha* (Cinnabaris), *She Xiang* (Moschus), *Mei Hua*
(Flos Mume), *Bing Pian* (Borneolum Syntheticum), *Ru Xiang* (Olibanum),
*Hong Hua* (Flos Carthami), *Mo Yao* (Myrrha), *Xue Jie* (Sanguis
Draconis) and *Er Cha* (Catechu)

\[Functions\] Resolves stasis, reduces swelling, alleviates pain and
stops bleeding

\[Indications\] Swelling and pain due to traumatic injuries, fracture or
knife wounds, unexplained swelling, burns or scalds. Patients with mild
conditions can apply the powder externally.

Both *Fu Yuan Huo Xue Tang* and *Qi Li San* can treat swelling and pain
due to blood stasis and qi stagnation following traumatic injuries. The
former circulates blood, resolves stasis, soothes the liver and unblocks
meridians and is indicated for intolerable pain due to retention of
stagnant blood in rib side. The latter circulates blood, resolves
stasis, stops bleeding and regenerates new tissues and is indicated for
swelling and pain due to traumatic injuries or knife wounds. It can be
taken internally or applied externally.

### ***Wen Jing Tang***  {#wen-jing-tang .unnumbered}

*Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
| Monarchs     | >       | *wú zhū  | 9g  | Fructus Evodiae           |
|              |  吴茱萸 | yú*      |     |                           |
+==============+=========+==========+=====+===========================+
|              | > 桂枝  | *guì     | 6g  | Ramulus Cinnamomi         |
|              |         | zhī*     |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
| Ministers    | > 当归  | *dāng    | 6g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis  |
|              |         | guī*     |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | > 川芎  | *chuān   | 6g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong        |
|              |         | xiōng*   |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | >       | *mŭ dān  | 6g  | Cortex Moutan             |
|              |  牡丹皮 | pí*      |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
| Assistants   | > 阿胶  | *ē jiāo* | 6g  | Colla Corii Asini         |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | > 白芍  | *bái     | 6g  | Radix Paeoniae Alba       |
|              |         | sháo*    |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | > 麦冬  | *mài     | 15g | Radix Ophiopogonis        |
|              |         | dōng*    |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | > 人参  | *rén     | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng  |
|              |         | shēn*    |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | > 半夏  | *bàn     | 9g  | Rhizoma Pinelliae         |
|              |         | xià*     |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
|              | > 生姜  | *shēng   | 6g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens |
|              |         | jiāng*   |     |                           |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+
| Assistant &  | > 甘草  | *gān     | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma          |
| envoy        |         | căo*     |     | Glycyrrhizae              |
+--------------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Wu Zhu Yu* and *Gui Zhi* warm meridians, dissipate cold, and
circulate blood.

Ministers: *Dang Gui* and *Chuan Xiong* nourish blood, resolve stasis
and regulate menstruation; *Mu Dan Pi* cools blood, clears heat and
resolves stasis.

Assistants: *E* *Jiao* nourishes blood and stops bleeding; *Bai Shao*
nourishes blood and softens the liver; *Mai Dong* nourishes yin and
clears heat; *Ren Shen* and *Gan Cao* supplement qi and strengthen the
spleen; *Ban Xia* and *Sheng Jiang* resolve masses, down-regulate
stomach qi, resolve stasis and regulate menstruation.

Envoy: Gan Cao coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula warms meridians, resolves stasis, soothes the liver and
strengthens the spleen.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water and melt *E* *Jiao*.

**\[Functions\]** Warms meridians, dissipates cold, nourishes blood and
resolves stasis.

**\[Indications\]** Blood stasis due to deficiency cold of the *Chong*
and *Ren* meridians: Persistent dysfunctional uterine bleeding with dark
blood clots, earlier, delayed or prolonged periods, more than 1 period a
month, amenorrhea, acute lower abdominal pain, abdominal fullness, fever
in late afternoon, feverish sensations in the palms, dry lips and mouth,
a dark red tongue and a thready hesitant pulse; it can also be used for
infertility due to cold retention in the uterus.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula to warm
meridians, nourish blood, resolve stasis and regulate menstruation.
Deficiency cold of the *Chong* and *Ren* meridians may cause stagnation
of qi and blood, resulting in acute lower abdominal pain and abdominal
fullness. Stagnant blood may disturb the flow of blood and result in
irregular menstruation, amenorrhea, or dysfunctional uterine bleeding.
Stasis due to deficiency cold may affect conception and result in
infertility. Stagnant blood may affect the generation of new blood,
causing dry lips and mouth. Fever in late afternoon and feverish
sensations in the palms are typical signs indicative of deficiency heat
due to consumption of yin blood. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to warm meridians, dissipate cold, resolve stasis, nourish blood,
and clear deficiency heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Irregular menstruation, cold pain
in the lower abdomen, menstruation containing blood clots, feverish
sensations, a dark red tongue and a thready hesitant pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe cold pain in the lower abdomen, remove *Mou Dan Pi* and
    *Mai Dong* and combine with *Pao Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis
    Praeparatum) and *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi) to dissipate cold and
    alleviate pain.

-   For qi stagnation due to cold retention, combine with *Xiang Fu*
    (Rhizoma Cyperi) and *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae) to regulate qi and
    alleviate pain.

-   For persistent uterine bleeding with pale-colored blood, combine
    with *Pao Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Praeparatum) and *Ai Ye*
    (Folium Artemisiae Argyi) to regulate menstruation and stop
    bleeding.

-   For severe qi deficiency, combine with *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali)
    and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae) to supplement qi
    and strengthen the spleen.

3\. Modern use: Dysfunctional uterine bleeding, chronic pelvic
inflammation, dysmenorrhea and infertility that can be diagnosed as
retention of stagnation blood due to deficiency cold of the *Chong* and
*Ren* meridians in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for irregular
menstruation due to excess heat or absence of internal stagnant blood.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Ai Fu Nuan Gong Wan* (Mugwort and Cyperus Palace
Warming Pill) from the *Shen Zhai Zhi Zhi* (\[Yang\] Ren-zhai's Direct
Guidance on Formulas)

\[Ingredients\] *Ai Ye* (Folium Artemisiae Argyi), *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma
Cyperi), *Wu Zhu Yu* (Fructus Evodiae), *Chuan Xiong* (Rhizoma
Chuanxiong), *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba), *Huang Qi* (Radix
Astragali), *Xu Duan* (Radix Dipsaci), *Sheng Di Huang* (Radix
Rehmanniae), *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus Cinnamomi) and *Dang Gui* (Radix
Angelicae Sinensis)

\[Functions\] Warms the uterus and meridians, nourishes and circulates
blood

\[Indications\] Deficiency cold of the uterus, profuse white leucorrhea,
a sallow complexion, pain in four limbs, lassitude, a poor appetite,
irregular menstruation, intermittent abdominal pain and infertility

Both *Wen Jing Tang* and *Ai Fu Nuan Gong Tang* are used for retention
of stagnant blood due to deficiency cold of the Chong and Ren meridians.
The former is better in nourishing blood and reinforcing deficiency. The
latter is better in warming meridians and dissipating cold.

### ***Sheng Hua Tang*** {#sheng-hua-tang .unnumbered}

*Fu Qing Zhu Nǚ Ke* (Fu Qing-zhu's \[Treatise on\] Gynecology)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch     当归     *dāng guī* 24g    Radix Angelicae Sinensis
  ----------- -------- ---------- ------ -------------------------------------
  Ministers   川芎     *chuān     9      Rhizoma Chuanxiong
                       xiōng*            

              桃仁     *táo rén*  6g     Semen Persicae

  Assistant   炮姜     *páo       2g     Rhizoma Zingiberis Praeparatum
                       jiāng*            

  Envoy       炙甘草   *zhì gān   6g     Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
                       căo*              Praeparata cum Melle
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large of *Dang Gui* is used to nourish and circulate blood,
resolve stasis, unblock meridians and alleviate pain.

Ministers: *Chuan Xiong* is pungent and dispersing and acts to warm and
unblock meridians, and circulate qi and blood; a small dose of *Tao Ren*
is used to circulate blood and resolve stasis.

Assistant: *Pao Jiang* enters blood to dissipate cold, warm meridians
and alleviate pain. Rice wine warms blood and helps to bring about the
medicinal potency.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* harmonizes the spleen and stomach and coordinates
the actions of other ingredients.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water or add some rice
wine.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates and nourishes blood, warms meridians and
alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Blood stasis due to blood deficiency and cold
retention: Retention of lochia after childbirth, cold pain in the lower
abdomen, a pale tongue with a white coating and a deep wiry or wiry
tense pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for abdominal
pain after childbirth due to cold retention and blood stasis. Blood
deficiency after delivery coupled with external contraction of cold may
affect the uterus and result in retention of lochia. Stasis and cold
affecting the uterus may cause cold pain in the lower abdomen. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to circulate and nourish blood,
warm meridians and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Retention of lochia after
childbirth and cold pain in the lower abdomen.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe stasis and pain, combine with *Pu Huang* (Pollen Typhae),
    *Yi Mu Cao* (Herba Leonuri) and *Yuan Hu* (Rhizoma Corydalis) to
    resolve stasis and alleviate pain.

-   For severe cold pain in the lower abdomen, combine with *Rou Gui*
    (Cortex Cinnamomi) to warm meridians and dissipate cold.

-   For a cold sensation in the abdomen with a stabbing pain, combine
    with *Shan Zha* (Fructus Crataegi), *Yi Mu Cao* (Herba Leonuri) and
    *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus Cinnamomi) to resolve stasis, dissipate cold and
    alleviate pain.

3\. Modern use: Incomplete womb restoration, uterine contraction pain
after delivery and retention of placenta that can be diagnosed as
internal stagnant blood due to blood deficiency and cold retention after
childbirth in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's more indicated for women with
blood deficiency and cold retention and contraindicated for women with
internal stagnant blood due to blood heat.

### ***Shi Xiao San*** {#shi-xiao-san .unnumbered}

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
| Monarch      | >      | *wŭ     | 6g  | Faeces Trogopterori        |
|              | 五灵脂 | líng    |     |                            |
|              |        | zhī*    |     |                            |
+==============+========+=========+=====+============================+
| Minister &   | > 蒲黄 | *pú     | 6g  | Pollen Typhae              |
| assistant    |        | huáng*  |     |                            |
+--------------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Wu Ling Zhi* is bitter, salty and sweet in taste and warm in
property. It enters liver meridian and acts to circulate blood, resolve
stasis and alleviate pain.

Minister & assistant: *Pu Huang* is sweet in taste and neutral in
property. Fried *Pu Huang* can stop bleeding.

*Wu Ling Zhi* and *Pu Huang* are often used in combination to resolve
stasis, eliminate masses and alleviate pain. Vinegar or rice wine can
enhance their effect in circulating blood and alleviating pain, and at
the same time, removing the four smell of *Wu Ling Zhi*.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder for each dose with rice
wine or vinegar; or wrap 6-12g (a dose everyday) to decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates blood, resolves stasis, eliminates masses
and alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Pain due to blood stasis: Stabbing cardiac or
abdominal pain, retention of lochia after childbirth, irregular
menstruation or acute lower abdominal pain.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a basic formula for pain due to
blood stasis. Stasis obstructing the vessels may cause stabbing
cardiac/abdominal pain or acute lower abdominal pain. Stasis obstructing
the uterus may cause irregular menstruation or retention of lochia after
childbirth. As a result, the treatment strategies are to circulate
blood, resolve stasis, eliminate masses and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Stabbing cardiac or abdominal
pain, irregular menstruation and acute lower abdominal pain.

2\. Modifications

-   For complication of blood deficiency, combine with *Si Wu Tang*
    (Four Substances Decoction) to nourish blood and regulate
    menstruation.

-   For severe pain, combine with *Ru Xiang* (Olibanum) and *Mo Yao*
    (Myrrha) to resolve stasis and alleviate pain.

-   For qi stagnation, combine with *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi) and
    *Chuan Lian Zi* (Fructus Toosendan) to circulate qi and alleviate
    pain.

-   For complication of cold, combine with *Pao Jiang* (Rhizoma
    Zingiberis Praeparatum) and *Ai Ye* (Folium Artemisiae Argyi) to
    warm meridians and dissipate cold.

3\. Modern use: Dysmenorrhea, coronary artery disease, hyperlipidemia,
ectopic pregnancy and chronic gastritis that can be diagnosed as
retention of stagnant blood in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for pregnant
women. Those with a weak spleen and stomach and women during period
should take this formula with caution.

### ***Dan Shen Yin***  {#dan-shen-yin .unnumbered}

*Shi Fang Ge Kuo* (Summary Verses of Post-classical Formulas)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| M      | > 丹参 | *dān    | 30g | Radix et Rhizoma Salviae         |
| onarch |        | shēn*   |     | Miltiorrhizae                    |
+========+========+=========+=====+==================================+
| Min    | > 檀香 | *tán    | 4   | Lignum Santali Albi              |
| isters |        | xiāng*  | .5g |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 砂仁 | *shā    | 4   | Fructus Amomi                    |
|        |        | rén*    | .5g |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: A large dose of *Dan Shen* (bitter in taste and slightly cold
in property) is used to circulate blood, resolve stasis and alleviate
pain without damaging qi and blood.

Ministers: *Tan Xiang* is pungent and warm and acts to regulate qi, warm
the spleen and stomach to dissipate cold and alleviate pain; *Sha Ren*
is pungent warm and acts to move qi, warm the spleen and stomach,
resolve dampness and harmonize the stomach.

This formula is characterized by 1) combination of cold- and
warm-property ingredients, especially a large dose of *Dan Shen*; and 2)
combination of qi-moving and blood-circulating ingredients, especially
blood-circulating ingredients.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Circulates blood, resolves stasis, moves qi and
alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Blood stasis and qi stagnation: Stabbing cardiac and
gastric pain, gastric distension and pain with a fixed location,
tenderness, chest tightness, gastric stuffiness and a wiry pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula to circulate qi,
resolve stasis and alleviate pain. Qi stagnation and blood stasis may
obstruct the flow of qi and blood and cause cardiac or gastric pain with
a fixed location. As a result, the treatment strategies are to circulate
blood, resolve stasis, move qi and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cardiac or gastric pain, chest
tightness and gastric stuffiness.

2\. Modifications

-   For stabbing pain due to severe stasis, combine with *Tao Ren*
    (Semen Persicae), *Hong Hua* (Flos Carthami) and *Ru Xiang*
    (Olibanum) to circulate blood and alleviate pain.

-   For severe gastric distension, combine with *Hou Pu* (Cortex
    Magnoliae Officinalis) and *Zhi Qiao* (Fructus Aurantii) to
    circulate qi and relieve stagnation.

-   For severe heat, combine with *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and
    *Shan Zhi* (Fructus Gardeniae) to clear heat and reduce fire.

3\. Modern use: Chronic gastritis, gastric and duodenal ulcers,
gastroneurosis and angina pectoris that can be diagnosed as blood stasis
and qi stagnation in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula circulates blood
and moves qi, women during pregnancy or period should take it with
caution.

### ***Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan***  {#gui-zhi-fu-ling-wan .unnumbered}

*Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| M      | > 桂枝 | *guì    | 6g  | Ramulus Cinnamomi                |
| onarch |        | zhī*    |     |                                  |
+========+========+=========+=====+==================================+
| Mi     | > 桃仁 | *táo    | 6g  | Semen Persicae                   |
| nister |        | rén*    |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
| Assi   | >      | *mŭ dān | 6g  | Cortex Moutan                    |
| stants | 牡丹皮 | pí*     |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 赤芍 | *chì    | 6g  | Radix Paeoniae Rubra             |
|        |        | sháo*   |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+
|        | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 6g  | Poria                            |
|        |        | líng*   |     |                                  |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Gui Zhi* is pungent and sweet in taste and warm in property.
It acts to warm meridians and resolve stasis.

Minister: *Tao Ren* is bitter and sweet in taste and neutral in
property. It acts to circulate blood, resolve stasis and assist Gui Zhi
to resolve masses.

Assistants: *Dan Pi* and *Chi Shao* are bitter in taste and slightly
cold in property. They circulate blood, resolve stasis and at the same
time, cool blood and clear heat transformed from stasis; *Chi Shao* also
alleviates pain; *Fu Ling* is sweet, tasteless and has a neutral
property. It acts to resolve dampness and phlegm, strengthen the spleen,
benefit the stomach and supplement anti-pathogenic qi.

Envoy: Honey is sweet and moist and acts to coordinate the actions of
other ingredients.

This formula was changed into *Cui Sheng Tang* (Labor-Assisting
Decoction) in *Ji Yin Gang Mu* (A Compendium of Female Disorders) for
abdominal pain and low back soreness in pregnant women after amniotic
fluid breaks.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 1 honey bolus (6g) for each dose,
1-2 doses a day; or 3 capsules (0.31g/capsule) for each dose, 3 doses a
day (follow the doctor's instructions); or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates blood, resolves stasis and eliminates
abdominal masses.

**\[Indications\]** Stasis obstructing the uterus: Abdominal masses,
dysfunctional uterine bleeding with dark purple blood, threatened
miscarriage, abdominal pain with tenderness, amenorrhea, retention of
lochia after childbirth, a dark purple tongue or a tongue with
ecchymosis and a deep hesitant pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for abdominal
masses and dysfunctional uterine bleeding. Blood stasis in the uterus
affecting the flow of blood may cause dysfunctional uterine bleeding
with dark purple blood. Stagnant blood and masses disturbing the fetus
may cause threatened miscarriage. Stagnant blood obstructing the flow of
blood may cause amenorrhea, retention of lochia after childbirth and
abdominal pain with tenderness. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to circulate blood, resolve stasis and eliminate masses.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal masses, dysfunctional
uterine bleeding with dark purple blood, abdominal pain with tenderness,
a dark purple tongue or a tongue with ecchymosis and a deep hesitant
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe abdominal pain with tenderness, combine with *Hong Hua*
    (Flos Carthami) and *Yi Mu Cao* (Herba Leonuri) to resolve stasis
    and alleviate pain.

-   For severe dysfunctional uterine bleeding, combine with *E* *Jiao*
    (Colla Corii Asini) and *Qian Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Rubiae) to
    resolve stasis and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Uterine fibroids, endometriosis, ovary cysts, adnexitis
and chronic pelvic inflammation that can be diagnosed as retention of
stagnant blood in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients should take this formula
according to the method of administration and exact dose. Women during
pregnancy or period should take this formula with caution.

### ***Bie Jia Jian Wan*** {#bie-jia-jian-wan .unnumbered}

*Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

|              | 葶苈子 | lì zĭ* |       |                            |

|              |        | fáng*  |       |                            |
+--------------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------+
|              | > 瞿麦 | *qú    | 15g   | Herba Dianthi              |
|              |        | mài*   |       |                            |
+--------------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------+
| Assistant &  | > 柴胡 | *chái  | 45g   | Radix Bupleuri             |
| Envoy        |        | hú*    |       |                            |
+--------------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: Bie Jia softens and disperses masses.

Ministers: *Chi Xiao*, *Da Huang*, *Zhe Chong*, *Qiang Lang*, *Tao Ren*
and *Shu Fu* resolve stasis, break masses and disperse masses.

Assistants: *Chai Hu*, *Huang Qin,* *Bao Shao*, *Gan Jiang* and *Gui
Zhi* soothe the liver and gallbladder, warm the spleen and stomach and
dissipate cold; *Hou Pu*, *She Gan*, Ting Li Zi and *Ban Xia* circulate
qi and resolve phlegm-fluid; *Mu Dan Pi*, *Zi Wei*, *Feng Fang* and
liquor circulate blood, resolve stasis and unblock meridians; *Qu Mai*
and *Shi Wei* promote diuresis to resolve dampness; *Ren Shen* and *E*
*Jiao* reinforce qi, nourish blood and supplement anti-pathogenic qi.

Assistant & envoy: *Chai Hu* guides other ingredients to enter liver
meridian.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 3-6 honey boluses for each dose
with warm water, 2-3 doses a day

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi and blood, resolves dampness and phlegm,
and softens and disperses masses.

**\[Indications\]** Malaria with splenomegaly and abdominal masses:
Chronic malaria with hard masses in the rib side, immobile abdominal
masses, abdominal pain, muscle wasting, a poor appetite, alternate fever
and chills, or amenorrhea.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for malaria with
splenomegaly and abdominal masses. Over time, chronic malaria may impair
anti-pathogenic qi and result in stagnation of qi and blood. This,
coupled with pathogenic cold, heat, dampness and phlegm, may result in
masses in the rib side. Abdominal masses also result from prolonged
period of qi stagnation and blood stasis. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to circulate qi and blood, resolve phlegm and dampness
and soften and disperse masses.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Immobile masses in the rib side,
abdominal pain, muscle wasting, a poor appetite, alternate fever and
chills and amenorrhea.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe qi stagnation, combine with *Zhi Qiao* (Fructus Aurantii)
    and *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) to circulate qi and relieve
    stagnation.

-   For severe cold dampness, combine with *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti
    Lateralis Praeparata) and *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi) to warm
    meridians and dissipate cold.

-   For severe damp heat, combine with *Yin Chen* (Herba Artemisiae
    Scopariae) and *Shan Zhi* (Fructus Gardeniae) to clear heat and
    resolve dampness.

-   For severe ascites, combine with *Fu Ling* (Poria), *Che Qian Zi*
    (Semen Plantaginis) and *Da Fu Pi* (Pericarpium Arecae) to circulate
    qi and promote diuresis.

3\. Modern use: Cirrhosis of the liver, hepatosplenomegaly, liver
cancer, uterine fibroids and ovary cysts that can be diagnosed as
stagnation of qi and blood in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for pregnant
women, the elderly and patients with a weak constitution.

**12.2 Formulas that Stop Bleeding**

Formulas that stop bleeding are indicated for hematemesis, nosebleed,
hemoptysis, blood in stools, blood in urine and dysfunctional uterine
bleeding. Because bleeding can be differentiated into cold, heat,
deficiency and excess and the location of bleeding varies, formulas that
stop bleeding may have different combinations of ingredients. For
bleeding coupled with stasis, ingredients that circulate blood need to
be combined to prevent leaving stasis behind. In addition, it's
important to combine ingredients according to etiological factors. For
example, for blood heat causes the blood to flow recklessly, use *Da Ji*
(Herba Cirsii Japonici), *Xiao Ji* (Herba Cirsii), *Bai Mao Gen*
(Rhizoma Imperatae) and *Huai Hua* (Flos Sophorae) to cool blood and
stop bleeding; for bleeding due to excess heat, use *Da Huang* (Radix et
Rhizoma Rhei) and *Shan Zhi* (Fructus Gardeniae) to clear heat and
reduce fire or *Shui Niu* *Jiao* (Cornu Bubali), *Dan Pi* (Cortex
Moutan) and *Chi Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Rubra) to cool blood and remove
toxins; and for bleeding due to yang deficiency, use *Zao Xin Tu* (Terra
Flava Usta), *Pao Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Praeparatum) and *Ai Ye*
(Folium Artemisiae Argyi) to warm yang and stop bleeding or *Bai Zhu*
(Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae) and *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti
Lateralis Praeparata) to warm yang and strengthen the spleen. Classic
formulas include *Shi Hui San* (Ten Charred Substances Powder), *Ke Xue
Fang* (Hemoptysis-Alleviating Powder), *Huai Hua San* (Flos Sophorae
Powder), *Xiao Ji Yin Zi* (Field Thistle Drink) and *Huang Tu Tang*
(Yellow Earth Decoction).

### ***Shi Hui San*** {#shi-hui-san .unnumbered}

*Shi Yao Shen Shu* (Divine Book of Ten Empirical Formulas for
Deficiency/Consumption-related Hematemesis)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

| narchs |        |         |     |                                   |

|        |        | jì*     |     |                                   |

| isters |        |         |     |                                   |
