|        | 侧柏叶 | yè*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *bái    | 9g  | Rhizoma Imperatae                 |
|        | 白茅根 | máo     |     |                                   |
|        |        | gēn*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *zōng   | 9g  | Petiolus Trachycarpi              |
|        | 棕榈皮 | lǚ pí*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 大黄 | *dà     | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei             |
| stants |        | huáng*  |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 山栀 | *shān   | 9g  | Fructus Gardeniae                 |
|        |        | zhī*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 茜草 | *qiàn   | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rubiae           |
|        |        | căo*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 丹皮 | *dān    | 9g  | Cortex Moutan                     |
|        |        | pí*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Da Ji* and *Xiao Ji* are sweet in taste and cool in property
and act to cool blood, stop bleeding and resolve stasis.

Ministers: *He Ye*, *Ce Bai Ye* and *Bai Mao Gen* cool blood and stop
bleeding; *Zong Lǚ Pi* astringes blood and stops bleeding.

Assistants: *Da Huang* and *Shan Zhi* clear heat, reduce fire and
promote bowel and bladder movements. When used in combination with *Da
Huang*, *Qian Cao* and *Dan Pi* cool blood, stop bleeding, and at the
same time, circulate blood and resolve stasis. Lotus root or radish
juice and pine-soot ink are mixed to enhance the effect of clearing
heat, cooling blood and stopping hemorrhage.

This formula cools blood, clears heat and at the same time, resolves
stasis.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water; or mix the charred
powder with lotus root or radish juice and pine-soot ink, take 9-15g for
each dose.

**\[Functions\]** Cools blood and stops bleeding

**\[Indications\]** Bleeding due to blood heat: Sudden hematemesis
(vomiting of blood), hemoptysis (coughing up blood) and nosebleed with
bright red blood, a red face and lips, restlessness, thirst, dark-yellow
scanty urine, a red tongue and a rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for bleeding of
the upper body. Bleeding of the upper body often results from reckless
blood flow due to ascending of exuberant fire heat. Vomiting of blood
occurs as a result of heat accumulating in the stomach and damaging the
stomach collaterals. Coughing up blood occurs as a result of heat
damaging the lung collaterals. As a result, the treatment strategies are
to cool and astringe blood and stop bleeding.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Bleeding of the upper body with
bright-red blood, a red tongue with a yellow coating and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications: For severe blood heat and ascending of fire, increase
the dosages of *Da Huang* and *Shan Zhi*, combine with *Niu Xi* (Radix
Achyranthis Bidentatae), *Dai Zhe Shi* (Haematitum) and *Shui Niu*
*Jiao* (Cornu Bubali) to induce blood to flow downward, and decoct with
water.

3\. Modern use: Upper gastrointestinal bleeding, bronchiectasis and
hemoptysis due to pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) that can be diagnosed as
heat-induced reckless blood flow in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & and contraindications: This powder can be taken orally or
used externally. It's worth noting that the ingredients need to be burnt
as charcoal with function preserved. It's contraindicated for bleeding
due to deficiency cold.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Si Sheng Wan* (Four Fresh-herb Pill) from the *Fu
Ren Da Quan Liang Fang* (The Complete Compendium of Fine Formulas for
Women)

\[Ingredients\] *Sheng He Ye* (Folium Nelumbinis), *Sheng Ai Ye* (Folium
Artemisiae Argyi), *Sheng Bai Ye* (Cacumen Platycladi) and *Sheng Di
Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae)

\[Functions\] Cools blood and stops bleeding

\[Indications\] Bleeding due to blood heat: Hematemesis and nosebleed
with bright red blood, a dry mouth and throat, a red or deep red tongue
and a wiry rapid pulse

Both *Si Sheng Wan* and *Shi Hui San* can cool blood and stop bleeding
in the upper body due to blood heat. *Shi Hui San* uses charred
ingredients and mainly targets the symptoms. *Si Sheng Wan* uses four
fresh raw ingredients and targets both the root cause and symptoms.

### ***Ke Xue Fang***  {#ke-xue-fang .unnumbered}

Dan Xi Xin Fa (Teachings of \[Zhu\] Dan-xi)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Mo     | > 青黛 | *qīng   | 6g  | Indigo Naturalis                  |
| narchs |        | dài*    |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
|        | > 山栀 | *shān   | 9g  | Fructus Gardeniae                 |
|        |        | zhī*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Min    | >      | *guā    | 9g  | Semen Trichosanthis               |
| isters | 瓜蒌仁 | lóu     |     |                                   |
|        |        | rén*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | >      | *hăi fú | 9g  | Pumex                             |
|        | 海浮石 | shí*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Ass    | > 诃子 | *hē zĭ* | 6g  | Fructus Chebulae                  |
| istant |        |         |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Qing Dai* and *Shan Zi* clear the liver, reduce fire, cool
blood and alleviate restlessness.

Ministers: *Gua Lou Ren* clears heat, resolves phlegm, moistens the lung
and stop coughing; *Hai Fu Shi* clears the lung, reduces fire, soften
masses and resolves phlegm.

*He Zi* clears and astringes the lung, resolves phlegm, and stops
coughing.

Although it does not contain ingredients that specifically stop
bleeding, this formula targets the root cause by clearing heat and
reducing fire to stop the blood from moving recklessly.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 9g honey bolus for each dose; or
decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Clears the liver and lung, cools blood and stops
bleeding

**\[Indications\]** Hemoptysis due to liver fire affecting the lung:
Coughing up thick phlegm with blood, difficult expectoration,
restlessness, irritability, pain in the chest and hypochondriac region,
a bitter mouth and dry throat, rosy cheeks, constipation, a red tongue
with a yellow coating and a wiry rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for hemoptysis
due to liver fire scorching the lung. Liver fire disturbing the
dispersing and descending of lung qi may cause coughing up thick phlegm
with blood and difficult expectoration. Internal liver fire may cause
restlessness, irritability, pain in the chest and hypochondriac region,
a bitter mouth and dry throat, rosy cheeks and constipation. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to clear the liver and lung, cool
blood and stop bleeding.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Coughing up phlegm with blood,
pain in the chest and hypochondriac region, a red tongue with a yellow
coating and a wiry rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe lung heat, combine with *Yu Xing Cao* (Herba Houttuyniae)
    and *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) to clear lung heat.

-   For fire heat damaging yin, combine with *Sha Shen* (Radix
    Adenophorae seu Glehniae) and *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) to
    nourish lung yin.

-   For severe coughing with profuse yellow phlegm, combine with *Chuan
    Bei* (Bulbus Fritillariae Cirrhosae), *Tian Zhu Huang* (Concretio
    Silicea Bambusae) and *Pi Pa Ye* (Folium Eriobotryae) to clear the
    lung, resolve phlegm and stop coughing.

3\. Modern use: Bronchiectasis and hemoptysis due to pulmonary
tuberculosis that can be diagnosed as liver fire affecting the lung.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula contains
ingredients are cold or cool in property, it's contraindicated for
patients with kidney yin deficiency or loose stools due to spleen
deficiency.

### ***Xiao Ji Yin Zi***  {#xiao-ji-yin-zi .unnumbered}

*Ji Sheng Fang* (Formulas to Aid the Living), recorded in *Yu Ji Wei Yi*
(Detailed Explanation of the Jade Pivot)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Mo     | > 小蓟 | *xiăo   | 15g | Herba Cirsii                    |
| narchs |        | jì*     |     |                                 |
+========+========+=========+=====+=================================+
|        | > 生地 | *shēng  | 30g | Radix Rehmanniae                |
|        |        | dì*     |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Min    | > 蒲黄 | *pú     | 6g  | Pollen Typhae                   |
| isters |        | huáng*  |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | > 藕节 | *ŏu     | 9g  | Nodus Nelumbinis Rhizomatis     |
|        |        | jié*    |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 滑石 | *huá    | 15g | Talcum                          |
| stants |        | shí*    |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | >      | *dàn    | 9g  | Herba Lophatheri                |
|        | 淡竹叶 | zhú yè* |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | > 木通 | *mù     | 6g  | Caulis Akebiae                  |
|        |        | tōng*   |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | > 山栀 | *shān   | 9g  | Fructus Gardeniae               |
|        |        | zhī*    |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | > 当归 | *dāng   | 6g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis        |
|        |        | guī*    |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|        | > 甘草 | *gān    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae   |
|        |        | căo*    |     |                                 |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Xiao Ji* clears heat, cools blood, stops bleeding, and
promotes diuresis; *Sheng Di* cools blood, stops bleeding, nourishes
yin, and clears heat.

Ministers: *Pu Huang* and *Ou Jie* cool blood, stop bleeding, and
resolve stasis.

Assistants: *Hua Shi*, *Dan Zhu Ye* and *Mu Tong* clear heat and promote
diuresis; *Shan Zhi* clears fire in upper, middle and lower *jiao*; and
*Dang Gui* nourishes and harmonizes blood and prevents blood stagnation
from cold/cool-property ingredients.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* alleviates pain and coordinates the actions of other
ingredients.

This formula stops bleeding and at the same time, resolves stasis; and
clears heat, at the same time, nourishes yin.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Cools blood, stops bleeding and promoted diuresis

**\[Indications\]** Blood stranguria or blood in the urine due to heat
accumulating in the lower *jiao*: Bloody urine, frequent and urgent
urination, hot, painful and hesitant urination, a red tongue with a
yellow coating and a slippery rapid and forceful pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a classic formula for blood
stranguria or blood in the urine due to heat accumulating in the urinary
bladder. Heat accumulating in the urinary bladder may damage blood
collaterals and result in blood in urine. Stasis and heat affecting the
qi transformation of the urinary bladder may cause frequent and urgent
urination with a hot, painful and hesitant sensation. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to cool blood, stop bleeding and promote
diuresis.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Blood in the urine, hot, painful
and hesitant urination, a red tongue and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a stabbing pain during urination, combine with *Hai Jin Sha*
    (Spora Lygodii) and *Hu Po* (Succinum) powder to promote diuresis,
    resolve stasis and alleviate pain.

-   For damage to qi and yin from chronic blood stranguria, remove
    cold-property ingredients such as *Mu Tong* and *Hua Shi* and
    combine with *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *E* *Jiao* (Colla
    Corii Asini) to reinforce qi and nourish yin.

3\. Modern use: Acute urinary infection, urinary stone and renal
tuberculosis that can be diagnosed as stagnant heat in the urinary
bladder in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since most ingredients in this formula
are cold/cool in property, it's indicated for excess heat syndrome only.
Patients with chronic blood stranguria or bloody urine coupled with fire
stirring due to yin deficiency or qi deficiency with normal urination
should not take this formula alone.

### ***Huai Hua San*** {#huai-hua-san .unnumbered}

*Pu Ji Ben Shi Fang* (Experiential Formulas for Universal Relief)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
| Monarch       | > 槐花 | *huái   | 9g  | Flos Sophorae              |
|               |        | huā*    |     |                            |
+===============+========+=========+=====+============================+
| Minister      | >      | *cè băi | 9g  | Cacumen Platycladi         |
|               | 侧柏叶 | yè*     |     |                            |
+---------------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
| Assistant &   | >      | *jīng   | 9g  | Spica Schizonepetae        |
| Envoy         | 荆芥穗 | jiè     |     |                            |
|               |        | suì*    |     |                            |
+---------------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------+
|               | > 枳壳 | *zhĭ    | 9g  | Fructus Aurantii           |
|               |        | qiào*   |     |                            |
+---------------+--------+---------+-----+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Huai Hua* resolves dampness and clears heat in the large
intestine, cools blood and stops bleeding.

Minister: *Ce Bai Ye* clears heat, stops bleeding and enhance the effect
of *Huai Hua* in cooling blood and stopping hemorrhage.

Assistant & Envoy: *Jing Jie Sui* is pungent and slightly warm in
property (but not dry) and acts to dispel wind and regulate blood; and
*Zhi Qiao* circulates qi.

This formula stops bleeding, and at the same time circulates qi; clears
heat in the large intestine, and at the same time, dispels wind.

**\[Method of administration\]** take 6g powder with warm water or rice
soup; or decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat in the large intestine, stops bleeding,
dispels wind and circulates qi

**\[Indications\] H**emorrhoid with bleeding (known as Chang Feng,
literally means intestinal wind) and hematochezia (known as *Zang Du*,
literally means toxins in zang organs): Bleeding before or after bowel
movements, blood in stools, hemorrhoid bleeding, a red tongue with a
yellow coating and a rapid pulse; the blood can be bright or dark red.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for hematochezia
and hemorrhoids with bleeding. Bleeding with clear bright red blood
indicates hematochezia and bleeding with turbid dark red blood indicates
hemorrhoid. Both occur as a result of wind heat or damp heat
accumulating in the large intestine and damaging blood collaterals. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat in the intestine,
cool blood, dispel wind and circulate qi.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Bright red blood in stools, a red
tongue and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe bloody stools, replace *Jing Jie* *Sui* (Spica
    Schizonepetae) with *Jing Jie Tan* (Herba Schizonepetae
    Carbonisatus) and combine with *Huang Qin Tan* (Radix Scutellariae
    Carbonisatus), *Di Yu Tan* (Radix Sanguisorbae Carbonisatus) and
    *Zong Lǚ Tan* (Petiolus Trachycarpi Carbonisatus) to enhance the
    effect of stopping hemorrhage.

-   For severe heat in the large intestine, combine *Huang Qin* (Radix
    Scutellariae) and *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis) to clear heat.

-   For blood deficiency due to chronic hematochezia, combine with *Shu
    Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata) and *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae
    Sinensis) to nourish and harmonize blood.

3\. Modern use: Bloody stools due to hemorrhoids, colitis or other
conditions that can be diagnosed as wind heat or damp heat accumulating
in the large intestine and damaging the collaterals. It can also be used
for bloody stools due to colon cancer.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since ingredients in this formula are
cold or cool in property, this formula should not be taken for a long
period of time. It's contraindicated for chronic hematochezia due to qi
or yin deficiency or a constitutional weakness of the spleen and
stomach.

### ***Huang Tu Tang*** {#huang-tu-tang .unnumbered}

*Jin Gui Yao Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| M      | >      | *zào    | 50g | Terra Flava Usta                  |
| onarch | 灶心土 | xīn tŭ* |     |                                   |
+========+========+=========+=====+===================================+
| Min    | > 白术 | *bái    | 9g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis              |
| isters |        | zhú*    |     | Macrocephalae                     |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 附子 | *fù zĭ* | 9g  | Radix Aconiti Lateralis           |
|        |        |         |     | Praeparata                        |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
| Assi   | > 生地 | *shēng  | 9g  | Radix Rehmanniae                  |
| stants |        | dì*     |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 阿胶 | *ē      | 9g  | Colla Corii Asini                 |
|        |        | jiāo*   |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 黄芩 | *huáng  | 9g  | Radix Scutellariae                |
|        |        | qín*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+
|        | > 甘草 | *gān    | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae     |
|        |        | căo*    |     |                                   |
+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Zao Xin Tu* is pungent, warm and astringent. It warms the
spleen and stomach and stops bleeding.

Ministers: *Bai Zhu* strengthens the spleen; and *Fu Zi* warms yang.

Assistants: *Sheng Di* and *E* *Jiao* nourish yin/blood and stop
bleeding and at the same time, restrain the warm-dry property of *Bai
Zhu* and *Fu Zi*; *Huang Qin* stops bleeding and restrains the warm-heat
property of other ingredients.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients.

This formula warms yang without damaging yin and nourishes yin without
damaging yang.

Both *Huang Tu Tang* and *Gui Pi Tang* can be used for bloody stools and
dysfunctional uterine bleeding due to failure of the spleen to control
blood within the vessels. The former is more indicated for bleeding due
to spleen yang deficiency. The latter is more indicated for bleeding due
to spleen qi deficiency.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct *Zao Xin Tu* with water first
and then decoct other ingredients. Melt *E* *Jiao* and drink with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms yang, strengthens the spleen, nourishes blood
and stops bleeding

**\[Indications\]** Hemorrhage due to yang deficiency: Bleeding after
bowel movements, hematemesis, nosebleed, dysfunctional uterine bleeding
with dark red blood, cold limbs, a sallow complexion, a pale tongue with
a white coating and a deep thready weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for bleeding due
to yang deficiency. The spleen controls blood within the vessels. Spleen
yang failing to hold blood may cause hematemesis, nosebleed,
hematochezia or dysfunctional uterine bleeding. Cold limbs, a dark red
blood color, a sallow complexion, a pale tongue with a white coating and
a deep thready weak pulse are typical signs indicative of spleen yang/qi
deficiency with damage to yin blood. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to warm yang and stop bleeding.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Bleeding with dark red blood, a
pale tongue with a white coating and a deep thready weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

For heavy bleeding, combine with *San Qi* (Radix et Rhizoma Notoginseng)
and *Bai Ji* (Rhizoma Bletillae) to stop bleeding.

-   For severe qi deficiency, combine with *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali)
    and *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) to supplement qi to hold
    blood.

-   For severe deficiency cold of the spleen and stomach, combine with
    *Pao Jiang Tan* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Praeparatum Carbonisatus) to
    warm the spleen and stomach and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Upper gastrointestinal bleeding and dysfunctional
uterine bleeding that can be diagnosed as spleen yang deficiency in
Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for bleeding due
to heat-induced reckless flow of blood.

## Summary {#summary .unnumbered}

There are a total of 15 formulas and 8 annex formulas in this chapter.
In terms of functions, these formulas are categorized into two types:
one to circulate blood to resolve stasis and the other to stop bleeding.

**Circulating blood to resolve stasis**

Formulas that circulate blood and resolve stasis are indicated for blood
stagnation or internal retention of stagnant blood.

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Formula**     **Functions**              **Indications**
  --------------- -------------------------- -----------------------------
  *Tao He Cheng   Breaks blood, resolves     Blood heat accumulating in
  Qi Tang*        stasis and clears heat     the lower *jiao*

  *Xue Fu Zhu Yu  Circulates blood, resolves Chest pain and headache due
  Tang*           stasis, moves qi and       to qi stagnation and blood
                  alleviates pain            stasis

  *Bu Yang Huan   Reinforces qi, circulates  Hemiplegia that results from
  Wu Tang*        blood and unblocks         blood stagnation (due to qi
                  meridian                   deficiency) blocking the
                                             meridians

  *Fu Yuan Huo    Circulates blood, resolves Traumatic injuries and pain
  Xue Tang*       stasis, soothes the liver  in the hypochondriac region
                  and unblocks meridians     

  *Wen Jing Tang* warms meridians,           Irregular menstruation due to
                  dissipates cold, nourishes deficiency cold of the
                  blood and resolves stasis  *Chong* and *Ren* meridians
                                             and internal retention of
                                             stagnant blood

  *Sheng Hua      Circulates blood, resolves lochia retention and lower
  Tang*           stasis, warms meridians    abdominal pain after
                  and alleviates pain        childbirth due to deficiency
                                             cold of blood

  *Shi Xiao San*  Circulates blood, resolves Cardiac and abdominal pain
                  stasis, disperses masses   due to blood stasis
                  and alleviates pain        

  *Gui Zhi Fu     Circulates blood, resolves Abdominal masses, persistent
  Ling Wan*       stasis and disperses       uterine bleeding during
                  masses                     pregnancy and threatened
                                             miscarriage

  *Bie Jia Jian   Circulates qi and blood,   Malaria with splenomegaly,
  Wan*            resolves phlegm and stasis abdominal masses and
                  and softens/disperses      amenorrhea
                  masses                     
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Stopping hemorrhage**

Formulas that stop hemorrhage are indicated for a variety of bleeding.
*Shi Hui San*, *Ke Xue Fang*, *Xiao Ji Yin Zi* and *Huai Hua San* are
all indicated for bleeding due to heat-induced reckless flow of blood.
*Shi Hui San* cools and astringes blood and resolves stasis. It is a
common formula for bleeding in the upper body. *Ke Xue Fang* clears
liver fire, resolves phlegm and stops coughing. It is more indicated for
hemoptysis due to liver fire affecting the lung. *Xiao Ji Yin Zi*
promotes diuresis and is indicated for blood stranguria or blood in the
urine. *Huai Hua San* promotes diuresis, dispels wind and clears heat in
the large intestine ad is indicated for hematochezia and hemorrhoids
with bleeding. *Huang Tu Tang* warms yang and strengthens the spleen to
hold blood and is indicated for bleeding (especially hematochezia and
dysfunctional uterine bleeding) due to spleen yang deficiency.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. Explain the significance of adding qi-regulating ingredients to   |
| formulas that circulate blood and resolve stasis and of adding        |
| blood-circulating ingredients to formulas that stop bleeding.         |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. Describe the similarities and differences of *Tao He Cheng Qi     |
| Tang* and *Da Cheng Qi Tang* in terms of their ingredients, functions |
| and indications.                                                      |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. What are the indications of *Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang*? Analyze its     |
| ingredients combination and role of *Jie Geng* in the formula.        |
|                                                                       |
| 4\. What are the indications of *Bu Yang Huan Wu Tang*? Analyze the   |
| role of *Huang Qi* in the formula.                                    |
|                                                                       |
| 5\. Analyze the role of *Dang Gui* in *Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang*, *Bu Yang  |
| Huan Wu Tang*, *Wen Jing Tang*, *Sheng Hua Tan*g, *Fu Yuan Huo Xue    |
| Tang* and *Xiao Ji Yin Zi*.                                           |
|                                                                       |
| 6\. What are the indications of *Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan*? Analyze the    |
| role of *Gui Zhi* in the formula.                                     |
|                                                                       |
| 7\. Analyze the role and preparation method of *Da Huang* in *Shi Hui |
| San* and *Fu Yuan Huo Xue Tang*.                                      |
|                                                                       |
| 8\. Both *Huai Hua San* and *Huang Tu Tang* can be used for bleeding  |
| in the lower body. Describe their similarities and differences.       |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 13 Formulas that Dispel or Stop Wind**

As the name suggests, formulas that dispel or stop wind are indicated
for external wind or internal wind. Pungent herbs are mainly used to
dispel external wind, whereas herbs that can soothe the liver and
relieve spasm are mainly used to stop internal wind.

Pathogenic wind can be external or internal. When external wind attacks
the surface of the body, meridians, muscles, bones and joints, patients
may present with headache, aversion to wind, skin itching, numb limbs,
muscle pain, and impaired joint flexion and extension. Alternatively,
deviation of the eye and mouth and opisthotonos may be present. Tetanus
due to any break in the skin is also regarded external wind. Internal
wind is associated with dysfunctions of internal organs. Patients may
present with vertigo, tremor, convulsions of the limbs, disuse atrophy,
and slurred speech. Alternately, sudden faint, unconsciousness,
deviation of the mouth and eye and hemiplegia may be present. Major
patterns of internal wind include extreme heat stirring wind, liver yang
transforming into wind and yin deficiency generating wind. The treatment
strategies are to dispel external wind and stop internal wind. As a
result, this chapter focuses on two categories of formula: one to dispel
external wind and the other to stop internal wind.

It's essential to distinguish external wind from internal wind prior to
treatment. For external wind, use pungent herbs and avoid tonic herbs.
For internal wind, avoid pungent herbs. Since external wind can trigger
internal wind and internal wind can associate with external wind,
clinical prescriptions are based on overall evaluation. Patients with
insufficiency of body fluids, yin deficiency or yang hyperactivity
should avoid or use wind-dispelling formulas with caution because of
their warm and dry properties.

**13.1 Formulas that Dispel External Wind**

Formulas that dispel external wind are indicated for wind attacking the
muscles, meridians, tendons, bones and joints. Pungent herbs are mainly
used to dispel external wind. Common ingredients include *Qiang Huo*
(Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii), *Du Huo* (Radix Angelicae Pubescentis),
*Jing Jie* (Herba Schizonepetae), *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae),
*Chuan Xiong* (Rhizoma Chuanxiong), *Bai Zhi* (Radix Angelicae
Dahuricae) and *Bai Fu Zi* (Rhizoma Typhonii), coupled with herbs that
nourish yin/blood, circulate blood, and resolve stasis to restrain the
warm-dry properties of wind-removing herbs. In addition, there is a
Chinese medicine saying goes, 'to treat wind first treat the blood; once
blood moves wind will be dispelled'. Classic formulas include *Chuan
Xiong Cha Tiao San* (Tea-Mix and Chuanxiong Powder), *Da Qin* *Jiao
Tang* (Large Gentian Decoction), *Xiao Feng San* (Wind-Dispersing
Powder), *Qian Zheng San* (Symmetry-Correcting Powder) and *Xiao Huo Luo
Dan* (Minor Channel-Activating Elixir).

### ***Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San*** {#chuan-xiong-cha-tiao-san .unnumbered}

### *Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping Imperial Pharmacy)  {#tai-ping-hui-min-he-ji-ju-fang-beneficial-formulas-from-the-taiping-imperial-pharmacy .unnumbered}

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
| Monarch | >     | *chuān   | 120g | Rhizoma Chuanxiong             |   |
|         |  川芎 | xiōng*   |      |                                |   |
+=========+=======+==========+======+================================+===+
| Mi      | >     | *fáng    | 45g  | Radix Saposhnikoviae           |   |
| nisters |  防风 | fēng*    |      |                                |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
|         | >     | *qiāng   | 60g  | Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii  |   |
|         |  羌活 | huó*     |      |                                |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
|         | >     | *bái     | 60g  | Radix Angelicae Dahuricae      |   |
|         |  白芷 | zhĭ*     |      |                                |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
|         | >     | *xì xīn* | 30g  | Radix et Rhizoma Asari         |   |
|         |  细辛 |          |      |                                |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
|         | >     | *bò hé*  | 120g | Herba Menthae                  |   |
|         |  薄荷 |          |      |                                |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
|         | >     | *jīng    | 120g | Herba Schizonepetae            |   |
|         |  荆芥 | jiè*     |      | (stalk-removed)                |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+
| Envoy   | >     | *gān     | 60g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae  |   |
|         |  甘草 | căo*     |      | (burned)                       |   |
+---------+-------+----------+------+--------------------------------+---+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Chuan Xiong* is fragrant and can ascend to the head and eyes.
Because it acts to dispel wind and circulate blood, *Chuan Xiong* is
known as a 'principal herb for headache involving the Shaoyang and
Jueyin meridians'.

Ministers: *Bo He*, *Qiang Huo*, *Bai Zhi*, *Xi Xin*, *Jing Jie* and
*Fang Feng* are used as the ministers. *Bo He* dispels wind and clears
the head and eyes; *Qiang Huo* dispels wind and alleviate Taiyang
headache; *Bai Zhi* dispels wind and alleviate Yangming headache; *Xi
Xin* dissipates cold and alleviate Shaoyin headache; and *Jing Jie* and
*Fang Feng* are both pungent and can dispel wind.

Tea and *Gan Cao* are used as assistant and envoy. Tea, bitter in taste
and cold in property, can clear the head and eyes and restrain the
warm-dry and dispersing properties of wind-removing herbs. *Gan Cao*
harmonizes the actions of other ingredients and protects qi from
over-consumption.

This formula has three characteristics: 1) combination of
wind-dispelling herbs; 2) combination of pungent-warm and pungent-cold
herbs, including a large dose of *Bo He*; and 3) combination of
ascending (dispersing) herbs and descending tea to dispel wind,
alleviate pain and restrain warm-dry properties of wind-dispelling
herbs.

**\[Method of Administration\]** Take the powder with tea, 6g for each
dose and 2 doses a day.

**\[Functions\]** Dispels wind and alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Headache due to external contraction of wind:
migraine, parietal headache, chills, fever, dizziness, nasal
obstruction, a thin white tongue coating and a superficial pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for headache due
to external contraction of wind. Headache and dizziness occur as a
result of wind affecting the head and eyes and disturbing the clean
yang; struggle between anti-pathogenic qi and pathogenic wind in the
exterior may cause chills and fever; since the lung dominates the skin,
wind attacking the exterior may impair the dispersing of lung qi and
result in nasal obstruction; A thin white coating and a superficial
pulse are typical signs indicative of wind attacking the exterior. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to dispel wind and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: aversion to wind, headache, a
thin white coating and a superficial pulse.

2\. Modification

-   For predominance of wind cold, combine with *Qiang Huo* (Rhizoma et
    Radix Notopterygii) and *Gao Ben* (Rhizoma Ligustici) to remove wind
    and dissipate cold.

-   For predominance of wind heat, combine with *Ju Hua* (Flos
    Chrysanthemi), *Sang Ye* (Folium Mori) and *Sheng Shi Gao* (Gypsum
    Fibrosum) to remove wind and clear heat.

-   For persistent headache, combine with *Quan Xie* (Scorpio), *Jiang
    Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus) and *Wu Gong* (Scolopendra) to remove
    wind and alleviate headache.

3\. Modern use: Migraine, cluster headache or headache due to chronic
rhinitis that can be diagnosed as external wind in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula should be taken in a
small dose and avoid long-term decoction. It is contraindicated for
headache due to qi deficiency, blood deficiency, yin deficiency of the
liver and kidney, hyperactivity of liver yang and internal stirring of
liver wind.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Ju Hua Cha Tiao San* (Tea-Mix and Chrysanthemum
Powder) from the *Yin Hai Jing Wei* (Essentials from the Silver Sea, a
book on ophthalmology)

\[Ingredients\] *Ju Hua* (Flos Chrysanthemi), *Chuan Xiong* (Rhizoma
Chuanxiong), Radix Notopterygii), *Du Huo* (Radix Angelicae
Pubescentis), *Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari), *Gan Cao* (Radix et
Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae), *Bai Zhi*
(Radix Angelicae Dahuricae), *Bo He* (Herba Menthae), *Qiang Huo*
(Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii), *Jiang Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus) and
*Chan Tui* (Periostracum Cicadae).

\[Functions\] Dispels wind, alleviates pain and clears the head and
eyes.

\[Indications\] Headache due to wind heat affecting the head and eyes:
migraine, parietal headache, dizziness, blurred vision and nasal
obstruction with turbid discharges.

\[Note\] *Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San* is commonly used for headache due to
wind cold. *Ju Hua Cha Tiao Sa*n, with addition of *Ju Hua* and *Jiang
Can*, is commonly used for chronic and recurrent headache due to wind
heat.

### ***Da Qin*** ***Jiao Tang***  {#da-qin-jiao-tang .unnumbered}

*Su Wen Bing Ji Qi Yi Bao Ming Ji* (Collection of Writings on
Pathomechanism, Suitability of Qi, and the Safeguarding of Life as
Discussed in the *Basic Questions*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
| Monarch  | > 秦艽  | *qín      | 90g  | Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae |
|          |         | jiāo*     |      |                              |
+==========+=========+===========+======+==============================+
| M        | > 羌活  | *qiāng    | 30g  | Rhizoma et Radix             |
| inisters |         | huó*      |      | Notopterygii                 |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 独活  | *dú huó*  | 60g  | Radix Angelicae Pubescentis  |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 防风  | *fáng     | 30g  | Radix Saposhnikoviae         |
|          |         | fēng*     |      |                              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 白芷  | *bái zhĭ* | 30g  | Radix Angelicae Dahuricae    |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 细辛  | *xì xīn*  | 15g  | Radix et Rhizoma Asari       |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
| As       | > 当归  | *dāng     | 60g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis     |
| sistants |         | guī*      |      |                              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 白芍  | *bái      | 60g  | Radix Paeoniae Alba          |
|          |         | sháo*     |      |                              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 川芎  | *chuān    | 60g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong           |
|          |         | xiōng*    |      |                              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 石膏  | *shí gāo* | 60g  | Gypsum Fibrosum              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 黄芩  | *huáng    | 30g  | Radix Scutellariae           |
|          |         | qín*      |      |                              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 熟地  | *shú dì*  | 30g  | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata  |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 生地  | *shēng    | 30g  | Radix Rehmanniae             |
|          |         | dì*       |      |                              |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 白术  | *bái zhú* | 30g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis         |
|          |         |           |      | Macrocephalae                |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
|          | > 茯苓  | *fú líng* | 30g  | Poria                        |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+
| Envoy    | > 甘草  | *gān căo* | 60g  | Radix et Rhizoma             |
|          |         |           |      | Glycyrrhizae                 |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Qin* *Jiao* acts to dispel wind, clear heat and unblock
meridians.

Ministers: *Qiang Huo*, *Du Huo, Fang Feng, Bai Zhi* and *Xi Xin* are
pungent in taste/smell and warm in property. They are used as the
ministers. Tongue stiffness and an inability to move one's hand/foot
result from failure of blood to nourish tendons.

Assistants: As wind-removing herbs are often dry in property and may
damage yin and blood, *Shu Di*, *Dang Gui*, *Bai Shao* and *Chuan Xiong*
are combined to nourish blood, soften tendons and protect yin and blood
from being damaged. The spleen and stomach are the acquired foundation
and source of qi and blood generation, *Bai Zhu* and *Fu Ling* are
combined to supplement spleen qi and generate qi and blood. Wind is a
yang pathogen and over time, may transform into heat, *Sheng Di*, *Shi
Gao* and *Huang Qin* are therefore combined to clear stagnant heat and
at the same time restrain the pungent-dry property of wind-removing
herbs.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* harmonizes the actions of other ingredients and is used
as an envoy.

In summary, the formula removes wind, clears heat, nourishes blood,
unblocks meridians, and nurtures muscles and tendons.

**\[Method of Administration\]** Decoct with water, 2 doses a day.

**\[Functions\]** Dispels wind, clears heat and nourishes and circulates
blood

**\[Indications\]** Wind stroke of meridians: deviation of the eye and
mouth, tongue stiffness with slurred speech, and an inability to move
one's hand/foot.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for wind stroke
of meridians. Deviation of the eye and mouth occurs as a result of
deficiency of anti-pathogenic qi coupled with external contraction of
wind: muscle flaccidity in affected areas where flows of qi and blood
are obstructed and therefore pulled by muscles with unobstructed flow of
qi and blood. Failure of blood to nourish tendons may cause an inability
to move one's hand/foot and tongue stiffness with slurred speech. Since
wind is characterized by constant moving and rapid changes, it may
affect multiple meridians. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
dispel wind, clear heat and nourish and circulate blood.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Sudden deviation of the eye and
mouth, tongue stiffness and inability to move one's hand/foot.

2\. Modification: For absence of internal heat, remove heat-clearing
*Huang Qin*, *Shi Gao* and *Sheng Di*. According to the original text,
add *Sheng Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens) in rainy or cloudy days;
add *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus) for epigastric stuffiness;
and add *Zhi M*u (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae) in spring and summer.

3\. Modern use: Facial paralysis, cerebral vasospasm and slurred speech
or hemiplegia due to cerebral thrombosis (wind stroke of meridians in
Chinese medicine).

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It is contraindicated for wind stroke
due to wind stirring due to hyperactivity of liver yang resulting from
yin deficiency of the liver and kidney.

### ***Xiao Huo Luo Dan (*originally known as *Huo Luo Dan)***  {#xiao-huo-luo-dan-originally-known-as-huo-luo-dan .unnumbered}

### *Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping Imperial Pharmacy) {#tai-ping-hui-min-he-ji-ju-fang-beneficial-formulas-from-the-taiping-imperial-pharmacy-1 .unnumbered}

### **\[Ingredients\]**  {#ingredients .unnumbered}

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarch  | >       | *zhì chuān  | 180g | Radix Aconiti Praeparata   |
|          |  制川乌 | wū*         |      |                            |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
|          | >       | *zhì căo    | 180g | Radix Aconiti Kusnezoffii  |
|          |  制草乌 | wū*         |      | Praeparata                 |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Minister | > 独活  | *dú huó*    | 180g | Rhizoma Arisaematis        |
|          |         |             |      | Praeparatum                |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | > 地龙  | *dì lóng*   | 180g | Pheretima                  |
| sistants |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 乳香  | *rŭ xiāng*  | 66g  | Olibanum Praeparata        |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 没药  | *mò yào*    | 66g  | Myrrha Praeparata          |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Zhi Chuan Wu* and *Zhi Cao Wu* act to remove wind, resolve
dampness, warm and unblock meridians, and alleviate pain.

Minister: *Tian Nan Xing* acts to dry dampness and resolve phlegm of
meridians.

Assistants: *Ru Xiang* and *Mo Yao* act to circulate qi and blood,
resolve stasis, and unblock meridians. They are better in pain relief.
*Di Long* acts is better in unblocking meridians.

Old wine is combined to guide and harmonize the actions of other
ingredients.

### **\[Method of administration\]** Take 1 honey bolus (3g) for 1 dose with old wine or warm water, 2 doses a day.  {#method-of-administration-take-1-honey-bolus-3g-for-1-dose-with-old-wine-or-warm-water-2-doses-a-day. .unnumbered}

### **\[Functions\]** Removes wind, resolves dampness and phlegm, unblock meridians, circulate blood and alleviate pain {#functions-removes-wind-resolves-dampness-and-phlegm-unblock-meridians-circulate-blood-and-alleviate-pain .unnumbered}

### **\[Indications\]** Bi-impediment syndrome due to wind, cold and dampness: muscle pain, numbness in the limbs, impaired joint flexion and extension and migratory pain; wind-stroke: persistent numbness in the hand/foot, heavy sensations in the low back and leg due to retention of dampness, phlegm and blood stasis in meridians or pain in the legs or arms.  {#indications-bi-impediment-syndrome-due-to-wind-cold-and-dampness-muscle-pain-numbness-in-the-limbs-impaired-joint-flexion-and-extension-and-migratory-pain-wind-stroke-persistent-numbness-in-the-handfoot-heavy-sensations-in-the-low-back-and-leg-due-to-retention-of-dampness-phlegm-and-blood-stasis-in-meridians-or-pain-in-the-legs-or-arms. .unnumbered}

### **\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for wind, cold, dampness, phlegm and stasis obstructing meridians. Retention of wind, cold and dampness in meridians may obstruct the flow of qi and blood and disharmonize *ying*-nutrients and *wei*-defense, resulting in muscle pain, numbness in the limbs and impaired joint flexion and extension. Retention of dampness, phlegm and stasis coupled with external contraction of wind cold may obstruct meridians and cause malnourishment of muscles and subsequently, numbness in the hand/foot and pain in the legs and arms. As a result, the treatment strategies are to remove wind, dissipate cold, resolve dampness and phlegm, circulate blood, and unblock meridians.  {#diagnosis-treatment-this-is-a-common-formula-for-wind-cold-dampness-phlegm-and-stasis-obstructing-meridians.-retention-of-wind-cold-and-dampness-in-meridians-may-obstruct-the-flow-of-qi-and-blood-and-disharmonize-ying-nutrients-and-wei-defense-resulting-in-muscle-pain-numbness-in-the-limbs-and-impaired-joint-flexion-and-extension.-retention-of-dampness-phlegm-and-stasis-coupled-with-external-contraction-of-wind-cold-may-obstruct-meridians-and-cause-malnourishment-of-muscles-and-subsequently-numbness-in-the-handfoot-and-pain-in-the-legs-and-arms.-as-a-result-the-treatment-strategies-are-to-remove-wind-dissipate-cold-resolve-dampness-and-phlegm-circulate-blood-and-unblock-meridians. .unnumbered}

### **\[Clinical use\]** {#clinical-use .unnumbered}

1\. Characteristics signs and symptoms: muscle pain, impaired joint
flexion and extension and a pale purple tongue with a white coating.

2\. Modern use: Rheumatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis and
osteoproliferation that can be diagnosed as wind, cold, dampness, phlegm
and stasis obstructing meridians.

3\. Cautions & contraindications: Because of its warm-dry property and
high potency, this formula is contraindicated for pregnant women and
patients with (yin) deficiency heat.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Da Huo Luo Dan* (Major Channel-Activating Elixir)
from the *Lan Tai Gui Fan* (Medical Standards of the Han Dynasty)

\[Ingredients\] *Bai Hua She She Cao* (Herba Hedyotis Diffusae), *Wu
Shao She* (Zaocys), *Wei Ling Xian* (Radix et Rhizoma Clematidis),
*Liang Tou Jian* (Rhizoma Anemones Raddeanae), Cao Wu (Radix Aconiti
Kusnezoffii), *Tian Ma* (Rhizoma Gastrodiae), *Quan Xie* (Scorpio),
*Shou Wu* (Radix Polygoni Multiflori), *Gui Ban* (Plastrum Testudinis),
*Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae), *Guan Zhong* (Rhizoma Cyrtomii), *Zhi Gan
Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae Praeparata), *Qiang Huo* (Rhizoma et
Radix Notopterygii), *Guan Gui* (Ramulus Cinnamomi), *Huo Xiang* (Herba
Agastachis), *Wu Yao* (Radix Linderae), *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis),
*Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata), *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma
Rhei), *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae), *Cheng Xiang* (Lignum Aquilariae
Resinatum), *Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari), *Chi Shao* (Radix
Paeoniae Rubra), *Mo Yao* (Myrrha), *Ding Xiang* (Flos Caryophylli), *Ru
Xiang* (Olibanum), *Jiang Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus), *Tian Nan Xing*
(Rhizoma Arisaematis), *Qing Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae Viride),
*Gu Sui Bu* (Rhizoma Drynariae), *Bai Dou Kou* (Fructus Amomi Kravanh),
*Hei Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata), *Huang Qin* (Radix
Scutellariae), *Fu Ling* (Poria), *Xiang Fu* (Rhizoma Cyperi), *Xuan
Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis
Macrocephalae), *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae), *Ge Gen* (Radix
Puerariae Lobatae), *Bao Gu* (Leopard bone), *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae
Sinensis), *Xue Jie* (Sanguis Draconis), *Di Long* (Pheretima
Praeparata), *Shui Niu* *Jiao* (Cornu Bubali), *She Xiang* (Moschus),
*Song Zhi* (Lignum Pini Nodi), *Bing Pian* (Borneolum Syntheticum) and
*Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng).

\[Functions\] Removes wind, resolves dampness, unblocks meridians,
alleviates pain, and benefits qi and blood.

\[Indications\] Paralysis after stroke, *Wei*-flaccidity,
*Bi*-impediment, phlegm syncope, scrofula, deep multiple abscesses and
traumatic injuries

Both formulas can remove wind and unblock meridians. *Xiao Huo Luo Dan*
contain fewer herbs that are dry in property and strong in potency and
targets more on removing pathogenic factors, it's therefore suitable for
patients with a strong build. *Da Huo Luo Dan* contain more herbs that
are moderate in potency and reinforces anti-pathogenic qi in addition to
removing pathogenic factors, it's therefore suitable for patient with a
weak constitution.

### ***Qian Zhen San***  {#qian-zhen-san .unnumbered}

### *Yang Shi Jia Cang Fang* (Secret Formulas of the Yang Family) {#yang-shi-jia-cang-fang-secret-formulas-of-the-yang-family .unnumbered}

### **\[Ingredients\]**  {#ingredients-1 .unnumbered}

+-----------+----------+---------------+-----------------------------+
| Monarchs  | > 白附子 | *bái fù zĭ*   | Rhizoma Typhonii            |
+===========+==========+===============+=============================+
| Ministers | > 全蝎   | *quán xiē*    | Scorpio                     |
+-----------+----------+---------------+-----------------------------+
|           | > 白僵蚕 | *bái jiāng    | Bombyx Batryticatus         |
|           |          | cán*          |                             |
+-----------+----------+---------------+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Bai Fu Zi* acts to remove wind (of the head and face) and
resolve phlegm.

Ministers: *Jiang Can* and *Quan Xie* remove wind of meridians. *Jiang
Can* is better in resolving phlegm and *Quan Xie* is better in relieving
spasm.

Warm wine is combined to circulate blood and assist the actions of other
ingredients to ascend to the head and face.

Both *Qian Zhen San* and *Da Qin* *Jiao Tang* are indicated for
deviation of the eye and mouth. *Qian Zheng San* is more indicated for
deviation of the eye and mouth due to wind phlegm affecting the head and
face along the pathways of meridians, whereas Da Qin *Jiao* Tang is more
indicated for deviation of the eye and mouth, tongue stiffness,
inability to move one's hand and foot due to deficiency of
anti-pathogenic qi coupled with external contraction of wind.

### **\[Method of administration\]** Take the powder with tea, 3g for each dose and 2-3 doses a day. {#method-of-administration-take-the-powder-with-tea-3g-for-each-dose-and-2-3-doses-a-day. .unnumbered}

### **\[Functions\]** Removes wind, resolves phlegm, unblocks meridians and relieves spasm.  {#functions-removes-wind-resolves-phlegm-unblocks-meridians-and-relieves-spasm. .unnumbered}

### **\[Indications\]** Wind phlegm affecting the meridians on the head and face: deviation of the eye and mouth, facial twitching, and a pale red tongue with a white coating.  {#indications-wind-phlegm-affecting-the-meridians-on-the-head-and-face-deviation-of-the-eye-and-mouth-facial-twitching-and-a-pale-red-tongue-with-a-white-coating. .unnumbered}

### **\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for deviation of the eye and mouth due to wind stroke. Wind phlegm affecting the meridians on the head and face may cause malnourishment of the localized muscles, leading to deviation of the eye and mouth. As a result, the treatment strategies are to remove wind, resolve phlegm, unblock meridians and relieve spasm.  {#diagnosis-treatment-this-is-a-common-formula-for-deviation-of-the-eye-and-mouth-due-to-wind-stroke.-wind-phlegm-affecting-the-meridians-on-the-head-and-face-may-cause-malnourishment-of-the-localized-muscles-leading-to-deviation-of-the-eye-and-mouth.-as-a-result-the-treatment-strategies-are-to-remove-wind-resolve-phlegm-unblock-meridians-and-relieve-spasm. .unnumbered}

### **\[Clinical use\]** {#clinical-use-1 .unnumbered}

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Sudden deviation of the eye and
mouth and a pale tongue with a white coating.

2\. Modifications

-   In early stage, combine with *Qiang Huo* (Rhizoma et Radix
    Notopterygii) and *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae) to remove wind
    and resolve phlegm.

-   For complication of facial twitching, combine with *Wu Gong*
    (Scolopendra), *Di Long* (Pheretima) and *Tian Ma* (Rhizoma
    Gastrodiae) to unblock meridians and stop wind.

3\. Modern use: Facial palsy, trigeminal neuralgia and migraine that can
be diagnosed as wind phlegm affecting meridians on the head and face in
Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Since *Bai Fu Zi* is warm and dry in
property, this formula is contraindicated for deviation of the eye and
mouth or hemiplegia due to qi deficiency with blood stasis or internal
stirring of liver wind. In addition, *Bai Fu Zi* and *Quan Xie* are both
toxic, this formula should be used with caution: a small dose for a
short period of time.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Zhi Jing San* (Spasm-Relieving Powder) from *Fang
Ji Xue* (Chinese Herbal Formulas), Shanghai College of Traditional
Chinese Medicine

\[Ingredients\] *Quan Xie* (Scorpio) and *Wu Gong* (Scolopendra)

\[Functions\] Removes wind and relieves spasm

\[Indications\] Convulsive syncope, convulsions of the limbs, persistent
headache and joint pain

Both *Qian Zheng San* and *Zhi Jing Sa*n can remove wind and relieve
spasm and therefore are indicated for wind phlegm obstructing meridians.
*Qian Zheng San* is better in removing wind and resolving phlegm and
more indicated for deviation of the eye and mouth due to wind phlegm
obstructing meridians on the head and face, whereas *Zhi Jing San* is
better in relieving spasm and more indicated for convulsive syncope,
headache and joint pain due to wind phlegm obstructing meridians over
the body.

### ***Yu Zhen San***  {#yu-zhen-san .unnumbered}

### *Wai Ke Zheng Zong* (Orthodox Lineage of External Medicine) {#wai-ke-zheng-zong-orthodox-lineage-of-external-medicine .unnumbered}

### **\[Ingredients\]**  {#ingredients-2 .unnumbered}

+----------+---------+-----------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarchs | >       | *tiān nán | 6g   | Rhizoma Arisaematis        |
|          |  天南星 | xīng*     |      |                            |
+==========+=========+===========+======+============================+
|          | >       | *bái fù   | 6g   | Rhizoma Typhonii           |
|          |  白附子 | zĭ*       |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+----------------------------+
| M        | > 羌活  | *qiāng    | 6g   | Rhizoma et Radix           |
| inisters |         | huó*      |      | Notopterygii               |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 防风  | *fáng     | 6g   | Radix Saposhnikoviae       |
|          |         | fēng*     |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 白芷  | *bái zhĭ* | 6g   | Radix Angelicae Dahuricae  |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+----------------------------+
| A        | > 天麻  | *tiān má* | 6g   | Rhizoma Gastrodiae         |
| ssistant |         |           |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-----------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Bai Fu Zi* and *Tian Nan Xing* are used as the monarchs. *Bai
Fu Zi* acts to dispel wind and relieve spasms/convulsions and *Tian Tan
Xing* acts to remove wind and resolve phlegm of meridians.

Ministers: *Qiang Huo*, *Fang Feng* and *Bai Zhi* dispel wind out of
meridians and are used as ministers.

Assistant: *Tian Ma* is used as assistant to stop wind and relieve
convulsions.

Envoy: Warm wine or urine of boys (\<12) is used as the envoy to unblock
meridians and circulate qi and blood. In summary, the formula removes
toxic wind out of meridians, resolves phlegm, and releases spasm.

### This formula targets both the root cause (remove wind and resolve phlegm) and clinical manifestations (releases spasms/convulsions).  {#this-formula-targets-both-the-root-cause-remove-wind-and-resolve-phlegm-and-clinical-manifestations-releases-spasmsconvulsions. .unnumbered}

### **\[Method of administration\]** Take the powder with tea, 3-6g for each dose, 2-3 doses a day.  {#method-of-administration-take-the-powder-with-tea-3-6g-for-each-dose-2-3-doses-a-day. .unnumbered}

### **\[Functions\]** Dispels wind and relieves spasms/convulsions {#functions-dispels-wind-and-relieves-spasmsconvulsions .unnumbered}

### **\[Indications\]** This is a common formula for tetanus: lockjaw, rigidity of the body, opisthotonos, and a wiry tense pulse. In severe cases, jaw clenching with a retracted tongue may also be present.  {#indications-this-is-a-common-formula-for-tetanus-lockjaw-rigidity-of-the-body-opisthotonos-and-a-wiry-tense-pulse.-in-severe-cases-jaw-clenching-with-a-retracted-tongue-may-also-be-present. .unnumbered}

### **\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for tetanus. After attacking the surface of the body or meridians through any break in the skin, pathogenic toxic wind may disharmonize *ying*-nutrients and *wei-*defense and obstruct the circulation of body fluids, resulting in lockjaw, convulsions of the limbs and opisthotonos. As a result, the treatment strategies are to dispel wind, relieve convulsions and release spasm.  {#diagnosis-treatment-this-is-a-common-formula-for-tetanus.-after-attacking-the-surface-of-the-body-or-meridians-through-any-break-in-the-skin-pathogenic-toxic-wind-may-disharmonize-ying-nutrients-and-wei-defense-and-obstruct-the-circulation-of-body-fluids-resulting-in-lockjaw-convulsions-of-the-limbs-and-opisthotonos.-as-a-result-the-treatment-strategies-are-to-dispel-wind-relieve-convulsions-and-release-spasm. .unnumbered}

### **\[Clinical use\]** {#clinical-use-2 .unnumbered}

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: a history of wound, trismus,
rigidity of the body and opisthotonos.

2\. Modification: This formula has better efficacy in removing wind and
resolving phlegm than releasing spasms/convulsions. To facilitate the
efficacy in releasing spasms/convulsions, *Zhi Jing San*
(Spasm-Relieving Powder) can be used in combination.

3\. Cautions & contraindications: It's advisable to use raw herbs in
decoction and roll in a blanket/quilt after drinking the decoction, so
as to induce sweating and dispel wind. In addition, it's important to
stay away from wind afterwards. As warm-dry herbs may consume qi and
damage body fluids, patients with damaged qi and body fluids should
avoid or use this formula with caution. Since *Bai Fu Zi* and *Tian Nan
Xing* are toxic, patients should limit their dose. This formula is
contraindicated for pregnant women.

### ***Xiao Feng San***  {#xiao-feng-san .unnumbered}

### *Wai Ke Zheng Zong* (Orthodox Lineage of External Medicine) {#wai-ke-zheng-zong-orthodox-lineage-of-external-medicine-1 .unnumbered}

### **\[Ingredients\]**  {#ingredients-3 .unnumbered}

### **\[Explanations of the formula\]**  {#explanations-of-the-formula .unnumbered}

Monarchs: *Jing Jie*, *Fang Feng*, *Niu Bang Zi* and *Chan Tui* remove
wind and stop itching;

Ministers: *Cang Zhu* releases the exterior and dries dampness, *Ku
Shen* clears heat and dries dampness, *Mu Tong* resolves damp heat, and
*Shi Gao* and *Zhi Mu* clear heat and fire;

Assistants: *Dang Gui*, *Hu Ma Re*n and *Sheng Di* nourish and circulate
blood, nourish yin and moisten dryness;

Envoy: *Gan Cao* clears heat, removes toxins and harmonizes the actions
of other ingredients.

This formula integrates four methods: removing wind, nourishing blood,
clearing heat and resolving dampness. In addition to removing pathogenic
factors, it also supplements anti-pathogenic qi and protects it from
being damaged.

### **\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water {#method-of-administration-decoct-with-water .unnumbered}

### **\[Functions\]** Dispels wind, nourishes blood, clears heat and resolves dampness {#functions-dispels-wind-nourishes-blood-clears-heat-and-resolves-dampness .unnumbered}

### **\[Indications\]** Urticaria and eczema: red skin rashes or patches, itching, scratching blisters, a white or yellow tongue coating and a superficial rapid pulse.  {#indications-urticaria-and-eczema-red-skin-rashes-or-patches-itching-scratching-blisters-a-white-or-yellow-tongue-coating-and-a-superficial-rapid-pulse. .unnumbered}

### **\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for urticaria and eczema. The struggle between pathogenic wind and damp heat in the surface of the body may cause rashes. Wind heat or wind dampness affecting the blood vessels may cause red rashes and scratching blisters. Wind and damp heat may cause skin itching. As a result, the treatment strategies are to dispel wind, nourish blood, clear heat and resolve dampness.  {#diagnosis-treatment-this-is-a-common-formula-for-urticaria-and-eczema.-the-struggle-between-pathogenic-wind-and-damp-heat-in-the-surface-of-the-body-may-cause-rashes.-wind-heat-or-wind-dampness-affecting-the-blood-vessels-may-cause-red-rashes-and-scratching-blisters.-wind-and-damp-heat-may-cause-skin-itching.-as-a-result-the-treatment-strategies-are-to-dispel-wind-nourish-blood-clear-heat-and-resolve-dampness. .unnumbered}

### **\[Clinical use\]** {#clinical-use-3 .unnumbered}

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Red skin rashes or patches and
skin itching.

2\. Modifications

-   For exuberant heat in early stage, combine with *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos
    Lonicerae Japonicae) and *Lian Qiao* (Fructus Forsythiae) to clear
    heat and remove toxins.

-   For severe skin itching, combine with *Mu Dan Pi* (Cortex Moutan),
    *Chi Shao Yao* (Radix Paeoniae Rubra) and *Zi Cao* (Radix Arnebiae)
    to cool blood and remove toxins.

-   For predominance of damp heat, combine with *Di Fu Zi* (Fructus
    Kochiae), *Che Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis) and *Huang Bai* (Cortex
    Phellodendri Chinensis) to clear heat and resolve dampness.

3\. Modern use: Urticaria, allergic dermatitis, paddy field dermatitis,
drug-induced dermatitis and neurodermatitis that can be diagnosed as
toxic wind, heat and dampness in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions: Patients should avoid hot, spicy and sea food and stop
smoking cigarettes or drinking alcohol and strong tea during the
treatment.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Dang Gui Yin Zi* (Chinese Angelica Drink) from
the *Ji Sheng Fang* (Formulas to Aid the Living)

\[Ingredients\] *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Bai Shao* (Radix
Paeoniae Alba), *Chuan Xiong* (Rhizoma Chuanxiong), *Sheng Di Huang*
(Radix Rehmanniae), Bai Ji Li (), *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae),
*Jing Jie Sui* (Spica Schizonepetae), *He Shou Wu* (Radix Polygoni
Multiflori) and *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali)

\[Functions\] Nourishes and circulates blood, removes wind and stops
itching.

\[Indications\] Dry skin due to blood deficiency coupled with external
contraction of wind: skin boils, bumpy rashes, itching, red rashes and
skin peeling.

**13.2 Formulas that Stop Internal Wind**

Internal wind is mainly associated with the liver. Common patterns
include extreme heat generating wind, liver yang transforming into wind
and yin deficiency stirring wind. Extreme heat generating wind manifests
as persistent high-grade fever, convulsions of the hands/feet and in
severe cases, loss of consciousness. Yin deficiency stirring wind
manifests as mental fatigue, involuntary convulsions of the limbs and a
weak pulse. Hyperactive yang transforming into wind due to yin
deficiency of the liver and kidney manifests as a drunk-like complexion,
vertigo, faint and hemiplegia. Common wind-dispelling ingredients
include *Ling Yang* *Jiao* (Cornu Saigae Tataricae), *Gou Teng* (Ramulus
Uncariae Cum Uncis), *Shi Jue Ming* (Concha Haliotidis), *Dai Zhe Shi*
(Haematitum), *Long Gu* (Os Draconis) and *Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae).
Classic formulas include *Ling* *Jiao Gou Teng Tang* (Antelope Horn and
Uncariae Decoction), *Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang* (Liver-Soothing and
Wind-Extinguishing Decoction) and *Da Ding Feng Zhu* (Major
Wind-Stabilizing Pill).

### ***Ling*** ***Jiao Gou Teng Tang*** {#ling-jiao-gou-teng-tang .unnumbered}

*Tong Su Shang Han Lun* (Popular Guide to the *Treatise on Cold Damage*)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| M       | >      | *líng    | 4   | Cornu Saigae Tataricae (decoct |
| onarchs | 羚羊角 | yáng     | .5g | first)                         |
|         |        | jiăo*    |     |                                |
+=========+========+==========+=====+================================+
|         | > 钩藤 | *gōu     | 9g  | Ramulus Uncariae Cum Uncis     |
|         |        | téng*    |     | (decoct later)                 |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Mi      | > 桑叶 | *sāng    | 6g  | Folium Mori                    |
| nisters |        | yè*      |     |                                |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|         | > 菊花 | *jú huā* | 9g  | Flos Chrysanthemi              |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Ass     | > 白芍 | *bái     | 9g  | Radix Paeoniae Alba            |
| istants |        | sháo*    |     |                                |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|         | > 生地 | *shēng   | 15g | Radix Rehmanniae               |
|         |        | dì*      |     |                                |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|         | > 竹茹 | *zhú rú* | 15g | Caulis Bambusae in Taenia      |
|         |        |          |     | (decoct first)                 |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|         | > 贝母 | *bèi mŭ* | 12g | Bulbus Fritillaria             |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
|         | > 松节 | *sōng    | 9g  | Lignum Pini Nodi               |
|         |        | jié*     |     |                                |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+
| Envoy   | > 甘草 | *gān     | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae  |
|         |        | căo*     |     |                                |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ling Yang* *Jiao* acts to clear heat, soothe the liver and
stop wind; Gou Teng acts to clear heat, soothe the liver, stop wind and
relieve convulsion.

Ministers: Both *Sang Ye* and *Ju Hua* are pungent cool and act to
disperse wind and clear heat. They facilitate the effect of clearing
heat and stopping wind as well as inducing heat out of the body.

Assistants: *Sheng Di* clears heat and nourishes yin. *Bai Shao* and
*Gan Cao* are sour and sweet in taste. When used in combination, they
nourish yin, soften the liver and relax tendons. *Zhu Ru* and *Bei Mu*
clear heat and resolve phlegm. *Song Jie* soothes the liver and calms
the mind.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* harmonizes the actions of other ingredients.

This formula targets both the root cause and clinical symptoms by using
herbs to clear heat, soothe the liver and stop wind coupled with herbs
to nourish yin.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Soothes the liver, stop wind, nourish yin and relax
tendons.

**\[Indications\]** Liver heat generating wind: persistent high-grade
fever, restlessness, vexation, convulsions of the hands/feet, convulsive
syncope or even loss of consciousness, a deep red or dry thorny tongue
and a wiry rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for extreme heat
generating wind. Pathogenic heat affecting the liver meridian may cause
excessive yang heat and subsequently, persistent high-grade fever,
restlessness and vexation; extreme heat generating wind and wind and
fire stirring up each other may cause malnourishment of tendons and
subsequently, convulsions of the hands/feet and convulsive syncope. A
dry or deep red tongue and a wiry rapid pulse are typical signs
indicative of heat damaging yin. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to soothe the liver, stop wind, nourish yin and relax tendons.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: High-grade fever, restlessness,
convulsions of the hands/feet and a wiry rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For loss of consciousness due to retention of internal heat, combine
    with *An Gong Niu Huang Wan* (Palace-Calming Bovine Bezoar Pill) or
    *Zi Xue Dan* (Purple Snow Elixir) to clear heat and restore
    consciousness.

-   For heat consuming body fluids, combine with *Shi Hu* (Caulis
    Dendrobii), *Mai Men Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) and *Tian Men Dong*
    (Radix Asparagi) to nourish yin fluids.

-   For severe convulsions, combine with *Quan Xie* (Scorpio), *Jiang
    Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus) and *Wu Gong* (Scolopendra) to stop wind
    and relieve convulsions.

3\. Modern use: pre-eclampsia, epidemic encephalitis B and hypertension
that can be diagnosed as liver heat generating wind in Chinese medicine.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with wind stirring due to yin
deficiency in later stage febrile disease should avoid this formula.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Gou Teng Yin* (Uncariae Drink) from the *Yi Zong
Jin Jian* (Golden Mirror of the Medical Tradition)

\[Ingredients\] *Gou Teng* (Ramulus Uncariae Cum Uncis) (decoct later),
*Ling Yang* *Jiao* (Cornu Saigae Tataricae) (powder), *Quan Xie*
(Scorpio) (toxin removed), *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Tian
Ma* (Rhizoma Gastrodiae) and *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma
Glycyrrhizae Praeparata cum Melle)

\[Functions\] Clears heat, stops wind, supplements qi and relieves
convulsions.

\[Indications\] Infantile convulsions: trismus, convulsions of the
hands/feet, high-grade fever and the head and eyes fixed upward, coupled
with qi deficiency.

*Ling Yang* *Jiao* and *Gou Teng* are monarchs in both *Ling* *Jiao Gou
Teng Tang* and *Gou Teng Yin*. The former is better in relieving
convulsions and at the same time reinforcing anti-pathogenic qi and
therefore more indicated for infantile convulsions due to liver heat
generating wind coupled with compromised anti-pathogenic qi. The latter
is better in clearing heat and more indicated for convulsions due to
high fever.

### ***Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang*** {#zhen-gan-xi-feng-tang .unnumbered}

### *Yi Xue Zhong Zhong Can Xi Lu* (Records of Chinese Medicine with Reference to Western Medicine) {#yi-xue-zhong-zhong-can-xi-lu-records-of-chinese-medicine-with-reference-to-western-medicine .unnumbered}
