+---------+--------+----------+-----+--------------------------------+

### **\[Explanations of the formula\]** {#explanations-of-the-formula-1 .unnumbered}

Monarch: *Huai Niu Xi* guides blood downward and reinforces the liver
and kidney.

Ministers: *Zhe Shi* is weighty and tends to descend. Combined *Niu Xi*
and *Zhi Shi* facilitate the effect of inducing adverse ascending qi and
blood to flow downward. *Long Gu* and *Mu Li* can produce a strong
sedative effect and restrain yang.

The monarch and ministers down-regulate qi, restrain liver yang and stop
wind to alleviate symptoms.

Assistants: *Gui Ban*, *Xuan Shen*, *Tian Dong* and *Bai Shao* nourish
yin fluids to submerge yang to target the root cause. As a resolute
organ, the liver likes to have free will and hates to be restrained;
over-restraint may trigger its rebellion. Consequently, *Yin Chen*,
*Chuan Lian Z*i and *Mai Y*a are combined to clear liver heat and soothe
liver qi.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the actions of other ingredients,
harmonizes and protects the stomach from being affected by heavy
materials.

This formula is characterized by nourishing yin (for the root cause) and
soothing the liver (form the symptoms).

### **\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water {#method-of-administration-decoct-with-water-1 .unnumbered}

### **\[Functions\]** Soothes the liver, stops wind, nourish yin and submerge yang {#functions-soothes-the-liver-stops-wind-nourish-yin-and-submerge-yang .unnumbered}

### **\[Indications\]** Internal wind stroke: dizziness, blurred vision, a distending sensation of the eyes, tinnitus, a hot sensation and pain in the head, a feverish sensation in the chest and a drunk-like complexion; alternatively, frequent belching, impaired movement of the limbs, deviation of the mouth corner may be present. In severe cases, vertigo, faint, loss of consciousness, hemiplegia and listlessness can be present. The pulse is wiry, long and forceful.  {#indications-internal-wind-stroke-dizziness-blurred-vision-a-distending-sensation-of-the-eyes-tinnitus-a-hot-sensation-and-pain-in-the-head-a-feverish-sensation-in-the-chest-and-a-drunk-like-complexion-alternatively-frequent-belching-impaired-movement-of-the-limbs-deviation-of-the-mouth-corner-may-be-present.-in-severe-cases-vertigo-faint-loss-of-consciousness-hemiplegia-and-listlessness-can-be-present.-the-pulse-is-wiry-long-and-forceful. .unnumbered}

### **\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for internal wind stroke. Yin deficiency of the liver and kidney may cause hyperactivity of liver yang. As a result, blood ascend along with adverse rising of qi to disturb the brain, leading to dizziness, blurred vision, a distending sensation of the eyes, tinnitus, a drunk-like complexion and a hot sensation and pain in the head; Liver yang affecting the spleen earth may cause adverse ascending of stomach qi, leading to frequent belching. Sudden ascending of blood due to hyperactivity of liver yang may cause vertigo, faint, loss of consciousness or hemiplegia. A wiry long and forceful pulse is a typical sign indicative of hyperactivity of liver yang and adverse ascending of qi and blood. As a result, the treat strategies are to soothe the liver, stop wind, nourish yin and submerge yang.  {#diagnosis-treatment-this-is-a-common-formula-for-internal-wind-stroke.-yin-deficiency-of-the-liver-and-kidney-may-cause-hyperactivity-of-liver-yang.-as-a-result-blood-ascend-along-with-adverse-rising-of-qi-to-disturb-the-brain-leading-to-dizziness-blurred-vision-a-distending-sensation-of-the-eyes-tinnitus-a-drunk-like-complexion-and-a-hot-sensation-and-pain-in-the-head-liver-yang-affecting-the-spleen-earth-may-cause-adverse-ascending-of-stomach-qi-leading-to-frequent-belching.-sudden-ascending-of-blood-due-to-hyperactivity-of-liver-yang-may-cause-vertigo-faint-loss-of-consciousness-or-hemiplegia.-a-wiry-long-and-forceful-pulse-is-a-typical-sign-indicative-of-hyperactivity-of-liver-yang-and-adverse-ascending-of-qi-and-blood.-as-a-result-the-treat-strategies-are-to-soothe-the-liver-stop-wind-nourish-yin-and-submerge-yang. .unnumbered}

### **\[Clinical use\]** {#clinical-use-4 .unnumbered}

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: dizziness, blurred vision, a
distending pain in the head, a drunk-like complexion, a feverish
sensation in the chest and a wiry long and forceful pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a severe feverish sensation in the chest, combine with *Sheng
    Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) 30g.

-   For profuse phlegm, combine with *Dan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) 6g.

-   For a deep weak pulse at *Chi* region, combine with *Shu Di Huang*
    (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata) 24g and *Shan Yu Rou* (Fructus Corni)
    15g.

-   For loose stools, remove *Gui Ban* and *Zhe Shi* and combine with
    *Chi Shi Zhi* (Halloysitum Rubrum) 30g.

3\. Modern use: Hypertension, vascular headache, stroke and sequelae of
stroke that can be diagnosed as hyperactivity of liver yang due to yin
deficiency of the liver and kidney.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with extreme heat generating
wind should avoid this formula; patients with weak spleen and stomach
should use this formula with caution, because heavy metals and shells
may affect the stomach.

### **\[Annex formula\]** *Jiang Ling Tang* (Down Sweeping Decoction) from the *Yi Xue Zhong Zhong Can Xi Lu* (Records of Chinese Medicine with Reference to Western Medicine) {#annex-formula-jiang-ling-tang-down-sweeping-decoction-from-the-yi-xue-zhong-zhong-can-xi-lu-records-of-chinese-medicine-with-reference-to-western-medicine .unnumbered}

\[Ingredients\] *Huai Shan Yao* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae), *Huai Niu Xi*
(Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae), *Sheng Zhe Shi* (Haematitum), *Sheng
Long Gu* (Os Draconis), *Sheng Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae), *Sheng Di Huang*
(Radix Rehmanniae), *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba), and *Bai Zi Ren*
(Semen Platycladi)

\[Functions\] Soothes the liver, stop wind, nourish yin, submerge yang
and tranquilize the mind

\[Indications\] Hyperactivity of liver yang: dizziness, blurred vision,
a distending sensation of the eyes, tinnitus, palpitations,
forgetfulness, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep and a wiry, long and
forceful pulse.

Both *Jian Ling Tang* and *Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang* can nourish yin,
submerge yang, soothe the liver and stop wind and are indicated for
hyperactivity of liver yang due to yin deficiency of the liver and
kidney. The former is better in calming the mind and indicated for
insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep and restlessness due to internal
stirring of liver wind alone (without disorder of qi and blood). The
latter has a strong sedative effect in submerging yang and nourishing
yin and is therefore indicated for a hot sensation and pain in the head,
a drunk-like complexion and impaired movement of the limbs due to
hyperactivity of liver yang coupled with disorder of qi and blood.

### ***Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin*** {#tian-ma-gou-teng-yin .unnumbered}

### *Zhong Yi Nei Ke Za Bing Zheng Zhi Xin Yi* (New Explanations of Diagnosis and Treatment of Internal Miscellaneous Diseases) {#zhong-yi-nei-ke-za-bing-zheng-zhi-xin-yi-new-explanations-of-diagnosis-and-treatment-of-internal-miscellaneous-diseases .unnumbered}

**\[Indications\]**

| onarchs |        | má*      |     |                                |
+=========+========+==========+=====+================================+

|         |        | téng*    |     | (decoct later)                 |

|         | 夜交藤 | téng*    |     |                                |

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Tian Ma* soothes liver yang and stops liver wind; *Gou Teng*
soothes the liver, clears heat, stops wind and relieves convulsions.

Ministers: *Shi Jue Ming* soothes liver yang; *Shan Zhi* and *Huang Qin*
clear heat and reduce fire.

Assistants: *Yi Mu Cao* circulates blood and promotes urination; *Niu
Xi* circulates blood, induces blood downward and helps to soothe liver
yang; *Du Zhong* and *Sang Ji Sheng* reinforce yin of the liver and
kidney. *Ye* *Jiao Teng* and *Fu Shen* tranquilize the mind.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Soothes the liver, stops wind, clears heat, circulates
blood and reinforces the liver and kidney.

**\[Indications\]** Hyperactivity of liver yang and ascending of liver
wind: headache, vertigo, insomnia, a red tongue with a yellow coating
and a wiry large pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for hyperactivity
of liver yang and ascending of liver wind due to yin deficiency of the
liver and kidney. Liver wind ascending may cause headache and vertigo.
Hyperactive yang transforming into heat and heat disturbing the mind may
cause restlessness and insomnia. A red tongue with a yellow coating and
a wiry pulse are typical signs indicative of hyperactivity of liver yang
and ascending of liver fire. As a result, the treatment strategies are
to soothe the liver, stop wind, clear heat, circulate blood and
reinforce the liver and kidney.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: headache, vertigo, insomnia and a
wiry large pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a dry red tongue and a thready rapid pulse due to severe yin
    deficiency of the liver and kidney, remove *Yi Mu Cao* and combine
    with *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba), *Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae)
    and *Tian Dong* (Radix Rehmanniae) to nourish yin and clear heat.

-   For severe dizziness and headache due to hyperactivity of liver
    yang, combine with *Zhen Zhu Mu* (Concha Margaritiferae Usta),
    *Sheng Mu Li* (Concha Ostreae), *Sang Ye* (Folium Mori) and *Ju Hua*
    (Flos Chrysanthemi) to soothe the liver and restrain yang.

3\. Modern use: Hypertension, menopausal syndrome and cluster headache
that can be diagnosed as hyperactivity of liver yang.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with headache due to damp
heat or excessive fire in the liver meridian should avoid this formula.

### ***Da Ding Feng Zhu*** (**Major Wind-Stabilizing Pill)** {#da-ding-feng-zhu-major-wind-stabilizing-pill .unnumbered}

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Febrile Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
| M       | >      | *jī zǐ   | 2   | Fresh egg yolk                |
| onarchs | 鸡子黄 | huáng*   |     |                               |
+=========+========+==========+=====+===============================+
|         | > 阿胶 | *ē jiāo* | 9g  | Colla Corii Asini             |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
| Mi      | > 白芍 | *bái     | 18g | Radix Paeoniae Alba           |
| nisters |        | sháo*    |     |                               |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
|         | >      | *wŭ wèi  | 6g  | Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis |
|         | 五味子 | zĭ*      |     |                               |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
|         | > 地黄 | *dì      | 18g | Radix Rehmanniae Recens       |
|         |        | huáng*   |     |                               |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
|         | > 麦冬 | *mài     | 18g | Radix Ophiopogonis            |
|         |        | dōng*    |     |                               |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
|         | > 麻仁 | *má rén* | 6g  | Fructus Cannabis              |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
| Ass     | > 龟板 | *guī     | 12g | Plastrum Testudinis           |
| istants |        | băn*     |     |                               |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
|         | > 鳖甲 | *biē     | 12g | Carapax Trionycis             |
|         |        | jiă*     |     |                               |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
|         | > 牡蛎 | *mŭ lì*  | 12g | Concha Ostreae                |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+
| Envoy   | >      | *zhì gān | 12g | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae |
|         | 炙甘草 | căo*     |     | Praeparata                    |
+---------+--------+----------+-----+-------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ji Zi Huang* and *E* *Jiao* are sweet in taste and neutral in
property and act to nourish yin (blood) fluids to stop internal wind.

Ministers: Combined *Bai Shao*, *Wu Wei Zi* and *Zhi Gan Cao* nourish
yin and soften the liver. *Di Huan*g and *Mai Dong* nourish yin fluids.
*Ma Ren* moistens dryness and nourishes yin.

Assistants: *Gui Ban* and *Bie Jia* nourish yin and submerge yang.
*Sheng Mu Li* acts to suppress yang and astringe yin.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* harmonizes the actions of other ingredients.

Although this formula aims to stop wind, it does not contain herbs that
directly stop liver wind. Instead, with yin-nourishing and
yang-submerging herbs, it intends to nourish yin to stop wind and
targets the root cause rather than symptoms.

Both *Ling* *Jiao Gou Teng Tang* and *Da Ding Feng Zhu* are indicated
for wind stirring. The former is more indicated for high fever and
convulsions due to hyperactivity of pathogenic heat (excess) and wind
fire stirring up each other (based on the strategy of *soothing the
liver to stop wind*), whereas the latter is more indicated for mental
fatigue, involuntary convulsions of the limbs, a weak pulse, and a deep
red tongue with a scanty coating due to yin deficiency (based on the
strategy of *nourishing yin to stop wind*).

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water, add melted *E*
*Jiao*, mix with egg yolk and administer it warm.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin and stops wind.

**\[Indications\]** Wind stirring due to yin deficiency and symptoms in
later stage febrile disease: mental fatigue, involuntary convulsions of
the limbs, a weak pulse and a deep red tongue with a scanty coating and
feeling like faint.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for wind
stirring due to yin deficiency. Severe damage to yin in later stage
febrile disease may cause malnourishment of the liver and tendons,
resulting in mental fatigue and involuntary convulsions of the limbs. A
deep red tongue with a scanty coating and a weak pulse are typical signs
indicative of yin deficiency. Exhaustion of kidney water, failure of yin
to restrain yang and the tendency of yin-yang separation may cause
frequent feeling like faint. As a result, the treatment strategies are
to nourish yin and stop wind.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristics signs and symptoms: mental fatigue, involuntary
convulsions of the limbs, a weak pulse and a deep red tongue with a
scanty coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For panting, combine with *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng).

-   For spontaneous sweating, combine with *Long Gu* (Os Draconis), *Ren
    Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) and *Xiao Mai* (Fructus Tritici).

-   For palpitations, combine with *Fu Shen* (Sclerotium Poriae
    Pararadicis), *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) and *Xiao Mai*
    (Fructus Tritici).

3\. Modern use: Similar symptoms seen in epidemic encephalitis B.

4\. Cautions: Patients with deficiency of yin fluids coupled with
excessive heat should avoid using this formula.

**\[Annex formula\]**

Compared with *Da Ding Feng Zhu*, the *San Jia Fu Mai Tang* (Three
Shells Pulse-Restoring Decoction) removes *Ma Ren*, *Ji Zi Huang* and
*Wu Wei Zi* and acts to nourish yin, submerge yang, nurture blood and
restore pulse. It only has a mild wind-stopping effect and is mainly
indicated for retention of pathogenic heat (in febrile disease) in the
lower *jiao*: fever, cold limbs, a thready rapid pulse, palpitations,
cardiac pain or convulsions of the hands/feet. The *E* *Jiao Ji Zi Huang
Tang* (Donkey-Hide Gelatin and Egg Yolk Decoction) aims to nourish yin
and blood and is indicated for wind stirring due to yin deficiency
coupled with retention of pathogenic heat in febrile disease: muscle
spasms, dizziness, blurred vision and a deep red tongue with a scanty
coating.

**Summary**

There are a total of 10 formulas and 8 annex formulas in this chapter.
In terms of functions, these formulas are categorized into two types:
one to dispel external wind and the other to stop internal wind.

**Dispelling externa wind**

Of the six formulas to dispel external wind, *Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San*
is more indicated for headache due to wind affecting the head and face;
*Da Qin* *Jiao Tang* is more indicated for sudden deviation of the eye
and mouth, tongue stiffness and an inability to move one's hand and foot
due to wind stroke of meridians coupled with deficiency of
anti-pathogenic qi; *Qian Zheng San* is more indicated for deviation of
the eye and mouth due to wind phlegm obstructing the meridians on the
head and face; *Yu Zhen San* is more indicated for lockjaw (in tetanus)
due to toxic wind attacking the body through any break in the skin;
*Xiao Huo Luo Dan* is more indicated for numbness and pain in the limbs
due to retention of wind, cold, dampness, phlegm and stasis in
meridians; and *Xiao Feng San* is more indicated for urticaria or eczema
due to wind, heat and dampness affecting the skin.

**Stopping internal wind**

Of the four formulas to stop internal wind, *Ling* *Jiao Gou Teng Tang*
is more indicated for high fever, restlessness and convulsions of the
hands/feet due to excessive heat in the liver meridian and extreme heat
generating wind; *Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang* is more indicated for wind
stroke with vertigo, tinnitus and a drunk-like complexion due to
disorder of qi and blood and hyperactivity of liver yang due to yin
deficiency of the liver and kidney; *Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin* is more
indicated for dizziness and headache due to hyperactive liver yang
transforming into wind; and *Da Ding Feng Zhu* is more indicated for
involuntary convulsions of the hands/feet due to severe yin deficiency
and subsequent wind stirring in later stage febrile disease.

| the formula.                                                          |
|                                                                       |

+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 14 Formulas that Treat Dryness**

Formulas that treat dryness help to dispel external dryness with
pungent, light-weight ingredients or eliminate internal dryness with
sweet, cold and yin-nourishing ingredients.

Dryness can be external or internal. External dryness is one of the six
pathogenic factors. It is prevalent in autumn and often classified into
cool dryness and warm dryness. Because dryness easily consumes fluids,
in addition to fever and chills, patients may present with a dry
mouth/nose and sore throat, and unproductive coughs or coughs with
scanty, sticky phlegm. Internal dryness results from insufficient fluids
in zang-fu organs. It can be classified into dryness in the upper
*jiao*, dryness in the middle *jiao* and dryness in the lower *jiao*.
Lung dryness may cause unproductive coughs. Stomach dryness may cause
retching and a poor appetite. Dryness involving the kidney and large
intestine may cause wasting & thirsting or constipation. Formulas that
dryness are subdivided into two types---formulas to dispel external
dryness and formulas to nourish yin and moisten internal dryness.

It's essential to differentiate external dryness from internal dryness.
The treatment strategy for external dryness is to dispel dryness with
light-weight ingredients, whereas the treatment strategy for internal
dryness is to nourish yin. As zang-fu organs are mutually connected, in
clinical settings, complicated dryness may be present. It's important to
identify patterns prior to treatment.

Since moistening ingredients may obstruct the flow of qi, patients with
spleen deficiency or constitutional dampness should use formulas that
treat dryness with caution. In addition, it's not advisable to use
pungent, fragrant, bitter and cold ingredients to treat dryness.

**14.1 Formulas that disperse external dryness**

## Formulas that disperse external dryness are indicated for external contraction of cool dryness or warm dryness. Clinical manifestations of cool dryness affecting the lung may include chills, headache, cough, nasal obstruction and a dry mouth and throat. Common ingredients are *Su Ye* (Folium Perillae), *Jie Geng* (Radix Platycodonis), *Qian Hu* (Radix Peucedani) and *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum). A typical formula is *Xing Su San* (Apricot Kernel and Perilla Powder). Clinical manifestations of warm dryness affecting the lung may include fever, headache, a dry mouth and nose, unproductive cough, panting, restlessness and thirst. Common ingredients are *Sang Ye* (Folium Mori), *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum), *Sha Shen* (Radix Adenophorae seu Glehniae) and *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis). Typical formulas are *Sang Xing Tang* (Mulberry Leaf and Apricot Kernel Decoction) and *Qing Zao Jiu Fei Tang* (Dryness-Clearing and Lung-Saving Decoction).  {#formulas-that-disperse-external-dryness-are-indicated-for-external-contraction-of-cool-dryness-or-warm-dryness.-clinical-manifestations-of-cool-dryness-affecting-the-lung-may-include-chills-headache-cough-nasal-obstruction-and-a-dry-mouth-and-throat.-common-ingredients-are-su-ye-folium-perillae-jie-geng-radix-platycodonis-qian-hu-radix-peucedani-and-xing-ren-semen-armeniacae-amarum.-a-typical-formula-is-xing-su-san-apricot-kernel-and-perilla-powder.-clinical-manifestations-of-warm-dryness-affecting-the-lung-may-include-fever-headache-a-dry-mouth-and-nose-unproductive-cough-panting-restlessness-and-thirst.-common-ingredients-are-sang-ye-folium-mori-xing-ren-semen-armeniacae-amarum-sha-shen-radix-adenophorae-seu-glehniae-and-mai-dong-radix-ophiopogonis.-typical-formulas-are-sang-xing-tang-mulberry-leaf-and-apricot-kernel-decoction-and-qing-zao-jiu-fei-tang-dryness-clearing-and-lung-saving-decoction. .unnumbered}

***Xing Su San***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 苏叶 | *sū yè* | 6g  | Folium Perillae                    |
| archs |        |         |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 杏仁 | *xìng   | 6g  | Semen Armeniacae Amarum            |
|       |        | rén*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 前胡 | *qián   | 6g  | Radix Peucedani                    |
| sters |        | hú*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 桔梗 | *jié    | 6g  | Radix Platycodonis                 |
|       |        | gĕng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 枳壳 | *zhĭ    | 6g  | Fructus Aurantii                   |
|       |        | qiào*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 半夏 | *bàn    | 6g  | Rhizoma Pinelliae                  |
| tants |        | xià*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 橘皮 | *jú pí* | 6g  | Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae      |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 6g  | Poria                              |
|       |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 甘草 | *gān    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| E     | > 生姜 | *shēng  | 6g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens          |
| nvoys |        | jiāng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 大枣 | *dà     | 2 p | Fructus Jujubae                    |
|       |        | zăo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Su Ye* is warm, pungent but not dry. It disperses lung qi and
expels cool dryness. *Xing Ren* is bitter, warm and moist. It bears down
lung qi, stops coughing and resolves phlegm.

Ministers: *Qian Hu* dispels wind, bears qi down, resolves phlegm and
assists *Xing Ren* and *Su Ye* in resolving phlegm. *Jie Geng* and *Zhi
Qiao* assist *Xing Ren* in dispersing lung qi.

Assistants: *Ban Xia, Ju Pi* and *Fu Ling* regulate qi and resolve
phlegm. *Gan Cao* works in combination with *Jie Geng* to disperse the
lung and resolve phlegm.

Envoys: *Sheng Jiang* and *Da Zao* harmonize the Ying-nutrients and
Wei-defense and circulate fluids.

This formula uses pungent, bitter and mildly warm ingredients to
disperse the lung, bear down lung qi, and at the same time, resolve
phlegm and stop coughing.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Disperses cool dryness, regulates the lung and
resolves phlegm.

**\[Indications\]** External contraction of cool dryness: Mild headache,
chills, absence of sweating, coughing with thin phlegm, nasal
obstruction, a dry throat, a dry, white coating and a wiry, tight pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for cool dryness.
Cool dryness affecting the descending of lung qi and obstructing the
skin pores may cause chills, absence of sweating, and headache. Cool
dryness affecting the distribution of fluids may cause cough with thin
phlegm. Since the lung opens into the nose and throat is connected to
the lung, cool dryness obstructing lung qi may cause nasal obstruction
and a dry throat. As a result, the treatment strategies are to dispel
cool dryness, disperse the lung and resolve phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chills, absence of sweating,
cough with thin phlegm, a dry throat, a white tongue coating, and a wiry
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For absence of sweating, body ache, a wiry or tight pulse due to
    wind cold affecting the exterior, add *Qiang Huo* (Rhizoma et Radix
    Notopterygii) and *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae) to release the
    exterior, promote sweating, dispel cold and alleviate pain.

-   For scanty phlegm, remove *Ban Xia* and *Fu Ling*.

3\. Modern use: Flu, chronic bronchitis and emphysema that can be
diagnosed as external cool dryness affecting lung qi or wind cold
affecting the lung coupled with internal phlegm dampness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for patients with
external contraction of warm dryness.

***Sang Xing Tang***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 桑叶 | *sāng   | 3g  | Folium Mori                        |
| archs |        | yè*     |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 杏仁 | *xìng   | 4   | Semen Armeniacae Amarum            |
|       |        | rén*    | .5g |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 豆豉 | *dòu    | 3g  | Semen Sojae Praeparatum            |
| sters |        | chĭ*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 贝母 | *bèi    | 3g  | Bulbus Fritillaria                 |
|       |        | mŭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 沙参 | *shā    | 6g  | Radix Adenophorae seu Glehniae     |
|       |        | shēn*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 栀皮 | *zhī    | 3g  | Pericarpium Gardeniae              |
| tants |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 梨皮 | *lí pí* | 6g  | Pericarpium Pyrus                  |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Sang Ye* is light in weight. It disperses dry heat. Xing Ren
bears down lung qi, moistens the lung and stops coughing.

Ministers: *Dou Chi* is warm and pungent. It releases the exterior and
assists *Sang Ye* in clearing heat. *Bei Mu* moistens the lung, clears
heat, resolves phlegm and assists *Xing Ren* in stopping coughing. *Sha
Shen* moistens the lung, generates fluids and stops coughing.

Assistants: *Zhi Pi* is light in weight and enters the upper *jiao*. It
clears lung heat. *Li Pi* clears heat, moistens dryness, stops coughing
and resolves phlegm.

This formula clears warm dryness without damaging fluids and moistens
the lung without causing greasiness.

*Sang Xing Tang* and *Xing Su San* can both be used for cough due to
external dryness. *Xing Su San* is indicated for abnormal fluids
distribution due to external cool dryness. It uses *Xing Ren* and *Su
Ye* as monarchs and combines lung-dispersing and phlegm-resolving
ingredients, known as the bitter, warm, sweet and pungent method. *Sang
Xing Tang* is indicated for external warm dryness scorching lung fluids.
It uses *Xing Ren* and *Sang Ye* as monarchs and combines ingredients to
clear heat, moisten dryness, generate fluids and stop coughing, known as
the pungent, cool, sweet and moist method.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Disperses warm dryness, moistens the lung and stops
coughing.

**\[Indications\]** External contraction of warm dryness: Headache,
low-grade fever, thirst, a dry throat and nose, unproductive coughs or
cough with scanty, sticky phlegm, a red tongue with a dry, thin and
white coating and a superficial, rapid and large (on the right side)
pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for mild warm
dryness affecting the lung. Warm dryness affecting the dispersing and
descending of lung qi may cause unproductive coughs, a dry throat,
thirst, or coughs with scanty, sticky phlegm. Since the lung dominates
the skin and skin hair, mild warm dryness affecting the exterior may
cause low-grade fever and headache. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to dispel warm dryness, moisten the lung and stop
coughing. .

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Low-grade fever, unproductive
coughs or coughs with scanty, sticky phlegm, and a red tongue with a
dry, thin coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For high-grade fever, add *Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae Japonicae) and
    *Lian Qiao* (Fructus Forsythiae) to clear heat.

-   For severe cough due to ascending of lung qi, add *Bai Bu* (Radix
    Stemonae) and *Pi Pa Ye* (Folium Eriobotryae) to bear down lung qi
    and stop coughing.

3\. Modern use: Upper respiratory fact infection, acute bronchitis,
hemoptysis due to bronchiectasis, and whooping cough that can be
diagnosed as external warm dryness scorching lung fluids.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is indicated for mild
conditions. Considering the small doses of ingredients in this formula,
it's not advisable to decoct for a long period of time. Just as the
original remarks in the text, "Light-weight ingredients shall not be
used in large doses; otherwise, they may exceed the affected site".

***Qing Zao Jiu Fei Tang***

*Yi Men Fa Lǜ* (Precepts for Physicians)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 桑叶 | *sāng   | 9g  | Folium Mori                        |
| narch |        | yè*     |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 石膏 | *shí    | 8g  | Gypsum Fibrosum                    |
| sters |        | gāo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 4g  | Radix Ophiopogonis                 |
|       |        | dōng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 杏仁 | *xìng   | 2g  | Semen Armeniacae Amarum            |
| tants |        | rén*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *pí pá  | 3g  | Folium Eriobotryae                 |
|       | 枇杷叶 | yè*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 阿胶 | *ē      | 3g  | Colla Corii Asini                  |
|       |        | jiāo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *hú má  | 3g  | Fructus Cannabis                   |
|       | 胡麻仁 | rén*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 人参 | *rén    | 2g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng           |
|       |        | shēn*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 甘草 | *gān    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Sang Ye* disperses dryness.

Ministers: *Shi Gao* and *Mai Dong* clear heat in the lung meridian and
moisten the lung.

Assistants: *Xing Ren* and *Pi Pa Ye* regulate lung qi. *E* *Jiao* and
*Hu Ma Ren* moisten lung yin. *Ren Shen* and *Gan Cao* supplement qi and
harmonize the middle *jiao*.

This formula integrates five treating strategies---dispersing, clearing,
moistening, reinforcing and draining downward. It clears dry heat,
nourish lung qi and yin and bear lung qi down. That's why it gets the
name to "clear dryness and save the lung".

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears dryness and moistens the lung.

**\[Indications\]** Warm dryness damaging the lung: Headache, fever,
unproductive coughs, panting, a dry throat, mouth and nose, chest
fullness, pain in the hypochondriac region, restlessness, thirst, a dry
tongue with no coating and a deficient, large and rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for severe
dryness damaging the lung. Dry heat affecting the exterior may cause
headache and fever. Dry heat damaging lung qi and yin may cause
unproductive coughs, panting, thirst and a dry throat and nose. Failure
of lung qi to descend may cause chest fullness. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to clear dry heat, supplement qi and nourish
yin.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, unproductive coughs,
panting, a red tongue with a scanty coating and a deficient, large and
rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For profuse, sticky phlegm, add *Bei Mu* (Bulbus Fritillaria), *Gua
    Lou* (Fructus Trichosanthis) and *Tian Hua Fen* (Radix
    Trichosanthis) to moisten the lung and resolve phlegm.

-   For high-grade fever, add *Sheng Shi Gao* (Gypsum Fibrosum) and
    *Ling Yang* *Jiao* (Cornu Saigae Tataricae) to clear heat and remove
    toxins.

3\. Modern use: Pneumonia, bronchial asthma, acute/chronic bronchitis,
emphysema and lung cancer that can be diagnosed as dry heat damaging
lung qi and yin.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for patients with
chest fullness and internal phlegm dampness due to spleen deficiency.

**14.2 Formulas that nourish yin and moisten internal dryness**

## Formulas that nourish yin and moisten internal dryness are indicated for fluids insufficiency in the Zang-fu organs. Clinical manifestation of the upper *jiao* dryness may include unproductive coughing, a sore throat or hemoptysis due to lung dryness and yin damage. The treatment strategy is primarily to moisten the lung. Common ingredients are *Bai He* (Bulbus Lilii), *Tian Dong* (Radix Asparagi), *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) and *Sha Shen* (Radix Adenophorae seu Glehniae). Clinical manifestation of the middle *jiao* dryness may include fast hunger after eating food, a dry mouth, thirst or difficulty swallowing due to stomach dryness and yin damage. The treatment strategy is to nourish stomach yin. Common ingredients are *Yu Zhu* (Rhizoma Polygonati Odorati), *Shi Hu* (Caulis Dendrobii) and *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis). Clinical manifestations of the lower *jiao* dryness may include thirst, a dry throat, flushed cheeks, restlessness or constipation. The treatment strategy is to nourish the kidney. Common ingredients are *Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae), *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata) and *Bai Mi* (white honey). Typical formulas are *Yang Yin Qing Fei Tang* (Yin-Nourishing and Lung-Clearing Decoction), *Bai He Gu Jin Tang* (Lily Bulb Metal-Securing Decoction), *Mai Men Dong Tang* (Ophiopogon Decoction), *Yi Wei Tang* (Stomach-Benefiting Decoction) and *Zeng Ye Tang* (Fluids-Increasing Decoction).  {#formulas-that-nourish-yin-and-moisten-internal-dryness-are-indicated-for-fluids-insufficiency-in-the-zang-fu-organs.-clinical-manifestation-of-the-upper-jiao-dryness-may-include-unproductive-coughing-a-sore-throat-or-hemoptysis-due-to-lung-dryness-and-yin-damage.-the-treatment-strategy-is-primarily-to-moisten-the-lung.-common-ingredients-are-bai-he-bulbus-lilii-tian-dong-radix-asparagi-mai-dong-radix-ophiopogonis-and-sha-shen-radix-adenophorae-seu-glehniae.-clinical-manifestation-of-the-middle-jiao-dryness-may-include-fast-hunger-after-eating-food-a-dry-mouth-thirst-or-difficulty-swallowing-due-to-stomach-dryness-and-yin-damage.-the-treatment-strategy-is-to-nourish-stomach-yin.-common-ingredients-are-yu-zhu-rhizoma-polygonati-odorati-shi-hu-caulis-dendrobii-and-mai-dong-radix-ophiopogonis.-clinical-manifestations-of-the-lower-jiao-dryness-may-include-thirst-a-dry-throat-flushed-cheeks-restlessness-or-constipation.-the-treatment-strategy-is-to-nourish-the-kidney.-common-ingredients-are-sheng-di-radix-rehmanniae-shu-di-radix-rehmanniae-praeparata-and-bai-mi-white-honey.-typical-formulas-are-yang-yin-qing-fei-tang-yin-nourishing-and-lung-clearing-decoction-bai-he-gu-jin-tang-lily-bulb-metal-securing-decoction-mai-men-dong-tang-ophiopogon-decoction-yi-wei-tang-stomach-benefiting-decoction-and-zeng-ye-tang-fluids-increasing-decoction. .unnumbered}

***Yang Yin Qing Fei Tang***

*Chong Lou Yu Yao* (Medical Text on Laryngology by Zheng Honggang)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| Mo    | > 生地  | *shēng  | **6**g   | Radix Rehmanniae            |
| narch |         | dì*     |          |                             |
+=======+=========+=========+==========+=============================+
| Mini  | > 麦冬  | *mài    | 4g       | Radix Ophiopogonis          |
| sters |         | dōng*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 白芍  | *bái    | 3g       | Radix Paeoniae Alba         |
|       |         | sháo*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 玄参  | *xuán   | 5g       | Radix Scrophulariae         |
|       |         | shēn*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| Assis | > 贝母  | *bèi    | 3g       | Bulbus Fritillaria          |
| tants |         | mŭ*     |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 丹皮  | *dān    | 3        | Cortex Moutan               |
|       |         | pí*     |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 薄荷  | *bò hé* | 2        | Herba Menthae               |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| >     | > 甘草  | *gān    | 3g       | Radix et Rhizoma            |
| Envoy |         | căo*    |          | Glycyrrhizae                |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: Sheng Di nourishes kidney and moistens the lung.

Ministers: *Mai Dong* nourishes yin, moistens the lung, benefits the
stomach and generates fluids. *Bai Shao* astringes yin, nourishes blood,
harmonizes the Ying-nutrients and clears heat. *Xuan Shen* clears
deficiency fire and removes toxins.

Assistants: *Bei Mu* moistens the lung, resolves phlegm, clears heat and
disperses stagnation. *Dan Pi* clears heat, cools blood, resolves stasis
and eliminates swelling. *Bo He* benefits the throat and assists the
monarch and ministers in nourishing yin and clearing the lung.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* moistens the lung, removes toxins and coordinates the
other ingredients.

This formula nourishes yin of the lung and kidney, at the same time,
cools blood, eliminates swelling, removes toxins and relieves sore
throat. It is especially effective for epidemic diphtheria.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin, clears the lung, removes toxins and
benefits the throat.

**\[Indications\]** A white curd-like membrane in the throat that is
difficult to scrape off, a sore, swollen throat, absence or presence of
fever, a dry nose, chopped lips, absence or presence of coughs, rapid
breathing, panting, a rapid, weak or thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for white-curd
like membranes in the throat due to dry heat from yin deficiency.
Contributing factors include constitutional yin deficiency (internal
heat accumulation) coupled with contraction of epidemic pathogens.
Because the throat is connected to the lung, the kidney meridian reaches
the base of the tongue, yin deficiency of the lung and kidney may cause
deficiency fire to ascend. This coupled with contraction of epidemic
pathogens may cause a white-curd like membrane in the throat, a sore,
swollen throat, a dry nose and chopped lips. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to nourish yin, clear the lung, remove toxins and benefit
the throat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: A white curd-like membrane in the
throat that is difficult to scrape off, a sore, swollen throat, a dry
nose, chopped lips and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modification:

-   For a weak constitution, add *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata).

-   For severe heat, remove *Bai Shao* and add *Lian Qiao* (Fructus
    Forsythiae).

-   For severe dryness, add *Tian Dong* (Radix Asparagi) and *Fu Ling*
    (Poria).

-   For sore, swollen throat, an insufflation formula can be used in
    combination: *Qing Guo Tan* (Fructus Canarii Carbonisatus) 6g,
    *Huang Bai* (Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis), 3g, *Chuan Bei Mu*
    (Bulbus Fritillariae Cirrhosae) 3g, *Bing Pian* (Borneolum
    Syntheticum) 1.5g, *Er Cha* (Catechu) 3g, *Bo He* (Herba Menthae)
    3g, and *Feng Huang Yi* (Endothelium Corneum Gigeriae Galli) 1.5g

3\. Modern use: Acute tonsillitis, acute pharyngitis and nasopharyngeal
carcinoma that can be diagnosed as dry heat due to yin deficiency.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: As constitutional yin deficiency may cause build-up of internal heat, it's not advisable to use diaphoretic formulas to release the exterior, especially the warm, pungent formulas; otherwise, they may consume more yin fluids and aggravate deficiency heat.  {#cautions-contraindications-as-constitutional-yin-deficiency-may-cause-build-up-of-internal-heat-its-not-advisable-to-use-diaphoretic-formulas-to-release-the-exterior-especially-the-warm-pungent-formulas-otherwise-they-may-consume-more-yin-fluids-and-aggravate-deficiency-heat. .unnumbered}

***Bai He Gu Jin Tang***

*Shen Zhai Yi Shu* (Medical Text by Zhou Zhigan)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| Mon   | > 百合  | *băi    | 4.5g     | Bulbus Lilii                |
| archs |         | hé*     |          |                             |
+=======+=========+=========+==========+=============================+
|       | > 生地  | *shēng  | 9g       | Radix Rehmanniae            |
|       |         | dì*     |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 熟地  | *shú    | 9g       | Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata |
|       |         | dì*     |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| Mini  | > 麦冬  | *mài    | 4.5g     | Radix Ophiopogonis          |
| sters |         | dōng*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 玄参  | *xuán   | 2g       | Radix Scrophulariae         |
|       |         | shēn*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| Assis | > 当归  | *dāng   | 9g       | Radix Angelicae Sinensis    |
| tants |         | guī*    |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 白芍  | *bái    | 3g       | Radix Paeoniae Alba         |
|       |         | sháo*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 贝母  | *bèi    | 4.5g     | Bulbus Fritillaria          |
|       |         | mŭ*     |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
| E     | > 桔梗  | *jié    | 2g       | Radix Platycodonis          |
| nvoys |         | gĕng*   |          |                             |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+
|       | > 甘草  | *gān    | 3g       | Radix et Rhizoma            |
|       |         | căo*    |          | Glycyrrhizae                |
+-------+---------+---------+----------+-----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Bai He* nourishes yin, clears heat, moistens the lung, and
stops coughing. *Sheng Di* and *Shu Di* nourish the kidney. Sheng Di
also cools blood and stops bleeding.

Ministers: *Mai Dong* is sweet and cold. It nourishes yin, clears heat,
moistens the lung, and stops coughing. *Xuan Shen* is salty and cold. It
nourishes yin, clears deficiency fire and benefits the throat.

Assistants: *Dang Gui* moistens the lung and bears qi down. *Bai Shao*
nourishes and circulates blood. *Bei Mu* clears heat, moistens the lung,
resolves phlegm, and stops coughing.

Envoys: *Jie Geng* disperses the lung, benefits the throat, resolves
phlegm, and guides the other ingredients to ascend. *Gan Cao* clears
heat, purges fire and coordinates the other ingredients.

Compatibility characteristics: 1) this formula nourishes the kidney,
moistens the lung and stops coughing; 2) in addition to nourishing the
lung and kidney, this formula cools blood, stops bleeding, disperses
lung and resolves phlegm.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin, moistens the lung, resolves phlegm, and
stops coughing.

**\[Indications\]** Ascending of deficiency fire due to yin deficiency
of the lung and kidney: Cough with hemoptysis, a dry, sore throat,
feverish sensations on the palms and soles, hot flushes, night sweats, a
red tongue with a scanty coating and a thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for cough and
hemoptysis resulting from ascending of deficiency fire due to yin
deficiency of the lung and kidney. Yin deficiency may cause internal
heat. Ascending of deficiency fire may cause a dry, sore throat.
Deficiency fire scorching lung collaterals may cause cough and
hemoptysis. Feverish sensations on the palms and soles, bone-steaming
fever, night sweats, a red tongue with a scanty coating and a thready,
rapid pulse are all signs indicative of internal heat due to yin
deficiency. As a result, the treatment strategies are to nourish yin of
the lung and kidney, clear deficiency heat, stops coughing and resolve
phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough, a dry, sore throat, a red
tongue with a scanty coating and a thready, rapid pulse.

2\. Modification: For severe hemoptysis due to major damage to lung
collaterals, remove Jie Geng and add *Xian He Cao* (Herba Agrimoniae),
*Bai Ji* (Rhizoma Bletillae) and *Ou Jie* (Nodus Nelumbinis Rhizomatis)
to clear heat, cool blood and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Pulmonary tuberculosis, chronic bronchitis, hemoptysis
due to bronchiectasis, chronic pharyngitis and spontaneous pneumothorax
that can be diagnosed as yin deficiency of the lung and kidney.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with loose stools and a poor appetite due to spleen deficiency.

***Mai Men Dong Tang***

*Jin Gui Yao* *Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Monarch  | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 70g | Radix Ophiopogonis              |
|          |        | dōng*   |     |                                 |
+==========+========+=========+=====+=================================+
| Minister | > 人参 | *rén    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng        |
|          |        | shēn*   |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| As       | > 粳米 | *jīng   | 5g  | Oryza Sativa L.                 |
| sistants |        | mĭ*     |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|          | > 半夏 | *bàn    | 10g | Rhizoma Pinelliae               |
|          |        | xià*    |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
|          | > 大枣 | *dà     | 4 P | Fructus Jujubae                 |
|          |        | zăo*    |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Assista  | > 甘草 | *gān    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae   |
| nt/envoy |        | căo*    |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Mai Dong* is sweet, cold and moist and enters the lung and
stomach meridians. It nourishes yin, generates fluids and moistens
dryness.

Minister: *Ren Shen* supplements qi and generates fluids.

Assistants: *Gan Cao*, *Jing Mi* and *Da Zao* benefit stomach qi and
nourish stomach yin. Although *Ban Xia* is warm and dry, it harmonizes
the stomach and bears qi down when used in combination with a large dose
of *Mai Dong*.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* moistens the lung, benefits the throat and coordinates
the other ingredients.

This formula tonifies qi and yin, moistens the lung, benefits lung qi
and stops coughing. A large dose of sweet, cold and moist *Mai Dong* is
used (to nourish lung/stomach yin) in combination with a small dose of
dry, pungent *Ban Xia* (to bear qi down) to balance between moistening
and dryness and to dry without damaging yin. Lung atrophy is located in
the lung; however, the root cause lies in the stomach, as the earth
(spleen/stomach) promotes metal (lung), the formula treats both the lung
and stomach according to the principle of "reinforcing the mother for
deficiency syndrome".

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes the lung and stomach, bears qi down and
harmonize the middle *jiao*.

**\[Indications\]**

-   Lung atrophy due to adverse qi flow and yin deficiency of the lung
    stomach: Cough with phlegm, shortness of breath, panting, a dry
    throat, a dry, red tongue with a scanty coating and a deficient,
    rapid pulse.

-   Stomach yin deficiency: Vomiting, hiccups, a poor appetite, thirst,
    a dry throat, a red tongue with a scanty coating and a deficient,
    rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for lung atrophy
due to adverse flow of qi and yin damage of the lung and stomach. Qi and
yin damage of the lung and stomach may affect the descending of lung qi
and cause cough. Failure of the lung to regulate water passages and
deficiency heat scorching fluids may cause turbid phlegm. Stomach yin
deficiency may affect the normal function of the stomach and descending
of stomach qi, thus causing vomiting, hiccups and a poor appetite. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to nourish qi/yin of the lung and
stomach and bear qi down.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough with phlegm, shortness of
breath, panting, a dry throat, and a red tongue with a scanty coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe yin damage in lung atrophy, add *Bei Shao Shen* (Radix
    Glehniae), *Yu Zhu* (Rhizoma Polygonati Odorati) and *Sang Ye*
    (Folium Mori) to nourish yin and generate fluids.

-   For stomach pain with a burning sensation due to stomach yin
    deficiency, add *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba), *Wu Mei* (Fructus
    Mume) and *Mu Hu Die* (Semen Oroxyli) to astringe yin, harmonize the
    stomach and alleviate pain.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis, bronchiectasis, chronic pharyngitis,
silicosis and pulmonary tuberculosis that can be diagnosed as ascending
of qi and deficiency fire due to yin deficiency of the lung and stomach.
It can also be used for gastric/duodenal ulcer and chronic atrophic
gastritis that can be diagnosed as adverse qi flow and stomach yin
deficiency.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with cough due to cold phlegm accumulating in the lung or vomiting due to deficiency cold in the spleen and stomach.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-cough-due-to-cold-phlegm-accumulating-in-the-lung-or-vomiting-due-to-deficiency-cold-in-the-spleen-and-stomach. .unnumbered}

***Yi Wei Tang***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 生地 | *shēng  | 15g | Radix Rehmanniae                   |
| archs |        | dì*     |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 15g | Radix Ophiopogonis                 |
|       |        | dōng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | >      | *bĕi    | 9g  | Radix Glehniae                     |
| sters | 北沙参 | shā     |     |                                    |
|       |        | shēn*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 玉竹 | *yù     | 4   | Rhizoma Polygonati Odorati         |
|       |        | zhú*    | .5g |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy | > 冰糖 | *bīng*  | 3g  | Crystal sugar                      |
|       |        | *táng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Sheng Di* and *Mai Dong* are sweet and cold. They act to
nourish yin, clear heat, generate fluids and moisten dryness.

Ministers: *Bei Sha Shen* and *Yu Zhu* nourish yin, generate fluids and
enhance the efficacy of *Sheng Di* and *Mai Dong*.

Envoy: *Bing Tang* harmonizes the stomach and moistens the lung.

This formula clears heat without causing cold retention, and moistens
the lung without causing greasiness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with five cups of water into two
cups and drink in two times; and re-decoct the residue into another cup.

**\[Functions\]** Nourishes yin and benefits the stomach

**\[Indications\]** Yangming heat damaging stomach yin: Dull stomach
pain, hunger but no desire to eat food, a dry mouth and throat, a red
tongue with a scanty coating and a thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common stomach yin-nourishing
formula. Heat transformed from epidemic febrile disease may damage
fluids and cause constipation. Purgative formulas help to clear heat but
at the same time, damage stomach yin, resulting in hunger but no desire
to eat food, dull gastric pain and a dry mouth and throat. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to generate fluids, nourish yin and benefit
the stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Hunger but no desire to eat food,
a dry mouth and throat, a dry, red tongue and a thready, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For profuse sweating and shortness of breath due to qi deficiency,
    add *Dang Shen* (Radix Codonopsis) and *Wu Wei Zi* (Fructus
    Schisandrae Chinensis) (used in combination with *Sheng Mai San*) to
    supplement qi and astringe sweating.

-   For abdominal distension after eating food, add *Chen Pi*
    (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae) and *Shen Qu* (Massa Medicata
    Fermentata) to regulate qi and promote digestion.

3\. Modern use: Chronic gastritis, diabetes and poor digestion in
children that can be diagnosed as stomach yin deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with loose stools due to spleen deficiency.

**\[Annex formula\]**

*Yu Ye Tang* is a common formula for deficiency of qi and yin in chronic
wasting and thirst disorder. It generates fluids, moistens dryness,
tonifies qi, lifts yang and helps with fluids generation and
distribution. *Qiong Yu Gao* is indicated for lung dryness due to yin
deficiency.

***Zeng Ye Tang***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 玄参 | *xuán   | 30g | Radix Scrophulariae                |
| narch |        | shēn*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Min   | > 生地 | *shēng  | 24g | Radix Rehmanniae                   |
| ister |        | dì*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 麦冬 | *mài    | 24g | Radix Ophiopogonis                 |
| stant |        | dōng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Xuan Shen* is bitter, salty and cold. It clears heat,
nourishes yin, generates fluids and moistens the intestines.

Minister: *Sheng Di* is sweet, bitter and cold. It clears heat,
nourishes yin and generates fluids.

Assistant: The lung is internal-externally connected with the large
intestine. Mai Dong nourishes yin, moistens the lung and large intestine
and promote bowel movements.

This formula uses large doses of three tonic ingredients to "generate
fluids to promote bowel movements".

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Generates fluids and moistens dryness

**\[Indications\]** Yangming heat consuming fluids in the intestines:
Constipation, thirst, a dry, red tongue and a thready, rapid or deep,
weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for constipation
due to heat damaging fluids in the intestines. Heat consuming fluids in
the intestines may cause constipation. Failure of fluids to ascend may
cause thirst and a dry, red tongue. A thready, rapid or deep, weak pulse
is a sign indicative of yin deficiency or deficiency of anti-pathogenic
qi. As a result, the treatment strategies are to nourish yin, moisten
dryness and promote bowel movements.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Constipation, a dry, red tongue
and a thready, rapid or deep, weak pulse.

2\. Modification: For constipation due to severe dry heat, add *Da
Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and *Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas) to clear
heat and promote bowel movements.

3\. Modern use: Intestinal dryness, anal fissure, chronic periodontitis,
chronic pharyngitis, recurrent mouth ulcerations and diabetes that can
be diagnosed as yin deficiency.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with constipation due to kidney yang deficiency or spleen qi deficiency. {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-constipation-due-to-kidney-yang-deficiency-or-spleen-qi-deficiency. .unnumbered}

### **\[Annex formula\]** *Zeng Ye Cheng Qi Tang* (Humor-Increasing and Qi-Guiding Decoction) from the *Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases) {#annex-formula-zeng-ye-cheng-qi-tang-humor-increasing-and-qi-guiding-decoction-from-the-wen-bing-tiao-bian-systematic-differentiation-of-warm-diseases .unnumbered}

\[Ingredients\] *Xuan Shen* (Radix Scrophulariae), *Mai Dong* (Natrii
Sulfas), *Xi Sheng Di* (Radix Rehmanniae), *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma
Rhei) and *Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas)

\[Actions\] Nourishes yin, generates fluids, clears heat and promotes
bowel movements

\[Indications\] Heat damaging yin: Constipation, abdominal
fullness/distension, a dry mouth with chopped lips, a red tongue with a
yellow coating and a thready, rapid pulse.

This formula is modified from *Zeng Ye Tang* by adding *Da Huang* and
*Mang Xiao*. It nourishes yin, generates fluids and at the same time,
clears heat and promotes bowel movements.

**Summary**

There are a total of 8 formulas and 3 annex formulas in this chapter.
These formulas are classified into two categories: formulas to disperse
external dryness and formulas to nourish yin and moisten internal
dryness.

**Formulas that disperse external dryness**

These formulas are indicated for external dryness. *Xing Su San*
disperses the lung, resolves phlegm and warms and disperses cool
dryness. It is often used for external contraction of cool dryness
coupled with internal phlegm dampness. *Sang Xing Tang* and *Qing Zao
Jiu Fei Tang* clear and disperse dryness and moisten the lung. They are
both used for external contraction of warm dryness coupled with lung yin
damage. *Sang Xing Tang* is cool and pungent and more indicated for mild
lung damage by warm dryness. *Qing Zao Jiu Fei Tang* is sweet, cool and
moist. It nourishes lung yin and qi and therefore more indicated for
severe damage to lung qi and yin by warm dryness.

**Formulas that moisten internal dryness**

These formulas are sweet and cold and indicated for internal dryness.
*Yang Yin Qing Fei Tang* clears the lung, cools blood and removes
toxins. It is effective for white spots on the throat and sore throat
due to yin deficiency. *Bai He Gu Jin Tang* nourishes the kidney,
moistens the lung, clears heat and resolves phlegm. It is effective for
hemoptysis due to ascending of deficiency fire (yin deficiency of the
lung and kidney). *Mai Men Dong Tang* clears and nourishes the lung and
stomach, and bears qi down. It is effective for stomach yin deficiency
and lung atrophy due to adverse qi flow and yin deficiency of the lung
and stomach. *Yi Wei Tang* is a typical stomach yin-nourishing formula.
It is indicated for Yangming heat damaging stomach yin. *Zeng Ye Tang*
is indicated for constipation due to Yangming heat consuming fluids.

**Review Questions**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. How do you choose between *Sang Xing Tang* and *Qing Zao Jiu Fei  |
| Tang* in treating warm-dryness damaging the lung?                     |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. Describe the similarities and differences between *Yang Yin Qing  |
| Fei Tang* and *Bai He Gu Jin Tang* in ingredients, actions and        |
| indications.                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. How do you understand indications and compatibility               |
| characteristics of *Mai Men Dong Tang*? Why is dry, warm and pungent  |
| *Ban Xia* combined in the formula?                                    |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**\
**

**Chapter 15 Formulas that Resolve Dampness**

Formulas that resolve dampness resolve water retention and relieve
stranguria. They are indicated for water dampness syndrome and fall
under the category of reducing strategy *(Xiao Fa*).

Dampness can be external or internal. External dampness is located in
the surface of the body. It results from pathogenic dampness affecting
the exterior, meridians, muscles/bones and joints. Clinical
manifestations may include chills, fever, headache, body heaviness,
muscle ache or facial puffiness. Internal dampness is located in
internal organs. Its contributing factors include improper diet or yang
deficiency of the spleen and kidney. Clinical manifestations may include
epigastric stuffiness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, generalized edema or
jaundice. Dampness may transform into cold or heat. It can be diagnosed
into deficiency or excess. In addition, it tends to combine itself to
wind and summer heat. In terms of treatment, these formulas are
categorized into five types---formulas to resolve dampness and harmonize
the stomach, formulas to clear heat and resolve dampness, formulas to
promote urination and resolve dampness, formulas to warm and transform
cold dampness and formulas to remove wind and resolve dampness.

Mostly composed of pungent, aromatic, warm and dry or sweet, bland
ingredients, formulas that resolve dampness easily damage yin fluids. In
addition, pungent or aromatic ingredients may consume qi.
Downward-draining ingredients may damage the fetus. Consequently,
pregnant women or patients with constitutional yin deficiency or qi
deficiency after chronic disease should use these formulas with caution.
As dampness may obstruct the flow of qi, qi-regulating ingredients are
often combined in formulas that resolve dampness in order to transform
dampness along with transforming qi.

**15.1 Formulas that resolve dampness with aroma**

Formulas that resolve dampness with aroma are indicated for dampness
obstructing the qi flow of the spleen and stomach. Clinical
manifestations may include abdominal fullness, belching, acid reflux,
vomiting, diarrhea, a poor appetite, fatigue, and a white, greasy tongue
coating. Common aromatic ingredients like *Huo Xiang* (Herba
Agastachis), *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis) and *Hou Po* (Cortex
Magnoliae Officinalis) are often used in combination with
spleen-fortifying and stomach-harmonizing ingredients like *Chen Pi*
(Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Da Fu Pi* (Pericarpium Arecae), *Bai
Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae) and *Fu Ling* (Poria). Typical
formulas are *Ping Wei San* (Stomach-Calming Powder) and *Huo Xiang
Zheng Qi San* (Agastache Qi-Correcting Powder).

***Ping Wei San***

*Jian Yao Ji Zhong Fang* (Simple Formulas to Aid the General Public)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 苍术 | *cāng  | 12g   | Rhizoma Atractylodis             |
| narch |        | zhú*   |       |                                  |
+=======+========+========+=======+==================================+
| Min   | > 厚朴 | *hòu   | 9g    | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis     |
| ister |        | pò*    |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 陈皮 | *chén  | 6g    | Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae    |
| stant |        | pí*    |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Envoy | >      | *zhì   | 3g    | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
|       | 炙甘草 | gān    |       | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|       |        | căo*   |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Cang Zhu* is aromatic, pungent, bitter, dry and warm. It dries
and transforms dampness and benefits the spleen.

Minister: *Hou Po* dries dampness, circulates qi and relieves fullness.

*Cang Zhu* and *Hou Po* mutually reinforce each other to dry dampness,
resolve dampness, move qi and harmonize the spleen and stomach.

Assistant: *Chen Pi* regulates qi, resolves stagnation, dries dampness
and benefits the spleen. It assists *Cang Zhu* and *Hou Po* in
fortifying the spleen, resolving dampness, moving qi and harmonizing the
spleen and stomach.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* harmonizes the spleen and stomach and coordinates
the other ingredients.

Ginger and Chinese dates are combined to decoct with water.

Compatibility characteristics: This formula dries dampness to fortify
the spleen, and moves qi to resolve phlegm dampness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder for each dose with 2
pieces of fresh ginger and 2 Chinese dates, 2 doses a day.
Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Dries dampness, fortifies the spleen, moves qi and
harmonizes the stomach

**\[Indications\]** Damp retention in the spleen and stomach: Abdominal
fullness/distension, a poor appetite, tastelessness, nausea, vomiting,
belching, acid reflux, body heaviness, lassitude, diarrhea, a white,
thick and greasy tongue coating, and a delayed pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for damp
retention in the spleen and stomach. Dampness affecting the reception,
digestion, transportation and transformation of the spleen and stomach
may cause a poor appetite, tastelessness, belching, acid reflux, and
diarrhea. Dampness obstructing the qi flow of the spleen and stomach may
cause abdominal fullness/distension, body heaviness and lassitude. A
white, thick and greasy tongue coating indicates dampness. As a result,
the treatment strategies are to dry dampness, fortify the spleen, move
qi, and harmonize the stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal fullness/distension, a
greasy, sticky mouth, tastelessness and a white, thick and greasy tongue
coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For a bitter mouth and yellow, greasy tongue coating due to dampness
    transforming into heat, add *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis) and
    *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) to clear heat and dry dampness.

-   For abdominal pain with a cold sensation due to dampness
    transforming into cold, add *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and
    *Cao Dou Kou* (Semen Alpiniae Katsumadai) to warm and transform cold
    dampness.

-   For severe diarrhea, add *Zhu Ling* (Polyporus), *Fu Ling* (Poria)
    and *Ze Xie* (Rhizoma Alismatis) to drain dampness and stop
    diarrhea.

3\. Modern use: Acute or chronic gastroenteritis, gastrointestinal
neurosis, and gastric or duodenal ulcer that can be diagnosed as cold
dampness affecting the qi flow of the spleen and stomach.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As ingredients in this formula are
pungent, bitter, warm and dry, it's contraindicated for pregnant women
or patients with yin/qi deficiency.

**\[Annex formulas\]**

| ormula** | omposition** |              |                           |

| n Jin Zh | Zhu, Chen    | harmonizes   | with exterior cold        |
| eng Qi S | Pi, Ban Xia, | the stomach  | syndrome: Vomiting,       |

| -huan-ji |              |              | white, greasy tongue      |
| n-zheng- |              |              | coating                   |

| ai Ping  | Shen, Ban    | Shaoyang,    | ache, heaviness of        |
| San (*Ji | Xia, Huang   | resolves     | hands/feet, severe        |
| ng Yue Q | Qin, Gan     | dampness and | chills, mild fever and a  |
| uan Shu* | Cao, Chen    | harmonizes   | soft pulse                |
| ) {#chai | Pi, Hou Po,  | the stomach  |                           |

| n-jing-y | Sheng Jiang  |              |                           |
| ue-quan- | and Da Zao   |              |                           |
