*Bu Huan Jin Zheng Qi San* is modified from *Ping Wei San* by adding
*Huo Xiang* and *Ban Xia*. It resolves dampness, harmonizes the stomach,
bears qi down, and stops vomiting. In addition, it dispels cold and
releases the exterior. Consequently it's indicated for damp retention in
the middle *jiao* coupled with exterior cold syndrome. *Chai Ping San*
is combined *Xiao Chai Hu Tang* and *Ping Wei San*. It harmonizes
Shaoyang, dries dampness and harmonizes the stomach. It is indicated for
pathogenic factors affecting Shaoyang and damp retention in the middle
*jiao*. Patients may present with general ache, severe chills, mild
fever, heaviness of the hands/feet and a soft pulse (damp malaria).

***Huo Xiang Zheng Qi San***

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 藿香 | *huò    | 9g  | Herba Agastachis                   |
| narch |        | xiāng*  |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 紫苏 | *zĭ sū* | 3g  | Folium Perillae                    |
| sters |        |         |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 白芷 | *bái    | 3g  | Radix Angelicae Dahuricae          |
|       |        | zhĭ*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 半夏 | *bàn    | 6g  | Rhizoma Pinelliae                  |
|       |        | xià*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 6g  | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis       |
|       |        | pò*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 陈皮 | *chén   | 6g  | Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae      |
| tants |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *dà fù  | 3g  | Pericarpium Arecae                 |
|       | 大腹皮 | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 白术 | *bái    | 6g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae |
|       |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 3g  | Poria                              |
|       |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 桔梗 | *jié    | 6g  | Radix Platycodonis                 |
|       |        | gĕng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy | >      | *zhì    | 7   | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       | 炙甘草 | gān     | .5g | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Huo Xiang* is aromatic, pungent and warm. It dispels wind
cold, releases the exterior, transforms dampness, removes turbidity,
harmonizes the middle *jiao*, and stops vomiting. It is an essential
ingredient for vomiting or diarrhea in cholera.

Ministers: *Zi Su* dispels cold, releases the exterior, regulates qi and
harmonizes the middle *jiao*. *Bai Zhi* dispels wind cold and resolves
dampness. *Ban Xia* dries dampness, resolves stagnation, bears qi down
and harmonizes the stomach. *Hou Po* benefits the spleen, resolves
dampness, moves qi and regulates the spleen and stomach.

Assistants: *Chen Pi* regulates qi and dries dampness. *Da Fu Pi* moves
qi and transforms dampness. *Bai Zhu* and *Fu Ling* fortify the spleen
and resolve dampness. *Jie Geng* soothes the chest, disperses the lung
and releases the exterior.

*Sheng Jiang* and *Da Zao* are combined to regulate the spleen and
stomach. *Sheng Jiang* harmonizes the middle *jiao* and stops vomiting.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients.

This formula treats both the root cause and symptoms. In addition, it
dispels cold, releases the exterior, fortifies the spleen, and resolves
dampness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder with 3 pieces of fresh
ginger and 1 Chinese date, 3 doses a day; 0.45g/capsule, 2-4 capsules
for each dose, and two doses a day. Alternatively, decoct with water and
add 3 pieces of fresh ginger and 1 Chinese date.

**\[Functions\]** Releases the exterior, resolves dampness, regulates qi
and harmonizes the middle *jiao*.

**\[Indications\]** Exterior wind cold coupled with internal damp
retention: Cholera (vomiting/diarrhea), alternating chills and fever,
headache, abdominal pain, chest fullness, a white, greasy tongue
coating, and a superficial or soft, delayed pulse. It is also indicated
for malignant malaria.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for external
contraction of wind cold coupled with internal damp retention. Wind cold
binding the exterior and struggle between anti-pathogenic qi and
pathogenic factors may cause alternating chills and fever, headache and
a superficial pulse. Dampness obstructing the qi flow of the spleen and
stomach may cause abdominal pain, chest fullness, vomiting or diarrhea.
A white, greasy tongue coating and a soft, delayed pulse are signs
indicative of internal dampness. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to dispel wind cold, resolve phlegm-dampness, regulate qi and
harmonize the middle *jiao*.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Alternating chills and fever,
abdominal pain/distension, vomiting, diarrhea, a white, greasy tongue
coating, and a superficial pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For alternating chills and fever without sweats, add *Xiang Ru*
    (Herba Moslae) or increase the dose of *Su Ye* to release the
    exterior and dispel cold.

-   For abdominal pain/distension due to severe qi stagnation, add *Mu
    Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) and *Sha Ren* (Fructus Amomi) to move qi
    and alleviate pain.

-   For a white, thick and greasy coating due to severe dampness,
    replace *Ba Zhu* with *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis) to enhance
    the effect of resolving dampness.

3\. Modern use: Acute gastroenteritis, stomach flu, flu in summer months
that can be diagnosed as damp retention in the spleen and stomach
coupled with external contraction of wind cold.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Because ingredients in this formula
are pungent, dry, warm and aromatic, it is contraindicated for patients
with vomiting or diarrhea due to damp heat. In addition, it's advisable
to decoct with a strong fire for a short period of time because of the
aromatic and dispersing properties of formula ingredients.

**15.2 Formulas that clear heat and resolve dampness**

Formulas that clear heat and resolve dampness are indicated for
damp-heat syndrome, including external damp heat, internal retention of
damp heat, or damp heat, jaundice, cholera, heat stranguria, dysentery,
diarrhea, *Wei-*flaccidity or *Bi*-impediment due to downward flow of
damp heat. Clinical manifestations may include a red tongue with a
yellow, greasy coating and a slippery, rapid pulse. Common heat-clearing
and damp-resolving ingredients like *Yin Chen* (Herba Artemisiae
Scopariae), *Hua Shi* (Talcum) and *Yi Yi Ren* (Semen Coicis) are often
used in combination with heat-clearing and heat-drying ingredients like
*Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis), *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and
*Huang Bai* (Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis) as well as lung-dispersing,
spleen-fortifying and stomach-harmonizing ingredients like *Xing Ren*
(Semen Armeniacae Amarum), *Bai Kou Ren* (Fructus Amomi Rotundus), *Hou
Po* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) and *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri
Reticulatae). Typical formulas are *Yin Chen Hao Tang* (Three Kernels
Decoction), *Ba Zheng San* (Eight Corrections Powder), *San Ren Tang*
(Three Kernels Decoction), *Gan Lu Xiao Du Dan* (Sweet Dew
Toxin-Removing Elixir), *Lian Po Yin* (Coptis and Officinal Magnolia
Bark Beverage), *Dang Gui Nian Tong Tang* (Chinese Angelica
Pain-Alleviating Decoction) and *Er Miao San* (Two Mysterious Powder).

***Yin Chen Hao Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 茵陈 | *yīn   | 18g   | Herba Artemisiae Scopariae       |
| narch |        | chén*  |       |                                  |
+=======+========+========+=======+==================================+
| Min   | > 栀子 | *zhī   | 9g    | Fructus Gardeniae                |
| ister |        | zĭ*    |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 大黄 | *dà    | 6g    | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei            |
| stant |        | huáng* |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Yin Chen* is bitter and cold. It clears heat, resolves
dampness and relieves jaundice.

Minister: *Zhi Zi* clears heat, reduces fire and resolves damp heat
through promoting urination.

Assistant: Da Huang clears heat and promotes bowel movement.

This formula drains dampness, clears heat, unblocks the Fu organs,
resolves stasis and thus alleviates jaundice.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, resolves dampness and alleviates jaundice

**\[Indications\]** Jaundice due to damp heat: Bright yellowish
pigmentation of the skin and whites of the eyes, fever, thirst, nausea,
vomiting, mild abdominal fullness, dark-yellow urine, a red tongue with
a yellow, greasy coating, and a deep, rapid or slippery, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for jaundice due
to damp heat. Damp heat accumulating in the middle *jiao* may affect the
qi flow of the liver and gallbladder, leading to the overflow of bile
and subsequently, bright yellowish pigmentation of the skin and whites
of the eyes. Damp heat affecting the transportation and transformation
of the spleen and stomach may cause a poor appetite, nausea, vomiting
and mild abdominal fullness. Scanty, dark yellow urine, thirst, a
yellow, greasy tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse are signs
indicative of internal build-up of damp heat. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to clear heat, resolve dampness and relieve jaundice.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Bright yellowish pigmentation of
the skin and whites of the eyes, dark yellow urine, a yellow, greasy
tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe dampness, add *Che Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis) and *Ze
    Xie* (Rhizoma Alismatis) to promote urination and drain dampness.

-   For severe heat, add *Hu Zhang* (Rhizoma Polygoni Cuspidati) and
    *Long Dan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Gentianae) to clear heat and
    resolve dampness. .

-   For pain in the chest and hypochondriac region, add *Chai Hu* (Radix
    Bupleuri), *Yu Jin* (Radix Curcumae) and *Chuan Lian Zi* (Fructus
    Toosendan) to soothe the liver, move qi and alleviate pain.

-   For severe jaundice, add *Tian Ji Huang* (Herba Hyperici Japonici),
    *Chui Pen Cao* (Herba Sedi), *Hu Zhang* (Rhizoma Polygoni Cuspidati)
    and *Qin* *Jiao* (Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae) to resolve dampness
    and alleviate jaundice.

3\. Modern use: Acute icteric hepatitis, hepatitis B, cholecystitis,
gall stones and leptospirosis that can be diagnosed as damp heat
accumulating in the liver, gallbladder, spleen and stomach.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As ingredients in this formula are
bitter and cold, it is contraindicated for patients with yin jaundice.
It's also worth noting that *Da Huang* is bitter, cold and purgative, a
big dose or long-term use may damage anti-pathogenic qi.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Yin Chen Si Ni Tang* (Artemisia Scoparia
Decoction for Frigid Extremities) from the *Shang Han Wei Zhi Lun*
(Implications of Treatise on Cold Damage)

\[Ingredients\] *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), *Yin Chen*
(Herba Artemisiae Scopariae), *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Fu
Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata)

\[Actions\] Warms yang, resolves dampness and alleviates jaundice

\[Indications\] Yin jaundice: Dark, gray and yellowish pigmentation of
the skin and whites of the eyes, a cold sensation in the skin, cold
intolerance in the back, cold hands/feet, body heaviness, fatigue, a
poor appetite, absence of thirst or preference for hot water, loose
stools, a pale tongue with a white coating and a tight, thready or deep,
thready and weak pulse.

*Yin Chen Hao Tang* clears heat, resolves dampness and alleviates
jaundice. It has strong efficacy in clearing heat, reducing fire and
promoting bowel/bladder movements. It is indicated for yang jaundice due
to damp heat accumulating in the liver, gallbladder, spleen and stomach.
*Yin Chen Si Ni Tang* warms yang, resolves dampness and alleviates
jaundice. It is indicated for yin jaundice due to cold dampness
accumulating in the liver, gallbladder, spleen and stomach.

***Ba Zheng San***

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 滑石 | *huá    | 9g  | Talcum                             |
| archs |        | shí*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 木通 | *mù     | 9g  | Caulis Akebiae                     |
|       |        | tōng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 萹蓄 | *biăn   | 9g  | Herba Polygoni Avicularis          |
| sters |        | xù*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 瞿麦 | *qú     | 9g  | Herba Dianthi                      |
|       |        | mài*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *chē    | 9g  | Semen Plantaginis                  |
|       | 车前子 | qián    |     |                                    |
|       |        | zĭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 栀子 | *zhī    | 9g  | Fructus Gardeniae                  |
| tants |        | zĭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 大黄 | *dà     | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei              |
|       |        | huáng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy | >      | *zhì    | 9g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       | 炙甘草 | gān     |     | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Hua Shi* is sweet, bland, heavy and cold. It drains heat from
the urinary bladder, resolves dampness and alleviates stranguria. *Mu
Tong* is bitter and cold. It clears heat, promotes urination and
alleviates stranguria.

Ministers: *Bian Xu*, *Qu Mai* and *Che Qian Zi* clear heat, promote
urination and alleviate stranguria.

Assistants: *Zhi Zi* clears heat, reduces fire and resolves damp heat in
San*jiao*. *Da Huang* is bitter and cold. It promotes bowel movements,
clears heat and reduces fire.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients, relieves spasm
and alleviates pain.

In addition, *Deng Xin Cao* (Medulla Junci) is added to decoct with
water.

This formula clears heat/fire, promotes urination and unblocks
stranguria.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder with *Deng Xin Cao*
decoction for each dose, 3 doses a day. Alternatively, add *Deng Xin
Cao* and decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, reduces fire, promotes urination and
alleviates stranguria

**\[Indications\]** Stranguria due to damp heat: Frequent, urgent and
painful urination, urinary dribbling, dark-yellow, cloudy urine, urine
retention, lower abdominal distension/fullness, a dry mouth and throat,
a yellow, greasy tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for stranguria
due to damp heat. Downward flow of damp heat to the urinary bladder may
impair the qi transformation of the bladder, leading to frequent, urgent
and painful urination, urinary dribbling, urine retention, and lower
abdominal distension/fullness. A yellow, greasy tongue coating and a
slippery, rapid pulse are signs indicative of internal build-up of damp
heat. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat, reduce
fire, promote urination and alleviate stranguria.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Frequent, urgent, and painful
urination, urinary dribbling, a yellow, greasy tongue coating and a
slippery, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For blood stranguria, add *Xiao Ji* (Herba Cirsii), *Bai Mao Gen*
    (Rhizoma Imperatae) and *Hu Po* (Succinum) to cool blood and stop
    bleeding.

-   For stone stranguria with painful urination, add *Jin Qian Cao*
    (Herba Lysimachiae), *Ji Nei Jin* (Endothelium Corneum Gigeriae
    Galli), *Hai Jin Sha* (Spora Lygodii) and *Dong Kui Zi* (Fructus
    Malvae) to remove stones and alleviate stranguria.

-   For severe toxic heat, add *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae Japonicae),
    *Zi Di Ding* (Herba Violae) and *Ye Ju Hua* (Flos Chrysanthemi
    Indici) to clear heat and remove toxins.

3\. Modern use: Cystitis, urinary tract infection, acute prostatitis,
urinary stone, nephropyelitis, and urine retention following surgery or
childbirth that can be diagnosed as downward flow of damp heat to the
urinary bladder.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As ingredients in this formula are
bitter and cold, it is contraindicated for pregnant women or patients
with chronic stranguria and kidney qi deficiency.

***San Ren Tang***

*Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Systematic Differentiation of Warm Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 杏仁 | *xìng   | 15g | Semen Armeniacae Amarum            |
| archs |        | rén*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | >      | *bái    | 6g  | Fructus Amomi Rotundus             |
|       | 白蔻仁 | kòu     |     |                                    |
|       |        | rén*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *yì yĭ  | 18g | Semen Coicis                       |
|       | 薏苡仁 | rén*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 6g  | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis       |
| sters |        | pò*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 半夏 | *bàn    | 15g | Rhizoma Pinelliae                  |
|       |        | xià*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 通草 | *tōng   | 6g  | Medulla Tetrapanacis               |
| tants |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 滑石 | *huá    | 18g | Talcum                             |
|       |        | shí*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 竹叶 | *zhú    | 6g  | Folium Phyllostachydis Henonis     |
|       |        | yè*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Xing Ren* is bitter and enters the lung to disperse lung qi
and transform dampness. *Bai Kou Ren* is aromatic and acts to resolve
dampness and move qi. *Yi Yi Ren* is sweet and mildly cold and acts to
drain heat, resolve dampness, and unblock San*jiao*.

Ministers: *Hou Po* resolves dampness, moves qi and relieves fullness.
*Ban Xia* dries dampness, resolves turbidity and harmonizes the stomach.

Assistants: *Tong Cao*, *Hua Shi* and *Zhu Ye* clear heat, drain
dampness and assist the monarchs to unblock the lower *jiao*.

This formula combines three dampness-resolving methods---transforming
dampness with aroma, draining dampness by promoting urination and drying
dampness with bitter, warm ingredients. It disperses lung qi (upper
*jiao*), fortifies the transportation and transformation of the spleen
and stomach (middle *jiao*) and promote urination (lower *jiao*).

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi, resolves dampness and clears heat

**\[Indications\]** Early stage damp heat or summer heat coupled with
dampness: Headache, cold intolerance, body ache/heaviness, a pale,
yellow complexion, chest tightness, a poor appetite, afternoon fever, a
white, greasy tongue coating and a wiry, thready and soft pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for early stage
damp heat or summer heat coupled with dampness (more damp than heat).
Early stage damp heat may obstruct the *Wei*-defensive yang and cause
cold intolerance, headache and body ache/heaviness. Hidden damp heat may
cause low-grade fever in the afternoon. Damp heat obstructing the qi
activity of the middle *jiao* may cause chest tightness and a poor
appetite. A white or white, greasy tongue coating and a wiry, thready
and soft pulse are signs indicative of internal damp heat. As for the
treatment, Wu Jutong mentioned in the *Wen Bing Tiao Bian* that
early-stage damp heat cannot be treated with strategies of "inducing
sweating", "draining downward" and "moistening". The proper strategies
are to drain dampness, clear heat, unblock qi flow of San*jiao* and
remove turbidities using aromatic, bitter, light and dispersing
ingredients.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Headache, cold intolerance, body
ache/heaviness, chest tightness, a poor appetite, afternoon fever, a
white tongue coating and absence of thirst.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe exterior syndrome in the *Wei*-defensive stage, add
    *Xiang Ru* (Herba Moslae) and *Huo Xiang* (Herba Agastachis) to
    release the exterior.

-   For severe damp heat, add *Shan Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae) and
    *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) to clear heat and reduce fire.

-   For severe turbidity, add *Huo Xiang* (Herba Agastachis), *Pei Lan*
    (Herba Eupatorii) and *Shi Chang Pu* (Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii) to
    resolve turbidity.

3\. Modern use: Enteric fever, gastroenteritis, nephropyelitis,
brucellosis, glomerular nephritis and arthritis that can be diagnosed as
damp heat (more damp than heat) obstructing the flow of qi.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As ingredients in this formula are
dry, bitter and aromatic and may damage yin qi, it should not be used
for a long period of time and contraindicated for patients with a
yellow, greasy tongue coating (more heat than dampness).

***Gan Lu Xiao Du Dan***

*Yi Xiao Mi Chuan* (Secret Formulas for Medical Efficacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 滑石 | *huá    | 15g | Talcum                             |
| archs |        | shí*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 茵陈 | *yīn    | 11g | Herba Artemisiae Scopariae         |
|       |        | chén*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 黄芩 | *huáng  | 10g | Radix Scutellariae                 |
|       |        | qín*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 藿香 | *huò    | 4g  | Herba Agastachis                   |
| sters |        | xiāng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *bái    | 4g  | Fructus Amomi Kravanh              |
|       | 白豆蔻 | dòu     |     |                                    |
|       |        | kòu*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shí    | 6g  | Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii          |
|       | 石菖蒲 | chāng   |     |                                    |
|       |        | pú*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 木通 | *mù     | 5g  | Caulis Akebiae                     |
|       |        | tōng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 连翘 | *lián   | 4g  | Fructus Forsythiae                 |
| tants |        | qiào*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 射干 | *shè    | 4g  | Rhizoma Belamcandae                |
|       |        | gān*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 贝母 | *bèi    | 5g  | Bulbus Fritillaria                 |
|       |        | mŭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 薄荷 | *bò hé* | 4g  | Herba Menthae                      |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Hua Shi* is sweet and bland and acts to clear heat and
resolve dampness. *Yin Chen* is bitter and mildly cold and acts to clear
heat and resolve dampness in the liver and gallbladder and alleviate
jaundice. *Huang Qin* clears heat, dries dampness, reduces fire and
removes toxins.

Ministers: *Huo Xian*g, *Bai Dou Kou* and *Shi Chang P*u are aromatic
and act to resolve turbidity, disperse qi flow, benefit the spleen and
transform dampness. *Mu Ton*g clear heat, resolves dampness and promotes
urination.

Assistants: *Lian Qiao* and *She Gan* clear heat, remove toxins, benefit
the throat and relieve stagnation. *Bei Mu* resolves phlegm and relieves
stagnation. *Bo He* clears heat and benefits the throat.

This formula is also known as *Pu Ji Xiao Du Dan* (Universal Relief
Toxin-Removing Elixir) and often used in summer times for summer heat
coupled with dampness. Wang Shixiong described this formula in the *Wen
Re Jing Wei* (Warp and Woof of Warm-Heat Diseases) as "an essential
formula for summer heat coupled with dampness".

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder plus *Shen Qu* (Massa
Medicata Fermentata) for each dose, 2 doses a day; or 9g paste pills
plus *Shen Qu* for each dose, 2 doses a day. Alternatively, decoct with
water.

**\[Functions\]** Resolves turbid dampness, clears heat and removes
toxins

**\[Indications\]** Summer heat coupled with dampness: Fever, thirst,
chest tightness, abdominal distension, body ache, lassitude, swollen
cheeks (parotitis), sore throat, yellowish pigmentation of the skin and
whites of the eyes, dark-yellow, cloudy urine, diarrhea, a white, greasy
or dry, yellow tongue coating, and a soft, rapid or slippery, rapid
pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for early stage
damp heat affecting the qi phase. Damp heat in the qi phase or ascending
of damp heat may cause fever, thirst, sore throat and swollen cheeks.
Damp heat obstructing the flow of qi may cause chest tightness,
abdominal distension, body ache and lassitude. Damp heat accumulating in
the liver and gallbladder may cause yellowish pigmentation of the skin
and whites of the eyes. Downward flow of damp heat to the intestines and
urinary bladder may cause dark yellow, cloudy urine and diarrhea. A
white, greasy or dry, yellow tongue coating and a soft, rapid or
slippery, rapid pulse are signs indicative of internal damp heat coupled
with retention of pathogenic factors in the qi phase. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to resolve turbid dampness, clear heat and
remove toxins.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fever, thirst, chest tightness,
abdominal distension, swollen cheeks, sore throat, dark yellow urine, a
white, greasy or dry, yellow tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe (bright) yellowish pigmentation of the skin and whites of
    the eyes, increase the dose of *Yin Chen* and add *Shan Zhi Zi*
    (Fructus Gardeniae), *Hu Zhang* (Rhizoma Polygoni Cuspidati) and *Da
    Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) to clear heat, resolve dampness and
    alleviate jaundice.

-   For severe swollen cheeks and sore throat, add *Ban Lan Gen* (Radix
    Isatidis), *Ma Bo* (Lasiosphaera seu Calvatia) and *Niu Bang Zi*
    (Fructus Arctii) to clear heat, remove toxins and alleviate sore
    throat and swollen cheeks.

-   For severe dampness with chest tightness and a poor appetite, add
    *Hou Po* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) and *Yi Yi Ren* (Fructus
    Alpiniae Oxyphyllae) to resolve dampness and harmonize the middle
    *jiao*.

3\. Modern use: Enteric fever, acute gastroenteritis, viral hepatitis,
leptospirosis and cholecystitis that can be diagnosed as damp heat
affecting the qi phase.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As ingredients in this formula are
dry, bitter and aromatic and may damage fluids, it is contraindicated
for patients with yin deficiency, damage to fluids or delirium and a
deep red tongue due to damp heat entering the Ying-nutrients phase.

***Lian Po Yin***

*Huo Luan Lun* (Treatise on Cholera)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 黄连 | *huáng  | 3g  | Rhizoma Coptidis                   |
| archs |        | lián*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 6g  | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis       |
|       |        | pò*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 栀子 | *zhī    | 9g  | Fructus Gardeniae                  |
| sters |        | zĭ*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shí    | 3g  | Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii          |
|       | 石菖蒲 | chāng   |     |                                    |
|       |        | pú*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 半夏 | *bàn    | 3g  | Rhizoma Pinelliae                  |
|       |        | xià*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 芦根 | *lú     | 60g | Rhizoma Phragmitis                 |
| tants |        | gēn*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *dàn    | 9g  | Semen Sojae Praeparatum            |
|       | 淡豆豉 | dòu     |     |                                    |
|       |        | chĭ*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Huang Lian* is biter and cold. It clears heat, dries
dampness, reduces fire and removes toxins. When fried with ginger juice,
it also harmonizes the stomach and stops vomiting. *Hou Po* resolves
dampness, moves qi and relieves fullness. The combination of *Huang
Lian* and *Hou Po* clears heat and resolves dampness in the spleen and
stomach, and regulates stomach qi.

Ministers: *Zhi Zi* clears heat, dries dampness, reduces fire and
assists Huang Lian in resolving dampness. Shi Chang Pu opens the
orifices, and resolves dampness. *Ban Xia* is pungent, warm and dry and
acts to dry dampness, bear qi down, and assist Hou Po in resolving
dampness and bearing down stomach qi.

Assistants: *Lu Gen* is sweet, cold and light. It clears heat,
harmonizes the stomach, relieves restlessness and stops vomiting. *Dan
Dou Chi* clears heat.

This formula clears heat, resolves dampness, harmonizes the spleen and
stomach, regulates qi activity and thus alleviates vomiting/diarrhea
(cholera), chest tightness and fullness in cholera.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water and drink when the
decoction is warm.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, resolves dampness, regulates qi and
harmonizes the middle *jiao*.

**\[Indications\]** Cholera due to damp heat: Vomiting, diarrhea, chest
tightness, abdominal fullness, restlessness, dark yellow urine, a
yellow, greasy tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for cholera due
to damp heat. Damp heat affecting the qi ascending and descending of the
spleen and stomach may cause cholera, vomiting, diarrhea, chest
tightness and abdominal fullness. Dark yellow urine, a yellow, greasy
tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse are signs indicative of damp
heat. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat, resolve
dampness, regulate qi and harmonize the middle *jiao*.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal
fullness, scanty, dark yellow urine, a yellow, greasy or dry, yellow
tongue coating and a slippery, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe diarrhea, add *Che Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis) and *Fu
    Ling* (Poria) to drain dampness and stop diarrhea.

-   For severe chest tightness and abdominal distension, add *Cao Guo*
    (Fructus Tsaoko) and *Rou Dou Kou* (Semen Myristicae) to regulate
    qi, resolve dampness, and alleviate fullness.

-   For a poor appetite, add *Sha Ren* (Fructus Amomi), *Shan Zha*
    (Fructus Crataegi) and *Shen Qu* (Massa Medicata Fermentata) to
    increase the appetite.

3\. Modern use: Acute gastroenteritis, enteric fever, and paratyphoid
fever that can be diagnosed as damp heat accumulating in the spleen and
stomach.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As ingredients in this formula are
dry, bitter, pungent and cold and may damage fluids and consume qi, it
is contraindicated for patients with loss of fluids and qi collapse due
to sudden, severe vomiting or diarrhea or cholera due to cold dampness.

***Dang Gui Nian Tong Tang (Nian Tong Tang)***

*Yi Xue Qi Yuan* (Origins of Medicine)

| archs |        | huó*    |     |                                    |

|       |        | gēn*    |     |                                    |

|       |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |

|       |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |

|       |        | shēn*   |     |                                    |

|       |        | guī*    |     |                                    |

| Envoy | > 甘草 | *zhì    | 7   | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       |        | gān     | .5g |                                    |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Qiang Huo* is bitter, pungent, warm, dry and aromatic. It
removes wind, resolves dampness, unblocks impediment and alleviates
pain. *Yin Chen* is cold and bitter. It clears heat and resolves
dampness.

Ministers: *Zhu Ling* and *Ze Xie* clear heat and resolve dampness.
*Huang Qin* and *Ku Shen* clear heat and dry dampness.

Assistants: *Fang Feng*, *Sheng Ma* and *Ge Gen* remove wind and assist
*Qiang Huo* in removing wind and resolving dampness. *Cang Zhu* and *Bai
Zhu* dry dampness and fortify the spleen. *Ren Shen* and *Dang Gui*
supplement qi and tonify blood. *Zhi Mu* clear heat, reduce fire and
nourish yin to prevent yin fluids from being consumed by dry, bitter
ingredients.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* clears heat, removes toxins and coordinates the other
ingredients.

This formula treats both the root cause and symptoms. It removes wind,
clears heat, resolves dampness, and at the same time, fortifies the
spleen, tonifies qi and nourishes blood.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, resolves dampness, removes wind and
alleviates pain.

**\[Indications\]** Damp heat coupled with external contraction of wind:
Generalized body ache and pain, heavy sensation in the shoulder and
back, swelling and pain in the lower limbs, multiple sores or
carbuncles, a white, greasy or slightly yellow tongue coating and a
soft, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for wind, damp
and heat retention in meridians or joints. Internal build-up of damp
heat coupled with external contraction of wind may cause the retention
of wind, damp and heat in meridians, muscles or joints, leading to
generalized body ache and pain, a burning sensation or heavy sensation
in the shoulder and back. Downward flow of damp heat may cause swelling,
pain or sores/carbuncles in the lower limbs, a white, greasy or slightly
yellow tongue coating and a soft, rapid pulse. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to clear heat, resolve dampness, remove wind
and alleviate pain.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Joint heaviness, swelling and
pain, a greasy and slightly yellow tongue coating and a rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe ankle/knee swelling, add *Fang Ji* (Radix Stephaniae
    Tetrandrae) and *Mu Gua* (Fructus Chaenomelis) to resolve dampness
    and eliminate swelling.

-   For severe body ache, add *Jiang Huang* (Rhizoma Curcumae Longae)
    and *Hai Tong Pi* (Cortex Erythrinae) to circulate blood, unblock
    meridians, and alleviate pain.

-   For ulceration in the foot or knee with a burning sensation and
    pain, add *Jin Yin Hua* (Flos Lonicerae Japonicae), *Lian Qiao*
    (Fructus Forsythiae) and *Ye Ju Hua* (Flos Chrysanthemi Indici) to
    clear heat and remove toxins.

3\. Modern use: Rheumatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, gout and
ulceration in the lower limbs that can be diagnosed as wind, damp and
heat.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: Because ingredients in this formula are bitter, cold, pungent and dispersing, it is contraindicated for patients with Bi-impediment syndrome due to wind, cold and dampness.  {#cautions-contraindications-because-ingredients-in-this-formula-are-bitter-cold-pungent-and-dispersing-it-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-bi-impediment-syndrome-due-to-wind-cold-and-dampness. .unnumbered}

***Er Miao San***

*Dan Xi Xin Fa* (Teachings of Zhu Dan-xi)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+--------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------------+
| M      | > 黄柏  | *huáng   | 9g  | Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis   |
| onarch |         | băi*     |     |                                 |
+========+=========+==========+=====+=================================+
| Mi     | > 苍术  | *cāng    | 9g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis            |
| nister |         | zhú*     |     |                                 |
+--------+---------+----------+-----+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Huang Bai* is bitter and cold. It clears heat and dries
dampness, especially damp heat in the lower *jiao*.

Ministers: *Cang Zhu* dries dampness, resolves dampness, fortifies the
spleen and dispels wind.

The combination of *Huang Bai* and *Cang Zhu* dries dampness and clears
heat.

Fresh ginger juice is combined to harmonize stomach qi and resolve
dampness.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder for each dose, 2 doses a
day; or take 6-9g pills for each dose, 2 doses a day. Alternatively,
decoct with water plus ginger juice.

**\[Functions\]** Dries dampness and clears heat.

**\[Indications\]** Downward flow of damp heat: Muscle/bone pain,
flaccidity and weakness of the feet/lower limbs, redness, pain and
swelling of the ankle and knee joints, vaginal discharges, scrotal
eczema, scanty, dark yellow urine, a yellow, greasy tongue coating and a
soft, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for downward flow
of damp heat. Downward flow of damp heat to joints, sinews and skin may
cause muscle/bone pain, redness, swelling and pain of the ankle/knee
joints, flaccidity of the lower limbs, and scrotal eczema. Downward flow
of damp heat to the Dai meridian, uterus and external genitalia may
cause yellow, thick and smelly vaginal discharge. Scanty, dark yellow
urine and a yellow, greasy tongue coating are signs indicative of damp
heat. As a result, the treatment strategies are to dry dampness and
clear heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: *Wei*-flaccidity,
*Bi*-impediment, vaginal discharge, scrotal eczema, scanty, dark yellow
urine and a yellow, greasy tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For *Wei*-flaccidity due to damp heat, add *Mu Gua* (Fructus
    Chaenomelis), *Wu Jia Pi* (Cortex Acanthopanacis) and *Niu Xi*
    (Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae) to remove wind, resolve dampness, and
    strengthen muscles/bones.

-   For athlete\'s foot due to damp heat, add *Chi Xiao Dou* (Semen
    Phaseoli), *Mu Gua* (Fructus Chaenomelis) and *Yi Yi Ren* (Semen
    Coicis) to fortify the spleen, drain dampness and benefit
    muscles/tendons.

-   For *Bi*-impediment due to damp heat, add *Wu Jia Pi* (Cortex
    Acanthopanacis), *Xi Xian Cao* (Herba Siegesbeckiae) and *Shi Nan
    Teng* (Caulis Photiniae) to resolve dampness, unblock meridians and
    alleviate pain.

-   For scrotal eczema, add *Chi Xiao Dou* (Semen Phaseoli), *Tu Fu
    Ling* (Rhizoma Smilacis Glabrae) and *Ku Shen* (Radix Sophorae
    Flavescentis) to clear heat, resolve dampness and remove toxins.

-   For vaginal discharge due to dam p heat, add *Tu Fu Ling* (Rhizoma
    Smilacis Glabrae) and *Chun Gen Pi* (Cortex Ailanthi) to clear heat,
    resolve dampness and stop leucorrhea.

3\. Modern use: Rheumatic arthritis, scrotal eczema and vaginitis that
can be diagnosed as downward flow of damp heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: As this formula clears heat and
resolves dampness, it's contraindicated for patients with cold dampness.

> *San Miao Wan* is modified from Er Miao San by adding Niu Xi to tonify
> the liver and kidney, strengthen the muscles/tendons, resolve wind
> dampness and guide the other ingredients to descend. It is indicated
> for numbness, pain and weakness of the lower limbs. *Si Miao Wan* is
> modified from *San Miao Wan* by adding *Yi Yi Ren* to resolve
> dampness, clear heat, fortify the spleen and relax muscles/tendons.
> It's indicated for numbness, weakness, swelling and pain of the lower
> limbs due to downward flow of damp heat.

**15.3 Formulas that promote urination and drain dampness**

Formulas that promote urination and drain dampness are indicated for
edema, diarrhea, urine retention or stranguria due to internal retention
of water dampness. Clinical manifestations may include dysuria, body
heaviness, body ache, numbness or edema. Urination-promoting and
dampness-draining ingredients like *Fu Ling* (Poria), *Ze Xie* (Rhizoma
Alismatis) and *Zhu Ling* (Polyporus) are often used in combination with
spleen-fortifying and qi-tonifying ingredients like *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma
Atractylodis Macrocephalae) and *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) as well as
yang-unblocking and qi-transforming ingredients like *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus
Cinnamomi). Typical formulas are *Wu Ling San* (Five Substances Powder
with Poria), *Zhu Ling Tang* (Polyporus Decoction), *Fang Ji Huang Qi
Tang* (Stephania Root and Astragalus Decoction) and *Wu Pi San*
(Five-Peel Powder).

***Wu Ling San***

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 泽泻 | *zé     | 15g | Rhizoma Alismatis                  |
| narch |        | xiè*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 9g  | Poria                              |
| sters |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 猪苓 | *zhū    | 9g  | Polyporus                          |
|       |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 白术 | *bái    | 9g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae |
| tants |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 桂枝 | *guì    | 6g  | Ramulus Cinnamomi                  |
|       |        | zhī*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ze Xie* is sweet and bland and enters the kidney and urinary
bladder. It promotes urination and drains dampness.

Ministers: *Fu Ling* and *Zhu Ling* drain dampness and mutually
reinforce with *Ze Xie*.

Assistants: Bai Zhu tonifies qi, fortifies the spleen and resolves
dampness. The combination of *Bai Zhu* and *Fu Ling* fortifies the
spleen, resolves dampness and transforms and distributes fluids. *Gui
Zhi* warms yang, transforms qi, resolves water retention and dispels
cold. The combination of *Gui Zhi* and *Ze Xie* promotes urination and
transforms qi.

It's advisable to drink plenty of water to release the exterior.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6-9g powder for each dose, 2 doses
a day; or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Promotes urination, drains dampness, warms yang and
transforms qi.

**\[Indications\]**

-   Water accumulation syndrome: Dysuria, headache, mild fever, thirst
    with a desire to drink water, vomiting immediately after drinking
    water, a white tongue coating and a superficial pulse.

-   Internal retention of water dampness: Edema, diarrhea, dysuria or
    vomiting/diarrhea in cholera.

-   Phlegm-fluid retention: Throb sensation below the umbilicus,
    vomiting of salivation, dizziness, shortness of breath and coughing.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for water
accumulation syndrome. Water accumulation syndrome results from cold
damage to Taiyang and unresolved pathogenic factors entering Fu organs
through meridians, which in turn impair qi transformation of the urinary
bladder. Patients may present with fever, headache and a superficial
pulse. Impaired qi transformation of the urinary bladder may cause
dysuria. Water accumulation affecting the lower *jiao* may cause failure
of fluids to ascend and subsequently, thirst with a desire to drink
water. Failure to distribute fluids may cause adverse ascending of water
and lead to vomiting immediately after drinking. Internal water
retention, over flow or affecting the intestines may cause edema,
diarrhea or phlegm-fluid retention. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to promote urination, drain dampness, warm yang, and
transform qi, coupled with dispelling cold and releasing the exterior.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Dysuria, a white tongue coating
and a superficial or delayed pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe exterior syndrome, add *Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae) and
    *Zi Su Ye* (Folium Perillae) to release the exterior and disperse
    the lung.

-   For edema or abdominal distension due to build-up of water dampness,
    add *Sheng Jiang Pi* (Cortex Zingiberis Rhizomatis), *Sang Bai Pi*
    (Cortex Mori), *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Da Fu Pi*
    (Pericarpium Arecae) and *Fu Ling* (Poria) to move qi, promote
    urination and relieve edema.

-   For vomiting/diarrhea in cholera, add *Huo Xiang* (Herba Agastachis)
    and *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis) to resolve dampness, harmonize
    the stomach and stop vomiting/diarrhea.

-   For edema, low back soreness, lower limb weakness, cold intolerance
    and cold limbs, replace *Gui Zhi* with *Rou Gui* (Cortex Cinnamomi)
    and add *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata) to warm the
    kidney and spleen.

3\. Modern use: Edema in acute or chronic nephritis, ascites due to
liver cirrhosis, cardiac edema, acute enteritis, urine retention and
hydrocephalus that can be diagnosed as internal retention of water
dampness.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: It's advisable to take this formula (in the dosage form of powder) with plenty of warm water. Considering its strong efficacy in promoting urination, patients should take this formula only for a short period of time. {#cautions-contraindications-its-advisable-to-take-this-formula-in-the-dosage-form-of-powder-with-plenty-of-warm-water.-considering-its-strong-efficacy-in-promoting-urination-patients-should-take-this-formula-only-for-a-short-period-of-time. .unnumbered}

| Ling San ( | Ling San  | relieves      | heat), dysuria            |
| *Jin Gui Y |           | jaundice      |                           |
| ao Lüè*) { |           |               |                           |
