*Si Ling San* is modified from *Wu Ling San* by removing *Gui Zhi*. It
acts to fortify the spleen, promote urination and drain dampness and is
indicated for diarrhea due to spleen failing to transport and transform.
*Yin Chen Wu Ling San* is modified from *Wu Ling San* by adding *Yin
Che*n. It acts to promote urination, drain dampness, clear heat and
relieve jaundice. It is indicated for jaundice due to damp heat (more
dampness than heat). *Wei Ling Tang* combines *Wu Ling San* and *Ping
Wei San*. It acts to promote urination, drain and dry dampness, move qi,
and harmonize the middle *jiao*. It is indicated for edema, diarrhea,
abdominal distension and a thick, greasy tongue coating due to water
damp obstructing the flow of qi.

***Zhu Ling Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 猪苓 | *zhū    | 9g  | Polyporus                          |
| narch |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 泽泻 | *zé     | 9g  | Rhizoma Alismatis                  |
| sters |        | xiè*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 9g  | Poria                              |
|       |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 滑石 | *huá    | 9g  | Talcum                             |
| tants |        | shí*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 阿胶 | *ē      | 9g  | Colla Corii Asini                  |
|       |        | jiāo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Zhu Ling* is sweet, bland and neutral, and enters the kidney
and urinary bladder. It promotes urination and drains water retention.

Ministers: *Ze Xie* and *Fu Lin*g are also sweet and bland. They
mutually reinforce with *Zhu Ling* in promoting urination and draining
water retention.

Assistants: *Hua Shi* is slippery and cold. It promotes urination and
clears heat. *E* *Jiao* moistens dryness, nourishes yin and at the same
time, protects yin from being damaged.

This formula promotes urination, drains dampness, and nourishes and
protects yin. It also alleviates stranguria, clears heat and stops
bleeding. Therefore it can be used for painful urination with a burning
sensation or bloody urine in heat/blood stranguria.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water, melt *E* *Jiao* in
two times, and drink the decoction when it's warm.

**\[Functions\]** Promotes urination, drains dampness, clears heat and
nourishes yin.

**\[Indications\]**

-   Water-heat stagnation: Dysuria, fever, thirst with a desire to drink
    water, restlessness, insomnia, cough, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, a
    red tongue with a white or slightly yellow coating and a thready,
    rapid pulse.

-   Heat or blood stranguria: Painful urination with a burning
    sensation, bloody urine, a red tongue with a dry, yellow coating and
    a thready, rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for water-heat
stagnation in the urinary bladder coupled with yin deficiency. Water
heat affecting the qi transformation may cause dysuria or painful
urination and fever. Heat damaging blood collaterals may cause bloody
urine. Heat damaging yin may cause thirst with a desire to drink water.
A red tongue with a yellow coating and a thready, rapid pulse are signs
indicative of heat damaging yin. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to promote urination, clear heat, and nourish yin.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Dysuria, thirst, fever, a red
tongue and a thready, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For stranguria due to heat, add *Che Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis),
    *Qu Mai* (Herba Dianthi) and *Bian Xu* (Herba Polygoni Avicularis)
    to enhance the effect of clearing heat and promoting urination.

-   For blood stranguria or bloody urine, add *Da Ji* (Herba Cirsii
    Japonici), *Xiao Ji* (Herba Cirsii) and *Bai Mao Gen* (Rhizoma
    Imperatae) to alleviate stranguria and stop bleeding.

3\. Modern use: Urinary tract infection, nephritis, cystitis and urine
retention after childbirth that can be diagnosed as intertwined
water-heat stagnation coupled with damage to yin.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: Since this formula promotes urination and clears heat, it's contraindicated for patients with internal heat and severe loss of yin fluids.  {#cautions-contraindications-since-this-formula-promotes-urination-and-clears-heat-its-contraindicated-for-patients-with-internal-heat-and-severe-loss-of-yin-fluids. .unnumbered}

***Fang Ji Huang Qi Tang***

*Jin Gui Yao* *Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Monarchs | > 防己 | *fáng   | 9g  | Radix Stephaniae Tetrandrae     |
|          |        | jĭ*     |     |                                 |
+==========+========+=========+=====+=================================+
|          | > 黄芪 | *huáng  | 12g | Radix Astragali                 |
|          |        | qí*     |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Minister | > 白术 | *bái    | 7   | Rhizoma Atractylodis            |
|          |        | zhú*    | .5g | Macrocephalae                   |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Assista  | > 甘草 | *gān    | 5g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae   |
| nt/envoy |        | căo*    |     |                                 |
+----------+--------+---------+-----+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Fang Ji* promotes urination, eliminates edema, removes wind,
resolves dampness, unblocks impediment and alleviates pain. H*uang Qi*
supplements qi, secures the exterior, promotes urination and eliminates
edema.

Minister: *Bai Zhu* supplements qi, fortifies the spleen, dries dampness
and assists *Huang Qi* in tonifying qi and securing the exterior.

Assistant/envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients. Ginger and
Chinese dates are combined to harmonize the *Ying*-nutrients and
*Wei*-defense.

This formula reinforces qi and removes pathogenic factors. It promotes
urination without damaging anti-pathogenic qi and secures the exterior
without retaining pathogenic factors.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Supplements qi, removes wind, fortifies the spleen and
promotes urination.

**\[Indications\]** Wind damp or wind water syndrome: Sweating, wind
intolerance, body heaviness/pain, dysuria, a pale tongue with a white
coating and a superficial pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for wind dampness
and wind water syndrome due to qi deficiency. Wind dampness affecting
the exterior coupled with qi deficiency may cause sweating and wind
intolerance. Internal retention of water dampness may cause body
heaviness and pain. Water dampness affecting the qi transformation may
cause dysuria. A white tongue coating and a superficial pulse are signs
indicative of exterior wind syndrome. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to supplement qi, fortify the spleen, secure the
exterior, remove wind and promote urination.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Sweating, wind intolerance, body
heaviness, dysuria, a white tongue coating and a superficial pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For sweating, wind intolerance and joint pain, add *Gui Zhi*
    (Ramulus Cinnamomi), *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba) and *Qin*
    *Jiao* (Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae) to remove wind and alleviate
    pain.

-   For cold intolerance, add *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis
    Praeparata), *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus Cinnamomi) and *Ma Huang* (Herba
    Ephedrae) to warm meridians, dispel cold and alleviate pain.

-   For edema in the lower limbs, add *Fu Ling* (Poria) and *Ze Xie*
    (Rhizoma Alismatis) to promote urination and eliminate edema.

3\. Modern use: Rheumatic arthritis, chronic glomerular nephritis and
cardiac edema that can be diagnosed as qi deficiency, wind dampness
affecting the exterior or internal retention of water dampness.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for patients with excessive edema coupled with gastrointestinal symptoms such as nausea, abdominal distension and loose stools. It's also contraindicated for patients with internal retention of water dampness, absence of sweating, wind intolerance and a superficial pulse. Patients with sweating and wind intolerance due to disharmony between the *Ying*-nutrients and *Wei*-defense should use this formula with caution.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-patients-with-excessive-edema-coupled-with-gastrointestinal-symptoms-such-as-nausea-abdominal-distension-and-loose-stools.-its-also-contraindicated-for-patients-with-internal-retention-of-water-dampness-absence-of-sweating-wind-intolerance-and-a-superficial-pulse.-patients-with-sweating-and-wind-intolerance-due-to-disharmony-between-the-ying-nutrients-and-wei-defense-should-use-this-formula-with-caution. .unnumbered}

***Wu Pi San***

*Hua Shi Zhong Cang Jing* (Central Treasury Classic by Hua Tuo)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | >      | *fú     | 9g  | Cutis Poriae                       |
| narch | 茯苓皮 | líng    |     |                                    |
|       |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | >      | *dà fù  | 9g  | Pericarpium Arecae                 |
| sters | 大腹皮 | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 陈皮 | *chén   | 9g  | Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae      |
|       |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | >      | *sāng   | 9g  | Cortex Mori                        |
| tants | 桑白皮 | bái pí* |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shēng  | 9g  | Cortex Zingiberis Rhizomatis       |
|       | 生姜皮 | jiāng   |     |                                    |
|       |        | pí*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Fu Ling Pi* is sweet and bland. It drains dampness (especially
water dampness in the surface of the body), promotes urination and
eliminates edema.

Ministers: *Da Fu Pi* moves qi, relieves stagnation and fullness,
promotes urination and eliminates edema. *Chen Pi* regulates qi,
harmonizes the stomach, benefits the spleen and resolves dampness.

Assistants: *Sang Bai Pi* bears down lung qi, regulates water passage
and thus promotes urination and eliminates edema. *Sheng Jiang Pi*
circulates water and eliminates edema.

This formula promotes urination, eliminates edema, regulates qi and
fortifies the spleen. It is therefore also used for edema during
pregnancy due to spleen deficiency.

Composed of five peels, this formula fortifies the spleen, resolves
dampness, bears down lung qi and circulates water. It is especially
effective for water dampness retention in the surface of the body.

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder for each dose, 2 doses a
day. Alternatively, decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Promotes urination, eliminates edema, regulates qi and
fortifies the spleen.

**\[Indications\]** Skin water syndrome due to water-qi stagnation:
General edema, body heaviness, abdominal fullness/distension, shortness
of breath, dysuria, a white, greasy tongue coating and a deep, delayed
pulse. It can also be used for edema due to pregnancy.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for skin water
syndrome resulting from qi stagnation coupled with water retention due
to spleen deficiency. Spleen failing to transport and transform may
cause internal water dampness and subsequently, general edema and body
heaviness. Dampness obstructing the flow of qi may cause abdominal
fullness/distension. Because the lung is the upper source of water,
water dampness affecting the lung may cause ascending of lung qi and
subsequently, shortness of breath. Lung failing to regulate water
passage may cause dysuria. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
promote urination, eliminate edema, regulate qi and fortify the spleen.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: General edema, abdominal
fullness/distension, dysuria, a white, greasy tongue coating and a deep,
delayed pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For exuberant water dampness, combine *Wu Ling San*.

-   For abdominal distension, vomiting or diarrhea, combine with *Ping
    Wei San*.

-   For lassitude, a poor appetite and loose stools due to spleen
    deficiency, combine with *Si Jun Zi Tang*.

-   For cold limbs and cold intolerance, add *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti
    Lateralis Praeparata) and *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) to warm
    yang and promote urination.

-   For severe shortness of breath due to failure of lung qi to descend,
    add *Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae) and *Ting Li Zi* (Semen Lepidii) to
    disperse lung and enhance the function of the lung in distributing
    water.

-   For pregnancy edema, add *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis
    Macrocephalae) and *Zi Su* (Folium Perillae) to fortify the spleen,
    regulate qi, and resolve dampness.

3\. Modern use: Edema due to chronic nephritis, cardiac edema, edema due
to liver cirrhosis, edema during period, and edema in pregnancy that can
be diagnosed as qi stagnation coupled with water dampness retention due
to spleen deficiency.

### 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula should not be taken for a long period of time, since it consumes body fluids.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-should-not-be-taken-for-a-long-period-of-time-since-it-consumes-body-fluids. .unnumbered}

**15.4 Formulas that warm and transform water dampness**

Formulas that warm and transform water dampness are indicated for phlegm
fluid or edema resulting from water dampness retention due to yang
deficiency or dampness transforming into cold. Clinical manifestations
may include general edema and dysuria. Interior-warming ingredients like
*Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis), *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus Cinnamomi) and *Fu
Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata) are often used in combination
with spleen-fortifying and dampness-resolving ingredients like *Fu Ling*
(Poria) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae). Typical
formulas are *Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang* (Poria, Cinnamon Twig, Atractylodes
Macrocephala and Licorice Decoction), *Zhen Wu Tang* (True Warrior
Decoction), *Shi Pi San* (Spleen-Strengthening Powder) and *Bi Xie Fen
Qing Yin* (Hypoglaucae Root Turbidity-Clearing Beverage).

***Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang***

*Jin Gui Yao* *Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 12g | Poria                              |
| narch |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Min   | > 桂枝 | *guì    | 9g  | Ramulus Cinnamomi                  |
| ister |        | zhī*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assi  | > 白术 | *bái    | 9g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae |
| stant |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Envoy | > 甘草 | *gān    | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Fu Ling* is sweet and bland. It fortifies the spleen and
drains dampness.

Minister: *Gui Zhi* warms yang and transforms phlegm fluid.

Assistant: *Bai Zhu* fortifies the spleen, dries dampness and assists
*Gui Zhi* in warming yang and transforming phlegm dampness.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* supplements qi, harmonizes the middle *jiao* and
coordinates the other ingredients.

This formula warms yang, resolves fluid, fortifies the spleen, resolves
dampness and promotes urination. The combination of yang-warming and
spleen-fortifying ingredients enables the formula to be warm but not
dry; and to promote urination in a moderate way.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms yang, transforms fluid, fortifies the spleen and
resolves dampness.

> **\[Indications\]** Phlegm-fluid retention due to spleen yang
> deficiency: Fullness in the chest and hypochondriac region, blurred
> vision, palpitations, shortness of breath, coughing, a white, slippery
> tongue coating and a wiry, slippery or deep, tight pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for phlegm fluid
retention due to spleen yang deficiency. Spleen yang failing to
transport and transform may cause phlegm dampness. Phlegm fluid
affecting the qi flow of the lung and heart may cause fullness in the
chest and hypochondriac region, palpitations, shortness of breath and
coughing. A white, slippery tongue coating and a deep, slippery pulse
are signs indicative of internal phlegm fluid. As a result, the
treatment strategies are to warm yang, transform fluid, fortify the
spleen and resolve dampness.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Fullness in the chest and
hypochondriac region, blurred vision, palpitations, and a white,
slippery tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For cough with profuse phlegm, add *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) and
    *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae) to dry dampness and
    resolve phlegm.

-   For fatigue and loose stools, add *Dang Shen* (Radix Codonopsis) and
    *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali) to enhance the effect of fortifying the
    spleen and supplementing qi.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis, bronchial asthma, cardiac edema,
edema due to chronic glomerular nephritis, Meniere\'s disease and
neurosis that can be diagnosed as water fluid retention in the middle
*jiao* due to spleen yang deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with fluid transforming into heat, fire hyperactivity due to
yin deficiency and cough with sticky phlegm due to damp heat.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Gan Cao Gan Jiang Fu Ling Bai Zhu Tang*
(Licorice, Ginger, Poria, and White Atractylodes Decoction) from the
*Jin Gui Yao* *Lüè* (Essentials from the Golden Cabinet)

\[Ingredients\] *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), *Gan Jiang*
(Rhizoma Zingiberis), *Fu Ling* (Poria) and *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma
Atractylodis Macrocephalae)

\[Actions\] Removes cold and resolves dampness

\[Indications\] Cold-damp affecting the kidney: Body heaviness, cold
pain below the back, extreme heavy sensation in the lower back, normal
appetite, absence of thirst, copious urination, a pale tongue with a
white coating and a deep, slow or deep, delayed pulse.

There is only one different ingredient between *Gan Cao Gan Jiang Fu
Ling Bai Zhu Tang* and *Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang*. Using *Fu Ling* as the
monarch and *Gui Zhi* as the minister, Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang warms yang
and transforms fluid. It is indicated for phlegm fluid retention due to
spleen yang deficiency. Using *Gan Jiang* as the monarch and *Fu Ling*
as the minister, *Gan Cao Gan Jiang Fu Ling Bai Zhu Tan*g warms the
middle *jiao*, dispels cold and resolves dampness. It is indicated for
cold dampness affecting the kidney.

***Zhen Wu Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 附子 | *fù zĭ* | 9g  | Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata |
| narch |        |         |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Mini  | > 白术 | *bái    | 6g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae |
| sters |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 9g  | Poria                              |
|       |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 生姜 | *shēng  | 9g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens          |
| tants |        | jiāng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 白芍 | *bái    | 9g  | Radix Paeoniae Alba                |
|       |        | sháo*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Fu Zi* is pungent and warm. It warms kidney yang to transform
qi and move water, warms spleen yang to resolve water dampness and
dispels cold to stop abdominal pain.

Ministers: *Bai Zhu* and *Fu Ling* supplement qi, fortify the spleen,
and resolve water dampness.

Assistants: *Sheng Jiang* resolves water retention, warms the stomach
and stops vomiting. *Bai Shao* astringes yin, harmonizes yang and
protects yin from being damaged by the yang-warming *Bai Zhu* and *Fu
Zi*. In addition, it relaxes the tendons and alleviates twitching.

This formula treats both the root cause and symptoms. It warms yang,
astringes yin and resolves water retention.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms yang and promotes urination.

> **\[Indications\]**

-   Water retention due to yang deficiency: generalized edema or
    heaviness, especially below the low back, cold intolerance, cold
    limbs, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dysuria, cough, panting, belching,
    vomiting, palpitations, dizziness, a pale, swollen tongue with a
    white, slippery coating, and a deep, thready pulse.

-   Cold damage to Taiyang: Unresolved fever upon sweating,
    palpitations, dizziness, body twitching and feeling of unsteadiness
    as if one were about to fall.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for edema due to
yang deficiency. Internal water damp retention due to yang deficiency of
the spleen and kidney may cause dysuria, generalized edema or heaviness.
Water dampness affecting the ascending of clean yang may cause
dizziness. Water dampness affecting the heart may cause palpitations.
Yang deficiency may cause cold intolerance, cold limbs, diarrhea and
abdominal pain. A pale, swollen tongue with a white, slippery coating
and a deep, thready pulse are signs indicative of water retention due to
yang deficiency. As a result, the treatment strategies are to warm yang
qi of the spleen and kidney, promote urination and eliminate edema.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Dysuria, body heaviness or edema,
a pale, swollen tongue with a white coating and a deep pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For cough due to cold damp cold affecting the lung, add *Xi Xin*
    (Radix et Rhizoma Asari), *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Wu
    Wei Zi* (Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis) to warm the lung, transform
    fluid retention, astringe the lung and stop coughing.

-   For diarrhea due to yang deficiency, remove *Bai Shao* and add *Bu
    Gu Zhi* (Fructus Psoraleae) and *Shan Yao* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae) to
    warm the kidney.

-   For vomiting due to cold damp affecting the stomach, add *Gan Jiang*
    (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) to warm the
    middle *jiao*, harmonize the stomach and stop vomiting.

3\. Modern use: Chronic glomerular nephritis, cardiac edema,
hypothyroidism, chronic bronchitis, chronic enteritis and vaginal
discharge that can be diagnosed as internal water dampness due to yang
deficiency of the spleen and kidney.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with dysuria or edema due to internal retention of damp heat.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Fu Zi Tang* (Aconite Decoction) from the *Shang
Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

\[Ingredients\] *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata), *Fu Ling*
(Poria), *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma
Atractylodis Macrocephalae) and *Shao Yao* (Radix Paeoniae)

\[Actions\] Warms yang and meridians, dispels cold and resolves dampness

\[Indications\] Internal retention of cold dampness: Bone/joint pain,
cold intolerance, cold limbs, a white, slippery tongue coating, and a
deep, feeble pulse.

Both *Fu Zi Tang* and *Zhen Wu Tang* warm yang, fortify the spleen and
resolve water dampness. They are both indicated for internal water
dampness due to deficiency cold in the spleen and kidney. However,
composed of large doses of *Fu Zi* and *Bai Zhu* coupled with *Ren
Shen*, *Fu Zi Tang* focuses on warming spleen yang and resolving cold
dampness. It is therefore indicated for bone/joint pain resulting from
internal cold dampness due to yang deficiency. Composed of large doses
of *Sheng Jiang* and small doses of *Fu Zi* and *Bai Zhu*, *Zhen Wu
Tang* focuses warming yang, dispelling cold and resolving water
retention. It is therefore indicated for dysuria and edema resulting
from internal water retention due to yang deficiency of the spleen and
kidney.

***Shi Pi San***

*Chong Ding Yan Shi Ji Sheng Fang* (Revised Yan's Formula to Aid the
Living)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 干姜 | *gān    | 6g  | Rhizoma Zingiberis                 |
| archs |        | jiāng*  |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
|       | > 附子 | *fù zĭ* | 6g  | Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 白术 | *bái    | 6g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae |
| sters |        | zhú*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú     | 6g  | Poria                              |
|       |        | líng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 木瓜 | *mù     | 6g  | Fructus Chaenomelis                |
|       |        | guā*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 厚朴 | *hòu    | 6g  | Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis       |
| tants |        | pò*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 木香 | *mù     | 6g  | Radix Aucklandiae                  |
|       |        | xiāng*  |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 槟榔 | *bīng   | 6g  | Semen Arecae                       |
|       |        | láng*   |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | > 草果 | *căo    | 6g  | Fructus Tsaoko                     |
|       |        | guŏ*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| **En  | >      | *zhì    | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae      |
| voy** | 炙甘草 | gān     |     | Praeparata cum Melle               |
|       |        | căo*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Gan Jiang* warms spleen yang and resolves dampness. *Fu Zi*
is pungent and warm. It warms yang to transform qi and move water. The
combination of *Gan Jiang* and *Fu Zi* warms the kidney and spleen,
activate yang qi of the spleen and kidney and thus warms and transforms
water dampness.

Ministers: *Bai Zhu* and *Fu Ling* supplement qi, fortify the spleen,
and resolve water dampness. *Mu Gua* resolves dampness with aroma,
benefits the spleen and harmonizes the stomach.

Assistants: *Hou Po*, *Mu Xiang*, *Bing Lang* and *Cao Guo* move qi,
relieve stagnation, resolve dampness and circulate water.

Envoy: Zhi Gan Cao supplements qi, harmonizes the middle *jiao* and
coordinates the other ingredients.

This formula warms yang, dispels cold, moves qi and resolves dampness.
It treats both the root cause and symptoms, particularly warms spleen
yang and thus called "*Shi Pi San* (spleen-strengthening powder)".

**\[Method of administration\]** Take 6g powder with 5 pieces of fresh
ginger and 1 Chinese date for each dose, 2 doses a day. Alternatively,
decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms yang, fortifies the spleen, moves qi and
resolves water retention.

> **\[Indications\]** Edema due to yang deficiency: Edema in the lower
> limbs, cold hands/feet, absence of thirst, abdominal
> fullness/distension, loose stools, a white, greasy tongue coating and
> a deep, slow pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for edema due to
yang deficiency. Internal water dampness retention due to yang
deficiency of the spleen and kidney may cause generalized edema,
especially in the lower limbs. Dampness affecting the flow of qi in the
middle *jiao* may cause abdominal fullness/distension, a poor appetite
and loose stools. A pale, swollen tongue with a white, greasy coating
and a deep, thready or deep, slow pulse are signs indicative of internal
water dampness due to yang deficiency. As a result, the treatment
strategies are to warm yang, fortify the spleen, move qi and resolve
water retention.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Edema in the lower limbs,
abdominal fullness/distension, loose stools, a pale, swollen tongue with
a white, greasy coating and a deep, slow pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For dysuria and severe edema, combine *Wu Ling San* to enhance the
    effect of resolving water retention and eliminating edema.

-   For constipation, add *Qian Niu Zi* (Semen Pharbitidis) to promote
    bowel movements.

-   For shortness of breath, fatigue and reluctance to talk, add *Huang
    Qi* (Radix Astragali) and *Dang Shen* (Radix Codonopsis) to
    supplement qi and fortify the spleen.

3\. Modern use: Chronic glomerular nephritis, cardiac edema, and ascites
due to liver cirrhosis that can be diagnosed as water-qi stagnation due
to yang deficiency of the spleen and kidney.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula has a strong efficacy in
warming yang and moving qi and it is therefore contraindicated for
patients with yang edema.

***Bi Xie Fen Qing Yin***

*Dan Xi Xin Fa* (Teachings of Zhu Dan-xi)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 萆薢 | *bì     | 9g  | Rhizoma Dioscoreae Hypoglaucae     |
| narch |        | xiè*    |     |                                    |
+=======+========+=========+=====+====================================+
| Min   | >      | *yì zhì | 9g  | Fructus Alpiniae Oxyphyllae        |
| ister | 益智仁 | rén*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
| Assis | > 乌药 | *wū     | 9g  | Radix Linderae                     |
| tants |        | yào*    |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+
|       | >      | *shí    | 9g  | Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii          |
|       | 石菖蒲 | chāng   |     |                                    |
|       |        | pú*     |     |                                    |
+-------+--------+---------+-----+------------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Bi Xie* resolves dampness and separates the clear from turbid.
It is a common herb for cloudy, milky or greasy urine.

Minister: *Yi Zhi Ren* warms yang, tonifies the kidney, secures essence,
and astringes urine. It enhances the efficacy of *Bi Xie* in warming
kidney qi and separating the clear from turbid.

Assistants: *Wu Yao* warms the lower *jiao*, moves qi and resolves
dampness. It also assists *Yi Zhi Ren* in warming the kidney. *Shi Chang
Pu* is aromatic and acts to resolve phlegm turbidity and open the
orifices.

This formula treats the root cause by warming yang and transforming qi,
coupled with resolving phlegm turbidity.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water and add a small
amount of salt when drinking the decoction.

**\[Functions\]** Warms the kidney, resolves dampness and separates the
clear from turbid.

> **\[Indications\]** Milky (cloudy) urine due to deficiency cold in the
> lower *jiao*: Frequent, urgent urination with turbid, milky
> (rice-washed water) urine, a pale tongue with a white coating and a
> deep pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for cloudy or
milky urine due to deficiency cold in the lower *jiao*. Kidney failing
to control the urinary bladder may cause frequent urgent urination with
turbid, cloudy urine. A pale tongue with a white coating and a deep
pulse are signs indicative of deficiency cold in the lower *jiao*. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to warm the kidney, resolve
dampness and separate the clear from turbid.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Frequent, urgent urination with
cloudy, milky urine, a pale tongue with a white coating and a deep
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For abdominal pain, add *Xiao Hui Xiang* (Fructus Foeniculi) to warm
    the interior and remove cold.

-   For weight loss and fatigue, add *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali), *Bai
    Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae), *Sheng Ma* (Rhizoma
    Cimicifugae) and *Chai Hu* (Radix Bupleuri) to supplement qi, and
    fortify the spleen.

-   For low back soreness and pain, add *Chuan Duan* (Radix Dipsaci),
    *Gou Ji* (Rhizoma Cibotii) and *Xian Mao* (Rhizoma Curculiginis) to
    tonify the kidney and strengthen the lumbar vertebrae.

-   For vaginal discharge due to cold dampness, add *Lu* *Jiao Shuang*
    (Cornu Cervi Degelatinatum), *Shan Yao* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae), *Tu Si
    Zi* (Semen Cuscutae) and *Zi Shi Ying* (Fluoritum) to warm the
    kidney and resolve dampness.

3\. Modern use: Chylous urine, chronic prostatitis, chronic
nephropyelitis, chronic nephritis, and chronic pelvic inflammation that
can be diagnosed as retention of turbid phlegm due to kidney deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with cloudy or milky urine due to downward flow of damp heat to
the urinary bladder.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Bi Xie Fen Qing Yin* (Hypoglaucae Root
Turbidity-Clearing Beverage) from the *Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical
Revelations)

\[Ingredients\] *Chuan Bi Xie* (Rhizoma Dioscoreae Hypoglaucae), *Huang
Bai* (Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis), *Shi Chang Pu* (Rhizoma Acori
Tatarinowii), *Fu Ling* (Poria), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis
Macrocephalae), *Lian Zi Xin* (Plumula Nelumbinis), *Dan Shen* (Radix et
Rhizoma Salviae Miltiorrhizae) and *Che Qian Zi* (Semen Plantaginis)

\[Actions\] Clears heat, resolves dampness and separates the clear from
turbid

\[Indications\] Stranguria due to damp heat: Cloudy urine, urinary
dribbling, and a yellow, greasy tongue coating.

*Bi Xie* and *Shi Chang Pu* are used in both formulas to resolve
dampness and separate the clear from turbid. They are both indicated for
milky or cloudy urine due to downward flow of dampness. However, the
former combines *Yi Zhi Ren* and *Wu Yao* to warm yang and dispel cold.
It is therefore indicated for milky urine due to deficiency cold in the
lower *jiao*. The latter combines *Huang Bai* and *Che Qian Zi* to clear
heat and resolve dampness. It is therefore indicated for greasy urine
due to damp heat in the lower *jiao*.

**15.5 Formulas that remove wind and resolve dampness**

Formulas that remove wind and resolve dampness are indicated for wind
damp affecting the surface of the body, meridians and joints. Clinical
manifestations may include headache, heaviness of the body, and numbness
or pain in the low back and knee joints. Common wind-removing
ingredients like *Qiang Huo* (Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii), *Du Huo*
(Radix Angelicae Pubescentis), *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae), *Qin*
*Jiao* (Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae) and *San Ji Sheng* (Herba Taxilli)
are often used in combination with qi-moving, blood-circulating,
liver/kidney-nourishing and qi/blood-tonifying ingredients like *Dang
Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Chuan Xiong* (Rhizoma Chuanxiong),
*Huai Niu Xi* (Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae), *Du Zhong* (Cortex
Eucommiae) and *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali). Typical formulas are *Qiang
Huo Sheng Shi Tang* (Notopterygium Dampness-Drying Decoction) and *Du
Huo Ji Sheng Tang* (Pubescent Angelica and Mistletoe Decoction).

***Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang***

*Pi Wei Lun* (Treatise on the Spleen and Stomach)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Mon   | > 羌活 | *qiāng | 6g    | Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii    |
| archs |        | huó*   |       |                                  |
+=======+========+========+=======+==================================+
|       | > 独活 | *dú    | 6g    | Radix Angelicae Pubescentis      |
|       |        | huó*   |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Mini  | > 防风 | *fáng  | 3g    | Radix Saposhnikoviae             |
| sters |        | fēng*  |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
|       | > 川芎 | *chuān | 1.5g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong               |
|       |        | xiōng* |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Assis | > 藁本 | *găo   | 3g    | Rhizoma Ligustici                |
| tants |        | bĕn*   |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
|       | >      | *màn   | 2g    | Fructus Viticis                  |
|       | 蔓荆子 | jīng   |       |                                  |
|       |        | zĭ*    |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+
| Envoy | >      | *zhì   | 3g    | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae    |
|       | 炙甘草 | gān    |       | Praeparata cum Melle             |
|       |        | căo*   |       |                                  |
+-------+--------+--------+-------+----------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Qiang Huo* and *Du Huo* are dry, aromatic, pungent, bitter,
warm and dispersing. They act to disperse wind cold, resolve dampness
and alleviate pain. *Qiang Huo* is especially good in removing wind
dampness of the upper body, whereas *Du Huo* is especially good in
removing wind dampness of the lower body. The combination of the two
removes wind dampness out of the entire body, unblocks joints, and
alleviates *Bi*-impediment pain.

Ministers: *Fang Feng* removes wind, resolves dampness and alleviates
pain. *Chuan Xiong* circulates blood, moves qi, removes wind and
alleviates pain.

Assistants: *Gao Ben* dispels wind cold and alleviates headache. *Man
Jing Zi* is light and acts to remove wind in the head and face and
alleviate pain.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* supplements qi, reinforces the middle *jiao* and
coordinates the other ingredients.

Consisting of pungent, warm, ascending and dispersing ingredients, this
formula removes wind, resolves dampness and alleviates pain; however,
considering its small dose and mild efficacy, it mainly aims to induce
sweating to remove wind dampness and release the exterior.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Removes wind, resolves dampness and alleviates pain

**\[Indications\]** Wind-dampness exterior syndrome: Headache, body
ache, pain in the shoulder, back, and low back, difficulty in rolling
over the body, a white tongue coating, and a superficial pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for wind
dampness affecting the surface of the body or meridians. External wind
dampness obstructing the qi flow of bladder meridian may cause headache,
body ache, pain in the shoulder, back and low back, difficulty in
rolling over the body, a white tongue coating and a superficial pulse.
As a result, the treatment strategies are to remove wind, resolve
dampness, unblock meridians and alleviate pain with pungent, warm and
dispersing ingredients.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Headache, body ache, low back
pain, a white tongue coating and a superficial pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe dampness with body ache or heaviness, add *Cang Zhu*
    (Rhizoma Atractylodis) and *Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari) to
    resolve dampness and unblock meridians.

-   For dampness transforming into heat with joint redness and swelling,
    add *Huang Bai* (Cortex Phellodendri Chinensis), *Ren Dong Teng*
    (Caulis Lonicerae Japonicae) and *Qin* *Jiao* (Radix Gentianae
    Macrophyllae) to clear heat and unblock meridians.

3\. Modern use: Rheumatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis,
osteohyperplasia or ankylosing spondylitis that can be diagnosed as wind
dampness affecting the exterior.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with yin/blood deficiency. In addition, it's advisable to avoid
too much sweating and stay away from wind cold after taking the
decoction.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Juan Bi Tang* (Painful Obstruction-Removing
Decoction) from the *Yang Shi Jia Cang Fang* (Secret Formulas of the
Yang Family)

\[Ingredients\] *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Qiang Huo*
(Rhizoma et Radix Notopterygii), *Jiang Huang* (Rhizoma Curcumae
Longae), *Huang Qi* (Radix Astragali), *Bai Shao* (Radix Paeoniae Alba),
*Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae) and *Zhi Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma
Glycyrrhizae Praeparata cum Melle)

\[Actions\] Supplements qi, nourishes blood, removes wind and resolves
dampness

\[Indications\] Wind, cold and dampness affecting the meridians: Pain
and impaired movement in the neck, shoulder and back, and numbness in
the hands/feet.

Both *Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang* and *Juan Bi Tang* can remove wind,
resolve dampness, unblock impediment and alleviate pain. With pungent
and dispersing ingredients, *Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tan*g has a strong
efficacy in resolving dampness and alleviating pain. It is indicated for
excessive syndrome with headache, body ache and pain in the neck, back
and low back due to wind dampness affecting the exterior. With
qi-supplementing and blood-nourishing ingredients like *Huang Qi, Dang
Gui* and *Bai Sha*o, *Juan Bi Tang* reinforces anti-pathogenic qi and is
indicated for chronic Bi-impediment in combined deficiency and excess
syndrome with pain or numbness in the limbs due to deficiency of qi and
blood.

***Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang***

*Bei Ji Qian Jin Yao Fang* (Important Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold
Pieces for Emergency)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Mo    | > 独活 | *dú huó*  | 9g  | Radix Angelicae Pubescentis     |
| narch |        |           |     |                                 |
+=======+========+===========+=====+=================================+
| Mini  | > 防风 | *fáng     | 6g  | Radix Saposhnikoviae            |
| sters |        | fēng*     |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 秦艽 | *qín      | 6g  | Radix Gentianae Macrophyllae    |
|       |        | jiāo*     |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 肉桂 | *ròu guì* | 6g  | Cortex Cinnamomi                |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 细辛 | *xì xīn*  | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Asari          |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Assis | >      | *sāng jì  | 6g  | Herba Taxilli                   |
| tants | 桑寄生 | shēng*    |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 牛膝 | *niú xī*  | 6g  | Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae    |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 杜仲 | *dù       | 6g  | Cortex Eucommiae                |
|       |        | zhòng*    |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 当归 | *dāng     | 6g  | Radix Angelicae Sinensis        |
|       |        | guī*      |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 川芎 | *chuān    | 6g  | Rhizoma Chuanxiong              |
|       |        | xiōng*    |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | >      | *gān dì   | 6g  | Radix Rehmanniae Recens         |
|       | 干地黄 | huáng*    |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 芍药 | *sháo     | 6g  | Radix Paeoniae                  |
|       |        | yào*      |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 人参 | *rén      | 6g  | Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng        |
|       |        | shēn*     |     |                                 |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
|       | > 茯苓 | *fú líng* | 6g  | Poria                           |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+
| Envoy | > 甘草 | *gān căo* | 3g  | Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae   |
+-------+--------+-----------+-----+---------------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Du Huo* is pungent, bitter and warm. It removes wind, dispels
cold, resolves dampness and alleviates pain. It is especially good in
removing wind, cold and dampness out of the lower body.

Ministers: *Fang Feng* and *Qin* *Jiao* remove wind, resolve cold,
unblock meridians and alleviate pain. *Rou Gui* and *Xi Xin* remove
wind, dispel cold, warm and unblock meridians and alleviate pain. *Rou
Gui* also unblocks blood vessels.

The combination of *Du Huo* and four minister ingredients removes wind,
resolves dampness, unblocks meridians, dispels cold, and alleviates pain
in the limbs, joints, muscles and bones.

Assistants: *Sang Ji Sheng*, *Niu Xi* and *Du Zhong* tonify the liver
and kidney, strengthen the bones and tendons, remove wind and resolve
dampness. *Dang Gui*, *Chuan Xiong*, *Gan Di Huang* and *Shao Yao*
nourish and circulate blood and alleviate *Bi*-impediment.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients.

This formula mainly removes wind, dispels cold and resolves dampness. At
the same time, it tonifies the liver and kidney and supplements qi and
blood.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Removes wind, resolves dampness, alleviates pain,
benefits the liver and kidney, and supplements qi and blood.

**\[Indications\]** Chronic *Bi*-impediment syndrome with deficiency of
the liver, kidney, qi and blood: Pain, numbness or flaccidity in the low
back and knee joints, impaired joint flexion and extension, cold
intolerance, palpitations, shortness of breath, a pale tongue with a
white coating, and a thready, weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a typical formula for chronic
*B*i-impediment coupled with deficiency of the liver, kidney, qi and
blood. Wind, cold and dampness obstructing the joints, meridians,
tendons or bones, coupled with deficiency of the liver, kidney, qi and
blood may cause chronic pain in the low back and knee joints, impaired
joint flexion and extension, or numbness in the limbs. Retention of cold
dampness coupled with deficiency of the liver, kidney, qi and blood may
cause cold intolerance, palpitations, shortness of breath, a pale
tongue, and a thready, weak pulse. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to remove wind, resolve dampness, alleviate pain, tonify the liver
and kidney and supplement qi and blood.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Pain in the low back and knee
joints with a cold sensation, impaired joint flexion and extension,
palpitations, shortness of breath, a pale tongue with a white coating,
and a thready, weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe pain in the low back, leg and joint, add *Zhi Chuan Wu*
    (Radix Aconiti Praeparata), *Zhi Cao Wu* (Radix Aconiti Kusnezoffii
    Praeparata) and *Bai Hua She She Cao* (Herba Hedyotis Diffusae) to
    remove wind, unblock meridians, and alleviate pain.

-   For severe cold retention, add *Fu Zi* (Radix Aconiti Lateralis
    Praeparata) and *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) to warm yang and
    remove cold.

-   For severe impairment of joint flexion and extension, add *Ji Xue
    Teng* (Caulis Spatholobi), *Shi Nan Teng* (Caulis Photiniae) and
    *Hai Feng Teng* (Caulis Piperis Kadsurae) to unblock meridians and
    alleviate joint pain.

3\. Modern use: Chronic rheumatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis,
sciatica, lumbar strain, osteohyperplasia or polio that can be diagnosed
as chronic Bi-impediment syndrome with deficiency of the liver, kidney,
qi and blood.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is warm and dispersing
and therefore contraindicated for patients with *Bi*-impediment syndrome
due to damp heat.

**\[Annex formula\]** *San Bi Tang* (Three Impediments Decoction) from
the *Fu Ren Da Quan Liang Fang* (The Complete Compendium of Fine
Formulas for Women)

\[Ingredients\] *Chuan Xu Duan* (Radix Dipsaci), *Du Zhong* (Cortex
Eucommiae), *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae), *Gui Xin* (Cortex
Cinnamomi), *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Bai Fu Ling*
(Poria), *Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis), *Bai Shao* (Radix
Paeoniae Alba), *Sheng Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens), *Gan Cao*
(Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae), *Qin* *Jiao* (Radix Gentianae
Macrophyllae), *Sheng Di Huang* (Radix Rehmanniae), *Chuan Xiong*
(Rhizoma Chuanxiong), *Du Huo* (Radix Angelicae Pubescentis), *Huang Qi*
(Radix Astragali) and *Chuan Niu Xi* (Radix Achyranthis Bidentatae)

\[Actions\] Tonifies the liver and kidney, supplements qi, harmonizes
blood, removes wind and resolves dampness

\[Indications\] Deficiency of the liver, kidney, qi and blood:
Contracture of hands/feet, wind-Bi impediment or qi-impediment.

San Bi Tang is modified from *Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang* by removing *Sang*
*Ji Sheng* and adding *Huang Qi*, *Xu Dua*n and *Sheng Jiang*. Both
formulas remove wind, resolve dampness, alleviate pain, benefit the
liver and kidney, and tonify qi and blood. *Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang* is
better in tonifying the liver and kidney and therefore indicated for low
back and leg pain. *San Bi Tang* is better in tonifying qi and therefore
indicated for contracture, numbness and pain of the hands/feet.

**Summary**

There are a total of 19 formulas and 13 annex formulas in this chapter.
These formulas are classified into three categories: formulas to resolve
dampness with aroma, formulas to clear heat and resolve dampness,
formulas to promote urination and drain dampness, formulas to warm and
transform water dampness, and formulas to remove wind and resolve
dampness.

**Formulas that resolve dampness with aroma**

Formulas that resolve dampness with aroma regulate qi and harmonize the
stomach. They are indicated for disharmony between the spleen and
stomach due to dampness obstructing the middle *jiao*. *Ping Wei San* is
dries dampness, benefits the spleen, moves qi and harmonizes the
stomach. It is a basic formula for dampness affecting the spleen and
stomach. *Huo Xiang Zheng Qi San* resolves dampness, regulates qi,
harmonizes the middle *jiao*, and dispels wind cold. It is indicated for
cholera (vomiting or diarrhea) due to dampness obstructing the qi
activity of the spleen and stomach coupled with exterior wind cold
syndrome.

**Formulas that clear heat and resolve dampness**

Formulas that clear heat and resolve dampness are indicated for external
contraction of damp heat, internal damp heat or downward flow of damp
heat. *Yin Chen Hao Tang* clears heat, resolves dampness and relieves
jaundice. It is indicated for yang jaundice due to damp heat
accumulating in the liver, gallbladder, spleen and stomach. *Ba Zheng
San* clears heat, promotes urination and relieves stranguria. It is
indicated for stranguria due to downward flow of damp heat to the
urinary bladder. *San Ren Tang* and *Gan Lu Xiao Du Dan* both work for
damp heat. *San Ren Tang* has a stronger efficacy in clearing heat and
regulating qi activity of San*jiao*. It is indicated for early-stage
damp heat when pathogenic factors affect the qi phase (more dampness
than heat). *Gan Lu Xiao Du Dan* clears heat and resolves dampness. It
is indicated for damp heat or seasonal pestilence when patients present
with both damp and heat. *Lian Pu Yi*n clears heat, resolves dampness,
regulates qi and harmonizes the middle *jiao*. It is a common formula
for cholera due to damp heat. *Dang Gui Nian Tong San* resolves
dampness, clears heat, removes wind and alleviates pain. It is indicated
for *Bi*-impediment syndrome due to damp heat. *Er Miao San* dries
dampness and clears heat. It is indicated for Wei-flaccidity syndrome
due to downward flow of damp heat, eczema or vaginal discharges.

**Formulas that promote urination and drain dampness**

Formulas that promote urination and drain dampness are indicated for
edema, diarrhea or stranguria due to internal retention of water
dampness. *Wu Ling San* and *Zhu Ling Tang* are both indicated for
dysuria. *Wu Ling San* warms yang, transforms qi and promotes urination.
It is indicated for water retention, edema, diarrhea, or phlegm-fluid
retention due to impaired qi transformation of the urinary bladder by
internal water dampness. *Zhu Ling Tang* promotes urination, clears
heat, and nourishes yin. It is indicated for dysuria due to intertwined
water and heat coupled with yin damage. *Fang Ji Huang Qi Tang*
supplements qi, secures the exterior, removes wind and resolves water
retention. It is indicated for wind-damp retention and wind water
syndrome due to deficiency of *Wei-*defensive qi and water dampness
affecting the exterior or meridians. *Wu Pi San* promotes urination,
resolves edema, fortifies the spleen and moves qi. It is indicated for
skin water syndrome due to water retention and qi stagnation.

**Formulas that warm and transform dampness**

Formulas that warm and transform dampness warm yang and resolve
dampness. They are indicated for edema or phlegm-fluid retention due to
yang qi failing to transform water. *Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang* warms yang
and resolves fluid retention. It is indicated for epigastric
phlegm-fluid retention due to spleen yang deficiency. Both *Zhen Wu
Tang* and *Shi Pi San* can warm the kidney and spleen, promote urination
and resolve edema. They are indicated for edema due to yang deficiency
of the spleen and kidney. *Zhen Wu Ta*ng warms kidney yang, astringes
yin and alleviates spasm. It is indicated for edema due to kidney yang
deficiency. *Shi Pi San* warms spleen yang, moves qi and relieves
fullness. It is indicated for edema due to spleen yang deficiency
coupled with water retention and qi stagnation. *Bi Xie Fen Qing Yin*
warms the kidney and resolves phlegm-turbidity. It is indicated for
white vaginal discharge and cloudy urine due to kidney yang deficiency.

**Formulas that remove wind and resolve dampness**

Formulas that remove wind and resolve dampness unblock meridians and
alleviate pain. They are indicated for wind damp affecting the exterior,
meridians or joints. Both *Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang* and *Du Huo Ji
Sheng Tang* remove wind, dispel cold and resolve dampness. They are
indicated for *Bi*-impediment syndrome due to wind, cold and dampness.
*Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang* removes wind, resolves dampness and
alleviates pain. It is indicated for wind dampness affecting the
exterior and meridians. *Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang* removes wind, dispels
cold, resolves dampness, tonifies the liver and kidney and supplements
qi and blood. It is indicated for chronic *Bi*-impediment syndrome (due
to wind, cold and dampness) coupled with deficiency of the liver,
kidney, qi and blood.

+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 16 Formulas that Resolve Phlegm**

Formulas that resolve phlegm are indicated for phlegm from all causes.
It falls into the category of reducing strategy (*Xiao Fa*).

Phlegm fluids are pathologic products resulting from metabolic disorder
of water/fluids. However, they are also pathogenic factors that result
in other problems. Phlegm fluids may affect the chest, GI system, head,
body, and four limbs and lead to a variety of conditions.

Based on the property and associated symptoms of phlegm, formulas that
resolve phlegm are subdivided into five types---formulas that dry
dampness and resolve phlegm, formulas that clear heat and resolve
phlegm, formulas that moisten the lung and resolve phlegm, formulas that
warm the lung and transform phlegm and formulas that stop wind and
resolve phlegm.

Formulas that resolve phlegm consist of phlegm-resolving ingredients,
coupled with ingredients that strengthen the spleen, warm the kidney and
disperse the lung. Three organs---the lung, spleen and kidney
(especially the spleen) are closely associated with the water metabolism
and distribution. As a result, phlegm fluids mainly result from
dysfunctions of the lung, spleen and kidney. The treatment strategies
are to resolve phlegm/fluid, strengthen the spleen (to stop the source
of phlegm generation), and circulate qi (to help resolve phlegm).

Because of the moving and reducing property of ingredients, formulas
that resolve phlegm shouldn't be taken for a long period of time. For
early-stage common cold and cough, it's not advised to use herbs that
moisten the lung and resolve phlegm to prevent the retention of
pathogenic qi. For difficult expectoration of thick phlegm or with a
tendency to hemoptysis, it's not advised to use warm, dry ingredients to
prevent reckless moving of blood. In summary, it's essential to identify
cold, heat, deficiency and excess as well as symptoms and the root cause
before treatment.

**16.1 Formulas that dry dampness and resolve phlegm**

## Formulas that dry dampness and resolve phlegm are indicated for phlegm dampness. Patients may present with cough with easy expectoration of profuse phlegm, chest/abdominal fullness, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, fatigue, a white, greasy or slippery tongue coating, and a delayed or slippery pulse. Common ingredients include dampness-drying and phlegm-resolving herbs like *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) and *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) and spleen-fortifying and qi-regulating herbs like *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae), *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis), *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae) and *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus). Typical formulas include *Er Chen Tang* (Two Matured Substances Decoction), *Wen Dan Tang* (Gallbladder-Warming Decoction) and *Fu Ling Wan* (Poria Pill).  {#formulas-that-dry-dampness-and-resolve-phlegm-are-indicated-for-phlegm-dampness.-patients-may-present-with-cough-with-easy-expectoration-of-profuse-phlegm-chestabdominal-fullness-nausea-vomiting-dizziness-fatigue-a-white-greasy-or-slippery-tongue-coating-and-a-delayed-or-slippery-pulse.-common-ingredients-include-dampness-drying-and-phlegm-resolving-herbs-like-ban-xia-rhizoma-pinelliae-and-dan-nan-xing-arisaema-cum-bile-and-spleen-fortifying-and-qi-regulating-herbs-like-bai-zhu-rhizoma-atractylodis-macrocephalae-cang-zhu-rhizoma-atractylodis-chen-pi-pericarpium-citri-reticulatae-and-zhi-shi-fructus-aurantii-immaturus.-typical-formulas-include-er-chen-tang-two-matured-substances-decoction-wen-dan-tang-gallbladder-warming-decoction-and-fu-ling-wan-poria-pill. .unnumbered}

***Er Chen Tang***

*Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang* (Beneficial Formulas from the Taiping
Imperial Pharmacy)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch      半夏     *bàn xià* 15g    Rhizoma Pinelliae
  ------------ -------- --------- ------ ------------------------------------
  Minister     橘红     *jú hóng* 15g    Exocarpium Citri Rubrum

  Assistants   茯苓     *fú líng* 9g     Poria

               炙甘草   *zhì gān  4.5g   Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
                        căo*             Praeparata cum Melle
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ban Xia* is dry and warm and acts to dry dampness, resolve
phlegm, bears down stomach qi and stops vomiting.

Minister: *Ju Hong* regulates qi, harmonizes the stomach, dries dampness
and resolves phlegm.

Assistant: *Fu Ling* strengthens the spleen and drains dampness. Fresh
ginger is warm and acts to transform phlegm fluid, bear qi down, and
harmonize the stomach. It counteracts the toxic property of *Ban Xia*
but enhances the phlegm-resolving efficacy of *Ban Xia*. *Wu Mei* (dark
plum) is sour and acts to protect anti-pathogenic qi from being damaged
by dry, warm and pungent herbs.

Envoy: *Zhi Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients, strengthens the
spleen, harmonizes the spleen and stomach, and prevents the generation
of phlegm.

Main ingredients in this formula dry dampness and resolve phlegm.
Ingredients that move qi, strengthen the spleen and drain dampness are
used in combination to disperse and astringe.

**\[Method of administration\]** Add 7 pieces of fresh ginger, 1 dark
plum and decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Dries dampness, resolves phlegm, regulates qi and
harmonizes the spleen and stomach.

**\[Indications\]** Phlegm dampness: Cough with easy expectoration of
profuse, white phlegm, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, dizziness, blurred
vision, palpitations, a white, slippery or greasy tongue coating, and a
slippery pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for phlegm
dampness. Phlegm dampness affecting the dispersing and descending of
lung qi may cause cough with easy expectoration of profuse, white
phlegm. Dampness disturbing stomach qi may cause nausea and vomiting.
Phlegm obstructing the flow of qi may cause fatigue, dizziness, blurred
vision or palpitations. As a result, the treatment strategies are to dry
dampness, resolve phlegm, regulate qi, and harmonize the spleen and
stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough with easy expectoration of
profuse phlegm, a white, slippery or greasy tongue coating, and a
slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For wind phlegm, add *Tian Nan Xing* (Rhizoma Arisaematis) and *Zhu
    Li* (Succus Bambusae) to stop wind and resolve phlegm.

-   For phlegm heat, add *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and *Dan Nan
    Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) to clear heat and resolve phlegm.

-   For cold phlegm, add *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis) and *Xi Xin*
    (Radix et Rhizoma Asari) to warm the lung and transforms fluid
    retention.

-   For phlegm coupled with food stagnation, add *Lai Fu Zi* (Semen
    Raphani) and *Shen Qu* (Massa Medicata Fermentata) to promote
    digestion and resolve phlegm.

-   For phlegm qi stagnation, add *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus)
    and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) to regulate qi and
    resolve phlegm.

-   For phlegm affecting Shaoyang meridian, add *Bai Jie Zi* (Semen
    Sinapis) to unblock meridians and resolve phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis, emphysema, chronic gastritis,
morning sickness, and neurogenic vomiting that can be diagnosed as
phlegm dampness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Considering the dry, warm properties
of some ingredients in this formula, it is contraindicated for patients
with hemoptysis or yin blood deficiency.

**\[Annex formulas\]**
