*Dao Tan Tang* is modified from *Er Chen Tang*---removed *Wu Mei* and
added *Tian Nan Xing* (Rhizoma Arisaematis) and *Zhi Shi* (Fructus
Aurantii Immaturus). It has a strong effect of resolving phlegm and
circulating qi and is indicated for cold limbs, or in severe cases,
syncope. *Di Tan Tang* is modified from *Dao Tan Tang* by adding *Shi
Chang Pu* (Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii), *Zhu Ru* (Caulis Bambusae in
Taenia) and *Ren Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng). It is indicated for
wind stroke and tongue stiffness. *Jin Shui Liu Jun Jian* is modified
from *Er Chen Tang* by adding *Shu Di* (Radix Rehmanniae Praeparata) and
*Dang Gui* (Radix Angelicae Sinensis) to nourish yin and blood. It is
indicated for yin deficiency of the lung and kidney, coupled with
internal phlegm dampness.

***Wen Dan Tang***

*San Yin Ji Yi Bing Zheng Fang Lun* (Treatise on Diseases, Patterns, and
Formulas Related to the Unification of the Three Etiologies)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch      半夏     *bàn xià* 6g     Rhizoma Pinelliae
  ------------ -------- --------- ------ ------------------------------------
  Minister     竹茹     *zhú rú*  6g     Caulis Bambusae in Taenia

  Assistants   枳实     *zhĭ shí* 6g     Fructus Aurantii Immaturus

               陈皮     *chén pí* 9g     Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae

               茯苓     *fú líng* 4.5g   Poria

  **Envoy**    炙甘草   *zhì gān  3g     Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
                        căo*             Praeparata cum Melle
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ban Xia* dries dampness, resolves phlegm, bears qi down and
harmonizes the stomach.

Minister: *Zhu Ru* clears the gallbladder, harmonize the stomach, stop
vomiting, and relieve restlessness.

Assistants: *Zhi Shi* and *Chen Pi* regulate qi and resolve phlegm. *Fu
Ling* strengthens the spleen and drain dampness.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients.

Fresh ginger and Chinese date are combined to harmonize the spleen and
stomach and counteract the toxic property of *Ban Xia*.

The formula resolves phlegm without causing dryness, clears heat without
causing coldness, and at the same time, harmonizes the gallbladder and
stomach. It is name "Gallbladder-Warming Decoction", because the
gallbladder houses wind (wood), likes free will and dislikes to be
restricted by bitter, cold herbs. In terms of compatibility, this
formula has two characteristics: One is combination of phlegm-resolving
and qi-regulating herbs; and the other is combination of warm and cool
property herbs (neither cold nor dry).

*Wen Dan Tang* and *Suan Zao Ren Tang* are both indicated for
restlessness and insomnia. The former is more indicated for insomnia due
to disharmony between the gallbladder and stomach coupled with internal
phlegm heat, whereas the latter is more indicated for insomnia due to
internal heat due to yin/blood deficiency of the liver and heart.

**\[Method of administration\]** Add 5 pieces of fresh ginger and 1
Chinese date and decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Regulates qi, resolves phlegm, clears the gallbladder
and harmonizes the stomach.

**\[Indications\]** Disharmony between the gallbladder and stomach and
internal phlegm-heat: Timidity, jumpiness or easily startled, dizziness,
palpitations, restlessness, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, vomiting,
hiccups, a yellow, greasy tongue coating, and a wiry, slippery pulse. It
can also be used for epilepsy.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for
disharmony between the gallbladder and stomach, coupled with internal
phlegm heat. The gallbladder governs the decision-making process. Phlegm
heat disturbing the gallbladder may cause timidity, jumpiness/easily
startled, insomnia, and dream-disturbed sleep. Phlegm heat disturbing
the mind may cause epilepsy. As stomach qi descends, the disharmony
between the gallbladder and stomach may cause ascending of stomach qi,
resulting in vomiting and hiccups. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to regulate qi, resolve phlegm, clear the gallbladder, and harmonize
the stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Restlessness, insomnia,
dizziness, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, a yellow, greasy tongue
coating, and a wiry, slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For restlessness due to internal heat, add *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma
    Coptidis) and *Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae) to clear heat and relieve
    restlessness.

-   For a dry mouth and tongue, remove *Ban Xia* and add *Gua Lou Ren*
    (Semen Trichosanthis) and *Tian Hua Fen* (Radix Trichosanthis) to
    moisten dryness and regenerate fluids.

-   For epileptic convulsions, add *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile),
    *Gou Teng* (Ramulus Uncariae Cum Uncis) and *Quan Xie* (Scorpio) to
    stop wind and relieve convulsions.

3\. Modern use: Neurosis, acute/chronic gastritis, chronic bronchitis,
Meniere's syndrome and morning sickness that can be diagnosed as
internal phlegm heat and disharmony between the gallbladder and stomach.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Despite the name of "Gallbladder
Warming Decoction", this formula has no effecting of warming the
gallbladder. It is contraindicated for cold phlegm.

***Fu Ling Wan***

*Shi Zhai Bai Yi Xuan Fang* (Selected Formulas from the Praiseworthy
Studio) quoted from the *Yu Sheng Zhi Mi Fang* (Comprehensive
Mystery-Solving Formulas)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch      半夏     *bàn xià* **9**g   Rhizoma Pinelliae
  ------------ -------- --------- -------- ------------------------------------
  Minister     茯苓     *fú líng* 6g       Poria

  Assistants   枳壳     *zhĭ      3g       Fructus Aurantii
                        qiào*              

               芒硝     *máng     3g       Natrii Sulfas
                        xiāo*              
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Ban Xia* dries dampness and resolves phlegm.

Minister: *Fu Ling* strengthens the spleen and drain dampness.

Assistants: Zhi Qiao soothes the chest, regulates qi and resolves
phlegm. *Mang Xiao* softens hardness, moistens dryness, and eliminates
hidden phlegm.

Fresh ginger is combined to counteract the toxic property of *Ban Xia*,
resolve phlegm and fluid retention.

The formula reduces and drains downward. It regulates qi, resolves
phlegm, and at the same time, disperses stagnation and relieves
swelling.

It is modified from *Er Cheng Tang* by removing *Ju Hong* and *Gan Cao*
but adding *Zhi Qiao* and *Mang Xia*o. It has the similar effect as *Er
Chen Tang* in drying dampness and moving qi; however, it has stronger
effect in resolving phlegm. Mang Xiao not only softens hardness and
resolves phlegm, but also cleanse the hidden, turbid phlegm through
bowel movements.

**\[Method of administration\]** 6g of paste pill for each dose; or
decoct with water with melted *Mang Xiao*.

**\[Functions\]** Dries dampness, moves qi, softens hardness and
resolves phlegm

**\[Indications\]** Phlegm retention in the spleen/stomach and
meridians: Pain in the arms, inability to lift arms, flaccidity of the
hands, edema in the limbs, a white, greasy tongue coating, and a deep,
thready or wiry, slippery pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for phlegm
retention in the spleen/stomach and meridians. The spleen dominates
muscles. Spleen deficiency may generate phlegm dampness. The phlegm
dampness affecting the flow of qi in four limbs may cause pain in the
arms, inability to lift arms, flaccidity of the hands and edema in the
limbs. Since phlegm results from spleen deficiency, it's not advised to
apply the same strategy as wind dampness. The treatment strategies
should be to dry dampness, move qi, soften hardness and resolve phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Pain in the arms, a white, greasy
tongue coating, and a deep, thready or wiry, slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For pain in the arms, limbs or joints, add *Sang Zhi* (Ramulus Mori)
    and *Di Long* (Pheretima) to unblock meridians and circulate blood.

-   For cough with thick phlegm and chest stuffiness, add *Hai Ge*
    (Concha Meretricis seu Cyclinae) and *Gua Lou* (Fructus
    Trichosanthis) to regulate qi and resolve phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Vasogenic edema in the upper limbs, cervical spondylosis
and chronic bronchitis that can be diagnosed as phlegm dampness.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: It's contraindicated for arm pain due
to wind dampness. Considering its harsh property, patients with a
weakened immune system should use with caution and stop immediately when
their symptoms are alleviated.

**16.2 Formulas that clear heat and resolve phlegm**

## Formulas that clear heat and resolve phlegm are indicated for heat and phlegm. Patients may present with cough with difficult expectoration of thick, yellow phlegm, chest pain, dizziness, epilepsy, a yellow, greasy tongue coating, and a slippery, rapid pulse. Common ingredients include heat-clearing and phlegm-resolving herbs like *Gua Lou* (Fructus Trichosanthis) and *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile), heat-clearing and fire-reducing herbs like *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae), *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis) and *Zhi Zi* (Fructus Gardeniae), and qi-regulating herbs like *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus) and *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae). Typical formulas include *Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan* (Qi-Clearing and Phlegm-Transforming Pill), *Xiao Xian Xiong Tang* (Minor Chest-Draining Decoction) and *Meng Shi Gun Tan Wan* (Chlorite Phlegm-Removing Pill).  {#formulas-that-clear-heat-and-resolve-phlegm-are-indicated-for-heat-and-phlegm.-patients-may-present-with-cough-with-difficult-expectoration-of-thick-yellow-phlegm-chest-pain-dizziness-epilepsy-a-yellow-greasy-tongue-coating-and-a-slippery-rapid-pulse.-common-ingredients-include-heat-clearing-and-phlegm-resolving-herbs-like-gua-lou-fructus-trichosanthis-and-dan-nan-xing-arisaema-cum-bile-heat-clearing-and-fire-reducing-herbs-like-huang-qin-radix-scutellariae-huang-lian-rhizoma-coptidis-and-zhi-zi-fructus-gardeniae-and-qi-regulating-herbs-like-zhi-shi-fructus-aurantii-immaturus-and-chen-pi-pericarpium-citri-reticulatae.-typical-formulas-include-qing-qi-hua-tan-wan-qi-clearing-and-phlegm-transforming-pill-xiao-xian-xiong-tang-minor-chest-draining-decoction-and-meng-shi-gun-tan-wan-chlorite-phlegm-removing-pill. .unnumbered}

***Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan***

*Yi Fang Kao* (Investigations of Medical Formulas)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarchs     胆南星     *dăn nán    9g     Arisaema cum Bile
                          xīng*              
  ------------ ---------- ----------- ------ ------------------------------
               瓜蒌仁     *guā lóu    6g     Semen Trichosanthis
                          rén*               

  Ministers    黄芩       *huáng qín* 6g     Radix Scutellariae

               半夏       *bàn xià*   9g     Rhizoma Pinelliae

  Assistants   枳实       *zhĭ shí*   6g     Fructus Aurantii Immaturus

               陈皮       *chén pí*   6g     Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae

               杏仁       *xìng rén*  6g     Semen Armeniacae Amarum

               茯苓       *fú líng*   6g     Poria
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Dan Nan Xing* resolves phlegm and clears fire. *Gua Lou Ren*
clears heat and moistens the intestine.

Ministers: *Huang Qin* clears and reduces lung fire. *Ban Xia* resolves
phlegm and disperses stagnation.

Assistants: *Zhi Shi* bears qi down and relieves stagnation. *Chen Pi*
regulates qi, soothes the chest, dries dampness and resolves phlegm.
*Xing Ren* regulates lung qi. *Fu Ling* strengthens the dampness, drains
dampness and resolves phlegm.

Ginger juice is used to resolve phlegm, harmonize the stomach, removes
the toxins of *Ban Xia* and *Dan Nan Xing*.

In terms of compatibility, this formula has two characteristics:
resolving phlegm and clearing heat; and strengthening the spleen to
drain dampness and regulating qi to stop cough.

**\[Method of administration\]** 6-9 water pills for each dose, 2 doses
a day (decrease the dose for children).

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, resolves phlegm, regulates qi and stops
cough

**\[Indications\]** Cough due phlegm heat: Difficult expectoration of
yellow, thick phlegm, chest stuffiness, shortness of breath, nausea,
vomiting, a red tongue with a yellow, greasy coating, and a slippery,
rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for cough
due to phlegm heat. Fire heat scorching fluids may generate phlegm and
result in cough with yellow, thick phlegm. The phlegm heat may affect
the flow of qi and result in chest stuffiness, shortness of breath,
nausea and vomiting. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear
heat, resolve phlegm, regulate qi, and stop cough.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough with yellow, thick phlegm,
a red tongue with a yellow coating, and a slippery, rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe lung heat with fever and thirst, add *Shi Gao* (Gypsum
    Fibrosum) and *Zhi Mu* (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae) to clear heat and
    reduce fire.

-   For profuse phlegm and shortness of breath, add *Yu Xing Cao* (Herba
    Houttuyniae) and *Sang Bai Pi* (Cortex Mori) to clear lung heat.

-   For constipation due to heat, add *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei)
    and *Mang Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas) to clear heat and promote bowel
    movements.

3\. Modern use: Pneumonia and bronchitis that can be diagnosed as
internal retention of phlegm heat.

## 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for coughs with white, thin and clear phlegm or with easy expectoration of white phlegm due to cold dampness.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-coughs-with-white-thin-and-clear-phlegm-or-with-easy-expectoration-of-white-phlegm-due-to-cold-dampness. .unnumbered}

***Xiao Xian Xiong Tang***

*Shang Han Lun* (Treatise on Cold Damage)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch     瓜蒌       *guā lóu*   20g    Fructus Trichosanthis
  ----------- ---------- ----------- ------ ------------------------------
  Minister    黄连       *huáng      6g     Rhizoma Coptidis
                         lián*              

  Assistant   半夏       *bàn xià*   12g    Rhizoma Pinelliae
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Gua Lou* is sweet, cold and moist and acts to clear heat,
resolve phlegm, soothe the chest and disperse stagnation.

Minister: *Huang Lian* is bitter and cold and acts to clear heat, reduce
fire, relieve restlessness, and assists *Gua Lou* in clearing heat and
bearing turbidity down.

Assistant: *Ban Xia* is bitter, warm, dry and pungent and acts to
resolve phlegm, bear qi down, disperse stagnation, and assists *Gua Lou*
in resolving phlegm and soothing the chest.

In terms of compatibility, this formula has two characteristics. One is
combination of moist and dry ingredients that enhance the moist property
of *Gua Lou* and inhibit the dry property of *Ban Xia*. The other is
combination of pungent, dispersing and bitter ingredients that bear qi
down and resolve phlegm.

Both *Xiao Xian Xiong Tang* and *Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan* are indicated for
phlegm heat. The former has a better effect for chest/abdominal fullness
and pain due to phlegm heat accumulating in the epigastric area, whereas
the latter has a better effect for cough with yellow phlegm due to
phlegm heat and adverse ascending of qi.

Both *Xiao Xian Xiong Tang* and *Da Xian Xiong Tan*g (Major
Chest-Draining Decoction) are indicated for phlegm heat affecting the
chest. However, the former is more indicated for epigastric pain upon
pressure due to phlegm heat accumulating in the epigastric area, whereas
the latter is more indicated for water heat accumulating in the chest
and abdomen. Patients may present with fullness and tenderness in
between the epigastric area and lower abdomen. Apparently, the two
formulas have different ingredients to target different etiological
factors, disease locations and severities.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water

**\[Functions\]** Clears heat, resolves phlegm, soothes the chest and
disperses stagnation

**\[Indications\]** Phlegm-heat stagnation: Chest/epigastric fullness
and pain upon pressure, chest stuffiness/pain, cough with yellow, thick
phlegm, a red tongue with a yellow, greasy coating, and a slippery,
rapid pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for phlegm
heat accumulating in the chest. Phlegm heat affecting the free flow of
qi may cause chest stuffiness and pain upon pressure. Phlegm heat
accumulating in the lung may cause coughing with yellow phlegm. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat, resolve phlegm,
regulate qi and disperse stagnation.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Chest/epigastric fullness and
pain upon pressure, a yellow, greasy tongue coating and a slippery,
rapid pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For pain in the rib area, add *Yu Jin* (Radix Curcumae) and *Chai
    Hu* (Radix Bupleuri) to soothe the liver and relieve pain.

-   For difficult expectoration of thick phlegm, add *Dan Nan Xing*
    (Arisaema cum Bile) and *Chuan Bei Mu* (Bulbus Fritillariae
    Cirrhosae) to clear heat and resolve phlegm.

-   For chest tightness and shortness of breath due to phlegm heat
    affecting the lung, add *Ting Li Zi* (Semen Lepidii) and *Huang Qin*
    (Radix Scutellariae) to clear lung heat.

3\. Modern use: Acute/chronic gastritis, pleurisy, pleural adhesion,
acute bronchitis, intercostal neuralgia and angina pectoris that can be
diagnosed as phlegm heat affecting the chest.

## 4. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for phlegm dampness, cold phlegm and abdominal fullness due to spleen deficiency. As *Gua Lou* has a mild effect of promoting bowel movements, patients with loose stools due to deficiency cold of the spleen and stomach should use with caution.  {#cautions-contraindications-this-formula-is-contraindicated-for-phlegm-dampness-cold-phlegm-and-abdominal-fullness-due-to-spleen-deficiency.-as-gua-lou-has-a-mild-effect-of-promoting-bowel-movements-patients-with-loose-stools-due-to-deficiency-cold-of-the-spleen-and-stomach-should-use-with-caution. .unnumbered}

**\[Annex formula\]** *Chai Hu Xian Xiong Tang* (Bupleurum
Chest-Draining Decoction) from the *Chong Ding Tong Su Shang Han Lun*
(Revised Popular Guide to 'Treatise on Cold Damage')

\[Ingredients\] *Chai Hu* (Radix Bupleuri), *Jiang Ban Xia* (Rhizoma
Pinelliae Praeparatum), *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis), *Jie Geng*
(Radix Platycodonis), *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae), *Gou Lou Zi*
(Semen Trichosanthis), *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus) and *Sheng
Jiang Zhi* (fresh ginger juice)

\[Actions\] Harmonizes Shaoyang, clears heat, resolves phlegm and
soothes the chest

\[Indications\] Shaoyang syndrome and phlegm heat accumulating in the
chest: Alternating chills and fever, chest fullness and pain upon
pressure, nausea, a poor appetite, a bitter, sticky taste, blurred
vision, cough with thick phlegm, a red tongue with a yellow, greasy
coating, and a wiry, slippery and rapid pulse.

*Chai Hu Xian Xiong Tang* is modified from *Xiao Chai Hu Tang* (Minor
Bupleurum Decoction) and *Xiao Xian Xiong Tang* (Minor Chest-Draining
Decoction). It clears heat, resolves phlegm, regulates qi, soothes the
chest and harmonizes Shaoyang. It's indicated for Shaoyang syndrome
coupled with internal phlegm heat.

***Meng Shi Guan Tan Wan***

*Yi Ji Wei Yi* (Detailed Explanation of the Jade Pivot)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch      礞石       *méng shí*  30g    Chlorite-schist
  ------------ ---------- ----------- ------ ------------------------------
  Minister     大黄       *dà huáng*  240g   Radix et Rhizoma Rhei

  Assistants   黄芩       *huáng qín* 240g   Radix Scutellariae

               沉香       *chén       15g    Lignum Aquilariae Resinatum
                          xiāng*             
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Meng Shi* is dry and weighty and acts to expel intractable
phlegm out of the body, especially when it is calcined with sodium
sulphate. In addition, it soothes the liver and stops cough.

Minister: *Da Huang* is bitter and cold and acts to clear heat and
promote bowel movements.

Assistants: *Huang Qin* is bitter and cold and acts to clear heat out of
the upper *jiao*. *Chen Xiang* bears qi down and alleviates panting.

This formula bears qi down, promotes bowel movements and resolves
phlegm.

**\[Method of administration\]** 8-10g water pills for each dose, 2
doses a day.

**\[Functions\]** Reduces fire and resolves phlegm

**\[Indications\]** Phlegm due to excessive heat: Mania, depression,
palpitations, anxiety, mental unconsciousness, coughing with thick
phlegm, chest stuffiness, dizziness, tinnitus, nodules in the neck,
twitching of the eyes and mouth, insomnia, weird dreams, joint pain,
restlessness, frequent sighing, constipation, a yellow, greasy tongue
coating, and a slippery, rapid and forceful pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for
excessive heat and phlegm retention. Phlegm heat disturbing the mind may
cause mania, depression, mental unconsciousness, palpitations and weird
dreams. Phlegm heat affecting the lung may cause cough with thick
phlegm, restlessness and frequent sighing. Phlegm heat affecting the
meridians and joints may cause twitching of the eyes and mouth and joint
pain. As a result, the treatment strategies are to clear heat and expel
phlegm out of the body.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Mania, depression, palpitations,
dry stools, a thick, yellow tongue coating and a slippery, rapid and
forceful pulse.

2\. Modern use: Schizophrenia, epilepsy and neurosis that can be
diagnosed as internal excessive heat and phlegm fire.

## 3. Cautions & contraindications: Considering the harsh properties of some ingredients in this formula, pregnant women and patients with spleen qi deficiency, yang deficiency of the spleen and kidney should use with caution; patients with a strong constitution should stop taking the formula as soon as the symptoms are alleviated.  {#cautions-contraindications-considering-the-harsh-properties-of-some-ingredients-in-this-formula-pregnant-women-and-patients-with-spleen-qi-deficiency-yang-deficiency-of-the-spleen-and-kidney-should-use-with-caution-patients-with-a-strong-constitution-should-stop-taking-the-formula-as-soon-as-the-symptoms-are-alleviated. .unnumbered}

**16.3 Formulas that moisten the lung and resolve phlegm**

## Formulas that moisten dryness and resolve phlegm are indicated for dry phlegm. Patients may present with difficult expectoration of scanty, thick phlegm, a dry throat, choking, a hoarse voice, and a dry, white coating. Dryness-moistening and phlegm-resolving herbs like *Bei Mu* (Bulbus Fritillaria) and *Gua Lou* (Fructus Trichosanthis) are often used in combination with herbs that nourish yin, clear heat and regulate qi. A typical formula is *Bei Mu Gua Lou San* (Fritillaria and Trichosanthis Fruit Powder).  {#formulas-that-moisten-dryness-and-resolve-phlegm-are-indicated-for-dry-phlegm.-patients-may-present-with-difficult-expectoration-of-scanty-thick-phlegm-a-dry-throat-choking-a-hoarse-voice-and-a-dry-white-coating.-dryness-moistening-and-phlegm-resolving-herbs-like-bei-mu-bulbus-fritillaria-and-gua-lou-fructus-trichosanthis-are-often-used-in-combination-with-herbs-that-nourish-yin-clear-heat-and-regulate-qi.-a-typical-formula-is-bei-mu-gua-lou-san-fritillaria-and-trichosanthis-fruit-powder. .unnumbered}

***Bei Mu Gua Lou San***

*Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical Revelations)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch      贝母       *bèi mŭ*    5g     Bulbus Fritillaria
  ------------ ---------- ----------- ------ ------------------------------
  Minister     瓜蒌       *guā lóu*   3g     Fructus Trichosanthis

  Assistants   天花粉     *tiān huā   2.5g   Radix Trichosanthis
                          fĕn*               

               橘红       *jú hóng*   2.5g   Exocarpium Citri Rubrum

               桔梗       *jié gĕng*  2.5g   Radix Platycodonis
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Bei Mu* moistens the lung, clears heat, resolves phlegm, and
stops coughing.

Minister: *Gua Lou* moistens the lung, clears heat, regulates qi, and
resolves phlegm.

Assistants: *Tian Hua Fen* moistens dryness, clears heat and resolves
phlegm. *Fu Ling* drains dampness and strengthens the spleen. *Ju Hong*
regulates qi and resolves phlegm. *Jie Geng* bears down lung qi, stops
coughing and resolves phlegm.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Moistens the lung, clears heat, regulates qi and
resolves phlegm

**\[Indications\]** Cough due to dryness and phlegm: Difficult
expectoration of thick phlegm, a dry throat, and a dry, white tongue
coating.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for cough
due to dryness and phlegm. The lung is a delicate organ. Dry heat may
scorch fluids and produce phlegm. The phlegm may, in turn, affect the
dispersing and descending of lung qi and thus result in difficult
expectoration of thick phlegm. As a result, the treatment strategies are
to moisten the lung, clear heat, regulate qi and resolve phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Difficult expectoration of
phlegm, a dry throat and a dry, white tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For cough with a hoarse voice due to lung yin damage, add *Sha Shen*
    (Radix Adenophorae seu Glehniae) and *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis)
    to nourish yin and moisten the lung.

-   For cough with hemoptysis, add *Xian He Cao* (Herba Agrimoniae) and
    *Qian Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Rubiae) to cool blood and stop
    bleeding.

-   For a dry, sore throat due to ascending of fire, add *Ma Bo*
    (Lasiosphaera seu Calvatia) and *Shan Dou Gen* (Radix et Rhizoma
    Sophorae Tonkinensis) to clear heat and relieve sore throat.

-   For ascending of lung qi and lung fire, add *Shi Gao* (Gypsum
    Fibrosum) and *Zhi Mu* (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae) to clear heat and
    generate fluids.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis, pneumonia and pulmonary TB that can
be diagnosed as dry heat affecting the lung.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
coughs due to dampness, deficient fire, and yin deficiency of the lung
and kidney (unproductive cough, hemoptysis, hot flushes, and night
sweats).

**16.4 Formulas that warm the lung cold and transform phlegm**

## Formulas that dispel cold and transform phlegm are indicated for cold phlegm. Patients may present with cough with white, thin and clear phlegm and a white, slippery tongue coating. Lung-warming and phlegm-resolving herbs like *Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari), *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma Zingiberis), *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae) and *Bai Jie Zi* (Semen Sinapis) are often used in combination with interior-warming and cold-dispelling herbs. Typical formulas are *Ling Gan Wu Wei Jiang Xin Tang* (Poria, Licorice, Schisandra, Ginger and Asarum Decoction) and *San Zi Yang Qin Tang* (Three-Seed Filial Devotion Decoction).  {#formulas-that-dispel-cold-and-transform-phlegm-are-indicated-for-cold-phlegm.-patients-may-present-with-cough-with-white-thin-and-clear-phlegm-and-a-white-slippery-tongue-coating.-lung-warming-and-phlegm-resolving-herbs-like-xi-xin-radix-et-rhizoma-asari-gan-jiang-rhizoma-zingiberis-ban-xia-rhizoma-pinelliae-and-bai-jie-zi-semen-sinapis-are-often-used-in-combination-with-interior-warming-and-cold-dispelling-herbs.-typical-formulas-are-ling-gan-wu-wei-jiang-xin-tang-poria-licorice-schisandra-ginger-and-asarum-decoction-and-san-zi-yang-qin-tang-three-seed-filial-devotion-decoction. .unnumbered}

***Ling Gan Wu Wei Jiang Xin Tang***

*Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical Revelations)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarch     干姜          *gān     9g     Rhizoma Zingiberis
                            jiāng*          
  ----------- ------------- -------- ------ ------------------------------
  Ministers   细辛          *xì xīn* 5g     Radix et Rhizoma Asari

              茯苓          *fú      12g    Poria
                            líng*           

  Assistant   五味子        *wŭ wèi  5g     Fructus Schisandrae Chinensis
                            zĭ*             

  Envoy       甘草          *gān     9g     Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
                            căo*            
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Gan Jiang* warms the lung, dispels old, resolves fluid
retention, and at the same time, warms spleen yang and transforms
dampness.

Ministers: *Xi Xin* warms the lung, dispels cold and assists *Gan Jiang*
in resolving fluid retention. *Fu Ling* strengthens the spleen, drains
dampness and works in combination with *Gan Jiang* to resolve fluid
retention and stop the source of phlegm generation.

Assistant: *Wu Wei Zi* astringes the lung and stops coughing. When used
in combination with *Gan Jiang* and *Xi Xin*, the formula disperses
without damaging anti-pathogenic qi, astringes without causing retention
of pathogenic factors, and harmonizes the lung.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* harmonizes the spleen and stomach, assists *Fu Ling* in
supplementing spleen qi, assists Gan Jiang in warming spleen yang and
coordinates the other ingredients.

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms the lung and transforms fluid retention

**\[Indications\]** Coughs due to cold phlegm: Cough with profuse,
white, thin and clear phlegm, chest stuffiness, a white, slippery tongue
coating, and a wiry, slippery pulse

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for cough
due to cold phlegm. Spleen yang deficiency may produce dampness. Lung qi
deficiency may cause abnormal distribution of body fluids, and
subsequently, fluid retention. Cold fluid affects the dispersing and
descending of lung qi, causing cough with profuse, thin and clear
phlegm. Fluid retention affecting the flow of qi causes chest
stuffiness. As a result, the treatment strategies are to warm the lung
and resolves fluid retention.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough with profuse, white, thin
and clear phlegm, a white, slippery tongue coating, and a wiry, slippery
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For profuse phlegm coupled with nausea, add *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma
    Pinelliae) and *Hou Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) to resolve
    phlegm, bear qi down and stop vomiting.

-   For adverse ascending of stomach qi, add *Gui Zhi* (Ramulus
    Cinnamomi) to warm the middle *jiao* and bear qi down.

-   For cough with rapid breathing, add *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae
    Amarum) and *Su Zi* (Fructus Perillae) to bear qi down, resolve
    phlegm and stop coughing.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis and emphysema that can be diagnosed
as cold phlegm affecting the lung.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
cough with hemoptysis due to phlegm heat damaging yin.

**\[Annex formula\]** *Leng Xiao Wan* (Cold Asthma Pill) from the *Zhang
Shi Yi Tong* (Comprehensive Medicine According to Master Zhang)

\[Ingredients\] *Ma Huang* (Herba Ephedrae), *Chuan Wu* (Radix Aconiti),
*Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari), *Shu* *Jiao* (Pericarpium
Zanthoxyli), *Bai Fan* (Alumen), *Zao* *Jiao* (Spina Gleditsiae), *Ban
Xia Qu* (Rhizoma Pinelliae Fermentata), *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum
Bile), *Xing Ren* (Semen Armeniacae Amarum), *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma
Glycyrrhizae), *Zi Wan Rong* (Radix et Rhizoma Asteris), and *Kuan Dong
Hua* (Flos Farfarae)

\[Actions\] Warms the lung, dispels cold, cleanses phlegm and transforms
fluid retention

\[Indications\] Asthma due to cold phlegm: Cold-induced panting,
coughing, chest stuffiness and inability to lie flat

\[Caution\] Considering the harsh property of ingredients in this
formula, it's not recommended for patients with a weakened immune
system.

***San Zi Yang Qin Tang***

*Za Bing Guang Yao* (General Principles on Miscellaneous Diseases)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------
  白芥子           *bái jiè   6g      Semen Sinapis
                   zĭ*                
  ---------------- ---------- ------- ------------------------------------
  苏子             *sū zĭ*    9g      Fructus Perillae

  莱菔子           *lái fú    9g      Semen Raphani
                   zĭ*                
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

*Bai Jie Zi* warms the lung, bears qi down, resolves phlegm and relieves
stagnation. *Su Zi* bears qi down, resolves phlegm and stops coughing.
*Lai Fu Zi* promotes digestion, moves qi and resolves phlegm. The
hierarchy (monarch, minister and assistant) of the three ingredients
depends on the patients' conditions. Consisting of three warm, pungent
herbs that move qi and resolve phlegm, this formula is directed at
symptoms. However, herbs that supplement qi should be used in
combination when the symptoms are well controlled.

**\[Method of administration\]** Grind into a fine powder, wrap up in
cotton gauze and decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Warms the lung, resolves phlegm, bears qi down and
promotes digestion

**\[Indications\]** Indigestion due to phlegm qi stagnation: Cough with
profuse phlegm, chest stuffiness, a poor appetite, abdominal discomfort,
a white, greasy tongue coating, and a slippery pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for
deficiency of the lung and spleen coupled with phlegm dampness in the
elderly. Spleen failing to transport and transform may produce phlegm
dampness. The phlegm dampness may, in turn, affect the dispersing and
descending of lung qi and result in cough with profuse phlegm, chest
stuffiness, a poor appetite and abdominal discomfort. A white, greasy
tongue coating and a slippery pulse are signs indicative of cold phlegm.
As a result, the treatment strategies are to bear qi down, promote
digestion, and resolve phlegm.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Cough with profuse phlegm, chest
stuffiness, a white, greasy tongue coating, and a slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   If patients mainly present with food stagnation/abdominal fullness,
    increase the dose of *Lai Fu Zi* (Semen Raphani) and add *Shan Zha*
    (Fructus Crataegi), *Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus) and *Shen
    Qu* (Massa Medicata Fermentata) to promote digestion and relieve
    abdominal fullness.

-   If patients mainly present with qi stagnation or adverse ascending
    of qi, increase the dose of *Su Zi* (Fructus Perillae) and add *Hou
    Pu* (Cortex Magnoliae Officinalis) and *Chen Xiang* (Lignum
    Aquilariae Resinatum) to circulate qi and bear qi down.

-   If patients mainly present with cold phlegm, increase the dose of
    *Bai Jie Zi* (Semen Sinapis) and add *Gan Jiang* (Rhizoma
    Zingiberis), *Xi Xin* (Radix et Rhizoma Asari) and *Ban Xia*
    (Rhizoma Pinelliae) to warm the lung, dispel cold and transform
    phlegm.

3\. Modern use: Chronic bronchitis, bronchial asthma and emphysema that
can be diagnosed as phlegm, adverse ascending of qi and food stagnation.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: Patients with a weak spleen should not
take this formula for a long period of time.

**16.5 Formulas that stop wind and resolve phlegm**

## Formulas that stop wind and resolve phlegm are indicated for wind phlegm. Wind phlegm may cause persistent coughs with sticky phlegm, and a scratchy throat. Wind-stopping and exterior-releasing herbs like *Jing Jie* (Herba Schizonepetae) and *Fang Feng* (Radix Saposhnikoviae) are often used in combination with phlegm-resolving herbs like *Zi Wan* (Radix et Rhizoma Asteris) and *Kuan Dong Hua* (Flos Farfarae). A typical formula is *Zhi Sou San* (Cough-Stopping Powder). Internal wind intertwined with phlegm may cause dizziness, headache, epilepsy or unconsciousness. Liver-soothing and wind-stopping ingredients like *Tian Ma* (Rhizoma Gastrodiae), *Quan Xie* (Scorpio) and *Jiang Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus) are often used in combination with phlegm-resolving herbs like *Ban Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae), *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) and *Zhu Li* (Succus Bambusae). In addition, herbs that strengthen the spleen, open the orifice and calm the mind are also supplemented. Typical formulas include *Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang* (Pinellia, Atractylodes Macrocephala and Gastrodia Decoction) and *Ding Xian Wan* (Seizure-Settling Pill).  {#formulas-that-stop-wind-and-resolve-phlegm-are-indicated-for-wind-phlegm.-wind-phlegm-may-cause-persistent-coughs-with-sticky-phlegm-and-a-scratchy-throat.-wind-stopping-and-exterior-releasing-herbs-like-jing-jie-herba-schizonepetae-and-fang-feng-radix-saposhnikoviae-are-often-used-in-combination-with-phlegm-resolving-herbs-like-zi-wan-radix-et-rhizoma-asteris-and-kuan-dong-hua-flos-farfarae.-a-typical-formula-is-zhi-sou-san-cough-stopping-powder.-internal-wind-intertwined-with-phlegm-may-cause-dizziness-headache-epilepsy-or-unconsciousness.-liver-soothing-and-wind-stopping-ingredients-like-tian-ma-rhizoma-gastrodiae-quan-xie-scorpio-and-jiang-can-bombyx-batryticatus-are-often-used-in-combination-with-phlegm-resolving-herbs-like-ban-xia-rhizoma-pinelliae-dan-nan-xing-arisaema-cum-bile-and-zhu-li-succus-bambusae.-in-addition-herbs-that-strengthen-the-spleen-open-the-orifice-and-calm-the-mind-are-also-supplemented.-typical-formulas-include-ban-xia-bai-zhu-tian-ma-tang-pinellia-atractylodes-macrocephala-and-gastrodia-decoction-and-ding-xian-wan-seizure-settling-pill. .unnumbered}

***Zhi Sou San***

*Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical Revelations)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarchs     紫菀          *zĭ wăn* 1kg    Radix et Rhizoma Asteris
  ------------ ------------- -------- ------ ------------------------------
               百部          *băi bù* 1kg    Radix Stemonae

  Ministers    桔梗          *jié     1kg    Radix Platycodonis
                             gĕng*           

               白前          *bái     1kg    Rhizoma et Radix Cynanchi
                             qián*           Stauntonii

  Assistants   陈皮          *chén    500g   Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae
                             pí*             

               荆芥          *jīng    1kg    Herba Schizonepetae
                             jiè*            

  Envoy        甘草          *gān     375g   Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
                             căo*            
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Zi Wan* and *Bai Bu* are bitter, warm and moist. They act to
bear qi down, resolve phlegm, benefit the lung and stop coughing.

Ministers: *Jie Geng* disperses lung qi and resolves phlegm. *Bai Qian*
bears qi down, resolves phlegm and stops coughing.

Assistants: *Chen Pi* regulates qi and resolves phlegm. *Jing Jie*
expels wind, releases the exterior and benefits the throat.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients. It can work in
combination with *Jie Geng* to benefit the throat and stop coughing.

With only 7 ingredients, this formula is warm but not dry and moist but
not greasy. It dispels cold without causing heat and releases the
exterior without damaging anti-pathogenic qi.

**\[Method of administration\]** 9g of powder form for each dose with
warm water or ginger soup; or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Expels wind, resolves phlegm, disperses lung qi and
stops coughs.

**\[Indications\]** Coughs due to wind phlegm: A scratchy throat,
difficult expectoration of phlegm, mild wind intolerance, fever, a thin,
white tongue coating, and a superficial, delayed pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for cough
due to wind phlegm. Herbs with a tendency to disperse are often used to
expel wind; however, cough may linger due to the failure of lung qi to
descend, coupled with difficult expectoration of phlegm and a scratchy
throat. Incomplete removal of pathogenic wind may cause mild wind
intolerance and fever. As a result, the treatment strategies are to
expel wind, resolve phlegm and disperse lung qi.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: A scratchy throat, persistent
coughing, difficult expectoration of phlegm, mild wind intolerance,
fever, and a thin tongue coating.

2\. Modifications

-   For cough with profuse phlegm, add *Bei Mu* (Bulbus Fritillaria) and
    *Gua Lou* (Fructus Trichosanthis) to moisten the lung and resolve
    phlegm.

-   For cough with yellow phlegm, add *Sang Bai Pi* (Cortex Mori) and
    *Dan Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) to clear heat and resolve phlegm.

-   For a dry throat and thirst, add *Sha Shen* (Radix Adenophorae seu
    Glehniae) and *Mai Dong* (Radix Ophiopogonis) to nourish yin and
    moisten the lung.

3\. Modern use: Upper respiratory tract infection, bronchitis, pneumonia
and flu that can be diagnosed as wind attacking the lung.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
exterior syndrome. Patients with chronic coughing due to yin deficiency
should use with caution.

***Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang***

*Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical Revelations)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarchs     半夏          *bàn     9g     Rhizoma Pinelliae
                             xià*            
  ------------ ------------- -------- ------ ------------------------------
               天麻          *tiān    6g     Rhizoma Gastrodiae
                             má*             

  Ministers    白术          *bái     15g    Rhizoma Atractylodis
                             zhú*            Macrocephalae

  Assistants   茯苓          *fú      6g     Poria
                             líng*           

               陈皮          *chén    6g     Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae
                             pí*             

  Envoy        甘草          *gān     3g     Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae
                             căo*            
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Ban Xia* dries dampness, resolves phlegm, bears qi down and
harmonizes the stomach. *Tian M*a soothes liver wind, nourishes yin and
alleviates vertigo.

Minister: *Bai Zhu* strengthens the spleen and dries dampness.

Assistants: *Fu Ling* strengthens the spleen, drains dampness and
assists the efficacy of *Bai Zhu*. *Chen Pi* regulates qi and resolves
phlegm. It works in combination with *Ban Xia* to bear qi down,
harmonize the stomach and resolve turbid phlegm.

Envoy: *Sheng Jiang* and *Da Zao* regulate the spleen and stomach. *Gan
Cao* harmonizes the spleen and stomach and coordinates the other
ingredients.

This formula treats both symptoms (due to wind phlegm) and the root
cause (spleen deficiency).

**\[Method of administration\]** Decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Resolves phlegm, stops wind, dries dampness and
strengthens the spleen

**\[Indications\]** Vertigo and headache due to wind phlegm, chest
stuffiness, nausea and vomiting.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for
vertigo due to wind phlegm. Spleen failing to transport and transform
may produce phlegm dampness. This, coupled with internal stirring of
liver wind may cause vertigo and headache. Phlegm obstructing the qi
activity in the chest may cause chest stuffiness, nausea and vomiting.
As a result, the treatment strategies are to resolve phlegm, stop wind,
dry dampness and strengthen the spleen.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Vertigo, headache, chest
stuffiness, nausea, vomiting, a white, greasy tongue coating, and a
wiry, slippery pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe vertigo, add *Jiang Can* (Bombyx Batryticatus) and *Dan
    Nan Xing* (Arisaema cum Bile) to stop wind and resolve phlegm.

-   For severe headache, add *Man Jing Zi* (Fructus Viticis), *Chuan
    Xiong* (Rhizoma Chuanxiong) and *Ju Hua* (Flos Chrysanthemi) to
    expel wind.

-   For hyperactivity of liver yang, add *Gou Teng* (Ramulus Uncariae
    Cum Uncis) and *Shi Jue Ming* (Concha Haliotidis) to submerge liver
    yang and expel wind.

3\. Modern use: Aural vertigo, neurogenic vertigo and hypertension that
can be diagnosed as wind phlegm disturbing the mind.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
patients with dizziness due to yin deficiency-induced yang
hyperactivity, deficiency of the liver and kidney, and deficiency of qi
and blood.

***Ding Xian Wan***

*Yi Xue Xin Wu* (Medical Revelations)

**\[Ingredients\]**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Monarchs     竹沥          *zhú lì*   100ml    Succus Bambusae
  ------------ ------------- ---------- -------- ---------------------------
               胆南星        *dăn nán   15g      Arisaema cum Bile
                             xīng*               

  Ministers    半夏          *bàn xià*  30g      Rhizoma Pinelliae

               茯苓          *fú líng*  30g      Poria

               陈皮          *chén pí*  4.5g     Pericarpium Citri
                                                 Reticulatae

               贝母          *bèi mŭ*   30g      Bulbus Fritillaria

               姜汁          *jiāng     50ml     Ginger juice
                             zhī*                

               天麻          *tiān má*  30g      Rhizoma Gastrodiae

               僵蚕          *jiāng     15g      Bombyx Batryticatus
                             cán*                

               全蝎          *quán xiē* 15g      Scorpio

  Assistants   石菖蒲        *shí chāng 15g      Rhizoma Acori Tatarinowii
                             pú*                 

               远志          *yuăn zhì* 4.5g     Radix Polygalae

               麦冬          *mài dōng* 60g      Radix Ophiopogonis

               丹参          *dān shēn* 60g      Radix et Rhizoma Salviae
                                                 Miltiorrhizae

               朱砂          *zhū shā*  9g       Cinnabaris

               茯神          *fú shén*  30g      Sclerotium Poriae
                                                 Pararadicis

               琥珀          *hŭ pò*    15g      Succinum

  Envoy        甘草          *gān căo*  120g     Radix et Rhizoma
                                                 Glycyrrhizae
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Zhu Li* clears heat, resolves phlegm, and calms the mind.
*Dan Nan Xing* clears heat, resolves phlegm, stops wind and alleviates
epilepsy.

Ministers: *Ban Xia* dries dampness and resolves phlegm. *Fu Ling*
drains dampness and resolves phlegm. *Chen Pi* regulates qi and resolves
phlegm. *Bei Mu* moistens dryness and resolves phlegm. *Jiang Zhi*
harmonizes the stomach and resolves phlegm. *Tian Ma*, *Jiang Can* and
*Quan Xie* soothe liver wind, unblock meridians and stop convulsions.

Assistants: *Shi Chang Pu* and *Yuan Zhi* open the orifices, resolve
phlegm and refresh the mind. *Mai Dong* and *Dan Shen* nourish yin,
clear heat, and circulate blood. *Zhu Sha*, *Fu Shen* and *Hu Po* calm
the heart mind and relieve epilepsy.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* coordinates the other ingredients.

This formula clears heat, resolves phlegm, soothes liver wind, refreshes
the mind and opens the orifices. It is a typical formula for epilepsy
(seizures) due to ascending of wind fire that carries phlegm.

**\[Method of administration\]** 6g of pills for each dose, 2 doses a
day.

**\[Functions\]** Cleanses phlegm, stops wind, opens the orifices and
refreshes the mind.

**\[Indications\]** Epilepsy due to wind phlegm coupled with heat:
Sudden epileptic seizures, collapse, mental unconsciousness, deviation
of the eyes and mouth, convulsions, drooling, making noises, a white,
greasy or slightly yellow tongue coating, and a wiry, slippery pulse. It
can also be used for mania or depression.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a commonly used formula for
epilepsy due to wind, phlegm and heat disturbing the mind. Emotional
disturbance, fear, fright or anger may cause liver wind (that carries
phlegm) to ascend to disturb the mind, leading to sudden collapse,
mental unconsciousness, drooling, and making noises. Internal stirring
of liver wind may cause deviation of the eyes and mouth and convulsions
of the four limbs. A slightly yellow, greasy tongue coating and a wiry,
slippery pulse are signs indicative of wind, phlegm and heat. As a
result, the treatment strategies are to cleanse phlegm, stop wind, open
the orifices and refresh the mind.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Sudden epileptic seizures, a
white, greasy or slightly yellow tongue coating, and a slippery, rapid
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For constipation, add *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) and *Mang
    Xiao* (Natrii Sulfas) to clear heat and promote bowel movements.

-   For frequent convulsions, add *Ling Yang* *Jiao* (Cornu Saigae
    Tataricae) and *Gou Teng* (Ramulus Uncariae Cum Uncis) to clear heat
    and stop wind.

-   After recovery, use *He Che Wan* (Placenta Pills) to reinforce
    yuan-primordial qi, nourish the heart mind and prevent further
    seizures.

3\. Modern use: Primary/secondary epilepsy, multi-infarct dementia,
severe functional disturbances of the autonomic nerve, schizophrenia and
cerebral cysticercosis that can be diagnosed as wind, phlegm and heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
epilepsy patients with spleen qi deficiency and yang hyperactivity due
to yin deficiency.

**Summary**

There are a total of 12 formulas and 5 annex formulas in this chapter.
In terms of functions, these formulas are subdivided into five
types---formulas that dry dampness and resolve phlegm, formulas that
clear heat and resolve phlegm, formulas that moisten the lung and
resolve phlegm, formulas that warm the lung and transform phlegm, and
formulas that stop wind and resolve phlegm.

**Formulas that dry dampness and resolve phlegm**

*Er Chen Tang* is a typical formula that dries dampness, resolves
phlegm, regulates qi, and harmonizes the spleen and stomach. *Wen Dan
Tang* regulates qi, resolves phlegm, clears the gallbladder and
harmonizes the stomach. It is indicated for disharmony between the
gallbladder and stomach, internal phlegm-heat, palpitation, depression,
and epilepsy. *Fu Ling Wan* dries dampness, moves qi, softens hardness
and resolves phlegm. It is indicated for phlegm retention in the spleen
and stomach, arm pain or edema in the limbs.

**Formulas that clear heat and resolve phlegm**

*Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan* clears heat, resolves phlegm, regulates qi, and
stops coughing. It is indicated for cough with yellow, thick phlegm due
to internal phlegm-heat. *Xiao Xian Xiong Tang* clears heat, resolves
phlegm, soothes the chest and disperses stagnation. It is indicated for
epigastric stuffiness due to phlegm heat retention. *Gun Tan Wan* drains
fire and resolves phlegm. It is indicated for palpitations, mania,
depression or mental unconsciousness due to excessive phlegm heat.

**Formulas that moisten the lung and resolve phlegm**

*Bei Mu Kuo Lou San* moistens the lung, clears heat, regulates qi, and
resolves phlegm. It is indicated for cough with difficult expectoration
of thick phlegm and dry throat due to retention of dry phlegm.

**Formulas that warm the lung and transform phlegm**

*Ling Gan Wu Wei Jiang Xin Tang* warms the lung and resolves fluid
retention. It is indicated for cough with profuse, thin, and clear
phlegm due to internal retention of cold fluid. *San Zi Yang Qin Tang*
warms the lung, resolves phlegm, bears qi down and promotes digestion.
It is indicated for cough, panting, a poor appetite and indigestion due
to phlegm qi stagnation.

**Formulas that stop wind and resolve phlegm**

*Zhi Sou San* stops wind, resolves phlegm, disperses lung qi, and stops
coughing. It is indicated or cough due to wind that carries phlegm. *Ban
Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang* resolves phlegm, expels wind, dries dampness
and strengthens the spleen. It is indicated for dizziness and headache
due to ascending of wind phlegm. *Ding Xian Wan* drains phlegm, reduces
fire, opens the orifices and refreshes the mind. It is indicated for
epilepsy due to wind phlegm that transforms into heat.

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1\. How do you understand the role of spleen-fortifying and           |
| qi-regulating herbs in formulas that resolve phlegm?                  |
|                                                                       |
| 2\. Explain the indications, compositions, and modifications of *Er   |
| Chen Tang*.                                                           |
|                                                                       |
| 3\. Explain the indications and compatibility of *Wen Dan Tang*.      |
|                                                                       |

| Wan*.                                                                 |
|                                                                       |

| Tang* and *Ling Gan Wu Wei Jiang Xin Tang* in compositions, actions   |

+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Chapter 17 Formulas that Promote Digestion**

As the name suggests, formulas that promote digestion are indicated for
food stagnation from different causes. They fall into the category of
reducing method (*Xiao Fa*).

Contributing factors of food stagnation include improper diet, binge
eating or spleen deficiency. According to its etiology, pathogenesis and
function characteristics, formulas in this chapter are subdivided into
two types---formulas that promote digestion to resolve stagnation and
formulas that strengthen the spleen to promote digestion.

Food stagnation may affect the ascending of spleen qi and descending of
spleen qi. The treatment strategies are to promote digestion, circulate
qi and strengthen the spleen. For food stagnation due to spleen
deficiency, the treatment strategies are to strengthen the spleen and
promote digestion. In addition, food stagnation may occur in combination
with cold or transform into heat, it's necessary to combine ingredients
to warm the interior or clear heat. Despite their moderate efficacy,
formulas that promote digestion shouldn't be taken for a long period of
time because of the harsh property in some ingredients, especially for
patients with a deficiency syndrome.

**17.1 Formulas that promote digestion to resolve food stagnation**

Formulas that promote digestion to resolve food stagnation are indicated
for chest/gastric fullness/discomfort, belching, acid reflux, nausea,
vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, a greasy tongue coating, and a
slippery pulse. Digestion-promoting herbs include *Shan Zha* (Fructus
Crataegi), *Shen Qu* (Massa Medicata Fermentata), *Mai Ya* (Fructus
Hordei Germinatus) and *Lai Fu Zi* (Semen Raphani). As food stagnation
may obstruct the flow of qi and result in stuffiness, distension and
fullness, qi-circulating herbs are often used in combination, such as
*Zhi Shi* (Fructus Aurantii Immaturus), *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri
Reticulatae), *Mu Xiang* (Radix Aucklandiae) and *Bing Lang* (Semen
Arecae). Typical formulas include *Bao He Wan* (Harmony-Preserving
Pill), *Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan* (Immature Bitter Orange Stagnation-Moving
Pill) and *Mu Xiang Bing Lang Wa*n (Aucklandia and Areca Seed Pill).

***Bao He Wan* (Harmony-Preserving Pill)**

*Dan Xi Xin Fa* (Teachings of \[Zhu\] Dan-xi)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarch  | > 山楂  | *shān zhā*  | 180g | Fructus Crataegi           |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
| Minis    | > 神曲  | *shén qū*   | 60g  | Massa Medicata Fermentata  |
| ter**s** |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | >       | *lái fú zĭ* | 30g  | Semen Raphani              |
|          |  莱菔子 |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | > 半夏  | *bàn xià*   | 90g  | Rhizoma Pinelliae          |
| sistants |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 陈皮  | *chén pí*   | 30g  | Pericarpium Citri          |
|          |         |             |      | Reticulatae                |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 茯苓  | *fú líng*   | 90g  | Poria                      |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 连翘  | *lián qiào* | 30g  | Fructus Forsythiae         |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Shan Zha* promotes food stagnation from all causes, especially
for stagnation of greasy food.

Minister: *Shen Qu* strengthens the spleen and promotes digestion
(especially of booze and binge eating). *Lai Fu Zi* bears qi down and
promotes digestion (especially of cereals or flour-based food).

Assistants: *Ban Xia* harmonizes the stomach, bears qi down and stops
vomiting. *Chen Pi* regulates qi and relieves abdominal distension. *Fu
Ling* strengthens the spleen, drains dampness and stops diarrhea. *Lian
Qiao* clears heat and resolves stagnation.

This formula promotes digestion, circulates qi, resolves dampness and
clears heat transformed from food stagnation.

**\[Method of administration\]** 6-9g water pills for each dose, 2 doses
a day (a smaller dose for children); or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Promotes digestion and harmonizes the stomach.

**\[Indications\]** Food stagnation: abdominal fullness, distension and
pain, belching, acid reflux, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, a thick, greasy
and slightly yellow tongue coating, and a slippery pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for food
stagnation. Overeat, binge eating, improper diet and over ingestion of
liquor, meat or oily food may all contribute to food stagnation. Food
stagnation may obstruct the flow of qi, causing abdominal fullness,
distension and pain. Food stagnation affecting the ascending of spleen
qi and descending of stomach qi may cause belching, acid reflux, nausea
and vomiting. As a result, the treatment strategies are to promote
digestion, relieve food stagnation, regulate qi and harmonize the
stomach.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal fullness and
distension, belching, nausea, a greasy tongue coating and a slippery
pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe abdominal fullness and distension, add *Zhi Shi* (Fructus
    Aurantii Immaturus), *E Zhu* (Rhizoma Curcumae) and *Bing Lang*
    (Semen Arecae) to promote digestion and relieve food stagnation.

-   For a yellow tongue coating and a rapid pulse due to food stagnation
    transforming into heat, add *Huang Qin* (Radix Scutellariae) and
    *Huang Lian* (Rhizoma Coptidis) to clear heat.

-   For constipation, add *Da Huang* (Radix et Rhizoma Rhei) to promote
    bowel movements.

3\. Modern use: Dyspepsia and acute/chronic gastroenteritis that can be
diagnosed as food stagnation.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula shouldn't be taken for a
long period of time. It's necessary to modify for patients with
anti-pathogenic qi deficiency or with deficiency cold.

**\[Annex Formula\]** *Da An Wan* (Great Tranquility Pill) from the *Yi
Fang Ji Jie* (Medical Formulas Collected and Analyzed)

\[Ingredients\] *Shan Zha* (Fructus Crataegi), *Shen Qu* (Massa Medicata
Fermentata), *Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Lai Fu Zi*
(Semen Raphani), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae), *Ban
Xia* (Rhizoma Pinelliae), and *Fu Ling* (Poria)

\[Actions\] Promotes digestion and strengthens the spleen

\[Indications\] Food stagnation coupled with spleen deficiency. It can
also be used for pediatric food stagnation.

***Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan* (Immature Bitter Orange Stagnation-Moving
Pill)**

*Nei Wai Shang Bian Huo Lun* (Clarifying Doubts about Damage from
Internal and External Causes)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarch  | > 大黄  | *dà huáng*  | 30g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei      |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
| Minister | > 枳实  | *zhĭ shí*   | 15g  | Fructus Aurantii Immaturus |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| As       | > 黄芩  | *huáng qín* | 9g   | Radix Scutellariae         |
| sistants |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 黄连  | *huáng      | 9g   | Rhizoma Coptidis           |
|          |         | lián*       |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 茯苓  | *fú líng*   | 9g   | Poria                      |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 泽泻  | *zé xiè*    | 6g   | Rhizoma Alismatis          |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 神曲  | *shén qū*   | 15g  | Massa Medicata Fermentata  |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 白术  | *bái zhú*   | 9g   | Rhizoma Atractylodis       |
|          |         |             |      | Macrocephalae              |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Da Huang* is bitter in taste and cold in property; it acts to
drain downward and clear heat.

Minister: *Zhi Shi* circulates qi, resolves stagnation, relieves
abdominal fullness, distension and pain, and assists *Da Huang* in
draining downward.

Assistants: *Huang Qin* and *Huang Lian* clear heat, dry dampness and
stop diarrhea. *Fu Ling* and *Ze Xie* drain dampness, stop diarrhea and
assist *Da Huang* in resolving dampness and clearing heat. *Shen Qu*
promotes digestion, harmonizes the stomach and assists *Da Huang* and
*Zhi Shi* in relieving food stagnation. Bai Zhu strengthens the spleen,
dries dampness and protects anti-pathogenic qi from being damaged.

This formula drains downward and clears heat transformed from
stagnation. It is especially indicated for severe food stagnation
coupled with damp heat. It also acts to stop diarrhea, known as
"treating diarrhea (due to food stagnation) with purgatives".

**\[Method of administration\]** 6-9g water pills for each dose, 2 doses
a day (a smaller dose for children); or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Promotes digestion, relieves food stagnation, clears
heat and resolves dampness.

**\[Indications\]** Food stagnation due to damp heat: Abdominal
distension and pain, diarrhea or constipation, dark yellow urine, a
yellow, greasy tongue coating, and a deep, forceful pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for food
stagnation due to damp heat. Over time, food stagnation may produce
dampness and transform into heat. Alternatively, patients may have damp
heat coupled with food stagnation. Food stagnation obstructing the flow
of qi causes abdominal distension and pain. Downward flow of damp heat
causes diarrhea. Damp heat obstructing the flow of intestinal qi causes
constipation. Downward flow of damp heat to the urinary bladder causes
dark yellow urine. As a result, the treatment strategies are to promote
digestion, relieve food stagnation, resolve dampness, and clear heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal distension and pain,
diarrhea or constipation, a yellow, greasy tongue coating, and a deep,
forceful pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For severe abdominal fullness and distension, add *Mu Xiang* (Radix
    Aucklandiae) and *Bing Lang* (Semen Arecae) to circulate qi and
    relieve distension.

-   For a poor appetite, add *Shan Zha* (Fructus Crataegi) and *Ji Nei
    Jing* (Endothelium Corneum Gigeriae Galli) to promote digestion and
    relieve food stagnation.

-   For severe abdominal pain, add *Shao Yao* (Radix Paeoniae) and *Gan
    Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma Glycyrrhizae) to relieve spams and stop pain.

3\. Modern use: functional GI disorder, bacillary dysentery, enteritis
and dyspepsia that can be diagnosed as food stagnation due to damp heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
pregnant women. It is not indicated for dysentery or diarrhea without
stagnation.

***Mu Xiang Bing Lang Wan* (Aucklandia and Areca Seed Pill)**

*Ru Men Shi Qin* (Confucians\' Duties to Their Parents)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarchs | > 木香  | *mù xiāng*  | 30g  | Radix Aucklandiae          |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+
|          | > 槟榔  | *bīng láng* | 30g  | Semen Arecae               |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| **Min    | >       | *qiān niú   | 120g | Semen Pharbitidis          |
| isters** |  牵牛子 | zĭ*         |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 大黄  | *dà huáng*  | 90g  | Radix et Rhizoma Rhei      |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| **Assi   | > 香附  | *xiāng fù*  | 120g | Rhizoma Cyperi             |
| stants** |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 莪术  | *é zhú*     | 30g  | Rhizoma Curcumae           |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 青皮  | *qīng pí*   | 30g  | Pericarpium Citri          |
|          |         |             |      | Reticulatae Viride         |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 陈皮  | *chén pí*   | 30g  | Pericarpium Citri          |
|          |         |             |      | Reticulatae                |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 黄连  | *huáng      | 30g  | Rhizoma Coptidis           |
|          |         | lián*       |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 黄柏  | *huáng băi* | 90g  | Cortex Phellodendri        |
|          |         |             |      | Chinensis                  |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarch: *Mu Xian*g and *Bing Lang* circulate qi, relieve food
stagnation, alleviate abdominal fullness, distension and pain, and
resolve tenesmus.

Ministers: *Qian Niu Zi* and *Da Huang* promote bowel movements and
clear heat.

Assistants: *Xiang Fu* and *E Zhu* soothe the liver and break qi. *Qing
Pi* and *Chen Pi* regulate qi and assist *Mu Xiang* and *Bing Lang* in
moving qi and relieving food stagnation. *Huang Lian* and *Huang Ba*i
clear heat, dry dampness and stop diarrhea.

The ingredients in this formula circulate qi, drain downward, clear heat
and circulate blood. It is indicated for diarrhea/dysentery due to food
stagnation (known as treating diarrhea with purgatives).

Like *Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan*, this formula is also indicated for dysentery
or constipation due to damp heat. However, it has a stronger effect in
moving qi and is therefore indicated for severe abdominal fullness and
distension due to qi stagnation, whereas *Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan* has a
strong effect in clearing heat and resolving dampness and is therefore
indicated for food stagnation due to damp heat.

**\[Method of administration\]** 3-6g water pills for each dose, 2 doses
a day (a smaller dose for children); or decoct with water.

**\[Functions\]** Circulates qi, relieves food stagnation, and clears
heat transformed from food stagnation.

**\[Indications\]** Dysentery, food stagnation: pediatric dysentery,
tenesmus, abdominal fullness and distension, constipation, a yellow,
greasy tongue coating, and a deep, excessive pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for severe food
stagnation coupled with damp heat. Food stagnation intertwined with damp
heat may greatly affect the flow of qi, causing constipation and
abdominal fullness, distension and pain. Damp heat impairing the
transformation and transportation of the spleen and stomach may cause
diarrhea, dysentery, and tenesmus. Since damp heat may flow downward to
aggravate food stagnation, the treatment strategies are to move qi,
relieve food stagnation, and clear heat.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: Abdominal distension and pain,
diarrhea or constipation, tenesmus, a yellow, greasy tongue coating, and
a deep, excessive pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For a poor appetite, add *Shan Zha* (Fructus Crataegi), *Shen Qu*
    (Massa Medicata Fermentata) and *Lai Fu Zi* (Semen Raphani) to
    promote digestion and harmonize the stomach.

-   For a thick, greasy tongue coating, add *Cang Zhu* (Rhizoma
    Atractylodis) to dry dampness and resolve turbidity.

3\. Modern use: bacillary dysentery, acute/chronic gastroenteritis and
acute/chronic cholecystitis that can be diagnosed as stagnation of damp
heat.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is contraindicated for
pregnant women. It is not indicated for dysentery or diarrhea without
stagnation.

**17.2 Formulas that strengthen the spleen to promote digestion**

Formulas that strengthen the spleen to promote digestion are indicated
for food stagnation due to spleen deficiency. Signs and symptoms include
a poor appetite, indigestion, abdominal stuffiness, loose stools,
fatigue, a pale tongue with a white coating, and a weak pulse.
Digestion-promoting herbs include *Shan Zha* (Fructus Crataegi), *Shen
Qu* (Massa Medicata Fermentata) and *Mai Ya* (Fructus Hordei
Germinatus). Spleen qi-supplementing herbs include *Ren Shen* (Radix et
Rhizoma Ginseng), *Dang Shen* (Radix Codonopsis), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma
Atractylodis Macrocephalae) and *Gan Cao* (Radix et Rhizoma
Glycyrrhizae). Typical formulas include *Jian Pi Wan* (Spleen-Fortifying
Pill) and *Zhi Shi Xiao Pi Wan* (Immature Bitter Orange and
Glomus-Dispersing Pill).

***Jian Pi Wan* (Spleen-Fortifying Pill)**

*Zheng Zhi Shun Sheng* (Standards for Diagnosis and Treatment)

**\[Ingredients\]**

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Monarchs | > 白术  | *bái zhú*   | 75g  | Rhizoma Atractylodis       |
|          |         |             |      | Macrocephalae              |
+==========+=========+=============+======+============================+

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| M        | > 神曲  | *shén qū*   | 30g  | Massa Medicata Fermentata  |
| inisters |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

| sistants |         |             |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 砂仁  | *shā rén*   | 30g  | Fructus Amomi              |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 陈皮  | *chén pí*   | 30g  | Pericarpium Citri          |
|          |         |             |      | Reticulatae                |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | >       | *ròu dòu    | 30g  | Semen Myristicae           |
|          |  肉豆蔻 | kòu*        |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 山楂  | *shān zhā*  | 30g  | Fructus Crataegi           |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
|          | > 黄连  | *huáng      | 30g  | Rhizoma Coptidis           |
|          |         | lián*       |      |                            |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+
| Envoy    | > 甘草  | *gān căo*   | 22g  | Radix et Rhizoma           |
|          |         |             |      | Glycyrrhizae               |
+----------+---------+-------------+------+----------------------------+

**\[Explanations of the formula\]**

Monarchs: *Bai Zhu* and *Fu Ling* strengthen the spleen, resolve
dampness and stop diarrhea.

Ministers: *Shen Qu* and *Mai Ya* promote digestion and harmonize the
stomach. *Ren Shen* and *Shan Yao* tonify qi and strengthen the spleen.

Assistants: *Mu Xiang*, *Sha Ren*, *Chen Pi* and *Rou Dou Kou* regulate
qi, increases appetite, resolve dampness and benefit the spleen. *Shan
Yao* and *Rou Dou Kou* can also astringe the intestine and stop
diarrhea. Huang Lian clears heat, and dries dampness.

Envoy: *Gan Cao* tonifies qi, harmonizes the spleen and stomach, and
coordinates the other ingredients.

The formula contains spleen qi-tonifying herbs and qi-circulating and
digestion-promoting herbs. It acts to reduce without damaging
anti-pathogenic qi and tonifies without causing stagnation. Because it
contains more spleen qi-tonifying herbs, it is primarily a tonifying
formula.

**\[Method of administration\]** 6-9g water pills for each dose, 2 doses
a day.

**\[Functions\]** Strengthens the spleen, harmonizes the stomach,
promotes digestion and stops diarrhea.

**\[Indications\]** Food stagnation due to spleen deficiency: A poor
appetite, indigestion, abdominal stuffiness, loose stools, fatigue, a
greasy and slightly yellow tongue coating, and a weak pulse.

**\[Diagnosis & treatment\]** This is a common formula for food
stagnation due to spleen deficiency. The spleen and stomach failing to
transport and transform causes a poor appetite, indigestion, and loose
stools. Insufficient generation of qi and blood causes fatigue and a
weak pulse. Food stagnation obstructing the flow of qi causes abdominal
stuffiness. Food stagnation transforming into heat may cause a greasy,
slightly yellow tongue coating. As a result, the treatment strategies
are to strengthen the spleen and promote digestion.

**\[Clinical use\]**

1\. Characteristic signs and symptoms: A poor appetite, loose stools,
abdominal stuffiness, a greasy tongue coating, and a weak pulse.

2\. Modifications

-   For deficiency cold, add *Gan Jiang* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng) and
    *Liang Jiang* (Rhizoma Alpiniae Officinarum) to warm the spleen and
    stomach and dispel cold.

-   For diarrhea due to dampness, add *Yi Yi Ren* (Semen Coicis), *Bian
    Dou* (Semen Lablab Album) and *Ze Xie* (Rhizoma Alismatis) to drain
    dampness and stop diarrhea.

3\. Modern use: Chronic gastritis, chronic enteritis and functional
dyspepsia that can be diagnosed as food stagnation due to spleen
deficiency.

4\. Cautions & contraindications: This formula is not indicated for
excessive food stagnation.

**\[Annex Formula\]** *Ge Hua Jie Jiu Tang* (Sober-up Decoction with
Flos Puerariae Lobatae) from the *Pi Wei Lun* (Treatise on the Spleen
and Stomach)

\[Ingredients\] *Bai Dou Kou* (Fructus Amomi Kravanh), *Sha Ren*
(Fructus Amomi), *Ge Hua* (Flos Puerariae Lobatae), *Gan Jiang* (Radix
et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Shen Qu* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Ze Xie*
(Rhizoma Alismatis), *Bai Zhu* (Rhizoma Atractylodis Macrocephalae),
*Chen Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae), *Zhu Ling* (Polyporus), *Ren
Shen* (Radix et Rhizoma Ginseng), *Fu Ling* (Poria), *Mu Xiang* (Radix
Aucklandiae) and *Qing Pi* (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae Viride).

\[Actions\] Relieves hangover, regulates qi and strengthens the spleen

\[Indications\] Alcoholic spleen deficiency coupled with dampness

This formula uses a large dose of *Ge Hua* to relieve hangover. It
contains digestion-promoting and qi-regulating herbs, coupled with
qi-supplementing and spleen-fortifying herbs. It induces sweating and
promotes urination. Compared with *Jian Pi Wan*, this formula is more
directed at tonifying the spleen following a long period of alcohol use,
whereas *Jian Pi Wan* is more directed at reducing food stagnation due
to spleen deficiency.

***Zhi Shi Xiao Pi Wan* (Immature Bitter Orange and Glomus-Dispersing
Pill)**

*Lan Shi Mi Cang* (Secrets from the Orchid Chamber)

**\[Ingredients\]**

| Mo    | > 枳实 | *zhĭ   | 15g | Fructus Aurantii Immaturus         |
| narch |        | shí*   |     |                                    |
+=======+========+========+=====+====================================+

| ister |        | pò*    |     |                                    |

|       |        | zhú*   |     |                                    |

|       |        | yá*    |     |                                    |
